topics.title: Familiarize and understand basics of network configuration files, networking commands and the functioning of system calls used for network programming in Linux
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarize and understand basics of network configuration files, networking commands and the functioning of system calls used for network programming in Linux

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme – PCERL407 (Computer Networking Lab)**, this experiment forms the foundational exposure to **Linux-based network administration and socket-level network programming**. The experiment is classified under **Course Outcome CO1** and **CO2** of the syllabus, which mandate that students "Familiarize with networking commands and configuration files in Linux" and "Implement network programming using system calls".

The topic is broadly divisible into three intellectual pillars:

1. **Network Configuration Files** — Static, text-based declarative files located under `/etc/` that govern hostname resolution, IP address assignment, routing tables, and name-service switches.
2. **Networking Commands** — Interactive shell utilities (both legacy `net-tools` and modern `iproute2`) used to inspect, configure, and troubleshoot the TCP/IP stack at runtime.
3. **System Calls for Network Programming** — Kernel-level entry points (wrapped by `glibc` in user-space) that allow a C program to create **sockets**, bind them to addresses, establish connections, and exchange data — the lowest user-accessible layer of the OSI model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Students are expected to demonstrate hands-on proficiency in **at least 12 networking commands**, **5 configuration files**, and **all 5 phases of the socket API** (`socket()` → `bind()` → `listen()` → `accept()` / `connect()` → `send()`/`recv()` → `close()`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal System"

To make this immediately intuitive, imagine your Linux machine as a **house in a city**, and the network as the **postal system**:

| Network Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why It Matters |
|-----------------|--------------------|----------------|
| IP Address | House's street address | Unique locator of your machine |
| Subnet Mask | Pin code / locality | Defines your neighborhood |
| Default Gateway | Local post office | Exit point to the outside world |
| DNS Server | Phone book directory | Translates "google.com" → "142.250.x.x" |
| `/etc/hosts` | Personal diary of nicknames | Manually maintained "phone book" |
| Port Number | Apartment number (0–65535) | Identifies the specific "tenant" (process) |
| Socket | Telephone instrument | Endpoint for two-way conversation |
| TCP Connection | Dedicated phone call | Reliable, ordered, connection-oriented |
| UDP Datagram | Postal letter | Connectionless, fire-and-forget |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Socket (RFC 793):**
> A *socket* is the **endpoint of a bidirectional inter-process communication flow** across a computer network. In Linux, it is represented by an integer file descriptor that the kernel returns after a successful `socket()` system call.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars at a Glance

**Pillar A — Configuration Files (Declarative):**
These files are *read by daemons and kernel subsystems at boot or via `systemctl restart`* to initialize the network stack. They do not actively "do" anything — they are blueprints.

**Pillar B — Networking Commands (Imperative):**
These are *active executables* that read/modify the kernel's **netlink** sockets in real time. Their changes are **ephemeral** (lost on reboot) unless persisted into the configuration files of Pillar A.

**Pillar C — System Calls (Programmatic):**
These are *library/kernel functions* that let a C program directly request network services from the kernel via the **POSIX Socket API** standardized in IEEE Std 1003.1.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Lab Exam:** Always begin your viva by stating these three pillars. Examiners love a structured answer, and you secure 2–3 bonus marks instantly.

## 1.4 The Layered Context in the TCP/IP Model

$$\text{Application Layer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Transport Layer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Internet Layer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Link Layer}$$

- **Configuration files** operate primarily at Layers 1–3 (link, IP, routing).
- **Networking commands** span Layers 2–4 (link, IP, transport).
- **System calls** expose Layers 3–4 (IP, TCP/UDP) and the Layer 5 socket interface.

This hierarchy will be the *spine* of all subsequent sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET

## 2.1 Pillar A — Essential Network Configuration Files

### 2.1.1 `/etc/hosts` — The Local Name Database

A plain-text file mapping **IP addresses to hostnames** that the resolver checks **before** querying DNS. The resolver consults this file based on the `hosts:` directive in `/etc/nsswitch.conf`.

```text
# Format: <IP-address>   <canonical-hostname>   [aliases...]
127.0.0.1       localhost
192.168.1.10    server.kerala.edu     server
10.0.0.5        db.internal
```

**Why it matters:** Used for *quick local resolution*, *blocking malicious domains* (pointing them to `0.0.0.0`), and *testing* before DNS propagation.

### 2.1.2 `/etc/resolv.conf` — DNS Resolver Configuration

Specifies the **DNS servers** and **search domains** used when `/etc/hosts` does not contain a match.

```text
# Generated by NetworkManager
nameserver 8.8.8.8
nameserver 8.8.4.4
search kerala.edu
options timeout:2 attempts:3
```

> [!NOTE]
> In modern systemd-based distros, `/etc/resolv.conf` is often a **symlink** to `/run/systemd/resolve/stub-resolv.conf`. Manual edits get overwritten by `resolvconf` or `NetworkManager`.

### 2.1.3 `/etc/hostname` — The System's Host Name

A single-line file containing the system's hostname. Modified by `hostnamectl` in modern Linux.

```text
kerala-pc-12
```

### 2.1.4 `/etc/network/interfaces` (Debian) or `/etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-*` (RHEL)

The **master file for static IP configuration** on traditional Linux systems. Example for a Debian-style system:

```text
auto eth0
iface eth0 inet static
    address 192.168.1.50
    netmask 255.255.255.0
    gateway 192.168.1.1
    dns-nameservers 8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4
```

In **RHEL/CentOS/Fedora** systems, each interface has its own file:

```text
# /etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-eth0
DEVICE=eth0
BOOTPROTO=static
ONBOOT=yes
IPADDR=192.168.1.50
NETMASK=255.255.255.0
GATEWAY=192.168.1.1
```

### 2.1.5 `/etc/nsswitch.conf` — Name Service Switch

Tells the C library **the order of databases** to consult for name resolution, authentication, and group lookups.

```text
passwd:     files systemd
group:      files systemd
shadow:     files
hosts:      files dns myhostname
networks:   files
protocols:  files db
services:   files db
```

The line `hosts: files dns` instructs the resolver to check `/etc/hosts` **first**, then fall back to **DNS**.

### 2.1.6 Other Important Files (For Advanced Marks)

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/etc/services` | Maps well-known port numbers to service names (e.g., 80 → http) |
| `/etc/protocols` | Lists protocol numbers (e.g., 6 → tcp, 17 → udp) |
| `/etc/hosts.allow` / `/etc/hosts.deny` | TCP Wrappers (legacy access control) |

## 2.2 Pillar B — Essential Networking Commands

The commands fall into **two generations** — KTU examiners expect knowledge of both.

| Generation | Package | Examples | Status |
|------------|---------|----------|--------|
| Legacy (BSD-style) | `net-tools` | `ifconfig`, `netstat`, `route`, `arp` | Deprecated, but still asked in exams |
| Modern (iproute2) | `iproute2` | `ip`, `ss`, `tc` | Preferred since Linux 2.2+ |

### 2.2.1 The `ip` Command (Modern Replacement for `ifconfig`)

| Sub-command | Purpose | Example |
|-------------|---------|---------|
| `ip addr` | Display all IP addresses | `ip addr show` |
| `ip link` | Display/configure L2 interfaces | `ip link set eth0 up` |
| `ip route` | Display/configure routing table | `ip route add default via 192.168.1.1` |
| `ip neigh` | Display ARP table | `ip neigh show` |
| `ip -s link` | Show interface statistics | `ip -s link show eth0` |

### 2.2.2 The `ifconfig` Command (Legacy)

```bash
ifconfig              # Show all active interfaces
ifconfig eth0         # Show specific interface
ifconfig eth0 192.168.1.50 netmask 255.255.255.0 up
```

### 2.2.3 Connectivity and Diagnostic Commands

| Command | Purpose | Protocol Layer |
|---------|---------|----------------|
| `ping <host>` | Test reachability using ICMP Echo | Layer 3 |
| `traceroute <host>` | Show the path packets take | Layer 3 |
| `tracepath <host>` | traceroute without root | Layer 3 |
| `mtr <host>` | Combines ping + traceroute | Layer 3 |
| `nslookup <domain>` | Query DNS (legacy) | Layer 7 |
| `dig <domain>` | Detailed DNS query (modern) | Layer 7 |
| `host <domain>` | Simple DNS lookup | Layer 7 |

### 2.2.4 Connection and Socket Inspection Commands

| Command | Purpose | Replaces |
|---------|---------|----------|
| `netstat -tulnp` | Show all listening/active TCP/UDP ports | (legacy) |
| `ss -tulnp` | Modern replacement for netstat | `netstat` |
| `arp -a` | Show ARP cache | `ip neigh` |
| `route -n` | Show kernel routing table | `ip route` |
| `tcpdump -i eth0` | Packet capture (requires root) | (n/a) |
| `wget <url>` / `curl <url>` | Test HTTP connectivity | (n/a) |

### 2.2.5 Hostname and Domain Commands

| Command | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `hostname` | Display/set system hostname |
| `hostname -I` | Display all assigned IP addresses |
| `domainname` | Show NIS domain name |
| `dnsdomainname` | Show DNS domain name |
| `fqdn` | Show fully qualified domain name |

## 2.3 Pillar C — System Calls for Network Programming (The POSIX Socket API)

The C programmer accesses the kernel's network stack through a **family of system calls** standardized by POSIX.1g / IEEE Std 1003.1.

### 2.3.1 The Five Core System Calls for TCP

| # | System Call | Header | Purpose |
|---|-------------|--------|---------|
| 1 | `socket()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Create an endpoint (returns a file descriptor) |
| 2 | `bind()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Associate socket with a local IP + port |
| 3 | `listen()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Mark socket as passive (server-side) |
| 4 | `accept()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Block until a client connects; return new FD |
| 5 | `connect()` | `<sys/socket.h>` | Actively open a connection (client-side) |

### 2.3.2 Data Transfer System Calls

| System Call | Purpose |
|-------------|---------|
| `send()` / `recv()` | TCP or connected UDP transfer |
| `sendto()` / `recvfrom()` | Unconnected UDP transfer |
| `read()` / `write()` | Generic I/O on a socket FD |
| `sendmsg()` / `recvmsg()` | Scatter-gather I/O (advanced) |
| `close(fd)` | Terminate the socket |

### 2.3.3 Name and Address Resolution System Calls

| System Call | Purpose |
|-------------|---------|
| `gethostbyname()` | Resolve hostname → IP (legacy, IPv4 only) |
| `getaddrinfo()` | Modern, protocol-agnostic resolution (IPv4 + IPv6) |
| `inet_aton()` | Convert dotted-decimal string → 32-bit network byte order |
| `inet_ntoa()` | Convert 32-bit network byte order → dotted-decimal string |
| `inet_pton()` | "Presentation to network" — modern, supports IPv6 |
| `inet_ntop()` | "Network to presentation" — modern, supports IPv6 |

### 2.3.4 Byte-Order Conversion System Calls

**The Network Byte Order problem:** Different CPU architectures store multi-byte integers in different orders (little-endian vs. big-endian). The network mandates **big-endian** transmission. The following functions ensure portability:

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `htons()` | Host-to-Network **Short** (16-bit, typically port) |
| `htonl()` | Host-to-Network **Long** (32-bit, typically IPv4) |
| `ntohs()` | Network-to-Host **Short** |
| `ntohl()` | Network-to-Host **Long** |

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET — Master Reference Table

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table mentally — it is the single most important summary for viva and Part A questions.**

### 2.4.1 Configuration Files

| File | Layer | Edited By | Persistent? |
|------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| `/etc/hosts` | L7 (resolution) | Admin manually | Yes |
| `/etc/resolv.conf` | L7 (DNS) | DHCP / NetworkManager | Yes (if not symlink) |
| `/etc/hostname` | L3/L7 | `hostnamectl` | Yes |
| `/etc/network/interfaces` | L1–L3 | Admin manually | Yes |
| `/etc/nsswitch.conf` | L7 (lookup order) | Admin | Yes |
| `/etc/services` | L4 (port→name) | System package | Yes |

### 2.4.2 Commands at a Glance

| Command | Layer | What It Reveals |
|---------|-------|-----------------|
| `ip addr` | L3 | IP, mask, scope |
| `ip route` | L3 | Routing table |
| `ip neigh` | L2 | ARP cache |
| `ping` | L3 | ICMP reachability |
| `traceroute` | L3 | Hop-by-hop path |
| `nslookup` / `dig` | L7 | DNS records |
| `netstat` / `ss` | L4 | Open sockets, connections |
| `arp` | L2 | IP↔MAC mappings |

### 2.4.3 System Call Family Tree

$$\text{Socket API} = \underbrace{\{\text{socket, bind, listen, accept, connect}\}}_{\text{Setup}} \cup \underbrace{\{\text{send, recv, read, write}\}}_{\text{I/O}} \cup \underbrace{\{\text{close, shutdown}\}}_{\text{Teardown}}$$

### 2.4.4 Function Signatures (Most Exam-Relevant)

$$ \text{int\ socket(int\ domain,\ int\ type,\ int\ protocol);} $$

$$ \text{int\ bind(int\ sockfd,\ const\ struct\ sockaddr\ *addr,\ socklen\_t\ addrlen);} $$

$$ \text{int\ listen(int\ sockfd,\ int\ backlog);} $$

$$ \text{int\ accept(int\ sockfd,\ struct\ sockaddr\ *addr,\ socklen\_t\ *addrlen);} $$

$$ \text{int\ connect(int\ sockfd,\ const\ struct\ sockaddr\ *addr,\ socklen\_t\ addrlen);} $$

$$ \text{ssize\_t\ send(int\ sockfd,\ const\ void\ *buf,\ size\_t\ len,\ int\ flags);} $$

$$ \text{ssize\_t\ recv(int\ sockfd,\ void\ *buf,\ size\_t\ len,\ int\ flags);} $$

### 2.4.5 Critical Constants (Must Memorize)

| Constant | Decimal | Hex | Meaning |
|----------|---------|-----|---------|
| `AF_INET` | **2** | 0x02 | IPv4 address family |
| `AF_INET6` | **10** | 0x0A | IPv6 address family |
| `SOCK_STREAM` | **1** | 0x01 | TCP (stream-oriented) |
| `SOCK_DGRAM` | **2** | 0x02 | UDP (datagram-oriented) |
| `INADDR_ANY` | **0** | 0x00000000 | Bind to all interfaces |
| `INADDR_LOOPBACK` | **0x7F000001** | 2130706433 | 127.0.0.1 |
| `INADDR_BROADCAST` | **0xFFFFFFFF** | 4294967295 | 255.255.255.255 |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters Beyond the Lab

- **Network Forensics:** `tcpdump`, `ss`, and `/var/log` correlation rely on understanding these primitives.
- **Cloud DevOps:** Every Dockerfile, Kubernetes pod, and AWS EC2 instance boots by reading `/etc/hosts`, `/etc/resolv.conf`, and `hostname`.
- **Embedded Linux / IoT:** Routers, smart-home devices, and automotive ECUs use a stripped-down subset of these system calls in their firmware.
- **Cybersecurity:** `nmap`, `ncat`, and reverse shells are all built on the same `socket()`/`connect()`/`bind()` primitives you are learning today.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **Section 3 Focus:** This is the "deep-work" section. We dissect the *why* and *how* of the key algorithms, conversions, and the canonical client-server flow. Every algebraic transition, every IP conversion, and every state-machine step is shown exhaustively.

## 3.1 Derivation — Subnet Mask and CIDR Notation

The **CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing)** notation `192.168.1.0/24` is derived from the binary structure of the IP address. This is a high-frequency KTU question.

### 3.1.1 The Algorithm

A 32-bit IPv4 address is divided into:
- **Network prefix:** the first $N$ bits (given by the slash number)
- **Host identifier:** the remaining $32 - N$ bits

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Conversion of `/24`

**Step 1:** Write 24 in binary.

$$24 = 16 + 8 = 11110000_2$$

**Step 2:** Build a 32-bit string with 24 ones followed by 8 zeros.

$$11111111\ 11111111\ 11111111\ 00000000$$

**Step 3:** Convert each octet back to decimal.

$$11111111_2 = 255,\quad 00000000_2 = 0$$

**Step 4:** The subnet mask is therefore:

$$255.255.255.0$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Example — `/27`

$$27 = 16 + 8 + 2 + 1 = 00011011_2$$

The 32-bit mask is:

$$11111111\ 11111111\ 11111111\ 11100000$$

Converting the last octet:

$$11100000_2 = 128 + 64 + 32 = 224$$

So `/27` $\equiv$ `255.255.255.224`.

### 3.1.4 The Number of Usable Hosts Formula

For a prefix of length $N$:

$$H_{\max} = 2^{32-N} - 2$$

The "$-2$" accounts for the **network address** (all host bits = 0) and the **broadcast address** (all host bits = 1).

**Worked Example for /27:**

$$H_{\max} = 2^{32-27} - 2 = 2^5 - 2 = 32 - 2 = 30\ \text{hosts}$$

### 3.1.5 Quick-Reference CIDR Table

| CIDR | Mask | Usable Hosts | Typical Use |
|------|------|--------------|-------------|
| `/8` | 255.0.0.0 | $2^{24} - 2 = 16{,}777{,}214$ | Class A legacy |
| `/16` | 255.255.0.0 | $2^{16} - 2 = 65{,}534$ | Class B legacy |
| `/24` | 255.255.255.0 | $2^8 - 2 = 254$ | Small LAN |
| `/27` | 255.255.255.224 | 30 | Departmental subnet |
| `/30` | 255.255.255.252 | 2 | Point-to-point link |
| `/32` | 255.255.255.255 | 1 (single host) | Loopback / host route |

## 3.2 Derivation — Network Byte Order Conversion

### 3.2.1 The Problem

Consider the 16-bit port number `8080`. Stored in **little-endian** memory (x86) it occupies two bytes as:

$$\text{Memory low} = 0x90,\quad \text{Memory high} = 0x1F$$

But the network expects **big-endian** transmission:

$$\text{First byte on wire} = 0x1F,\quad \text{Second byte on wire} = 0x90$$

### 3.2.2 The Algorithm of `htons()` on x86

**Step 1:** Receive the 16-bit unsigned integer.

$$x = 8080_{10}$$

**Step 2:** Convert to binary (16 bits).

$$8080 = 8192 - 112 = 0x1F90 = 0001\ 1111\ 1001\ 0000_2$$

**Step 3:** Swap the high and low bytes.

$$\text{After swap:}\ 1001\ 0000\ 0001\ 1111_2 = 0x901F$$

**Step 4:** This is now in **big-endian (network)** byte order, ready to be placed on the wire.

### 3.2.3 Verification of `htonl()` for an IPv4 Address

Let us convert `192.168.1.1` to network byte order.

**Step 1:** As a 32-bit integer (host byte order, little-endian x86):

$$\text{Memory layout:}\ 0x01,\ 0x01,\ 0xA8,\ 0xC0$$

**Step 2:** After `htonl()` byte-swap:

$$\text{Network layout:}\ 0xC0,\ 0xA8,\ 0x01,\ 0x01$$

**Step 3:** Reading the network layout as a 32-bit integer:

$$0xC0A80101 = 192 \cdot 16777216 + 168 \cdot 65536 + 1 \cdot 256 + 1 = 3232235777$$

This is why the `ip addr` command on a Linux server often shows addresses as large integers (e.g., `inet 192.168.1.1/24 brd 192.168.1.255`) — internally, the kernel stores them in network byte order.

## 3.3 Derivation — Port Number Ranges

The 16-bit port space is divided by IANA into three ranges. The derivation is based on the first bit(s) of the 16-bit value.

| Range | Numeric Span | Count | Bit Pattern (First 2 bits) | Purpose |
|-------|--------------|-------|----------------------------|---------|
| **Well-Known** | 0 – 1023 | 1024 | `0xxxxxxx xxxxxxxx` | Reserved for privileged services (HTTP, SSH, FTP) |
| **Registered** | 1024 – 49151 | 48128 | `10xxxxxx xxxxxxxx` | Assigned by IANA to user applications |
| **Dynamic/Private** | 49152 – 65535 | 16384 | `11xxxxxx xxxxxxxx` | Ephemeral client ports |

**Bit-pattern derivation:**

For the **Well-Known** range, the highest bit is `0`, so:

$$0 \le P \le 2^{15} - 1 = 32767\ \text{(upper bound for first bit = 0)}$$

IANA further restricts the first 10 bits to `0000000000`, yielding:

$$0 \le P \le 2^{10} - 1 = 1023$$

For the **Registered** range, the first two bits are `10`, so:

$$2^{15} = 32768\ \text{lower bound} \le P \le 2^{15} + 2^{14} - 1 = 49151$$

For the **Dynamic** range, the first two bits are `11`, giving:

$$49152 \le P \le 2^{16} - 1 = 65535$$

## 3.4 Step-by-Step — The Canonical TCP Server State Machine

The lifecycle of a TCP server, expressed as a state transition diagram implemented in C via system calls.

### 3.4.1 State Transition Table

| Current State | Event / System Call | Next State | Description |
|---------------|---------------------|------------|-------------|
| `CLOSED` | `socket()` | `CREATED` | FD obtained |
| `CREATED` | `bind()` | `BOUND` | Address assigned |
| `BOUND` | `listen()` | `LISTENING` | Accept queue armed |
| `LISTENING` | `accept()` blocks | `LISTENING` (waits) | No client yet |
| `LISTENING` | Client SYN arrives | `SYN_RCVD` (kernel) | Three-way handshake begins |
| `SYN_RCVD` | Kernel completes handshake | `ESTABLISHED` | New FD returned by `accept()` |
| `ESTABLISHED` | `send()` / `recv()` | `ESTABLISHED` | Data exchange |
| `ESTABLISHED` | `close()` (read/write) | `CLOSE_WAIT` → `LAST_ACK` | Graceful shutdown |
| `ESTABLISHED` | `close()` (RST) | `CLOSED` | Abrupt termination |

### 3.4.2 The Three-Way Handshake (Numerical Trace)

Let the client pick an initial sequence number (ISN) of $S_c = 100$ and the server pick $S_s = 300$.

| Step | Direction | TCP Flag | Seq | Ack | Meaning |
|------|-----------|----------|-----|-----|---------|
| 1 | Client → Server | `SYN` | $S_c = 100$ | — | Client requests connection |
| 2 | Server → Client | `SYN + ACK` | $S_s = 300$ | $S_c + 1 = 101$ | Server agrees, acknowledges |
| 3 | Client → Server | `ACK` | $S_c + 1 = 101$ | $S_s + 1 = 301$ | Connection established |

After step 3, both sides are in `ESTABLISHED` state. The client can now `send()` and the server can `recv()`.

### 3.4.3 The FIN-WAIT-2 Graceful Close

When the client calls `close()`, the following exchange occurs:

| Step | Direction | Flag | Meaning |
|------|-----------|------|---------|
| 1 | Client → Server | `FIN` | "I have no more data" |
| 2 | Server → Client | `ACK` | Acknowledges the FIN |
| 3 | Server → Client | `FIN` | "I have no more data either" |
| 4 | Client → Server | `ACK` | Acknowledges, enters `TIME_WAIT` |

The `TIME_WAIT` state lasts for $2 \times \text{MSL}$ (Maximum Segment Lifetime), typically 240 seconds, to ensure that any delayed segments are properly drained from the network.

## 3.5 The Analytical Matrix — System Call Decision Tree

This matrix helps a student choose the *right* system call for a given scenario during the lab exam.

| If You Want To... | Use This System Call | Return Value on Success | Common Error |
|-------------------|----------------------|------------------------|--------------|
| Create a TCP socket | `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)` | Non-negative FD | `EACCES`, `EPERM` |
| Create a UDP socket | `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)` | Non-negative FD | `EMFILE` |
| Bind to port 8080 | `bind(fd, addr, len)` | `0` | `EADDRINUSE` |
| Listen with queue=5 | `listen(fd, 5)` | `0` | `EOPNOTSUPP` |
| Accept a client | `accept(fd, NULL, NULL)` | New FD (blocking) | `EAGAIN` (non-blocking) |
| Connect to server | `connect(fd, addr, len)` | `0` | `ECONNREFUSED`, `ETIMEDOUT` |
| Send 100 bytes | `send(fd, buf, 100, 0)` | Number of bytes sent | `EPIPE` |
| Receive up to 1024 B | `recv(fd, buf, 1024, 0)` | Number of bytes received | `ECONNRESET` |
| Convert "127.0.0.1" | `inet_pton(AF_INET, "127.0.0.1", &addr)` | `1` on success | `0` (invalid format) |
| Resolve "kerala.edu" | `getaddrinfo("kerala.edu", NULL, ...)` | `0` | `EAI_NONAME` |
| Convert port 80 | `htons(80)` | Network-order 16-bit | N/A (no error path) |
| Close the socket | `close(fd)` | `0` | `EBADF` |

## 3.6 Derivation — Checksum (Conceptual, for Viva Bonus)

The TCP/UDP checksum is a **16-bit one's complement sum** of a pseudo-header plus the segment data. While KTU does not require coding it, understanding its derivation is a **5-mark viva booster**.

**Step 1:** Construct a pseudo-header containing source IP (32 bits), destination IP (32 bits), zero (8 bits), protocol (8 bits), and segment length (16 bits) — total **96 bits**.

**Step 2:** Append the TCP/UDP header and data, padding the last word with a zero byte if odd-length.

**Step 3:** Sum all 16-bit words using one's complement addition (wrap-around carry added back).

**Step 4:** Take the one's complement of the final sum.

Mathematically, for a sequence of $n$ 16-bit words $W_1, W_2, \dots, W_n$:

$$ \text{Checksum} = \overline{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i} \quad \text{(one's complement sum)} $$

**Why one's complement?** It is endian-agnostic and detects all 1-bit errors plus most multi-bit errors, with very low hardware cost — a design choice dating back to the 1970s ARPANET.

## 3.7 Exhaustive Client-Server Lifecycle (The "5-Phase" Algorithm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important algorithm** for the 14-mark question. Memorize the server's 5 phases and the client's 3 phases.

### 3.7.1 TCP Server — 5 Phases

**Phase 1 — Socket Creation**
```c
int sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);
if (sockfd < 0) { perror("socket"); exit(1); }
```

**Phase 2 — Binding**
```c
struct sockaddr_in serv_addr;
memset(&serv_addr, 0, sizeof(serv_addr));
serv_addr.sin_family = AF_INET;
serv_addr.sin_addr.s_addr = htonl(INADDR_ANY);
serv_addr.sin_port = htons(PORT);
bind(sockfd, (struct sockaddr*)&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr));
```

**Phase 3 — Listening**
```c
listen(sockfd, 5);
```

**Phase 4 — Accepting**
```c
int newsockfd = accept(sockfd, NULL, NULL);
if (newsockfd < 0) { perror("accept"); }
```

**Phase 5 — Communication and Closing**
```c
char buffer[1024];
int n = recv(newsockfd, buffer, sizeof(buffer), 0);
send(newsockfd, "ACK", 3, 0);
close(newsockfd);
close(sockfd);
```

### 3.7.2 TCP Client — 3 Phases

**Phase 1 — Socket Creation** (same as server)
```c
int sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);
```

**Phase 2 — Connect** (replaces bind + listen + accept)
```c
struct sockaddr_in serv_addr;
serv_addr.sin_family = AF_INET;
serv_addr.sin_port = htons(PORT);
inet_pton(AF_INET, "127.0.0.1", &serv_addr.sin_addr);
connect(sockfd, (struct sockaddr*)&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr));
```

**Phase 3 — Communication and Close**
```c
send(sockfd, "Hello", 5, 0);
recv(sockfd, buffer, sizeof(buffer), 0);
close(sockfd);
```

## 3.8 Step-by-Step — DNS Resolution Walkthrough

When you type `ping www.kerala.edu` in a terminal, the following sequence occurs at the resolver layer:

**Step 1:** `ping` invokes `getaddrinfo("www.kerala.edu", NULL, hints, &res)` via the C library.

**Step 2:** The glibc resolver checks `/etc/nsswitch.conf`. For `hosts: files dns`, it queries `/etc/hosts` first.

**Step 3:** If no match, it reads `/etc/resolv.conf` to find the configured **nameservers** (e.g., `8.8.8.8`).

**Step 4:** The resolver sends a **DNS query (UDP port 53)** to `8.8.8.8` asking for the A record of `www.kerala.edu`.

**Step 5:** Google's DNS resolver recursively queries the root, TLD (`.edu`), and authoritative servers.

**Step 6:** The IP address (e.g., `203.0.113.42`) is returned to the resolver, which caches it according to the TTL.

**Step 7:** `getaddrinfo()` returns this address to `ping`.

**Step 8:** `ping` constructs an ICMP Echo Request packet with the destination IP and sends it via `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, IPPROTO_ICMP)`.

**Step 9:** If the host is reachable, it replies with an ICMP Echo Reply. Round-trip time is measured and printed.

This 9-step trace is a **favourite 14-mark question** for examiners testing the student's understanding of the *full stack*.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> Questions are tagged with **simulated KTU past-year tags** to mirror the actual question paper style. Part A carries 3 marks each, Part B carries 14 marks each with internal choice (Q9 OR Q10, Q11 OR Q12, etc., per KTU ESE pattern).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: List any three essential network configuration files in Linux and state their purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

The three most essential network configuration files in Linux are:

1. **`/etc/hosts`** — Maps IP addresses to hostnames locally, checked by the resolver before DNS. Used for manual name resolution and to block malicious sites.
2. **`/etc/resolv.conf`** — Specifies the DNS nameservers and search domains used for domain name resolution.
3. **`/etc/network/interfaces`** (Debian) or **`/etc/sysconfig/network-scripts/ifcfg-eth0`** (RHEL) — Stores static IP address, netmask, gateway, and DNS configuration for each network interface.

(Any other valid files such as `/etc/hostname`, `/etc/nsswitch.conf`, `/etc/services` are also acceptable.)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between `netstat` and `ss` commands. Why is `ss` preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `netstat` | `ss` (Socket Statistics) |
|--------|-----------|--------------------------|
| Package | `net-tools` (legacy) | `iproute2` (modern) |
| Speed | Slower, reads from `/proc/net/tcp` | Faster, talks directly to kernel via netlink |
| Output detail | Basic | Detailed — shows TCP state, congestion window, timers |
| Status | Deprecated since 2011 | Actively maintained |

`ss` is preferred because it is **faster**, provides **richer information** (e.g., TCP internal state variables, congestion window size), and is part of the **iproute2 toolkit** which is the modern standard for Linux network administration.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (A) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the **5 phases** of a TCP server with suitable system calls and their syntax. Draw the server-side state machine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a C program for a TCP **client** that connects to a server at IP `127.0.0.1` and port `8080`, sends the string `"Hello"`, and prints the server's reply. Explain each system call. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **TCP server lifecycle** consists of five sequential phases, each implemented by a dedicated system call from the POSIX Socket API.

**Phase 1 — Socket Creation (`socket()`):**
This call creates an endpoint for communication and returns a file descriptor.

$$\text{int\ socket(int\ domain,\ int\ type,\ int\ protocol);}$$

- `domain = AF_INET` (IPv4)
- `type = SOCK_STREAM` (TCP)
- `protocol = 0` (default)

**Phase 2 — Binding (`bind()`):**
This assigns a local IP address and port number to the socket.

$$\text{int\ bind(int\ sockfd,\ const\ struct\ sockaddr\ *addr,\ socklen\_t\ addrlen);}$$

**Phase 3 — Listening (`listen()`):**
Marks the socket as **passive** (server-side) and arms the kernel's accept queue with a backlog.

$$\text{int\ listen(int\ sockfd,\ int\ backlog);}$$

**Phase 4 — Accepting (`accept()`):**
This call **blocks** until a client connects, then returns a **new file descriptor** dedicated to that client.

$$\text{int\ accept(int\ sockfd,\ struct\ sockaddr\ *addr,\ socklen\_t\ *addrlen);}$$

**Phase 5 — Communication and Close (`send()`, `recv()`, `close()`):**
Data is exchanged using `send()` and `recv()`. When done, `close()` terminates the connection.

**State Machine Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> CREATED: socket()
    CREATED --> BOUND: bind()
    BOUND --> LISTENING: listen()
    LISTENING --> SYN_RCVD: Client SYN
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED: 3-way handshake
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT: Client FIN
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK: Server close()
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED: Final ACK
    CLOSED --> [*]
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <arpa/inet.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>

#define PORT 8080
#define SERVER_IP "127.0.0.1"
#define BUF_SIZE 1024

int main() {
    int sockfd;
    struct sockaddr_in serv_addr;
    char buffer[BUF_SIZE];

    // Phase 1: Create the socket
    sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);
    if (sockfd < 0) {
        perror("socket failed");
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    // Initialize server address structure
    memset(&serv_addr, 0, sizeof(serv_addr));
    serv_addr.sin_family = AF_INET;
    serv_addr.sin_port = htons(PORT);
    inet_pton(AF_INET, SERVER_IP, &serv_addr.sin_addr);

    // Phase 2: Connect to the server
    if (connect(sockfd, (struct sockaddr *)&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr)) < 0) {
        perror("connect failed");
        close(sockfd);
        exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }

    // Phase 3: Send a message
    const char *msg = "Hello";
    send(sockfd, msg, strlen(msg), 0);
    printf("Message sent to server.\n");

    // Phase 4: Receive the reply
    int n = recv(sockfd, buffer, BUF_SIZE - 1, 0);
    if (n > 0) {
        buffer[n] = '\0';
        printf("Server reply: %s\n", buffer);
    }

    // Phase 5: Close the socket
    close(sockfd);
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of each system call:**

| Call | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)` | Creates a TCP (stream) socket using IPv4. |
| `memset(&serv_addr, 0, sizeof(serv_addr))` | Zero-initializes the address structure to avoid garbage. |
| `inet_pton(AF_INET, SERVER_IP, &serv_addr.sin_addr)` | Converts the dotted-decimal string `"127.0.0.1"` into binary network byte order. |
| `connect(sockfd, ...)` | Triggers the kernel's three-way handshake with the server. |
| `send(sockfd, msg, strlen(msg), 0)` | Sends 5 bytes over the established connection. |
| `recv(sockfd, buffer, BUF_SIZE-1, 0)` | Blocks until data arrives; returns the number of bytes received. |
| `close(sockfd)` | Closes the socket and triggers the four-way FIN handshake. |

---

### Question B1 (B) [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks — Internal Choice]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three-way handshake** used to establish a TCP connection. Show the sequence numbers and acknowledge numbers in each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the `ip`, `ss`, and `dig` commands with at least two practical examples for each. Justify why `iproute2` is preferred over `net-tools` in modern Linux. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **TCP three-way handshake** is the procedure used to establish a reliable connection between a client and a server before any data is exchanged. It ensures that both sides agree on initial sequence numbers and that the channel is full-duplex.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    C->>S: SYN, Seq=100, Ack=-
    Note over S: State: SYN_RCVD
    S-->>C: SYN+ACK, Seq=300, Ack=101
    Note over C: State: ESTABLISHED
    C->>S: ACK, Seq=101, Ack=301
    Note over S: State: ESTABLISHED
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

**Step 1 — SYN (Synchronize):**
The client sends a TCP segment with the `SYN` flag set. It picks an **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)** — say $S_c = 100$. The client enters the `SYN_SENT` state.

$$\text{Segment 1:}\ \text{Flags} = \text{SYN},\ \text{Seq} = S_c$$

**Step 2 — SYN + ACK (Synchronize + Acknowledge):**
The server responds with both flags set. It picks its own ISN, $S_s = 300$, and acknowledges the client's SYN by setting $\text{Ack} = S_c + 1 = 101$. The server enters the `SYN_RCVD` state.

$$\text{Segment 2:}\ \text{Flags} = \text{SYN, ACK},\ \text{Seq} = S_s,\ \text{Ack} = S_c + 1$$

**Step 3 — ACK (Acknowledge):**
The client sends a final ACK with $\text{Seq} = S_c + 1 = 101$ and $\text{Ack} = S_s + 1 = 301$. Both sides now enter the `ESTABLISHED` state and data transfer can begin.

$$\text{Segment 3:}\ \text{Flags} = \text{ACK},\ \text{Seq} = S_c + 1,\ \text{Ack} = S_s + 1$$

**Why three steps?** A minimum of three messages is required so that each side can both **send** an initial sequence number and **confirm receipt** of the other's sequence number. Two steps would only synchronize in one direction.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**The `ip` command** (part of the `iproute2` suite):
- **Example 1:** Display all IP addresses on all interfaces:
  ```bash
  ip addr show
  ```
- **Example 2:** Add a secondary IP address to `eth0`:
  ```bash
  sudo ip addr add 192.168.1.100/24 dev eth0
  ```

**The `ss` command** (Socket Statistics):
- **Example 1:** Show all listening TCP sockets with process info:
  ```bash
  ss -tulnp
  ```
- **Example 2:** Show all established TCP connections:
  ```bash
  ss -t state established
  ```

**The `dig` command** (Domain Information Groper):
- **Example 1:** Query the A record for `google.com`:
  ```bash
  dig google.com
  ```
- **Example 2:** Query the MX (mail exchange) records for a domain:
  ```bash
  dig kerala.edu MX
  ```

**Why `iproute2` is preferred over `net-tools`:**

1. **Performance:** `iproute2` uses the kernel's netlink socket directly, while `net-tools` reads from `/proc`, which is slower.
2. **Modern features:** `iproute2` supports **multiple routing tables**, **tunnels**, **TC (traffic control)**, **netns (network namespaces)**, and **multicast** — all features absent from `net-tools`.
3. **Active maintenance:** `net-tools` has been officially deprecated since 2011. `iproute2` is actively developed.
4. **Consistency:** All `iproute2` sub-commands (`ip addr`, `ip route`, `ip link`, `ip neigh`) follow a uniform syntax, unlike the inconsistent `ifconfig`/`route`/`arp` triplet in `net-tools`.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Compiled from KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `htons()` on the port number.** This is the #1 mistake. The compiler does not warn you, but the kernel sees a port like `13606` instead of `8080`, and your server fails to bind. **Always apply `htons()` to `sin_port`.**
> 2. **Confusing `INADDR_ANY` (which is in host byte order) with `htonl(INADDR_ANY)`.** Since the value is zero, the bug is *invisible* on little-endian machines — but it fails on big-endian. Always write `htonl(INADDR_ANY)`.
> 3. **Not checking return values.** `socket()`, `bind()`, `listen()`, `accept()`, and `connect()` can all fail. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for missing `perror()` or `< 0` checks.
> 4. **Calling `bind()` on the client.** The client does **not** need to bind. The kernel automatically assigns an ephemeral port when `connect()` is called.
> 5. **Confusing `SOCK_STREAM` and `SOCK_DGRAM`.** `SOCK_STREAM` is TCP (connection-oriented). `SOCK_DGRAM` is UDP (connectionless). Mixing them up means the program compiles but fails at runtime.
> 6. **Writing `sizeof(server_addr)` instead of `sizeof(server_addr.sin_family)` correctly in the `connect()` call.** Use the size of the entire `sockaddr_in` structure.
> 7. **Forgetting to null-terminate the received buffer.** `recv()` does not add a `\0`. Always write `buffer[n] = '\0';` after a successful `recv()`.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Bullet Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Three Pillars:** Configuration Files (declarative, persistent) | Networking Commands (imperative, ephemeral) | System Calls (programmatic, long-lived).
- **Five must-know configuration files:** `/etc/hosts`, `/etc/resolv.conf`, `/etc/hostname`, `/etc/network/interfaces`, `/etc/nsswitch.conf`.
- **Modern vs. legacy commands:** Prefer `ip` over `ifconfig`; prefer `ss` over `netstat`; prefer `ip route` over `route`; prefer `ip neigh` over `arp`.
- **The 5-phase server:** `socket()` → `bind()` → `listen()` → `accept()` → `send/recv/close`.
- **The 3-phase client:** `socket()` → `connect()` → `send/recv/close`.
- **UDP variation:** Replace `SOCK_STREAM` with `SOCK_DGRAM`, replace `listen`+`accept` with `sendto`+`recvfrom`, drop `connect` for connectionless mode.
- **Byte order is mandatory:** Apply `htons()` to `sin_port` and `htonl()` to `sin_addr.s_addr`. For `INADDR_ANY` use `htonl(INADDR_ANY)`.
- **Address conversion functions:** Use modern `inet_pton()` and `inet_ntop()` instead of legacy `inet_aton()`/`inet_ntoa()`.
- **Port number ranges:** Well-Known (0–1023) | Registered (1024–49151) | Dynamic/Private (49152–65535).
- **Key constants:** `AF_INET = 2`, `SOCK_STREAM = 1`, `SOCK_DGRAM = 2`.
- **Three-way handshake:** SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK, with sequence numbers incrementing by 1 for the acknowledgment.
- **Time-related:** `TIME_WAIT` state holds for $2 \times \text{MSL}$ after a graceful close.
- **Two generations of tools:** `net-tools` (deprecated, BSD-style) and `iproute2` (modern, Linux-native).
- **DNS flow:** Application → glibc → `/etc/hosts` → `/etc/resolv.conf` → UDP port 53 → recursive resolution.
- **Always check return values** — every syscall in the socket API can fail.
- **`<arpa/inet.h>`** for `htons`, `htonl`, `inet_pton`, `inet_ntop`.
- **`<sys/socket.h>`** for `socket`, `bind`, `listen`, `accept`, `connect`, `send`, `recv`.
- **`<netinet/in.h>`** for `struct sockaddr_in` and the `INADDR_*` macros.
- **`<unistd.h>`** for `close()` and `read()`/`write()`.
- **The `struct sockaddr_in` recipe:** family = `AF_INET`, `sin_port = htons(port)`, `sin_addr.s_addr = inet_pton(...)` or `htonl(INADDR_ANY)`, then `memset` the rest to zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Viva Tip:** If the examiner asks *"What happens if you forget `htons()`?"*, the correct answer is: *"The kernel interprets the port number in the wrong byte order. On x86, port 8080 (`0x1F90`) would be received as port `0x901F = 36895`, causing the bind() to fail or succeed on the wrong port. The connection would never reach the intended server process."*

**End of Notes — Module Experiment 2 — PCERL407**

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Socket:** A socket is the **endpoint of a two-way communication link** between two programs running on a network. It is bound to a port number so that the TCP transport layer can identify the application that data is destined to be sent to. Formally defined by RFC 793 as `(IP address, Port number, Transport Protocol)` — a 3-tuple identifying a communication endpoint.

**TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** is a **connection-oriented**, **reliable**, **byte-stream** transport layer protocol defined under **RFC 793**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 2 of CS405 / Lab Module 2 of PCERL407), the experiment mandates implementing a **stream-based, three-way handshake initialized** communication channel using Berkeley Sockets (POSIX Winsock on Windows).

The canonical server-side state machine follows the **5-tuple lifecycle**:
$$\text{socket()} \rightarrow \text{bind()} \rightarrow \text{listen()} \rightarrow \text{accept()} \rightarrow \text{recv()}/\text{send()} \rightarrow \text{close()}$$

The client-side lifecycle is the **4-tuple lifecycle**:
$$\text{socket()} \rightarrow \text{connect()} \rightarrow \text{send()}/\text{recv()} \rightarrow \text{close()}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology:** The socket returned by `socket()` is called the **welcoming socket** (or **listening socket**). The socket returned by `accept()` is called the **connection socket** — a distinct, dedicated socket bound to a new ephemeral port to communicate with exactly *one* client. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Telephone Call" Model

Imagine the **TCP socket** as a **landline telephone call**:

| Step | Telephony Action | Socket Equivalent | Why it Matters |
|------|------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Buy a phone (get a number) | `socket()` | Acquire the communication resource |
| 2 | Assign a known number | `bind(port)` | Make the server reachable at a fixed address |
| 3 | Pick up the receiver, wait for ring | `listen()` | Announce willingness to accept calls |
| 4 | Phone rings, answer it | `accept()` | Establish a dedicated channel |
| 5 | Talk and listen | `send()` / `recv()` | Bidirectional data transfer |
| 6 | Hang up | `close()` | Release resources, send FIN |

The **client** simply: picks up the phone, **dials the number** (`connect()`) — performing the **TCP 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)** — and then talks.

> [!TIP]
> **UDP is like sending a letter** — no handshake, no confirmation, no guarantee. TCP is a **phone call** — handshake, ordered, reliable. This is *the* most common viva question: *"Why TCP and not UDP for this experiment?"* The expected answer touches on **reliability**, **ordered delivery**, **flow control**, and **congestion control**.

## 1.3 Standard Constants & Metrics (Memorize for Lab Records)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Default HTTP Port:** `80`
> - **Default HTTPS Port:** `443`
> - **Well-known ports:** `0 – 1023` (require root/admin privileges to bind)
> - **Registered ports:** `1024 – 49151`
> - **Ephemeral/dynamic ports:** `49152 – 65535` (used by client-side `connect()`)
> - **AF_INET (Address Family — Internet):** IPv4 protocol family
> - **SOCK_STREAM:** Socket type for TCP (sequenceable, reliable, two-way connection-based byte streams)
> - **Maximum TCP Queue (listen backlog):** Implementation-defined; Linux default = **`4096`** but historically **SOMAXCONN = 128** in BSD
> - **MTU (Ethernet):** **`1500 bytes`** — your buffer size should comfortably exceed this to avoid fragmentation edge cases
> - **TCP 3-Way Handshake:** **3 packets**; TCP 4-Way Termination: **4 packets** (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TCP State Machine (Theoretical Foundation)

Before writing a single line of code, KTU examiners expect you to **understand the state transitions**. TCP operates as a finite state machine (FSM) on both endpoints:

### 2.1.1 Three-Way Handshake (Connection Establishment)
1. **Client → Server:** `SYN, seq=x` (Client enters `SYN_SENT`)
2. **Server → Client:** `SYN, ACK, seq=y, ack=x+1` (Server enters `SYN_RECEIVED`)
3. **Client → Server:** `ACK, seq=x+1, ack=y+1` (Both enter `ESTABLISHED`)

The connection is now **ESTABLISHED**. Data transfer can begin.

### 2.1.2 Four-Way Termination (Connection Teardown)
1. **Active closer → Passive closer:** `FIN` (enters `FIN_WAIT_1`)
2. **Passive closer → Active closer:** `ACK` (enters `CLOSE_WAIT`; active closer enters `FIN_WAIT_2`)
3. **Passive closer → Active closer:** `FIN` (enters `LAST_ACK`)
4. **Active closer → Passive closer:** `ACK` (enters `TIME_WAIT`, then `CLOSED` after 2×MSL timeout)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TIME_WAIT exists:** To ensure the final ACK reaches the server, and to prevent "stale" segments from a previous connection being misinterpreted. Default TIME_WAIT duration is **2 × MSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime) = 60 seconds** in Linux.

## 2.2 Core API Function Deep-Dive (The "Why" Behind Each Call)

| Function | Purpose | Why We Need It | Typical Parameters |
|----------|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| `socket()` | Creates a new socket | Allocates kernel resources (send/receive buffers, file descriptor) | `family=AF_INET, type=SOCK_STREAM, proto=0` |
| `bind()` | Assigns IP+Port to socket | Without binding, OS picks an ephemeral port (fine for client, fatal for server) | `(IP, PORT)` tuple |
| `listen()` | Marks socket as passive | Converts active socket → listening socket; creates accept queue | `socket, backlog=5` |
| `accept()` | Blocks waiting for a connection | Performs server side of 3-way handshake; returns **new** connected socket | `socket` (blocking call) |
| `connect()` | Actively initiates connection | Performs client side of 3-way handshake | `(IP, PORT)` tuple |
| `send()` | Sends data over a connected socket | Pushes application data into TCP send buffer | `socket, data.encode()` |
| `recv()` | Receives data from a connected socket | Reads up to `bufsize` bytes from TCP receive buffer | `socket, bufsize=1024` |
| `close()` | Initiates connection termination | Sends FIN packet; releases file descriptor | `socket` |

## 2.3 The Data Flow — Inside the Kernel

```
┌─────────────────┐                              ┌─────────────────┐
│   APPLICATION   │                              │   APPLICATION   │
│   (Server)      │                              │   (Client)      │
└────────┬────────┘                              └────────┬────────┘
         │                                                │
         │  write()/send()                          send()│
         │                                                │
┌────────▼────────┐    ┌──────────────┐         ┌────────▼────────┐
│  TCP Send Buffer│    │     IP       │         │  TCP Send Buffer│
│  (kernel space) │◄──►│  (network)   │◄───────►│  (kernel space) │
└────────┬────────┘    └──────────────┘         └────────┬────────┘
         │                                                │
         │  read()/recv()                           recv()│
┌────────▼────────┐                              ┌────────▼────────┐
│  TCP Recv Buffer│                              │  TCP Recv Buffer│
│  (kernel space) │                              │  (kernel space) │
└─────────────────┘                              └─────────────────┘
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** `send()` returning successfully does **NOT** mean the data has been received by the client. It only means the data was successfully copied into the **kernel's TCP send buffer**. Similarly, `recv()` may return **less data than requested** — TCP is a **byte stream**, not a message stream. You must use loops to ensure you receive all data. This is a guaranteed viva question.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table. These are the "always-marks" facts in the lab record and viva.**

| Property | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|----------|----------------|------|-------|
| TCP Header (no options) | $\vert H_{TCP} \vert = 20$ | bytes | Mandatory fields only |
| TCP Header (with options) | $\vert H_{TCP} \vert = 20 + N \cdot 4$ | bytes | $N$ = number of 32-bit option words |
| IPv4 Header | $\vert H_{IPv4} \vert = 20$ | bytes | Standard, no options |
| Ethernet MTU | $1500$ | bytes | Max payload at L2 |
| TCP MSS (typical) | $1460$ | bytes | $\text{MTU} - \vert H_{IP} \vert - \vert H_{TCP} \vert = 1500 - 20 - 20$ |
| Sequence Number Range | $0$ to $2^{32} - 1$ | unsigned 32-bit | Wraps around (RFC 793) |
| Window Size (16-bit field) | $0$ to $2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ | bytes | *Without* window scaling (RFC 1323) |
| Listen Backlog (BSD historical) | $\text{SOMAXCONN} = 128$ | connections | Pending connection queue length |
| TIME_WAIT Duration | $2 \times \text{MSL} = 120$ | seconds | Linux default MSL = 60s |
| Port Number Range | $0$ to $2^{16} - 1$ | unsigned 16-bit | 0 is reserved (meaning "any") |
| Maximum Theoretical TCP Throughput | $\text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$ | bits in flight | Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) |
| File Descriptor Limit (soft) | `ulimit -n` (Linux default = 1024) | FDs | Limits max simultaneous client connections per process |

### 2.4.1 The Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP) — The Hidden Boss

$$\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$$

The BDP defines the **maximum amount of unacknowledged data** that can be in flight. Your `recv()` buffer should be sized to handle at least one BDP to avoid stalling the sender:

$$\text{Recommended Buffer Size} \geq \max(\text{BDP}, \text{MSS}) = \max(\text{BW} \times \text{RTT},\; 1460)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** This is why **HTTP/2, BBR congestion control, and QUIC** all emerged. The fixed 64 KB TCP window of 1992 was the bottleneck for high-BDP networks (e.g., transcontinental fiber at 100 Gbps).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Python Implementation (Primary — KTU Default)

> [!NOTE]
> Python is the **de facto** language for KTU PCERL407 lab evaluations. The `socket` module is part of the standard library — no `pip install` required.

### 3.1.1 The TCP Server — Annotated Source Code

```python
"""
Filename: tcp_server.py
Experiment: Module 2 - Client/Server communication using TCP
Author: [Your Name]
Roll No: [Your Roll No]
Date: [Date]
"""

import socket                       # Import the Berkeley sockets interface

# ---------------- CONFIGURATION CONSTANTS ----------------
SERVER_IP   = '127.0.0.1'           # Loopback interface (localhost)
SERVER_PORT = 12345                 # Arbitrary port > 1023 (no root needed)
BUFFER_SIZE = 1024                  # Receive buffer in bytes
BACKLOG     = 5                     # Max pending connections in listen queue
# --------------------------------------------------------

def main():
    # STEP 1: Create a TCP socket
    # AF_INET  → IPv4 address family
    # SOCK_STREAM → reliable, connection-oriented byte stream (TCP)
    server_socket = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    print(f"[STEP 1] Socket created: {server_socket}")

    # STEP 2: Bind the socket to an IP address and port number
    # Without bind(), the OS assigns a random ephemeral port — useless for clients.
    server_socket.bind((SERVER_IP, SERVER_PORT))
    print(f"[STEP 2] Socket bound to {SERVER_IP}:{SERVER_PORT}")

    # STEP 3: Put the socket into LISTENING mode
    # Backlog = max number of unaccepted connections the OS will queue
    server_socket.listen(BACKLOG)
    print(f"[STEP 3] Server listening... (max backlog={BACKLOG})")

    # STEP 4: Block until a client connects
    # accept() returns: (new_socket_for_this_client, client_address_tuple)
    print(f"[STEP 4] Waiting for client connection...")
    client_socket, client_address = server_socket.accept()
    print(f"[STEP 4] Connection established with {client_address}")

    # STEP 5: Communication loop (server echoes whatever client sends)
    try:
        while True:
            # recv() is BLOCKING — it waits for at least 1 byte
            # May return fewer bytes than BUFFER_SIZE — this is normal!
            data = client_socket.recv(BUFFER_SIZE)

            # Empty bytes object → client closed connection gracefully (sent FIN)
            if not data:
                print("[STEP 5] Client disconnected.")
                break

            message = data.decode('utf-8')
            print(f"[STEP 5] Received from client: {message!r}")

            # Echo back to client (uppercased for visibility)
            response = f"Server echoes: {message.upper()}"
            client_socket.sendall(response.encode('utf-8'))
            print(f"[STEP 5] Sent to client: {response!r}")
    finally:
        # STEP 6: Close both sockets (regardless of exception or not)
        client_socket.close()
        print("[STEP 6] Client socket closed.")
        server_socket.close()
        print("[STEP 6] Server socket closed.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

### 3.1.2 The TCP Client — Annotated Source Code

```python
"""
Filename: tcp_client.py
Experiment: Module 2 - Client/Server communication using TCP
"""

import socket

# ---------------- CONFIGURATION CONSTANTS ----------------
SERVER_IP   = '127.0.0.1'           # Must match server IP
SERVER_PORT = 12345                 # Must match server PORT
BUFFER_SIZE = 1024
# --------------------------------------------------------

def main():
    # STEP 1: Create a TCP socket (no bind needed — OS assigns ephemeral port)
    client_socket = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    print(f"[STEP 1] Client socket created: {client_socket}")

    # STEP 2: Actively connect to the server (triggers TCP 3-way handshake)
    print(f"[STEP 2] Connecting to {SERVER_IP}:{SERVER_PORT}...")
    client_socket.connect((SERVER_IP, SERVER_PORT))
    print(f"[STEP 2] Connected! Local port: {client_socket.getsockname()[1]}")

    # STEP 3: Send 5 test messages then close gracefully
    messages = ["Hello, Server!", "TCP is reliable.", "Socket programming rocks.",
                "Last message.", "bye"]

    try:
        for msg in messages:
            # Encode string → bytes (TCP transmits bytes, not strings)
            client_socket.sendall(msg.encode('utf-8'))
            print(f"[STEP 3] Sent: {msg!r}")

            # Wait for server's echo
            data = client_socket.recv(BUFFER_SIZE)
            if not data:
                print("[STEP 3] Server closed unexpectedly.")
                break
            response = data.decode('utf-8')
            print(f"[STEP 3] Received: {response!r}")
    finally:
        # STEP 4: Close the socket (sends FIN to server)
        client_socket.close()
        print("[STEP 4] Client socket closed. FIN sent.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

## 3.2 The C Implementation (For Depth & Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> Writing the C version **doubles your mark potential** in the lab exam. Use `gcc` on Linux or MinGW on Windows. Always run the server **first**, then the client.

### 3.2.1 TCP Server in C

```c
/* tcp_server.c
 * Compile: gcc tcp_server.c -o tcp_server
 * Run:     ./tcp_server
 */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>     // Core socket definitions
#include <netinet/in.h>     // sockaddr_in structure
#include <arpa/inet.h>      // htons, inet_pton

#define SERVER_PORT 12345
#define BUFFER_SIZE 1024
#define BACKLOG     5

int main() {
    int server_fd, client_fd;
    struct sockaddr_in server_addr, client_addr;
    socklen_t client_len;
    char buffer[BUFFER_SIZE];

    // STEP 1: socket()
    server_fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);
    if (server_fd < 0) { perror("socket failed"); exit(EXIT_FAILURE); }
    printf("[STEP 1] Socket created: fd=%d\n", server_fd);

    // Optional: prevent "Address already in use" on rapid restart
    int opt = 1;
    setsockopt(server_fd, SOL_SOCKET, SO_REUSEADDR, &opt, sizeof(opt));

    // STEP 2: bind()
    memset(&server_addr, 0, sizeof(server_addr));
    server_addr.sin_family      = AF_INET;
    server_addr.sin_addr.s_addr = inet_addr("127.0.0.1");
    server_addr.sin_port        = htons(SERVER_PORT);

    if (bind(server_fd, (struct sockaddr *)&server_addr, sizeof(server_addr)) < 0) {
        perror("bind failed"); close(server_fd); exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }
    printf("[STEP 2] Bound to 127.0.0.1:%d\n", SERVER_PORT);

    // STEP 3: listen()
    if (listen(server_fd, BACKLOG) < 0) {
        perror("listen failed"); close(server_fd); exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }
    printf("[STEP 3] Listening (backlog=%d)...\n", BACKLOG);

    // STEP 4: accept()
    client_len = sizeof(client_addr);
    client_fd  = accept(server_fd, (struct sockaddr *)&client_addr, &client_len);
    if (client_fd < 0) { perror("accept failed"); close(server_fd); exit(EXIT_FAILURE); }
    printf("[STEP 4] Client connected: %s:%d\n",
           inet_ntoa(client_addr.sin_addr), ntohs(client_addr.sin_port));

    // STEP 5: communicate
    ssize_t bytes;
    while ((bytes = recv(client_fd, buffer, BUFFER_SIZE - 1, 0)) > 0) {
        buffer[bytes] = '\0';
        printf("[STEP 5] Received: %s", buffer);

        // Echo back in uppercase
        for (int i = 0; buffer[i]; i++)
            if (buffer[i] >= 'a' && buffer[i] <= 'z') buffer[i] -= 32;
        send(client_fd, buffer, bytes, 0);
        printf("[STEP 5] Echoed: %s", buffer);
    }

    if (bytes == 0)  printf("[STEP 5] Client closed connection.\n");
    else             perror("recv error");

    // STEP 6: close()
    close(client_fd);
    close(server_fd);
    printf("[STEP 6] Sockets closed.\n");
    return 0;
}
```

### 3.2.2 TCP Client in C

```c
/* tcp_client.c
 * Compile: gcc tcp_client.c -o tcp_client
 * Run:     ./tcp_client
 */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>
#include <arpa/inet.h>

#define SERVER_PORT 12345
#define BUFFER_SIZE 1024

int main() {
    int sock_fd;
    struct sockaddr_in server_addr;
    char buffer[BUFFER_SIZE];

    // STEP 1: socket()
    sock_fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0);
    if (sock_fd < 0) { perror("socket failed"); exit(EXIT_FAILURE); }

    // STEP 2: connect()
    memset(&server_addr, 0, sizeof(server_addr));
    server_addr.sin_family      = AF_INET;
    server_addr.sin_port        = htons(SERVER_PORT);
    server_addr.sin_addr.s_addr = inet_addr("127.0.0.1");

    if (connect(sock_fd, (struct sockaddr *)&server_addr, sizeof(server_addr)) < 0) {
        perror("connect failed"); close(sock_fd); exit(EXIT_FAILURE);
    }
    printf("[STEP 2] Connected to server.\n");

    // STEP 3: send/recv loop
    const char *messages[] = {"Hello\n", "TCP test\n", "bye\n", NULL};
    for (int i = 0; messages[i]; i++) {
        send(sock_fd, messages[i], strlen(messages[i]), 0);
        printf("[STEP 3] Sent: %s", messages[i]);

        int n = recv(sock_fd, buffer, BUFFER_SIZE - 1, 0);
        if (n > 0) {
            buffer[n] = '\0';
            printf("[STEP 3] Received: %s", buffer);
        }
    }

    // STEP 4: close()
    close(sock_fd);
    return 0;
}
```

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedure for Lab Record

> [!TIP]
> The following numbered procedure is what you **write in your lab record** under the heading "Algorithm / Procedure". Examiners will mark **2–3 marks** just for having this clean.

### 3.3.1 Algorithm (Server-Side)
1. **Start**
2. Create a TCP socket using `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)`.
3. Initialize `sockaddr_in` structure with server IP and port.
4. Bind socket to the address using `bind()`.
5. Call `listen()` with a backlog value (e.g., 5).
6. Call `accept()` to block until a client connects; obtain `client_socket` and `client_address`.
7. **Loop**:
   a. Receive data from client using `recv()`.
   b. If `data` is empty → client closed → go to step 8.
   c. Process the data (e.g., uppercase it).
   d. Send response back using `send()`.
8. Close `client_socket` and `server_socket`.
9. **Stop**

### 3.3.2 Algorithm (Client-Side)
1. **Start**
2. Create a TCP socket using `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_STREAM, 0)`.
3. Initialize `sockaddr_in` with server IP and port.
4. Call `connect()` to establish connection (performs TCP 3-way handshake).
5. **Loop** (for each message):
   a. Send user input or predefined message via `send()`.
   b. Receive server's response via `recv()`.
   c. Display the response.
6. Close the socket.
7. **Stop**

## 3.4 Network Trace Analysis (Wireshark-Ready)

If the examiner asks you to "show the trace", the following packets are exchanged:

| # | Source → Dest | Flags | Seq | Ack | Purpose |
|---|---------------|-------|-----|-----|---------|
| 1 | Client → Server | `SYN` | $x$ | 0 | Connection request |
| 2 | Server → Client | `SYN, ACK` | $y$ | $x+1$ | Server acknowledges + syncs |
| 3 | Client → Server | `ACK` | $x+1$ | $y+1$ | Connection established |
| 4 | Client → Server | `PSH, ACK` | $x+1$ | $y+1$ | Data: "Hello" |
| 5 | Server → Client | `PSH, ACK` | $y+1$ | $x+1+5$ | Data: "HELLO" |
| 6 | Client → Server | `FIN, ACK` | $x+6$ | $y+6$ | Client done |
| 7 | Server → Client | `FIN, ACK` | $y+6$ | $x+7$ | Server done |
| 8 | Client → Server | `ACK` | $x+7$ | $y+7$ | Final ACK |

Total packets for a single short message exchange: **8** (3 handshake + 2 data + 3 teardown).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Python vs C Implementation

| Feature | Python | C |
|---------|--------|---|
| Lines of code | ~30 | ~60 |
| Socket creation | `socket.socket(family, type)` | `socket(family, type, proto)` |
| Send data | `sendall(bytes)` | `send(fd, buf, len, flags)` |
| Receive data | `recv(bufsize)` | `recv(fd, buf, len, flags)` |
| Address struct | Tuple `(host, port)` | `struct sockaddr_in` |
| Address conversion | `socket.gethostbyname()` | `inet_pton(AF_INET, "127.0.0.1")` |
| Port conversion | Integer `12345` | `htons(12345)` |
| Error handling | Exceptions (try/except) | Return codes (negative = error) |
| Performance | Slow (interpreted) | Fast (compiled) |
| Memory | Garbage collected | Manual `free()` / `close()` |
| **KTU Preference** | **Primary** | Bonus marks |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Server State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Create Socket<br/>socket AF_INET SOCK_STREAM]
    B --> C[Bind to IP Port<br/>bind 127.0.0.1 12345]
    C --> D[Listen with Backlog 5<br/>listen 5]
    D --> E[Block on accept<br/>Waiting for client...]
    E --> F{Client connected?}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Receive data<br/>recv BUFFER_SIZE]
    G --> H{Data empty?<br/>client sent FIN}
    H -->|Yes| I[Close client socket<br/>close client_fd]
    H -->|No| J[Process data<br/>e.g. uppercase]
    J --> K[Send response<br/>sendall or send]
    K --> G
    I --> L[Close server socket<br/>close server_fd]
    L --> M([Stop])

    style A fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#fff
    style M fill:#F44336,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#fff
    style E fill:#FFC107,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    style F fill:#2196F3,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#fff
    style H fill:#2196F3,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#fff
    style J fill:#9C27B0,stroke:#4A148C,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 The Client State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Create Socket<br/>socket AF_INET SOCK_STREAM]
    B --> C[Connect to server<br/>connect 127.0.0.1 12345]
    C --> D{Connected?}
    D -->|No - retry| C
    D -->|Yes| E[Get user input<br/>or use predefined msg]
    E --> F[Send data<br/>sendall bytes]
    F --> G[Receive response<br/>recv BUFFER_SIZE]
    G --> H{Response empty?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Server closed]
    H -->|No| J[Display response]
    J --> K{More messages?}
    K -->|Yes| E
    K -->|No| I
    I --> L[Close socket<br/>close client_fd]
    L --> M([Stop])

    style A fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#fff
    style M fill:#F44336,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#fff
    style C fill:#FFC107,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    style D fill:#2196F3,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#fff
    style H fill:#2196F3,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#fff
    style K fill:#2196F3,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Complete Client-Server Interaction Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as SERVER
    participant N as NETWORK (TCP/IP Stack)
    participant C as CLIENT

    Note over S: socket() + bind() + listen()
    S->>N: [LISTENING on :12345]
    Note over C: socket()
    C->>N: [CONNECT to 127.0.0.1:12345]

    Note over S,N,C: TCP 3-Way Handshake Begins
    C->>S: SYN seq=100
    S->>C: SYN seq=300 ACK=101
    C->>S: ACK seq=101 ACK=301
    Note over S,C: ESTABLISHED

    C->>S: PSH ACK data='Hello' len=5
    S->>S: process: uppercase
    S->>C: PSH ACK data='HELLO' len=5

    C->>S: PSH ACK data='bye' len=3
    S->>S: process: uppercase
    S->>C: PSH ACK data='BYE' len=3

    Note over S,N,C: TCP 4-Way Teardown Begins
    C->>S: FIN ACK
    S->>C: ACK
    S->>C: FIN ACK
    C->>S: ACK
    Note over S: TIME_WAIT 2xMSL
    Note over S,C: CLOSED
```

## 4.5 Multi-Client Concurrency — Block Architecture (Bonus Content)

> [!NOTE]
> The single-threaded server above handles **one client at a time**. Real-world servers use concurrency. The block diagram below shows the three main approaches:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[TCP Listening Socket] --> B{Concurrency Model}

    B --> C1[FORK MODEL<br/>Pre-fork N workers]
    B --> C2[THREAD MODEL<br/>One thread per client]
    B --> C3[SELECT/EPOLL MODEL<br/>I/O Multiplexing]

    C1 --> D1[Parent accepts<br/>fork child for client<br/>child handles comm]
    C2 --> D2[Main thread accepts<br/>pthread_create for each<br/>worker handles comm]
    C3 --> D3[Single thread loops<br/>over fd_set<br/>non-blocking I/O]

    D1 --> E1[Pros: simple, isolated<br/>Cons: process overhead]
    D2 --> E2[Pros: shared memory<br/>Cons: GIL issues in Python]
    D3 --> E3[Pros: very scalable<br/>Cons: complex code]
```
