> **Common Pitfalls in Lab Exam:**
> 1. **Forgetting to print the iteration counter** in the C program — KTU examiners will deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Using the wrong infinity constant** (e.g., 0 instead of 9999) — causes infinite loops.
> 3. **Mixing up `dist[i][j]` with `cost[i][j]`** in the inner loop — produces a trivial all-zeros output.
> 4. **Not commenting the code** — KTU awards 1 mark for "code clarity" in the lab record.
> 5. **Confusing `DVR` (intra-domain) with `BGP` (inter-domain)** in the viva — they are *very* different protocols.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PCERL407 — Experiment 2**

- ☐ **DVR** = distributed, iterative routing using the **Bellman–Ford equation** $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$.
- ☐ Real-world DVR protocols: **RIPv1, RIPv2, IGRP** (Cisco proprietary). Modern descendant: **BGP (path-vector)**.
- ☐ **RIP metric** = hop count, range **1 – 15**, **16 = infinity** (unreachable).
- ☐ **Convergence bound** = at most $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations for non-negative edge weights.
- ☐ **Update trigger** = any change in a router's distance vector (synchronous DVR) OR periodic 30-second timer (asynchronous, RIP-style).
- ☐ **Count-to-infinity problem** is the signature failure mode of DVR; mitigated by **split-horizon**, **poison-reverse**, **hold-down timer (180 s)**, and **triggered updates**.
- ☐ **Bellman–Ford vs Dijkstra** — DVR uses Bellman–Ford (distributed, handles negative costs in theory); LSR uses Dijkstra (centralised, requires full topology).
- ☐ **Code must include** — initialisation, iterative Bellman–Ford relaxation, change detection, convergence termination, and per-iteration printing.
- ☐ **Lab viva favourites** — (i) "Why is RIP's infinity 16 and not larger?" (ii) "What is the difference between *split-horizon* and *poison-reverse*?" (iii) "Show the A→C distance evolution for the given topology."
- ☐ **Output expected in record** — iteration-by-iteration distance matrix, final next-hop table, NS-2/3 trace snippet, and a convergence plot.
- ☐ **Convergence test** = no row of the distance matrix changes in a complete pass over all $(i,j,v)$ triples.
- ☐ **Stable state** = Bellman optimality condition $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$ holds for all $(x,y)$ pairs.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCERL407 Computer Networking Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement Simple Mail Transfer Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement Simple Mail Transfer Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol)** is an application-layer, text-based protocol defined in **RFC 5321** that governs the transmission of electronic mail (e-mail) between a *Mail User Agent (MUA)*, a *Mail Submission Agent (MSA)*, and a *Mail Transfer Agent (MTA)* across IP networks, typically operating over **TCP port 25** (relay) or **TCP port 587** (submission with STARTTLS).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab context, "implementing SMTP" means **writing a program** (in Python/Java/C) that either:
1. Acts as an SMTP **client** — connects to a mail server and sends an email by exchanging the canonical SMTP command/response dialogue, **OR**
2. Acts as a minimal SMTP **server** — listens on a port, accepts the SMTP handshake (`HELO` → `MAIL FROM` → `RCPT TO` → `DATA` → `QUIT`), and stores/replays the message.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal System

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: SMTP is the "post office rules" of the internet.**
>
> Imagine you want to send a letter. You cannot just throw it into the air and hope it reaches your friend. You must:
> 1. Walk up to the post office counter (the **SMTP server**).
> 2. Greet the clerk (`HELO` / `EHLO`).
> 3. State **who the letter is from** (`MAIL FROM:`).
> 4. State **who the letter is to** (`RCPT TO:`).
> 5. Hand over the **letter content** (`DATA` ... `.`).
> 6. Say goodbye (`QUIT`).
>
> SMTP is the rigid, line-by-line "script" both the client and the server follow to make sure no letter is lost and the addresses are valid.

## 1.3 Key Actors in the SMTP Ecosystem

| Actor | Abbreviation | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Mail User Agent** | MUA | The end-user client (e.g., Outlook, Gmail web, Thunderbird) |
| **Mail Submission Agent** | MSA | Receives mail from authenticated MUA (port 587) |
| **Mail Transfer Agent** | MTA | Server-to-server relay (port 25) |
| **Mail Delivery Agent** | MDA | Final delivery to recipient's mailbox |
| **Mail Retrieval Agent** | MRA | Allows MUA to fetch mail (POP3/IMAP) |

## 1.4 Port Numbers — Mandatory Memory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Port 25** — MTA-to-MTA relay (legacy, often blocked by ISPs).
> - **Port 587** — MSA submission (modern, requires `STARTTLS`).
> - **Port 465** — SMTPS (SMTP over implicit TLS, older).
> - **Default unencrypted local lab port** — **2525** (often used in PCERL407 to avoid needing root).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture

SMTP is a **request-response (command-reply), connection-oriented, push protocol** built on TCP. The client sends ASCII commands terminated by `<CRLF>` (`\r\n`), and the server replies with a 3-digit numeric status code followed by a human-readable string.

### 2.1.1 The Four Logical Phases of an SMTP Session

1. **Connection Establishment** — TCP 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) on port 25/587. Server sends `220 Service Ready`.
2. **Handshake / Greeting** — Client sends `HELO domain` (RFC 821) or `EHLO domain` (Extended SMTP, RFC 1425). Server replies `250 OK`.
3. **Mail Transaction** — A repeated sequence of:
   - `MAIL FROM:<sender@domain>`
   - `RCPT TO:<receiver@domain>` (can be repeated for multiple recipients)
   - `DATA` → message body → single line containing only `.`
4. **Termination** — `QUIT` command, server replies `221 Service closing transmission channel`.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — SMTP Reply Codes

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiners love these 3-digit codes. Memorize the first digit — it tells you the category.**

| Code Range | Category | Meaning | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2yz** | **Positive Completion** | Command accepted | `220`, `250`, `354` |
| **3yz** | **Positive Intermediate** | Awaiting more input | `354 Start mail input` |
| **4yz** | **Transient Negative** | Try again later | `421`, `450` |
| **5yz** | **Permanent Negative** | Fatal, do not retry | `500`, `550`, `554` |

### The Six Codes You MUST Know

| Code | Verbatim Text | When It Appears |
|---|---|---|
| **220** | Service Ready | Server's opening banner after TCP accept |
| **250** | OK | After `HELO/EHLO`, `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO` |
| **354** | Start mail input; end with `<CRLF>.<CRLF>` | After successful `DATA` command |
| **421** | Service not available, closing transmission | Server is shutting down |
| **500** | Syntax error, command unrecognized | Typo in command |
| **550** | Mailbox unavailable / User not local | Recipient rejected |

## 2.2.1 Core SMTP Commands Reference Table

| Command | Argument | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `HELO` | Client domain | Identify client (basic SMTP) |
| `EHLO` | Client domain | Identify client (Extended SMTP, returns capability list) |
| `AUTH` | `LOGIN` / `PLAIN` | Authenticate (only with MSA on 587) |
| `MAIL FROM` | `<sender@host>` | Reverse-path / envelope sender |
| `RCPT TO` | `<recipient@host>` | Forward-path / envelope recipient |
| `DATA` | *(none)* | Begin message body; end with `\r\n.\r\n` |
| `RSET` | *(none)* | Abort current transaction, keep connection |
| `NOOP` | *(none)* | No-op; used to keep connection alive / test |
| `QUIT` | *(none)* | Close connection |
| `VRFY` | `<user>` | Verify if a mailbox exists (often disabled) |
| `STARTTLS` | *(none)* | Upgrade to TLS (RFC 3207) |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Engineering use case:** Every transactional email you receive (OTP, order confirmation, alert) was delivered by an SMTP engine — Sendmail, Postfix, Exim, Microsoft Exchange.
- **Lab relevance:** PCERL407 tests whether you understand the **Application Layer → Transport Layer (TCP) → Network Layer (IP)** stack by manually triggering each command and observing the byte stream.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Implementation Strategy — Three Lab-Approved Methods

The KTU 2024 syllabus accepts any of the following three approaches. **Method B is the highest-scoring** because it forces you to demonstrate the raw protocol bytes.

| Method | Language | Layer of Abstraction | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A. High-Level Library** | Python `smtplib` | Application | 6/10 (works, but hides protocol) |
| **B. Raw Socket Client** | Python `socket` | Transport (full protocol) | **10/10 (recommended)** |
| **C. Manual Telnet** | Terminal `telnet` | Hand-typed | 8/10 (good for viva) |

---

## 3.2 Method A — Python `smtplib` (Quick Implementation)

> This is the fastest way to complete the experiment. Useful as a sanity check.

```python
# File: smtp_send.py
import smtplib
from email.mime.text import MIMEText

# ---- Build the MIME message ----
msg = MIMEText("Hello from KTU PCERL407 Lab!")
msg["Subject"] = "PCERL407 Experiment 2"
msg["From"]    = "student@ktu.local"
msg["To"]      = "professor@ktu.local"

# ---- Connect to SMTP server (localhost for lab) ----
with smtplib.SMTP("127.0.0.1", 25, timeout=10) as server:
    server.set_debuglevel(1)         # <-- PRINTS every command & reply
    server.ehlo("client.ktu.local")  # Identify ourselves
    server.send_message(msg)         # Performs MAIL FROM / RCPT TO / DATA
    server.quit()
```

**Why `set_debuglevel(1)` is your best friend in the lab** — it prints the full SMTP conversation to stdout, which you can paste into your record as the *output screenshot*.

---

## 3.3 Method B — Raw Socket Implementation (Board-Exam Gold Standard)

> This implementation proves you understand the protocol. Every byte is under your control.

### 3.3.1 The Analytical Matrix — Server vs. Client Exchange

| Step | Direction | Command / Response | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Client → Server | `TCP SYN` to port 25 | Initiate connection |
| 2 | Server → Client | `220 ktu.local ESMTP Postfix` | Greeting banner |
| 3 | Client → Server | `EHLO client.ktu.local\r\n` | Extended handshake |
| 4 | Server → Client | `250-ktu.local\r\n250 SIZE 10240000\r\n` | Capability list |
| 5 | Client → Server | `MAIL FROM:<alice@ktu.local>\r\n` | Envelope sender |
| 6 | Server → Client | `250 2.1.0 Ok` | Sender accepted |
| 7 | Client → Server | `RCPT TO:<bob@ktu.local>\r\n` | Envelope recipient |
| 8 | Server → Client | `250 2.1.5 Ok` | Recipient accepted |
| 9 | Client → Server | `DATA\r\n` | Begin payload |
| 10 | Server → Client | `354 End data with <CR><LF>.<CR><LF>` | Awaiting body |
| 11 | Client → Server | `Subject: Hi\r\n\r\nHello World\r\n.\r\n` | Message + terminator |
| 12 | Server → Client | `250 2.0.0 Ok: queued` | Message accepted |
| 13 | Client → Server | `QUIT\r\n` | Close |
| 14 | Server → Client | `221 2.0.0 Bye` | Close acknowledged |

### 3.3.2 Full Python Source Code (Copy-Paste Lab Ready)

```python
# File: raw_smtp_client.py
# PCERL407 — Experiment 2 — Raw Socket SMTP Client
import socket

HOST = "127.0.0.1"   # localhost SMTP server (e.g., Postfix, smtpd)
PORT = 25
TIMEOUT = 10

def recv_response(sock):
    """Read one full multiline SMTP response from the server."""
    data = b""
    while True:
        chunk = sock.recv(4096)
        if not chunk:
            break
        data += chunk
        # A response line is "XYZ " (intermediate) or "XYZ " (final, last digit with space)
        # It is complete when we get a line that starts with "XYZ " (final), not "XYZ-"
        lines = data.split(b"\r\n")
        if len(lines) >= 2 and (lines[-2][:4] in [b"250 ", b"220 ", b"221 ", b"354 ", b"221 ", b"550 "]):
            break
    print("[SERVER]", data.decode(errors="replace").strip())
    return data

def send_cmd(sock, cmd):
    """Send a CRLF-terminated SMTP command and print it."""
    print("[CLIENT]", cmd)
    sock.sendall((cmd + "\r\n").encode())

# ============================================================
# 1) TCP CONNECT
# ============================================================
s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
s.settimeout(TIMEOUT)
s.connect((HOST, PORT))
recv_response(s)                      # 220 banner

# ============================================================
# 2) HANDSHAKE
# ============================================================
send_cmd(s, "EHLO client.ktu.local")
recv_response(s)                      # 250 + capability list

# ============================================================
# 3) MAIL TRANSACTION
# ============================================================
send_cmd(s, "MAIL FROM:<alice@ktu.local>")
recv_response(s)                      # 250 2.1.0 Ok

send_cmd(s, "RCPT TO:<bob@ktu.local>")
recv_response(s)                      # 250 2.1.5 Ok

# ============================================================
# 4) DATA PHASE
# ============================================================
send_cmd(s, "DATA")
recv_response(s)                      # 354 End data with <CR><LF>.<CR><LF>

message = (
    "From: alice@ktu.local\r\n"
    "To: bob@ktu.local\r\n"
    "Subject: PCERL407 SMTP Test\r\n"
    "\r\n"
    "Hello Bob,\r\n"
    "This message was sent by a raw-socket SMTP client.\r\n"
    ".\r\n"                            # <-- mandatory terminator
)
print("[CLIENT] <<< MESSAGE BODY >>>")
s.sendall(message.encode())
recv_response(s)                      # 250 2.0.0 Ok: queued

# ============================================================
# 5) TERMINATE
# ============================================================
send_cmd(s, "QUIT")
recv_response(s)                      # 221 2.0.0 Bye
s.close()
print("\n[+] SMTP session completed successfully.")
```

### 3.3.3 Expected Output Trace

```
[SERVER] 220 ktu.local ESMTP Postfix
[CLIENT] EHLO client.ktu.local
[SERVER] 250-ktu.local
        250-PIPELINING
        250-SIZE 10240000
        250 HELP
[CLIENT] MAIL FROM:<alice@ktu.local>
[SERVER] 250 2.1.0 Ok
[CLIENT] RCPT TO:<bob@ktu.local>
[SERVER] 250 2.1.5 Ok
[CLIENT] DATA
[SERVER] 354 End data with <CR><LF>.<CR><LF>
[CLIENT] <<< MESSAGE BODY >>>
[SERVER] 250 2.0.0 Ok: queued as ABC123
[CLIENT] QUIT
[SERVER] 221 2.0.0 Bye
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Line-ending discipline:** SMTP mandates `\r\n` (CRLF), NOT `\n`. Linux terminals often default to `\n`. Forgetting `\r\n` is the #1 reason raw-socket SMTP clients fail silently.

---

## 3.4 Method C — Manual Telnet Verification (Viva Trick)

If you have Postfix or `python -m smtpd` running locally, you can demonstrate the protocol by hand:

```bash
$ telnet 127.0.0.1 25
Trying 127.0.0.1...
Connected to 127.0.0.1.
Escape character is '^]'.
220 ktu.local ESMTP Postfix
EHLO viva.ktu.local
250-ktu.local
250 SIZE 10240000
MAIL FROM:<examiner@ktu.local>
250 2.1.0 Ok
RCPT TO:<student@ktu.local>
250 2.1.5 Ok
DATA
354 End data with <CR><LF>.<CR><LF>
Subject: Viva Demo
This is a single dot on the next line to terminate:
.
250 2.0.0 Ok: queued
QUIT
221 2.0.0 Bye
```

### 3.4.1 The "Dot-Stuffing" Rule (Board-Exam Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the **message body itself** contains a line that begins with a `.`, the client MUST prefix it with an extra `.` (a process called **dot-stuffing**). The server will strip the first dot. This prevents the line `.` from being mistaken for the message-end terminator.
>
> Example: `Hi.\r\nYesterday was great.\r\n` becomes `Hi..\r\nYesterday was great.\r\n` on the wire.

---

## 3.5 Setting Up a Local SMTP Server for the Lab

Pick **one** of these to install on your lab machine:

### Option 1 — Python's built-in debugging server (fastest)

```bash
$ python3 -m smtpd -n -c DebuggingServer 127.0.0.1:2525
```

### Option 2 — `aiosmtpd` (modern async server, recommended)

```bash
$ pip install aiosmtpd
$ python3 -m aiosmtpd -n -l 127.0.0.1:2525
```

> [!WARNING]
> The **legacy `smtpd` module was removed in Python 3.12**. On modern KTU lab machines (Ubuntu 24.04 / Python 3.12+), you MUST use `aiosmtpd` or an actual MTA like Postfix.

### Option 3 — Install Postfix (most realistic)

```bash
$ sudo apt install postfix
$ sudo systemctl start postfix
$ telnet 127.0.0.1 25
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SMTP Transaction Flow (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> CONNECTED : TCP SYN port 25
    CONNECTED --> GREETED : Send EHLO domain
    GREETED --> MAIL_SENT : Send MAIL FROM
    MAIL_SENT --> RCPT_SENT : Send RCPT TO
    RCPT_SENT --> DATA_OPENED : Send DATA
    DATA_OPENED --> MSG_QUEUED : Send CRLF DOT CRLF
    MSG_QUEUED --> RCPT_SENT : Optional more RCPT
    RCPT_SENT --> CLOSED : Send QUIT
    MSG_QUEUED --> CLOSED : Send QUIT
    CLOSED --> [*]

    note right of CONNECTED : Server sends 220
    note right of GREETED : Server sends 250
    note right of DATA_OPENED : Server sends 354
    note right of MSG_QUEUED : Server sends 250
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SMTP Client–Server

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Layer SMTP Client] --> B[Transport Layer TCP Socket]
    B --> C[Network Layer IP]
    C --> D[Data Link Layer Ethernet]
    D --> E[Physical Layer Cable]

    F[Physical Layer NIC] --> G[Data Link Layer NIC Driver]
    G --> H[Network Layer IP Stack]
    H --> I[Transport Layer TCP Listener Port 25]
    I --> J[Application Layer SMTP Server MTA]

    A -- "EHLO MAIL RCPT DATA QUIT" --> J
    J -- "220 250 354 250 221" --> A

    style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000
    style J fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    style B fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000
    style I fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#000
```

## 4.3 SMTP Message Envelope vs. Header — The Two-Address Model

> [!NOTE]
> SMTP has **TWO sets of addresses** — the **envelope** (used for routing) and the **header** (shown to the user). They don't have to match, and spammers exploit this.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENVELOPE["ENVELOPE (SMTP Commands — Hidden)"]
        EF["MAIL FROM: bounce@ktu.local"]
        RT["RCPT TO: realuser@ktu.local"]
    end

    subgraph HEADER["HEADER (Inside DATA — Visible)"]
        HF["From: Friendly Name <real@ktu.local>"]
        HT["To: Real User <realuser@ktu.local>"]
        HS["Subject: Hello"]
    end

    EF -.-> ROUTING[(MTA Routing Decision)]
    RT -.-> ROUTING
    HF -.-> DISPLAY[User Inbox Display]
    HT -.-> DISPLAY
    HS -.-> DISPLAY

    style ENVELOPE fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    style HEADER fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Complete Mail Transfer

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as SMTP Client
    participant S as SMTP Server
    participant D as Recipient Mailbox

    C->>S: TCP SYN :25
    S-->>C: 220 Service Ready
    C->>S: EHLO client.domain
    S-->>C: 250 OK + capabilities
    C->>S: MAIL FROM:<alice@domain>
    S-->>C: 250 Sender OK
    C->>S: RCPT TO:<bob@domain>
    S-->>C: 250 Recipient OK
    C->>S: DATA
    S-->>C: 354 Send body, end with CRLF.DOT.CRLF
    C->>S: Subject: Hi \\n\\n Hello \\n .
    S-->>C: 250 Queued
    C->>S: QUIT
    S-->>C: 221 Bye
    S->>D: Deliver to mailbox
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three SMTP response codes with their meaning.**

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **220** — Service Ready (sent by server at connection time).
> 2. **250** — Requested mail action OK, completed (after EHLO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO).
> 3. **354** — Start mail input; end with `<CRLF>.<CRLF>` (after `DATA` command).
> 4. *(Bonus)* **550** — Mailbox unavailable (recipient rejected).
> 5. *(Bonus)* **221** — Service closing transmission channel (after `QUIT`).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between SMTP port 25 and port 587.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Port 25 | Port 587 |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | MTA-to-MTA relay | MSA — Mail submission from MUA |
| **Authentication** | Not required | **Mandatory** (AUTH LOGIN / PLAIN) |
| **Encryption** | Optional | Expected (`STARTTLS` upgrade) |
| **Modern Usage** | Server-to-server, often blocked by residential ISPs | Client-to-server submission |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of SMTP with a neat block diagram showing the MUA, MSA, MTA, and MDA. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Write a Python program using raw sockets to implement an SMTP client that sends an email to a local server. Show the expected output trace. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

SMTP uses a **store-and-forward** architecture with four components:

1. **MUA (Mail User Agent)** — The end-user software (Outlook, Gmail web). Composes the message and submits it to an MSA.
2. **MSA (Mail Submission Agent)** — Receives mail from authenticated MUAs on **port 587**. Performs validation, anti-spam checks, and hands off to MTA.
3. **MTA (Mail Transfer Agent)** — The core routing engine. Uses **DNS MX records** to find the destination MTA. May relay through multiple intermediate MTAs.
4. **MDA (Mail Delivery Agent)** — Final hop. Writes the message to the recipient's local mailbox (mbox or Maildir format).
5. **MRA (Mail Retrieval Agent)** — POP3 (port 110) or IMAP (port 143) server that lets the recipient's MUA fetch stored messages.

```
SENDER SIDE                                RECEIVER SIDE
+--------+   submit    +------+  SMTP 25   +------+  SMTP 25   +------+  store   +------+
|  MUA   | ----------> | MSA  | ---------> | MTA  | ---------> | MDA  | -------->| Mbox |
+--------+             +------+            +------+            +------+          +------+
                                              |
                                              v
                                       (DNS MX Lookup)
```

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

> See the complete `raw_smtp_client.py` source code in **Section 3.3.2** of these notes. The expected output trace is in **Section 3.3.3**.
>
> **Marking scheme breakdown:**
> - Correct socket creation & connection: 2 marks
> - `EHLO` handshake with response handling: 1 mark
> - `MAIL FROM` + response: 1 mark
> - `RCPT TO` + response: 1 mark
> - `DATA` + body + `\r\n.\r\n` terminator: 2 marks
> - `QUIT` + clean close: 1 mark
> - Output trace with 220/250/354/250/221: 1 mark (for viva justification)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **(a)** Describe the four phases of an SMTP session. List the major SMTP commands used in each phase. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** With a sequence diagram, explain how an email flows from `alice@ktu.edu` to `bob@ktu.edu` through an SMTP server. Discuss the significance of dot-stuffing. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**The Four Phases of an SMTP Session:**

| Phase | Commands Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Connection** | (TCP-level only) | Establish TCP connection on port 25/587 |
| **2. Handshake / Greeting** | `HELO`, `EHLO` | Client identifies itself; server advertises extensions |
| **3. Mail Transaction** | `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA`, `RSET` | Define envelope, transmit body |
| **4. Termination** | `QUIT`, `NOOP` | Graceful close; optional keep-alive |

**Detailed phase-by-phase behaviour:**

1. **Connection Phase** — The client opens a TCP socket to the server's port. The server sends `220 <hostname> Service Ready`. If the server is busy, it sends `421` and closes.
2. **Handshake Phase** — Client sends `EHLO domain.com`. If the server supports ESMTP, it returns a multiline `250-` list of capabilities (`250-SIZE`, `250-STARTTLS`, `250-AUTH PLAIN LOGIN`, etc.) and ends with a `250 ` line (note the space after `250` marks the *last* line). Without ESMTP, `HELO` returns a single `250 OK`.
3. **Mail Transaction Phase** — Sequence is strict:
   - `MAIL FROM:<reverse-path>` → `250`
   - `RCPT TO:<forward-path>` (repeatable) → `250`
   - `DATA` → `354` → message body → `.` line → `250 queued`
4. **Termination Phase** — `QUIT` → `221 Bye`, then TCP `FIN`.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

> See **Section 4.4 — Sequence Diagram** for the full flow. Below is a textual reconstruction suitable for the answer sheet.

**Email flow narrative:**

1. Alice's MUA composes a message and submits it to her ISP's MSA on port 587.
2. The MSA authenticates Alice, then forwards the message to her domain's primary MTA on port 25.
3. The sending MTA performs a **DNS MX lookup** for `ktu.edu` to find the receiving MTA's hostname.
4. The sending MTA opens a TCP connection to the receiving MTA (port 25).
5. The two MTAs exchange the SMTP handshake (`EHLO` → `250`).
6. The sender relays the envelope (`MAIL FROM` → `RCPT TO` → `DATA`).
7. The receiving MTA accepts (`250 queued`) and hands the message to its local MDA.
8. The MDA stores the message in Bob's mailbox.
9. Later, Bob's MUA retrieves the message via **POP3 (port 110)** or **IMAP (port 143)**.

**Significance of Dot-Stuffing:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SMTP reserves the line containing only a single `.` as the **end-of-message marker**. If the user types a line in the body that happens to start with a dot (e.g., a price like `.99` or a sentence like `.This is great.`), the receiver would terminate the message early, truncating the content.
>
> **Dot-stuffing** solves this: the **transmitting client** transparently adds an extra `.` at the start of any body line that begins with a dot. The **receiving server** removes the extra dot before delivering the message. This makes the protocol transparent to the end user.

**Marking scheme for Q-B(b):**
- Sequence diagram with all 4 actors: 3 marks
- Step-by-step flow narrative: 2 marks
- Dot-stuffing explanation with example: 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners dock marks for:**
> 1. **Using `\n` instead of `\r\n`** — SMTP is strictly CRLF. You will lose 2 marks for any Linux-style newline.
> 2. **Forgetting the dot terminator** — The message body MUST end with a line containing ONLY `.` and CRLF. Without it, the server will hang waiting for more data, and your program will time out.
> 3. **Confusing envelope and header** — `MAIL FROM` (envelope) ≠ `From:` (header). Examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Sending `EHLO` but not reading the multi-line response** — ESMTP replies are multiline (250-). You must read until the final `250 ` line.
> 5. **Not closing the socket** — A leaked socket will cause `OSError: Too many open files` in long viva demonstrations.
> 6. **Port 25 blocked on cloud VMs** — If your lab machine is on AWS/Azure, install Postfix and use port 25 only after opening the firewall, or use port 2525 with `aiosmtpd`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision — read this aloud 5 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **SMTP** = Application Layer protocol, defined in **RFC 5321**, runs on **TCP**.
- [ ] Default ports: **25** (MTA-MTA), **587** (MSA submission with auth), **465** (SMTPS, deprecated but common).
- [ ] SMTP is a **text-based, command-response** protocol using **CRLF (`\r\n`)** line endings.
- [ ] Mandatory session commands in order: `EHLO` → `MAIL FROM` → `RCPT TO` → `DATA` → body → `.` → `QUIT`.
- [ ] The message body terminator is a line containing **only** a single period (`.\r\n`).
- [ ] **Dot-stuffing** prevents body lines starting with `.` from being mistaken for the terminator.
- [ ] SMTP reply codes are **3 digits**: `2xx` = success, `3xx` = intermediate, `4xx` = retry, `5xx` = fatal.
- [ ] Must-know codes: **220, 250, 354, 421, 500, 550, 221**.
- [ ] `EHLO` returns a **multiline** capability list; the last line has a space after the code (`250 `), intermediate lines have a dash (`250-`).
- [ ] **Envelope addresses** (`MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`) drive routing; **header addresses** (`From:`, `To:`) are for display.
- [ ] DNS **MX record** lookup is used to find the destination MTA.
- [ ] POP3 (port 110) / IMAP (port 143) are **retrieval** protocols, NOT part of SMTP's send path.
- [ ] For the lab, **use raw sockets** — it proves protocol-level understanding and scores higher than `smtplib`.
- [ ] If using modern Python (3.12+), the legacy `smtpd` module is gone — install `aiosmtpd` instead.
- [ ] Always wrap the socket in a `try/except/finally` block and call `s.close()` to avoid resource leaks.
- [ ] State machine to remember: **CLOSED → CONNECTED → GREETED → MAIL_SENT → RCPT_SENT → DATA_OPENED → MSG_QUEUED → CLOSED**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement File Transfer Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement File Transfer Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: IMPLEMENT FILE TRANSFER PROTOCOL

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **File Transfer Protocol (FTP)** is an **application-layer** standard internet protocol (defined in **RFC 959**, with extensions in RFC 4217 for IPv6 and RFC 2228 for security) used for the **transfer of computer files** between a **client** and a **server** on a computer network. FTP operates as a **client-server architecture** built on top of the **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)**, providing a reliable, connection-oriented, and stateful file exchange service.

FTP is a **text-based** protocol where both commands and responses are transmitted as **human-readable ASCII strings** over dedicated control and data channels, distinguishing it from binary-oriented protocols such as **HTTP/2** or **SMB**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Library Courier Service"**
> Imagine a large central library (the **FTP Server**) and a reader at home (the **FTP Client**). The reader cannot walk into the library stacks, so they:
> 1. Pick up a **landline telephone** and call the librarian — this is the **Control Connection (Port 21)**. They negotiate: "I want book X, send it to me."
> 2. The librarian then sends a **separate delivery truck** with the book — this is the **Data Connection (Port 20 in active mode, or an ephemeral port in passive mode)**.
> 3. The telephone line stays open for the entire conversation (control), while the truck arrives, drops the book, and may return multiple times (data).
> 4. When the reader hangs up the phone, the session ends.

The **telephone line** = persistent **control channel**; the **truck** = transient **data channel**. This *dual-channel* design is the defining feature of FTP and the reason it differs from HTTP (which uses a single connection).

### 1.3 Core Quantitative Metrics & Standard Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Channel Port** | **21/TCP** | Always reserved for FTP commands |
| **Active Data Channel Port** | **20/TCP** | Server initiates data from port 20 |
| **Passive Data Channel Port** | **Ephemeral (≥ 1024)** | Server tells client a high port to use |
| **Default Transfer Mode** | **ASCII** | Text files; switch to Binary for media |
| **FTP Response Code Range** | **1xx – 5xx** | 3-digit status indicators |
| **Data Representation Types** | **ASCII, EBCDIC, Image (Binary)** | Three defined types in RFC 959 |
| **Stream Structure Types** | **File, Record, Page** | Three structural models |
| **Transmission Modes** | **Stream, Block, Compressed** | Three data compression methods |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Examiners love asking the difference between **Port 20** and **Port 21**, or whether FTP is a **stateful or stateless** protocol. FTP is **stateful** — the server maintains session information (current directory, authentication state, transfer mode) throughout the connection.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of FTP Architecture

FTP's uniqueness stems from its **dual-connection model**. Let us dissect its operational logic step-by-step:

### Step 1 — TCP Control Connection Establishment
- The **client** opens a TCP connection from an **ephemeral port** (e.g., 50000) to the server's **well-known Port 21**.
- This connection **persists** for the entire FTP session.
- All FTP **commands** (sent by the client) and **responses** (sent by the server) traverse this channel.
- Commands are **4-byte ASCII codes** (e.g., `USER`, `PASS`, `LIST`, `RETR`, `STOR`).

### Step 2 — User Authentication
- Client sends `USER <username>` → Server replies with `331 Password required`.
- Client sends `PASS <password>` → Server replies with `230 Login successful` or `530 Login incorrect`.

### Step 3 — Data Connection Negotiation
- FTP uses either **Active Mode** (PORT command) or **Passive Mode** (PASV command) to establish the second TCP connection for actual file transfer.

### Step 4 — File Operations
- `RETR <filename>` — Download a file from server to client.
- `STOR <filename>` — Upload a file from client to server.
- `LIST` — List directory contents (transferred over data connection).
- `DELE <filename>` — Delete a file.
- `MKD <dirname>` — Create a directory.

### Step 5 — Session Termination
- Client sends `QUIT` → Server replies `221 Goodbye` → Control connection is closed.

## 2.2 Active Mode vs. Passive Mode — The Core Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most frequently asked KTU question on FTP. Memorize the direction of connection initiation for both modes.**

### Active Mode (PORT)
1. Client opens control connection to server port 21.
2. Client sends `PORT 192,168,1,10,5,234` (encoded: IP + 2-byte port = high×256+low).
3. **Server initiates** the data connection **FROM** its port 20 **TO** the client's specified port.
4. Problem: Client-side firewalls often **block inbound** connections → Active mode fails.

### Passive Mode (PASV)
1. Client opens control connection to server port 21.
2. Client sends `PASV`.
3. Server replies with `227 Entering Passive Mode (h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2)`.
4. **Client initiates** the data connection to the server's advertised IP/port.
5. Solves the firewall problem because the client always initiates outbound.

## 2.3 FTP Response Code Taxonomy

| Code Range | Category | Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx** | Positive Preliminary | `150` | File status OK, about to open data connection |
| **2xx** | Positive Completion | `200`, `220`, `226` | Command successful |
| **3xx** | Positive Intermediate | `331`, `350` | Further information needed |
| **4xx** | Transient Negative | `421`, `450` | Try again later |
| **5xx** | Permanent Negative | `500`, `530`, `550` | Command not executed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Do not confuse **421 (Service not available, closing control connection)** with **530 (Not logged in)**. A 530 means credentials failed; a 421 means the server is shutting down.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although FTP is a protocol (not a math topic), KTU examiners test **port-decoding formulas** in Passive/Active mode. This is your "equation sheet":

$$
\text{Decoded Port} = (p_1 \times 256) + p_2
$$

where $p_1$ and $p_2$ are the last two bytes of the PASV/PORT response.

**Worked Example:**
Server returns: `227 Entering Passive Mode (192,168,1,5,19,136)`
- $p_1 = 19$, $p_2 = 136$
- Decoded port: $(19 \times 256) + 136 = 4864 + 136 = 5000$
- Therefore, the client connects to **192.168.1.5 on port 5000** for data transfer.

## 2.5 FTP vs. Other File Transfer Protocols

| Feature | FTP | TFTP | SFTP | HTTP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP | UDP | TCP (over SSH) | TCP |
| **Port** | 21 (Ctrl), 20 (Data) | **69** | **22** | **80/443** |
| **Authentication** | Yes (USER/PASS) | No | Yes (SSH keys/password) | Optional |
| **Encryption** | None (plaintext) | None | **Yes (SSH)** | Optional (HTTPS) |
| **Complexity** | High | Very Low | High | Medium |
| **Use Case** | Web hosting, legacy systems | PXE boot, router configs | Secure file transfer | Web downloads |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web hosting**: Hosting providers (GoDaddy, HostGator) expose FTP/SFTP for uploading website files.
- **IoT firmware updates**: Many embedded devices use TFTP for bootloader firmware retrieval.
- **Data centers**: Automated backup scripts use FTP in batch mode for nightly archival.
- **Software mirrors**: Linux distros mirror ISO files via anonymous FTP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Procedure & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section follows the **standard KTU lab record format**: **Aim → Algorithm/Procedure → Commands → Sample Output → Result**.

## 3.1 Aim

To implement the **File Transfer Protocol (FTP)** on a Linux system, demonstrate file upload, download, directory listing, and analyze the FTP session using command-line utilities and **Wireshark**.

## 3.2 Algorithm / Procedure

### Part A: Connecting to an FTP Server via Command Line

**Step 1 — Open a terminal** on a Linux/Unix system (Ubuntu, Fedora, or Kali Linux recommended).

**Step 2 — Verify FTP client is installed**
```bash
which ftp
```
If not installed, use:
```bash
sudo apt-get install ftp        # Debian/Ubuntu
sudo yum install ftp            # Red Hat/Fedora
```

**Step 3 — Connect to the FTP server**
```bash
ftp <server_ip_or_hostname>
```
For practice, you may use a public mirror such as:
```bash
ftp ftp.gnu.org
```

**Step 4 — Authenticate**
- For anonymous FTP: enter username `anonymous` and your email as password.
- For private server: enter the credentials provided by the administrator.

**Step 5 — Navigate the remote directory**
```bash
pwd          # Print working directory on server
ls           # List files in current directory
cd <dir>     # Change directory
mkdir test   # Create a directory named 'test'
```

**Step 6 — Download a file (RETR command)**
```bash
get <filename>              # Download to local current directory
get <filename> <new_name>   # Download and rename
mget *.txt                  # Download multiple files (prompt will appear)
```

**Step 7 — Upload a file (STOR command)**
```bash
put <filename>              # Upload a single file
mput *.txt                  # Upload multiple files
```

**Step 8 — Set transfer mode**
```bash
binary       # Set to binary mode (for images, executables)
ascii        # Set to ASCII mode (for text files)
```

**Step 9 — Terminate the session**
```bash
bye          # or 'quit' or 'exit'
```

### Part B: FTP Implementation via Python Socket Programming

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **Computer Networking Lab** (CS branch), socket programming is the **expected programming component** for this experiment. Python's `ftplib` is a high-level wrapper; raw `socket` usage demonstrates deeper protocol understanding.

**Approach 1: Using Python's `ftplib` (High-Level)**

```python
# ftp_client_demo.py
from ftplib import FTP

# Step 1: Connect to FTP server
ftp = FTP()
ftp.connect('ftp.dlptest.com', 21, timeout=10)  # Public test FTP server

# Step 2: Login (use test credentials: dlptestuser / dlpuser)
print("Welcome:", ftp.getwelcome())
ftp.login(user='dlptestuser', passwd='rNrKYTX9g7z3RgJRmxWuGHbeu')

# Step 3: Print current directory
print("Current Dir:", ftp.pwd())

# Step 4: List directory contents
print("Files:", ftp.nlst())

# Step 5: Upload a local file
with open('upload_demo.txt', 'rb') as f:
    ftp.storbinary('STOR upload_demo.txt', f)
print("Upload complete.")

# Step 6: Download a file
with open('downloaded_demo.txt', 'wb') as f:
    ftp.retrbinary('RETR upload_demo.txt', f.write)
print("Download complete.")

# Step 7: Close connection
ftp.quit()
```

**Approach 2: Raw Socket Implementation (Low-Level — Bonus Marks Material)**

This shows the **raw FTP command exchange** over a TCP socket:

```python
# raw_ftp_client.py
import socket

def send_cmd(sock, cmd):
    sock.sendall((cmd + '\r\n').encode())
    return sock.recv(1024).decode()

# Step 1: Establish control connection
sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
sock.connect(('ftp.dlptest.com', 21))
print(sock.recv(1024).decode())   # 220 Welcome

# Step 2: Authenticate
print(send_cmd(sock, 'USER dlptestuser'))   # 331 Password required
print(send_cmd(sock, 'PASS rNrKYTX9g7z3RgJRmxWuGHbeu'))  # 230 Login successful

# Step 3: Enter passive mode
resp = send_cmd(sock, 'PASV')
print("PASV Response:", resp)
# Parse: 227 Entering Passive Mode (h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2)
import re
match = re.search(r'\((\d+),(\d+),(\d+),(\d+),(\d+),(\d+)\)', resp)
h1, h2, h3, h4, p1, p2 = map(int, match.groups())
data_ip = f"{h1}.{h2}.{h3}.{h4}"
data_port = (p1 * 256) + p2
print(f"Data Channel -> {data_ip}:{data_port}")

# Step 4: Open data channel
data_sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
data_sock.connect((data_ip, data_port))

# Step 5: List directory
print(send_cmd(sock, 'LIST'))
print(data_sock.recv(4096).decode())
data_sock.close()

# Step 6: Quit
print(send_cmd(sock, 'QUIT'))
sock.close()
```

**Mathematical Verification of Port Decoding** (referenced from the formula sheet):
$$
\text{Data Port} = (p_1 \times 256) + p_2 = (195 \times 256) + 129 = 49920 + 129 = 50049
$$

## 3.3 Sample Output (Terminal Capture)

```
Connected to ftp.dlptest.com.
220 Welcome to the DLP Test FTP Server.
Name (ftp.dlptest.com:student): dlptestuser
331 Please specify the password.
Password:
230 Login successful.
Remote system type is UNIX.
ftp> pwd
257 "/" is the current directory
ftp> ls
227 Entering Passive Mode (127,0,0,1,156,64).
150 Here comes the directory listing.
-rw-r--r--    1 1000     1000         1024 Jan 01 12:00 readme.txt
226 Directory send OK.
ftp> get readme.txt
local: readme.txt remote: readme.txt
227 Entering Passive Mode (127,0,0,1,156,80).
150 Opening BINARY mode data connection.
226 Transfer complete.
1024 bytes received in 0.05 secs (20.48 KB/s)
ftp> bye
221 Goodbye.
```

## 3.4 Wireshark Analysis Steps

1. Start **Wireshark** on the client interface.
2. Apply display filter: `ftp or ftp-data`.
3. Perform FTP operations (login, list, get, put).
4. Observe:
   - `Request: USER ...`, `Request: PASS ...` (visible in **plaintext** — security risk!).
   - `Response: 220`, `Response: 230`, `Response: 227`.
   - FTP-DATA packets on port 50049 (decoded from PASV).
5. **Inference for record**: FTP transmits credentials in cleartext, justifying the use of **SFTP/FTPS** in production.

## 3.5 Result

The File Transfer Protocol was successfully implemented, demonstrating:
- ✅ Authentication via `USER`/`PASS` commands.
- ✅ Directory listing via `LIST` over a passive-mode data channel.
- ✅ File upload (`STOR`) and download (`RETR`).
- ✅ Port-decoding from the PASV response.
- ✅ Wireshark capture confirming the dual-channel architecture and **plaintext credential exposure**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FTP Client-Server Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA[FTP Client Application] -->|Command Channel TCP 21| serverA[FTP Server Daemon vsftpd]
    serverA -->|Response Codes 1xx to 5xx| clientA
    clientA -->|Data Channel Active Port 20| serverA
    clientA -->|Data Channel Passive Ephemeral Port| serverA
    clientA -->|USER PASS LIST RETR STOR QUIT| serverA
    serverA -->|File Stream Binary or ASCII| clientA
```

> **Legend:** Blue arrows = Control Plane (Commands/Responses), Red arrows = Data Plane (File Payload).

## 4.2 FTP Session Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as FTP Client
    participant S as FTP Server
    Note over C,S: TCP 3-Way Handshake on Port 21
    C->>S: SYN
    S->>C: SYN-ACK
    C->>S: ACK
    S->>C: 220 Service Ready
    C->>S: USER anonymous
    S->>C: 331 Password Required
    C->>S: PASS user@example.com
    S->>C: 230 Login Successful
    C->>S: PASV
    S->>C: 227 Entering Passive Mode h1 h2 h3 h4 p1 p2
    Note over C,S: Client opens TCP to advertised port
    C->>S: LIST
    S->>C: 150 Opening Data Channel
    S-->>C: Directory Listing over data port
    S->>C: 226 Transfer Complete
    C->>S: QUIT
    S->>C: 221 Goodbye
    Note over C,S: FIN handshake closes control channel
```

## 4.3 Active vs Passive Mode — Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ActiveMode[ACTIVE MODE PORT]
        A1[Client sends PORT command with its IP and port] --> A2[Server initiates data connection from port 20]
        A2 --> A3[Client firewall may block inbound data]
    end

    subgraph PassiveMode[PASSIVE MODE PASV]
        P1[Client sends PASV command] --> P2[Server responds with IP and ephemeral port]
        P2 --> P3[Client initiates data connection outbound]
        P3 --> P4[Client firewall friendly]
    end

    style ActiveMode fill:#ffe5e5
    style PassiveMode fill:#e5f5ff
```

## 4.4 FTP Protocol Stack Positioning

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Application Layer FTP RFC 959] --> L4[Transport Layer TCP]
    L4 --> L3[Network Layer IP]
    L3 --> L2[Data Link Layer Ethernet or WiFi]
    L2 --> L1[Physical Layer Cable or Radio]
    style L5 fill:#ffeb99
    style L4 fill:#b3d9ff
    style L3 fill:#c2f0c2
    style L2 fill:#ffcc99
    style L1 fill:#d9b3ff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*With a neat diagram, explain the FTP client-server model. Why does FTP use two separate TCP connections?*

**Model Answer:**

FTP uses a **client-server model** where the client initiates a control connection to the server's port **21** for sending commands and receiving responses. A **second, separate TCP connection** is established for actual data transfer (port 20 in active mode, or an ephemeral port in passive mode).

**Why two connections?**
1. **Separation of concerns**: Commands can be sent while a file transfer is in progress (e.g., abort a transfer with `ABOR` mid-stream).
2. **State persistence**: The control connection remains open for the entire session, while data connections are transient and reopened per file.
3. **Efficiency**: Multiple data transfers (directory listings, multiple file downloads) can reuse the same control channel.

(Diagram: Two arrows — one persistent solid line on port 21 for commands, and one dashed transient line on port 20/ephemeral for data.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]** 
*Differentiate between Active and Passive FTP modes. Which mode is preferred when the client is behind a firewall?*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Active Mode (PORT) | Passive Mode (PASV) |
|---|---|---|
| Data connection initiator | **Server** connects to client | **Client** connects to server |
| Port used by server | **20** (fixed) | **Ephemeral** (negotiated) |
| Client firewall issue | **Yes** — inbound blocked | **No** — outbound allowed |
| Command used | `PORT h1,h2,h3,h4,p1,p2` | `PASV` (server replies with port) |

**Preferred mode when client is behind a firewall:** **Passive Mode**, because the firewall allows outbound connections but typically blocks unsolicited inbound connections. Passive mode keeps the client as the initiator, satisfying firewall rules.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

#### (a) Describe the FTP protocol architecture in detail. Explain the function of control and data connections with port numbers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

FTP architecture consists of three major components: the **FTP Client (User-PI)**, the **FTP Server (Server-PI)**, and **two TCP channels**.

**1. Control Connection (Port 21):**
- Initiated by the **client** to the server's well-known port **21**.
- **Persistent** for the entire session.
- Carries **FTP commands** (`USER`, `PASS`, `CWD`, `LIST`, `RETR`, `STOR`, `QUIT`) and **response codes** (`220`, `230`, `331`, `550`).
- Uses **Telnet protocol** semantics — commands are NVT ASCII strings terminated by `\r\n`.
- The **Protocol Interpreter (PI)** on each side parses these commands.

**2. Data Connection (Port 20 / Ephemeral):**
- Used for actual file content transfer.
- In **Active mode**: server connects FROM its port 20 TO a client-specified port.
- In **Passive mode**: client connects TO a server-specified ephemeral port.
- Uses the **Data Transfer Process (DTP)** to handle the actual byte stream.
- Closes after each file transfer; reopened for the next.

**3. User & Server Protocol Interpreters (PI):**
- **User-PI**: Issues commands; manages control connection.
- **Server-PI**: Listens on port 21, parses commands, sends responses, and signals the DTP to open data connections.

**Diagram (textual):**
```
Client PI --------(Port 21, Control)--------> Server PI
Client DTP <-------(Port 20/Ephemeral, Data)-- Server DTP
```

(7 marks = 2 marks diagram, 3 marks explanation of control, 2 marks explanation of data)

#### (b) Explain the FTP Passive mode operation with a step-by-step session example. Include the port-decoding formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Client opens TCP connection to server port 21.
**Step 2:** Server replies `220 Service Ready`.
**Step 3:** Client sends `USER john` → Server replies `331 Password required`.
**Step 4:** Client sends `PASS secret123` → Server replies `230 Login successful`.
**Step 5:** Client sends `PASV` → Server replies:
`227 Entering Passive Mode (192,168,1,5,19,136)`
**Step 6:** Port decoding:
$$
\text{Port} = (p_1 \times 256) + p_2 = (19 \times 256) + 136 = 5000
$$
**Step 7:** Client opens a NEW TCP connection from an ephemeral port to `192.168.1.5:5000`.
**Step 8:** Client sends `RETR report.pdf` over the control channel.
**Step 9:** Server replies `150 Opening BINARY mode data connection` on the data channel.
**Step 10:** File bytes flow server → client on port 5000.
**Step 11:** Server replies `226 Transfer complete` on the control channel.
**Step 12:** Data connection closes; control connection remains.
**Step 13:** Client sends `QUIT` → Server replies `221 Goodbye`.

(7 marks = 2 marks steps, 2 marks decoding, 2 marks diagram, 1 mark termination)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

#### (a) Implement an FTP client program in Python using sockets to list the contents of a remote directory. Show the code and explain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```python
import socket
import re

def ftp_list(host, user, password):
    # Step 1: Open control socket on port 21
    ctrl = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    ctrl.connect((host, 21))
    print(ctrl.recv(1024).decode())   # 220 banner

    # Step 2: Login
    def cmd(s, c): s.sendall((c+'\r\n').encode()); return s.recv(1024).decode()
    print(cmd(ctrl, f'USER {user}'))
    print(cmd(ctrl, f'PASS {password}'))

    # Step 3: Enter Passive Mode and parse port
    resp = cmd(ctrl, 'PASV')
    m = re.search(r'\((\d+),(\d+),(\d+),(\d+),(\d+),(\d+)\)', resp)
    p1, p2 = int(m.group(5)), int(m.group(6))
    data_port = (p1 * 256) + p2
    data = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    data.connect((host, data_port))

    # Step 4: Request listing
    print(cmd(ctrl, 'LIST'))
    print(data.recv(4096).decode())
    data.close()
    ctrl.sendall(b'QUIT\r\n')
    ctrl.close()

ftp_list('ftp.dlptest.com', 'dlptestuser', 'rNrKYTX9g7z3RgJRmxWuGHbeu')
```

**Explanation (4 key points):**
1. **Control socket** is created on port 21 using `SOCK_STREAM` (TCP).
2. **USER** and **PASS** commands perform authentication.
3. **PASV** response is parsed with a **regular expression** to extract the 6-tuple of IP/port bytes; the port is decoded as $256 p_1 + p_2$.
4. **LIST** triggers the server to push directory entries over the data socket.

#### (b) Compare FTP with TFTP and SFTP in terms of transport protocol, port number, authentication, and security. When would you choose each? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | FTP | TFTP | SFTP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP (reliable) | UDP (unreliable) | TCP (over SSH) |
| **Port** | 21 (ctrl), 20 (data) | **69** | **22** (shared with SSH) |
| **Authentication** | Username + password (cleartext) | **None** | SSH key or password |
| **Encryption** | **None** (plaintext credentials) | **None** | **AES/3DES** (SSH) |
| **File size limit** | None | 32 MB (RFC 2347) | None |
| **Complexity** | High (state machine) | Very low | Medium |
| **Best use case** | Web hosting, LAN file sharing | PXE boot, router config, simple firmware | **Secure enterprise file transfer** |

**Selection criteria:**
- Use **FTP** only on **trusted internal networks** where simplicity matters and security is handled at the network layer.
- Use **TFTP** for **diskless booting**, **VoIP config provisioning**, or **router firmware** where a minimal footprint is critical.
- Use **SFTP** for **any internet-facing or sensitive file transfer** — it is the modern default and satisfies compliance requirements (PCI-DSS, HIPAA).

(7 marks = 4 marks table, 2 marks selection logic, 1 mark real-world example)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes Students Make in FTP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Swapping Port 20 and 21**: Port 21 is for **control**; Port 20 is for **active-mode data only**. Writing the opposite costs 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Confusing PASV initiator**: In passive mode, the **CLIENT** initiates the data connection, NOT the server. Examiners mark this as a "fundamental conceptual error" (-2 marks).
> 3. **Forgetting the port-decoding formula**: KTU requires the formula $(p_1 \times 256) + p_2$. Writing just the raw bytes (e.g., "19,136") without decoding costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Calling FTP "stateless"**: FTP is **stateful** — it maintains session info (current dir, auth state, transfer mode). Saying otherwise = -1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring security**: Any FTP answer that does **not** mention plaintext credential transmission and the need for SFTP/FTPS is considered incomplete. Add a one-line security caveat to recover those marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the FIN/QUIT termination**: The control connection closes only on `QUIT`; data connections close after each transfer. Mention this in sequence diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ FTP = **Application-layer**, **TCP-based**, **client-server**, **stateful**, **text-based** protocol (RFC 959).
- ✅ **Control Port = 21**, **Active Data Port = 20**, **Passive Data Port = Ephemeral**.
- ✅ FTP uses **TWO TCP connections**: persistent control + transient data.
- ✅ **Active mode**: server initiates data (blocked by client firewalls).
- ✅ **Passive mode**: client initiates data (firewall-friendly, default in modern clients).
- ✅ Port-decoding formula: $\text{port} = (p_1 \times 256) + p_2$.
- ✅ Commands: `USER`, `PASS`, `LIST`, `RETR` (download), `STOR` (upload), `DELE`, `MKD`, `CWD`, `QUIT`.
- ✅ Response codes: **1xx Preliminary**, **2xx Success**, **3xx Intermediate**, **4xx Transient Fail**, **5xx Permanent Fail**.
- ✅ **Anonymous FTP**: username = `anonymous`, password = email (still insecure).
- ✅ FTP transmits **passwords in cleartext** — use **SFTP (port 22)** or **FTPS (port 990)** for security.
- ✅ TFTP uses **UDP port 69**, no authentication — for trusted networks only.
- ✅ In Wireshark: filter with `ftp or ftp-data`; expect to see `USER`/`PASS` in plaintext.
- ✅ Modern replacement: **SFTP** (SSH File Transfer Protocol) — same command semantics, encrypted channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Specific Note:** The lab exam for this experiment typically asks students to (1) demonstrate an FTP session using terminal commands, (2) capture packets in Wireshark and identify control vs. data channels, and (3) write a short Python socket program to automate the session. **Always include sample input/output in your lab record** — examiners award full marks only when the program is **executed and verified**, not merely written.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Wireshark** is a free, open-source, cross-platform **packet analyzer** (also historically called a *network protocol analyzer* or *packet sniffer*) used for **network troubleshooting, analysis, software and protocol development, and education**. It captures live network traffic from a wired or wireless interface and decodes the captured frames into a human-readable, layered representation based on standard protocol dissectors.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCERL407 – Computer Networking Lab)**, Wireshark is positioned as the primary **passive measurement tool** that allows students to *observe* the behavior of TCP/IP protocols that they earlier studied only theoretically in the Computer Networks theory course.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Examiner's Viewpoint)**
> - **Wireshark is a *passive* sniffer.** It only listens to frames reaching the network interface card (NIC) in promiscuous/monitor mode. It does *not* generate traffic.
> - The captured file format is **`.pcap`** (legacy libpcap) or **`.pcapng`** (PCAP Next Generation, default in Wireshark ≥ 1.8).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of network traffic as **letters being delivered through a postal system**:

- The **Ethernet cable / Wi-Fi medium** is the *physical highway*.
- Each **frame** is an *envelope* with many nested envelopes inside (TCP inside IP inside Ethernet).
- Normally, you (the host) only see envelopes addressed to *you*. Switches, routers, and other devices along the way also see only what is *for them*.
- **Wireshark is like putting a *transparent glass mailbox* on that highway.** Every envelope that passes by becomes visible — you can read the outer label, the inner letter, and even the signature at the bottom.

Another everyday analogy: **Wireshark is a "black box flight recorder" for your network.** Pilots use it after incidents; network engineers use Wireshark to reconstruct what really happened on the wire.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal & Ethical Note (Frequently Asked in Viva)**
> Capturing traffic on a network you do not own or administer may be illegal in many jurisdictions. In lab settings, you should only capture on the dedicated lab network or on loopback traffic to `127.0.0.1`.

## 1.3 Key Metrics to Remember (Always Bolded in Answers)

| Metric | Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Default Ethernet **MTU** | **1500 bytes** | Maximum payload of a standard Ethernet frame (excluding 14-byte header + 4-byte FCS) |
| Minimum Ethernet frame | **64 bytes** | Required for collision detection on legacy shared media |
| Maximum Ethernet frame | **1518 bytes** | 14 header + 1500 payload + 4 FCS (untagged); **1522 bytes** with 802.1Q VLAN tag |
| TCP header (no options) | **20 bytes** | Minimum TCP header length |
| IP header (no options) | **20 bytes** | Minimum IPv4 header length |
| Default Wireshark capture file extension | **`.pcapng`** | The format you will save and submit for lab records |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Wireshark Architecture — Three Logical Layers

Wireshark is built on a modular, layered architecture. Understanding this is essential for viva questions and for diagnosing why a protocol "does not decode."

1. **Capture Layer (`libpcap` / `Npcap` / `WinPcap`)**
   - A *separate* OS-level library (not written by the Wireshark team) that interfaces directly with the NIC driver.
   - Promiscuous mode is enabled here so the NIC forwards *every* frame, not just those addressed to your MAC.
   - On Windows, **Npcap** is the modern replacement for the deprecated **WinPcap**.

2. **Dissection / Decoding Layer**
   - Each supported protocol has a **dissector** (a C or Lua plugin).
   - Dissectors are stacked: the Ethernet dissector hands off the payload to the IP dissector, which hands off to TCP/UDP, which hands off to HTTP/DNS/etc.
   - If a dissector is missing, the packet is shown as raw data — this is a classic viva question.

3. **Presentation / GUI Layer**
   - The three-pane window:
     - **Pane 1 (Top) – Packet List:** One row per captured frame.
     - **Pane 2 (Middle) – Packet Details:** Protocol tree (expandable).
     - **Pane 3 (Bottom) – Packet Bytes:** Raw hexadecimal + ASCII view.

## 2.2 Capture vs. Display Filters — A Critical Distinction

This is **the single most tested concept** in Wireshark exams.

| Feature | **Capture Filter** | **Display Filter** |
|---|---|---|
| When applied | *Before* the packet is saved to memory | *After* the packet is captured, in real time |
| Syntax engine | **Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF)** — same as `tcpdump` | **Wireshark Display Filter (WDF)** — proprietary |
| Example | `tcp port 80` | `tcp.port == 80` |
| Can it reduce disk usage? | **Yes** — discarded packets are never stored | **No** — packets are still captured and stored |
| Common operators | `and`, `or`, `not`, `host`, `net`, `port` | `==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `contains`, `matches` |
| Case sensitive? | Yes (protocols/types) | No (field names) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake in Lab Records**
> Students often type `tcp.port == 80` in the *Capture Options* dialog — this silently fails because BPF does not understand `==`. Always use the BPF syntax in capture filters and WDF syntax in display filters.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.3.1 Common Display Filters (must memorize)

| Goal | Filter Expression |
|---|---|
| Only HTTP traffic | `http` |
| Only DNS | `dns` |
| Traffic to/from a specific IP | `ip.addr == 192.168.1.10` |
| Only TCP SYN packets (connection start) | `tcp.flags.syn == 1 && tcp.flags.ack == 0` |
| TCP retransmissions | `tcp.analysis.retransmission` |
| HTTP GET requests only | `http.request.method == "GET"` |
| Packets with errors | `tcp.analysis.flags` |
| Specific conversation (5-tuple) | `tcp.stream eq 4` |
| ICMP echo request (ping) | `icmp.type == 8` |
| Traffic on a subnet | `ip.src == 192.168.1.0/24` |

### 2.3.2 Common Capture Filters (BPF Syntax)

| Goal | Capture Filter |
|---|---|
| Single host | `host 192.168.1.10` |
| Network range | `net 192.168.1.0/24` |
| Specific port | `port 80` |
| TCP only | `tcp` |
| UDP only | `udp` |
| Combine conditions | `host 10.0.0.1 and tcp port 443` |
| Exclude a host | `not host 192.168.1.5` |

### 2.3.3 Encapsulation Offsets (Critical for Manual Hex Analysis)

When asked to "identify the protocol field at byte X" in an exam:

| Layer | Header starts at byte (after Ethernet preamble/SFD) | Header length |
|---|---|---|
| Ethernet II | **0** | 14 bytes (DA 6 + SA 6 + EtherType 2) |
| IP (IPv4) | **14** | 20 bytes minimum |
| TCP | **14 + IP Header Length × 4** | 20 bytes minimum |
| UDP | **14 + IP Header Length × 4** | 8 bytes |
| Application (e.g., HTTP) | **14 + IP + TCP/UDP** | Variable |

The IP Header Length field is found at the **first byte of the IP header** (the low nibble), e.g., `0x45` → 5 words → 5 × 4 = **20 bytes**.

### 2.3.4 Key TCP Flags (Bit-Mask) — Must Memorize

| Flag (Bit) | Decimal value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| FIN | **0x01** | Finish, graceful close |
| SYN | **0x02** | Synchronize, connection start |
| RST | **0x04** | Reset, abrupt close / error |
| PSH | **0x08** | Push buffered data to application |
| ACK | **0x10** | Acknowledgment |
| URG | **0x20** | Urgent pointer valid |

> A packet with **SYN = 1, ACK = 0** is a connection initiation. A packet with **SYN = 1, ACK = 1** is a SYN-ACK (second step of 3-way handshake). This is a guaranteed viva question.

### 2.3.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Engineers:** Diagnose slow applications, find rogue DHCP servers, identify broadcast storms.
- **Security Analysts:** Detect ARP spoofing, malware C2 traffic, data exfiltration.
- **QA/Test Engineers:** Verify protocol compliance in embedded/IoT devices.
- **Developers:** Debug socket-level code without instrumenting the application.
- **KTU Students:** Correlate theory (e.g., 3-way handshake, TTL behavior, windowing) with real observed bytes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Lab Procedure — Capturing and Analyzing Live Traffic

The following is the **exact sequence** expected in a KTU lab record for this experiment.

### Step 1 — Install and Launch
- **Linux:** `sudo apt install wireshark` (or via the official PPA for the latest stable).
- **Windows:** Download the official 64-bit installer from `wireshark.org`. During install, accept the **Npcap** driver prompt (required to capture packets).
- Launch with **root / administrator privileges** — capturing is a privileged kernel operation.

### Step 2 — Select the Capture Interface
- Open *Capture → Options*.
- Choose the correct NIC:
  - `eth0`, `wlan0`, `enp0s3` (Linux)
  - `Ethernet`, `Wi-Fi` (Windows)
  - `lo` / `Loopback` — captures only traffic to/from `127.0.0.1`. **Use this for offline labs to avoid noise.**

### Step 3 — Set the Capture Filter (Optional but Recommended)
- In the *Capture Filter* field, type a BPF expression.
  - Example: `host 192.168.1.10 and tcp port 80`
- Click *Start* (the shark-fin icon).

### Step 4 — Generate Traffic to Capture
- In a separate terminal/command prompt, induce known traffic:
  - `ping 8.8.8.8` → produces ICMP.
  - `nslookup google.com` → produces DNS.
  - `curl http://example.com` → produces HTTP.
  - Open any web page → produces HTTP/HTTPS/DNS.

### Step 5 — Stop and Save the Capture
- Click the red *Stop* square.
- *File → Save As…* with extension **`.pcapng`**.
- This file is what you submit as the lab record (along with screenshots of the three panes).

### Step 6 — Apply a Display Filter
- Type into the green filter bar above the packet list.
- Press *Enter*.
- Use the **Expression…** button if you forget a field name.

### Step 7 — Follow a Stream
- *Right-click a packet → Follow → TCP Stream (or UDP Stream)*.
- Wireshark filters `tcp.stream eq <id>` and reconstructs the application-layer conversation in a new window.
- This is the **definitive way** to extract HTTP request/response bodies, SMTP messages, etc.

### Step 8 — Export Objects (HTTP Only)
- *File → Export Objects → HTTP…*
- Lists every HTTP file transferred. You can save images, HTML, JavaScript, etc. directly to disk. **This only works for unencrypted HTTP**, not HTTPS.

### Step 9 — Use Statistics
- *Statistics → Conversations* → list of all L2/L3/L4 endpoints.
- *Statistics → Endpoints* → list of unique hosts.
- *Statistics → Protocol Hierarchy* → pie chart of protocols observed.
- *Statistics → Flow Graph* → visual sequence diagram of the conversation.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Decoding a TCP SYN from a Hex Dump

> This type of calculation is a **favorite 7-mark question** in KTU ESE.

**Given:** The first 54 bytes of a captured frame (in hexadecimal):

```
ff ff ff ff ff ff 00 1a 2b 3c 4d 5e 08 00 45 00
00 28 00 01 00 00 40 06 00 00 c0 a8 01 0a c0 a8
01 01 00 50 00 51 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 50 02
20 00 00 00 00 00
```

**Step 1 — Identify the Ethernet Header (first 14 bytes):**

$$\text{Destination MAC} = \texttt{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (broadcast)}$$

$$\text{Source MAC} = \texttt{00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E}$$

$$\text{EtherType} = \texttt{0x0800 (IPv4)}$$

**Step 2 — Decode the IP Header (next 20 bytes, starting at offset 14):**

Byte at offset 14 = `0x45`. The lower nibble `5` is the header length in 32-bit words.

$$\text{IP Header Length} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}$$

Byte at offset 23 = `0x40` → the **Flags + Fragment Offset** field. The high nibble `4` is the **Don't Fragment (DF) flag = 1**.

Byte at offset 26 = `0x06` → **Protocol = 6 (TCP)**.

$$\text{Source IP} = \text{offset 26–29} = \texttt{C0.A8.01.0A} = 192.168.1.10$$

$$\text{Destination IP} = \text{offset 30–33} = \texttt{C0.A8.01.01} = 192.168.1.1$$

**Step 3 — Decode the TCP Header (next 20 bytes, starting at offset 34):**

$$\text{Source Port (2 bytes, big-endian)} = \texttt{0x0050} = 80 \text{ (HTTP)}$$

$$\text{Destination Port} = \texttt{0x0051} = 81$$

Byte at offset 46 of the TCP header (= frame offset 80 — wait, 34 + 12 = 46) is `0x50`. This is the high nibble of offset 12 inside the TCP header, which is the **Data Offset (header length) field**:

$$\text{TCP Data Offset} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes (no options)}$$

Byte at offset 47 = `0x02` → **TCP Flags = 0x02 = SYN**.

> **Conclusion:** This is a TCP SYN packet from `192.168.1.10:80` to `192.168.1.1:81`. Since the source port is well-known (80), the *responder* `192.168.1.10` is actually the server being contacted by the client. The exam may ask you to identify which side initiated the connection — **the side that sent the SYN is the initiator (client)**. Therefore `192.168.1.1` is the client, even though the source port is 80.

**Step 4 — Compute Payload Length:**

Total IP packet length (from IP header bytes 16–17, big-endian) = `0x0028` = 40 bytes.

$$\text{TCP Payload} = 40 - 20_{\text{IP header}} - 20_{\text{TCP header}} = 0 \text{ bytes}$$

> A SYN packet carries **no payload** — this is consistent with the TCP spec. Full marks in the exam require this confirmation.

## 3.3 Common Pitfalls During Lab Execution

| Pitfall | What You Will See | The Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Capturing on the wrong interface | Only broadcast/multicast traffic, or empty | Pick the NIC with a non-zero packet count before stopping |
| Forgot to enable promiscuous mode | Only your own traffic | Check *Capture → Options → Enable promiscuous mode* |
| HTTPS filter shows nothing | Modern web is encrypted | Use `tls` filter, or analyze HTTP on `http://neverssl.com` for lab use |
| Display filter turns red | Syntax error | Click *Expression…* to build the filter, do not free-type |
| Loopback not captured on Windows | Npcap disables loopback by default | Install with *"Support raw 802.11 traffic"* off and *"Restrict Npcap…"* unchecked |
| Wireshark "drops packets" warning | NIC or CPU cannot keep up | Reduce capture to one protocol, or use ring buffer |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All Mermaid diagrams below use **purely alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters** (e.g., `n1`, `n2a`, `stepB`) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safety rules. No reserved keywords are used as node names. All node labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting.

## 4.1 The Wireshark Three-Pane Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n1["PHYSICAL NIC ETHERNET OR WI-FI"]
    n2["CAPTURE LAYER NPCAP OR LIBPCAP DRIVER IN PROMISCUOUS MODE"]
    n3["DISSECTION ENGINE STACKED PROTOCOL DISSECTORS"]
    n4["GUI LAYER THREE PANE INTERFACE"]

    n5["PANE ONE PACKET LIST ONE ROW PER FRAME"]
    n6["PANE TWO PACKET DETAILS EXPANDABLE PROTOCOL TREE"]
    n7["PANE THREE PACKET BYTES HEX PLUS ASCII DUMP"]

    n8["SAVED FILE PCAPNG FORMAT FOR LATER ANALYSIS"]

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n4 --> n6
    n4 --> n7
    n2 -.FILE SAVE.-> n8
```

## 4.2 Capture Workflow — End-to-End

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["STEP 1 LAUNCH WIRESHARK AS ADMIN"]
    s2["STEP 2 CAPTURE OPTIONS DIALOG"]
    s3["STEP 3 SELECT INTERFACE ETH OR WLAN OR LO"]
    s4["STEP 4 OPTIONAL CAPTURE FILTER BPF SYNTAX"]
    s5["STEP 5 CLICK SHARK FIN TO START"]
    s6["STEP 6 GENERATE TARGET TRAFFIC PING OR CURL"]
    s7["STEP 7 APPLY DISPLAY FILTER GREEN BAR"]
    s8["STEP 8 FOLLOW STREAM OR EXPORT OBJECTS"]
    s9["STEP 9 STOP CAPTURE RED SQUARE"]
    s10["STEP 10 SAVE PCAPNG AND SCREENSHOT"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10
```

## 4.3 Encapsulation as Wireshark Sees It

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    e1["ETHERNET II HEADER 14 BYTES"]
    i1["IP HEADER 20 BYTES NO OPTIONS"]
    t1["TCP HEADER 20 BYTES MIN"]
    a1["APPLICATION PAYLOAD HTTP OR DNS OR TLS"]

    e1 --> i1 --> t1 --> a1

    d1["DISSECTOR STACK ETHERNET THEN IP THEN TCP THEN APP"]
    e1 -."FEEDS".-> d1
    i1 -."FEEDS".-> d1
    t1 -."FEEDS".-> d1
    a1 -."FEEDS".-> d1
```

## 4.4 TCP Three-Way Handshake Observed in Wireshark

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CLIENT 192.168.1.1
    participant S as SERVER 192.168.1.10
    participant W as WIRESHARK DISPLAY

    C->>S: SYN SEQ X
    W-->>W: PANE TWO FLAGS SYN EQUALS 1 ACK EQUALS 0
    S->>C: SYN ACK SEQ Y ACK X PLUS 1
    W-->>W: PANE TWO FLAGS SYN EQUALS 1 ACK EQUALS 1
    C->>S: ACK SEQ X PLUS 1 ACK Y PLUS 1
    W-->>W: PANE TWO FLAGS ACK EQUALS 1
    Note over C,S: CONNECTION ESTABLISHED
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Differentiate between a *capture filter* and a *display filter* in Wireshark. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**

| Aspect | Capture Filter | Display Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | Applied at the *capture* stage, before the packet is stored in memory | Applied to the *displayed* packets after they are already in memory |
| Syntax | BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) | Wireshark Display Filter (WDF) |
| Effect on disk usage | Reduces memory and disk usage — discarded packets are not stored | **Does not** affect memory or disk usage |
| Example | `tcp port 80` | `tcp.port == 80` |

> **Examiner's Note (1 mark reserved):** Students who only write the syntax example and skip the *stage* explanation lose 1 mark.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**What is meant by *promiscuous mode*? Why is it required for Wireshark to capture traffic not addressed to the host?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Promiscuous mode** is a NIC configuration in which the network interface card forwards **every frame** it sees on the wire to the operating system, *regardless of the destination MAC address*.
- Normally, a NIC discards frames whose destination MAC does not match its own, reducing CPU load.
- Wireshark *requires* promiscuous mode because the user wants to observe traffic between *other* hosts (e.g., between two other PCs on the same switch).
- On a **switched** network, even promiscuous mode only sees broadcast, multicast, and the host's own traffic — a *port mirror (SPAN)* or hub is needed to see all traffic. (Worth 1 extra mark in viva.)

> **Examiner's Note:** A common student mistake is to confuse promiscuous mode with *monitor mode* (used for Wi-Fi to capture all 802.11 frames including management frames).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided** — exactly as per the KTU ESE pattern. Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU S6]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **three-pane architecture** of the Wireshark GUI. Describe what information is shown in each pane. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A student captures a TCP segment. The Ethernet frame contains 66 bytes in total. The IP header is 20 bytes, and the TCP header is 20 bytes. Calculate:
- (i) The TCP payload length.
- (ii) The maximum theoretical payload if the standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes and the headers are as above.
- (iii) State two reasons why the actual observed throughput is **always less** than this maximum. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

The three panes of the Wireshark GUI are:

1. **Pane 1 — Packet List Pane (top):**
   - One row per captured frame.
   - Columns: **No., Time, Source, Destination, Protocol, Length, Info**.
   - Color-coded by protocol (e.g., green for HTTP, light blue for UDP, black for TCP errors).
   - Clicking a row updates the other two panes.

2. **Pane 2 — Packet Details Pane (middle):**
   - Expandable **protocol tree** for the selected frame.
   - Shows each protocol layer (Ethernet → IP → TCP → Application).
   - Clicking a field highlights the corresponding bytes in Pane 3.
   - Used to verify that the dissector has correctly identified the protocol.

3. **Pane 3 — Packet Bytes Pane (bottom):**
   - Raw **hexadecimal dump** on the left and **ASCII** on the right.
   - Offsets are shown on the far left (byte 0 at the top).
   - Essential for *manual* verification when a dissector fails or when a custom protocol is used.

*(Block diagram already provided in Section 4.1 of these notes — students may reproduce the three-pane Mermaid block for full marks.)*

**Real-world utility:** This separation mirrors the **OSI encapsulation model** and helps students understand that "decoding" a packet means peeling off one layer at a time.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**(i) TCP Payload Length:**

Total frame size = 66 bytes.
Ethernet header (14) + Ethernet FCS (4) = 18 bytes of overhead.
IP header (20) + TCP header (20) = 40 bytes of overhead.
Total overhead = 18 + 40 = 58 bytes.

$$\text{TCP Payload} = 66 - 18 - 40 = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

**(ii) Maximum Theoretical Payload:**

Maximum Ethernet frame payload (MTU) = 1500 bytes.
IP + TCP headers = 40 bytes.

$$\text{Max Payload} = 1500 - 40 = 1460 \text{ bytes}$$

> Note: 1460 bytes is the standard **TCP MSS (Maximum Segment Size)** for Ethernet — worth 1 extra mark.

**(iii) Two reasons actual throughput is less than the maximum:**

1. **TCP 3-way handshake and connection teardown** — SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK, FIN, FIN-ACK, ACK packets carry *zero* application payload but consume bandwidth and time.
2. **TCP acknowledgments and retransmissions** — every data packet must be acknowledged; lost packets are retransmitted, consuming additional bandwidth.
3. *(Bonus mark)* **Flow control and congestion control** — the receiver's advertised window and the sender's congestion window can throttle the sender below the link capacity, especially on high-BDP (bandwidth-delay product) paths.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU S6]

**(a)** Explain the **TCP three-way handshake** with a sequence diagram. For each step, state the **TCP flags** set in the Wireshark Packet Details pane. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a **Wireshark display filter** for each of the following and explain when you would use it:
- (i) Show all HTTP GET requests issued by host `192.168.1.50`.
- (ii) Show only the packets involved in the 7th TCP conversation seen in the capture.
- (iii) Show packets with a TCP segment that has been retransmitted.
- (iv) Show all DNS queries (but not responses) issued from your machine. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**The TCP Three-Way Handshake:**

The handshake establishes sequence numbers, window sizes, and MSS between two endpoints before any application data is exchanged.

| Step | Direction | TCP Flags | Wireshark Display | Sequence Number Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Client → Server | **SYN = 1**, ACK = 0 | `Flags: 0x002 (SYN)` | Client sends its Initial Sequence Number (ISN), e.g., `x` |
