| **2** | Server → Client | **SYN = 1, ACK = 1** | `Flags: 0x012 (SYN, ACK)` | Server ACKs client's `x+1` and sends its own ISN `y` |
| **3** | Client → Server | **ACK = 1** | `Flags: 0x010 (ACK)` | Client ACKs server's `y+1`; connection is now **ESTABLISHED** |

> **Marking guidance:** A labelled sequence diagram (such as the Mermaid one in Section 4.4 of these notes) earns full credit. The *flags value in hexadecimal* (e.g., `0x002`) is a 1-mark bonus.

**Why "three-way"?** Because two hosts must each confirm that the *other's* SYN was received, and one round-trip alone is insufficient — the server cannot know if the client received the server's ISN. Therefore an additional ACK is required.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**(i) HTTP GET requests from `192.168.1.50`:**

```
http.request.method == "GET" and ip.src == 192.168.1.50
```

- **Use case:** Identifying what web resources a specific user is fetching — common in web-traffic auditing.

**(ii) Packets in the 7th TCP conversation:**

```
tcp.stream eq 7
```

> **Note:** Wireshark uses **0-based** stream indexing. The 7th conversation has `stream eq 6` if 0-based, `eq 7` if 1-based. In exam answers, state this assumption clearly. (1 mark for the clarification.)

- **Use case:** Isolating a single TCP session (e.g., one HTTP download) from a busy capture.

**(iii) TCP retransmissions:**

```
tcp.analysis.retransmission
```

- **Use case:** Diagnosing packet loss, slow networks, or faulty NIC drivers. Each retransmission wastes bandwidth and inflates RTT.

**(iv) DNS queries only (not responses):**

```
dns.flags.response == 0
```

- **Use case:** Measuring the *outbound* DNS request rate of a host, useful for detecting DNS-tunneling malware that generates unusually high query volume.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 3 Reasons Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing capture filter syntax with display filter syntax** — most common, costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the assumption** about 0-based vs 1-based stream indexing in TCP stream filters.
> 3. **Writing `tcp.port == 80` as a capture filter** — this silently fails in BPF; use `tcp port 80`. Examiners check the *operator* (== vs space).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Wireshark is a **passive** packet analyzer — it does *not* generate traffic.
- [ ] Default capture file extension is **`.pcapng`**; legacy is **`.pcap`**.
- [ ] Modern Windows builds use **Npcap**, not WinPcap.
- [ ] **Three-pane GUI:** Packet List (top), Packet Details (middle), Packet Bytes (bottom).
- [ ] **Capture filters use BPF syntax** (`tcp port 80`); **Display filters use WDF syntax** (`tcp.port == 80`).
- [ ] Capture filters reduce memory/disk usage; display filters do *not*.
- [ ] **Promiscuous mode** is required to see frames not addressed to the host; on a switch you still only see your own + broadcast traffic (use a SPAN port or a hub).
- [ ] **MTU** = 1500 bytes for standard Ethernet; **MSS** = 1460 bytes (MTU − 40) for typical TCP.
- [ ] TCP minimum header is **20 bytes**; IP minimum header is **20 bytes**.
- [ ] Three-way handshake flags: **SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK**. Hex flag values: **0x002, 0x012, 0x010**.
- [ ] Encapsulation offsets: Ethernet starts at byte **0**, IP at byte **14**, TCP/UDP at byte **34** (assuming 20-byte IP).
- [ ] Most common display filters to memorize: `http`, `dns`, `tcp`, `ip.addr == x.x.x.x`, `tcp.analysis.retransmission`, `tcp.stream eq N`, `tcp.flags.syn == 1`.
- [ ] Lab record must contain: a **`.pcapng` file** + **screenshots of all three panes** + **at least one filtered view** + **one Follow-Stream or Statistics screenshot**.
- [ ] **Loopback capture** (`lo` / `127.0.0.1`) is the safest lab environment — no external network noise and no legal concerns.
- [ ] When decoding a hex dump manually: read the **IP header length nibble first** to locate where the TCP/UDP header begins; do *not* assume 20 bytes if options are present.
- [ ] **Examiner's favourite line:** "Explain how a display filter differs from a capture filter." Memorize the *stage*, the *syntax engine*, and the *effect on memory*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement congestion control using a leaky bucket algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement congestion control using a leaky bucket algorithm # 🪣 Module Experiment 2: Congestion Control using Leaky Bucket Algorithm ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Leaky Bucket Algorithm** is a classic **traffic-shaping** and **congestion-control** mechanism used in computer networks to regulate the rate at which packets are transmitted into the network. It enforces a **maximum average rate** and an **average burst size**, transforming a **bursty and irregular input stream** into a **smooth, constant-rate output stream** (often modelled at the bit or packet level). Formally stated under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCERL407) syllabus: > The Leaky Bucket Algorithm is an abstraction in which a host transmits a packet to the network as soon as the bucket has room for it, and the network (or the source) drains the bucket at a **constant output rate ρ**. When the bucket is full, any newly arriving packet is **discarded (dropped)** to prevent network overflow. The algorithm is mathematically grounded in **queueing theory** and is often associated with the *M/D/1/K* queueing model — a single server with deterministic service and finite buffer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCERL407):** > The experiment is intended to demonstrate **traffic shaping** at the source. Students are expected to implement the algorithm in C / C++ / Java / Python, and verify the number of packets **generated**, **transmitted**, and **dropped** for a given input trace and configurable bucket capacity. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 💧 Imagine a **bucket with a small hole at the bottom** placed under a tap: | Real-World Object | Network Equivalent | |---|---| | Bucket | Internal buffer of fixed capacity **B** | | Water drops pouring in from the tap | Bursty, irregular incoming packets | | Water leaking out the bottom hole | Packets transmitted at **constant rate ρ** | | Bucket overflow at the top | **Packet drop / congestion signal** | | Tap kept open too long | Source sending faster than the network can absorb | **What happens?** - Water (packets) may arrive at the tap **erratically** — sometimes 50 drops per second, sometimes none. - Regardless of how fast water comes in, the bucket **leaks water at a constant rate** through the hole. - If water enters **faster** than it leaks, the bucket fills up. - If the bucket is **already full**, any extra water simply **spills over the rim** — analogous to **packet loss** in the network. This is exactly what the Leaky Bucket does: it **decouples the input traffic profile from the output traffic profile**, ensuring the network sees a **uniform, predictable load**. > [!NOTE] > **Why is this important in the real world?** > Bursty traffic causes **jitter**, **delay spikes**, and **buffer overruns** in routers. By smoothing traffic at the source (or at the network edge), the Leaky Bucket provides **QoS guarantees** for voice, video, and real-time applications. ## 1.3 Terminology You Must Know - **B (Bucket Capacity):** The maximum number of packets the bucket can hold. Units: **packets** (or bytes). - **ρ (Output / Leak Rate):** The constant rate at which packets are drained and transmitted. Units: **packets/second**. - **λ(t) (Input / Arrival Rate):** The instantaneous rate at which packets arrive from the application. Often bursty and variable. - **b(t) (Bucket Occupancy):** The number of packets currently in the bucket at time *t*. - **Drop Condition:** A packet is discarded if **b(t) = B** at its arrival instant. - **Steady State:** When **λ ≤ ρ**, the system never overflows, and *b(t)* remains bounded. - **Unstable State:** When **λ > ρ** persistently, the bucket keeps filling and eventually overflows. > [!TIP] > The KTU lab record for this experiment typically asks you to **tabulate** packet generation time, packet length, **status (accepted/dropped)**, and the **content of the bucket after every event**. Always keep this table in your lab record — it carries the highest mark weightage in the viva. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Variants of the Leaky Bucket In the KTU 2024 syllabus, you may encounter the algorithm described in **two distinct forms**. Examiners love to ask the difference, so internalize both. ### Variant 1: The Leaky Bucket as a **Meter** (Drop-Tail Policer) - Packets arrive at the bucket. - If the bucket is **not full**, the packet is **added** to the bucket (token-equivalent). - If the bucket is **full**, the packet is **dropped immediately**. - The bucket itself **does not queue** packets for transmission — it only polices the *arrival* rate. $$ b(t^+) = \begin{cases} b(t) + 1, & \text{if } b(t) < B \\ \text{drop}, & \text{if } b(t) = B \end{cases} $$ This is the **"Token Bucket under counter-pressure"** interpretation commonly used in **ATM ABR policing** and **frame-relay policing**. ### Variant 2: The Leaky Bucket as a **Queue** (Traffic Shaper) ✅ *Most Common in KTU Lab* - Packets arrive and are **enqueued** in a finite FIFO buffer (the "bucket"). - The bucket drains at a **constant rate ρ** by dequeuing one packet every **1/ρ seconds**. - If the buffer is full at arrival, the packet is **dropped**. - The **output** to the network is therefore a perfectly paced stream. $$ \text{Output}(t) = \text{Packet}_i \text{ sent at time } t_i = i \cdot \frac{1}{\rho} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **lab experiment** for KTU 2024 Scheme implements **Variant 2** — the queueing form. The implementation typically uses a fixed-size circular array and a timer / counter to simulate the leak. ## 2.2 Algorithmic Logic — Step by Step Below is the **canonical logic** you must understand and (for the lab) implement. 1. **Initialize** bucket capacity **B** and output rate **ρ**. 2. **Initialize** the bucket to empty: `bucket_count = 0`. 3. For each incoming packet at time **t_i**: - **Step 3a:** If `bucket_count + packet_length ≤ B`, accept the packet → enqueue it. - **Step 3b:** Else (overflow), **drop** the packet and increment `dropped_count`. 4. Independently of input, at every **1/ρ seconds** (or every tick of a simulation clock), **dequeue** one packet from the bucket and transmit it. If bucket is empty, transmit nothing. 5. Log the bucket state and the number of packets transmitted / dropped. 6. **Terminate** when the input packet list is exhausted and the bucket is fully drained. ## 2.3 The Core Mathematical Model Let us denote the bucket content just after the *n-th event* (either an arrival or a leak) as **b[n]**. The recurrence is: $$ b[n+1] = \min\bigl(B, \; b[n] + a[n] - d[n]\bigr) $$ where: - $a[n]$ = number of packets that **arrive** in the *n-th* interval. - $d[n]$ = number of packets that **depart** in the *n-th* interval (= $\rho \cdot \Delta t$, constant in steady state). - $B$ = bucket capacity (hard upper bound). The **drop count** over $N$ intervals is: $$ D_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \max\bigl(0,\; a[n] - (B - b[n-1] + d[n])\bigr) $$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) 📝 > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **minimum formula set** you should memorize for the KTU ESE viva and written exam on this topic. Note: vertical bars (absolute value) are written as `\vert` to keep markdown table syntax intact. | # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $b[n+1] = \min(B, b[n] + a[n] - d[n])$ | Recurrence for bucket occupancy | packets | | 2 | $0 \le b[n] \le B$ | Bucket feasibility constraint | packets | | 3 | $D = \sum \max(0, a[n] - (B - b[n-1] + d[n]))$ | Total drops over $N$ intervals | packets | | 4 | $\rho_{\text{out}} = \text{constant}$ | Output (leak) rate | packets/sec | | 5 | $\lambda_{\text{in}}(t)$ | Variable input rate (bursty) | packets/sec | | 6 | $T_{\text{leak}} = 1 / \rho_{\text{out}}$ | Inter-packet transmission interval | seconds | | 7 | $L_{\max} = B$ | Maximum burst size the system can absorb | packets | | 8 | $\Delta t_{\text{sim}}$ | Simulation time quantum | seconds | | 9 | Throughput = $\min(\lambda_{\text{avg}}, \rho_{\text{out}})$ | Effective goodput out of the bucket | packets/sec | | 10 | $\eta = \rho_{\text{out}} / \lambda_{\text{avg}}$ | Efficiency ratio ($\eta \le 1$ for stability) | dimensionless | ## 2.5 Stability Criterion (Engineering Insight) The system is **stable** (i.e., the bucket does not grow unboundedly) **if and only if**: $$ \mathbb{E}[\lambda_{\text{in}}] \le \rho_{\text{out}} $$ If the **long-run average input rate exceeds the leak rate**, the bucket saturates at $b = B$ and any extra arrivals are dropped — this is precisely the *congestion control* effect. > [!TIP] > For KTU viva: When the examiner asks *"Why is the algorithm called a 'leaky' bucket?"*, answer crisply: > *"Because the bucket metaphorically leaks packets at a constant rate irrespective of the input rate — just as a hole in a bucket releases water at a constant rate regardless of how fast water is poured in."* ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The Leaky Bucket (and its sibling, the Token Bucket) is the **cornerstone of traffic policing** in: - **ATM networks** — for ABR / CBR traffic classes. - **Frame Relay networks** — using the **CIR (Committed Information Rate)** and **Bc (Committed Burst Size)** parameters — these are **literally** leaky-bucket parameters. - **QoS routers and switches** — Cisco, Juniper, and Huawei all implement traffic shaping with leaky/token bucket algorithms in their QoS modules. - **Linux `tc qdisc`** — the `tbf` (Token Bucket Filter) is a generalization of the leaky bucket. - **Streaming servers** — to rate-limit HTTP adaptive streaming manifests (HLS / DASH). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Numerical Walkthrough — A KTU-Style Problem ### Problem Statement > A host uses a **Leaky Bucket** for traffic shaping. The **bucket capacity is B = 5 packets**, and the **output (leak) rate is ρ = 2 packets/second**. Packets arrive at the host in the following order with the sizes and arrival times shown. Assume each "unit" in the packet column represents a packet. Compute, at every event, the **bucket content**, the **number of packets transmitted**, and the **number of packets dropped**. | Packet # | Arrival Time (s) | Packets Arriving | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 0.0 | 2 | | 2 | 0.2 | 1 | | 3 | 0.4 | 3 | | 4 | 0.6 | 1 | | 5 | 0.8 | 2 | | 6 | 1.0 | 1 | | 7 | 1.2 | 2 | | 8 | 1.4 | 1 | The simulation quantum is $\Delta t_{\text{sim}} = 0.2$ seconds, which corresponds to **1 packet leaked per tick** (since $\rho = 2$ pkts/s × 0.2 s = 0.4 ... let us use the cleaner assumption that the bucket drains **1 packet per simulation tick** by setting $\rho_{\text{eff}} = 1$ packet per 0.2 s). We will keep **B = 5** and use a continuous-time accounting in the table below. ### Step 1 — Initialize State - Bucket capacity $B = 5$ packets. - Leak rate $\rho = 2$ packets/second → between two arrival events spaced 0.2 s apart, $\rho \cdot \Delta t = 2 \times 0.2 = 0.4$ packets leaked. - For integer simplicity in KTU exam answers, assume **1 packet leaks per 0.5 s** $\Rightarrow \rho = 2$ pkts/s, and we tabulate at 0.5 s intervals. Let us re-tabulate the input for a clean integer-tick simulation at $\Delta t = 0.5$ s: | Packet # | Arrival Time (s) | Packets Arriving | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 0.0 | 2 | | 2 | 0.5 | 1 | | 3 | 1.0 | 3 | | 4 | 1.5 | 1 | | 5 | 2.0 | 2 | | 6 | 2.5 | 1 | | 7 | 3.0 | 2 | | 8 | 3.5 | 1 | Leak per tick: $d = \rho \cdot \Delta t = 2 \times 0.5 = 1$ packet per tick. ### Step 2 — Apply the Recurrence at Every Tick We use: $$ b[t+1] = \min\bigl(B,\; \max(0,\; b[t] - d) + a[t+1]\bigr) $$ > **Logic per tick:** *(i)* first leak `d` packets (clamp at 0), *(ii)* then add arriving packets, *(iii)* clamp total at capacity `B`, *(iv)* record drop = `a[t+1] − accepted`. ### Step 3 — Build the Event Log We will perform the calculation step-by-step. Define: - $b_{\text{before}}$ = bucket content **before** the arrival this tick. - $a$ = arrivals this tick. - $\text{accepted} = \min(a, \; B - b_{\text{before}})$. - $\text{dropped} = a - \text{accepted}$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = b_{\text{before}} + \text{accepted}$. - $\text{leaked} = \min(b_{\text{after-arrival}}, d)$ (since the leak happens *after* enqueue in our convention). - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = b_{\text{after-arrival}} - \text{leaked}$. > **Convention used:** leak happens *after* the arrival within the same tick. --- **Tick 1 (t = 0.0 s):** - $b_{\text{before}} = 0$ (initial), $a = 2$. - $\text{accepted} = \min(2, \; 5 - 0) = 2$. - $\text{dropped} = 0$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 0 + 2 = 2$. - $\text{leaked} = \min(2, 1) = 1$. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 2 - 1 = 1$. --- **Tick 2 (t = 0.5 s):** - $b_{\text{before}} = 1$, $a = 1$. - $\text{accepted} = \min(1, \; 5 - 1) = 1$. - $\text{dropped} = 0$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 1 + 1 = 2$. - $\text{leaked} = \min(2, 1) = 1$. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 2 - 1 = 1$. --- **Tick 3 (t = 1.0 s):** - $b_{\text{before}} = 1$, $a = 3$. - $\text{accepted} = \min(3, \; 5 - 1) = 3$. - $\text{dropped} = 0$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 1 + 3 = 4$. - $\text{leaked} = \min(4, 1) = 1$. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 4 - 1 = 3$. --- **Tick 4 (t = 1.5 s):** - $b_{\text{before}} = 3$, $a = 1$. - $\text{accepted} = \min(1, \; 5 - 3) = 1$. - $\text{dropped} = 0$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 3 + 1 = 4$. - $\text{leaked} = \min(4, 1) = 1$. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 4 - 1 = 3$. --- **Tick 5 (t = 2.0 s):** - $b_{\text{before}} = 3$, $a = 2$. - $\text{accepted} = \min(2, \; 5 - 3) = 2$. - $\text{dropped} = 0$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 3 + 2 = 5$ (bucket is now **full**). - $\text{leaked} = \min(5, 1) = 1$. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 5 - 1 = 4$. --- **Tick 6 (t = 2.5 s):** - $b_{\text{before}} = 4$, $a = 1$. - $\text{accepted} = \min(1, \; 5 - 4) = 1$. - $\text{dropped} = 0$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 4 + 1 = 5$. - $\text{leaked} = \min(5, 1) = 1$. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 5 - 1 = 4$. --- **Tick 7 (t = 3.0 s):** - $b_{\text{before}} = 4$, $a = 2$. - $\text{accepted} = \min(2, \; 5 - 4) = 1$ ⚠️ **partial acceptance**. - $\text{dropped} = 2 - 1 = 1$ ❌ **(first drop!)**. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 4 + 1 = 5$. - $\text{leaked} = \min(5, 1) = 1$. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 5 - 1 = 4$. --- **Tick 8 (t = 3.5 s):** - $b_{\text{before}} = 4$, $a = 1$. - $\text{accepted} = \min(1, \; 5 - 4) = 1$. - $\text{dropped} = 0$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 4 + 1 = 5$. - $\text{leaked} = \min(5, 1) = 1$. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 5 - 1 = 4$. ### Step 4 — Final Summary Table | Tick | t (s) | b_before | a | accepted | dropped | b_after_arr | leaked | b_after_leak | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 0.0 | 0 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | | 2 | 0.5 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | | 3 | 1.0 | 1 | 3 | 3 | 0 | 4 | 1 | 3 | | 4 | 1.5 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 4 | 1 | 3 | | 5 | 2.0 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 5 | 1 | 4 | | 6 | 2.5 | 4 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 5 | 1 | 4 | | 7 | 3.0 | 4 | 2 | 1 | **1** | 5 | 1 | 4 | | 8 | 3.5 | 4 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 5 | 1 | 4 | ### Step 5 — Aggregated Result - **Total packets arrived:** $\sum a = 2+1+3+1+2+1+2+1 = 13$. - **Total packets accepted (transmitted):** $\sum \text{accepted} = 2+1+3+1+2+1+1+1 = 12$. - **Total packets dropped:** $\sum \text{dropped} = 0+0+0+0+0+0+1+0 = 1$. - **Output rate (constant):** $\rho = 2$ packets/second. - **Total simulation time:** 3.5 s + 0.5 s (drain) = 4.0 s → total transmitted if no early drop ≈ 8 packets, but we have 12 accepted; the rest leaves after the input ends. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check:** Drop occurs **only when the bucket is full AND more packets arrive in the same tick than there is free space**. In our example, the bucket first reaches capacity `B = 5` at tick 5, and the first overflow happens at tick 7 — exactly when an attempt is made to add 2 packets to a bucket that has only 1 free slot. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When Does a Drop Occur? A drop happens at tick $t$ if and only if all of the following hold: $$ a[t] > 0, \quad b_{\text{before}}[t] = B, \quad \text{leak event} \le d_{\text{max}} $$ Equivalently, a drop count for the tick is: $$ \text{drop}[t] = \max\bigl(0,\; a[t] - (B - b_{\text{before}}[t])\bigr) $$ This single line is the **defining equation** of the Leaky Bucket algorithm in its queueing form. Memorize it. ## 3.3 Why Output Is *Always* Smooth After every leak, the bucket drains by exactly `d` packets (or empties fully if `b < d`). Therefore, **regardless of the input burstiness, the output departs at a constant rate of $\rho$**. This is the **fundamental invariance property** of the Leaky Bucket: $$ \text{Var}(\text{output rate}) = 0 \quad \text{even when} \quad \text{Var}(\text{input rate}) > 0 $$ This is the mathematical reason it is the preferred algorithm for shaping jitter-sensitive traffic. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Flowchart) The following Mermaid block renders the **algorithmic flowchart** of the Leaky Bucket — the diagram you should reproduce in your KTU lab record. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start Simulation]) --> initA[Initialize Bucket Capacity B and Leak Rate rho] initA --> setA[Set bucket_count = 0, dropped = 0, transmitted = 0] setA --> recvA{Packet Arriving?} recvA -- No --> leakA[Leak d = rho times Delta_t packets from bucket] leakA --> outA{Output packet to network} outA -- Yes --> transA[transmitted = transmitted + 1] outA -- No --> idleA[No output this tick] transA --> recvA idleA --> recvA recvA -- Yes --> chkA{bucket_count less than B?} chkA -- Yes --> addA[bucket_count = bucket_count + 1] addA --> leakA chkA -- No --> dropA[Drop the packet] dropA --> cntA[dropped = dropped + 1] cntA --> leakA leakA --> endA{Input stream exhausted and bucket empty?} endA -- No --> recvA endA -- Yes --> stopA([Stop: Print transmitted and dropped]) ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level System Architecture The diagram below illustrates the **placement of the Leaky Bucket inside an end-to-end TCP/IP stack** — this is the picture KTU examiners expect when they ask "Where does the Leaky Bucket sit in the network model?" ```mermaid flowchart LR appA[Application Layer Process] --> sockA[Socket Buffer] sockA -->|-- Bursty packet flow lambda in t --| bucketA{{Leaky Bucket Shaper
Capacity B packets
Leak rate rho pkts per sec}} bucketA -->|-- Constant rate output rho --| nwA[Network Interface Card NIC] bucketA -.->|- Drop when bucket full -| dropA[(Discarded Packets Dropped)] nwA --> wanA[WAN / Internet Link] wanA --> rtrA[Remote Router R] rtrA --> dstA[Destination Host] dropA -.-> monA[QoS Monitoring and Statistics] bucketA -.-> monA ``` **Explanation of the architecture:** 1. The application produces **bursty** packets at variable intervals. 2. The packets first enter the **socket buffer** of the OS. 3. The Leaky Bucket **shaper** (implemented in software / firmware) admits packets at most up to its capacity. 4. The bucket **drains at constant rate ρ** to the NIC, ensuring a smooth wire-rate. 5. **Overflow packets are dropped** — this is the *congestion-control* signal. 6. The shaper also reports **drop statistics** to a QoS monitor for live diagnostics. ## 4.3 Time-Space Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid gantt title Leaky Bucket: Input vs Output Time Trace dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Bursty Input Packet 1 (t=0.0) :a1, 0, 1 Packet 2 (t=0.5) :a2, 5, 6 Packet 3 (t=1.0) :a3, 10, 13 Packet 4 (t=1.5) :a4, 15, 16 Packet 5 (t=2.0) :a5, 20, 22 Packet 6 (t=2.5) :a6, 25, 26 Packet 7 (t=3.0) :a7, 30, 32 Packet 8 (t=3.5) :a8, 35, 36 section Constant Output (rho=1 per 0.5s) Output 1 :o1, 0, 5 Output 2 :o2, 5, 10 Output 3 :o3, 10, 15 Output 4 :o4, 15, 20 Output 5 :o5, 20, 25 Output 6 :o6, 25, 30 Output 7 :o7, 30, 35 Output 8 :o8, 35, 40 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Notice how the input line has **clustered (bursty)** activity (e.g., three packets in a 0.1 s window around t = 1.0 s), while the output line is **evenly spaced** at 0.5 s intervals. This visual contrast is the *single most important takeaway* of the experiment. ## 4.4 Reference C Implementation Outline (for Lab Record) The KTU 2024 lab manual typically accepts implementations in **C / C++ / Java / Python**. The following is a **clean, well-commented C outline** that you can transcribe into your record. ```c #include #include #define BUCKET_SIZE 5 #define LEAK_RATE 1 // packets per simulation tick int main(void) { int bucket = 0; int arr_time, packets_in, accepted, dropped_total = 0, transmitted = 0; int n; printf("Enter number of packet groups in input: "); scanf("%d", &n); for (int t = 0; t < n; ++t) { printf("\nTick %d -> packets arriving: ", t); scanf("%d", &packets_in); if (bucket + packets_in <= BUCKET_SIZE) { accepted = packets_in; printf(" All %d packets accepted.\n", accepted); } else { accepted = BUCKET_SIZE - bucket; int dropped = packets_in - accepted; dropped_total += dropped; printf(" %d accepted, %d DROPPED.\n", accepted, dropped); } bucket += accepted; int leaked = (bucket < LEAK_RATE) ? bucket : LEAK_RATE; bucket -= leaked; transmitted += leaked; printf(" After leak: bucket=%d, total_transmitted=%d, total_dropped=%d\n", bucket, transmitted, dropped_total); } while (bucket > 0) { int leaked = (bucket < LEAK_RATE) ? bucket : LEAK_RATE; bucket -= leaked; transmitted += leaked; printf("Drain tick -> bucket=%d, transmitted=%d\n", bucket, transmitted); } printf("\nFinal: Transmitted=%d, Dropped=%d\n", transmitted, dropped_total); return 0; } ``` > [!TIP] > For your **viva**, be ready to explain: *(i)* why `BUCKET_SIZE` is a hard ceiling, *(ii)* how the time complexity is **O(n)** where *n* is the number of input groups, and *(iii)* how the algorithm differs from **Token Bucket** in handling idle periods. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** ### Q1. Define the Leaky Bucket Algorithm. List any two of its advantages. **Model Answer (≈ 90 words):** The Leaky Bucket Algorithm is a traffic-shaping mechanism that controls the rate at which packets enter a network. The host accepts packets into a finite buffer (the "bucket") of capacity **B**. The bucket is drained at a **constant output rate ρ**, irrespective of the bursty input rate. When the bucket is full, newly arriving packets are **dropped**. **Advantages:** 1. Converts irregular traffic into a smooth, constant-rate stream → reduces jitter. 2. Simple, stateless, and easily implementable in hardware or software. 3. Provides inherent congestion control by discarding excess packets. > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Style]** ### Q2. Differentiate between the Leaky Bucket and the Token Bucket algorithm. **Model Answer (tabular form expected):** | Feature | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket | |---|---|---| | Output rate | **Always constant ρ** | **Bursty up to capacity B**, average ≤ ρ | | Idle period handling | **No credit accumulation** — idle periods are *wasted* | Tokens accumulate → can send burst when idle ends | | Mechanism | Drains at constant rate | Adds tokens at ρ; each packet consumes one token | | Use case | **Traffic shaping** (smooth output) | **Traffic policing + shaping** (allows controlled bursts) | | Complexity | Simpler | Slightly more complex | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with internal choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 2, Q. 8]** ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **Leaky Bucket algorithm** as a traffic-shaping mechanism. Draw a neat diagram showing the bucket, input flow, output flow, and the drop path. Discuss the role of bucket capacity **B** and leak rate **ρ** in controlling congestion. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The Leaky Bucket algorithm models the network interface as a bucket that holds a maximum of **B** packets. Packets arrive from the upper layer at a **variable (bursty) rate** and are enqueued in the bucket. The bucket is drained at a **constant leak rate ρ**, which is the rate at which packets are transmitted onto the wire. When the bucket is full, additional arriving packets are **dropped**, providing a congestion-control feedback signal. **Role of Bucket Capacity B:** - $B$ controls the **maximum burst size** the system can absorb. A larger $B$ allows larger bursts (more memory cost). - It also bounds the **worst-case delay** a packet may experience: $D_{\max} = B / \rho$ seconds. **Role of Leak Rate ρ:** - $\rho$ is the **sustained output rate** — it sets the long-run average throughput. - It is the **maximum sustainable input rate**; any input whose long-run average exceeds $\rho$ will eventually cause drops. **Diagram (mandatory in KTU answer scripts):** ``` [Application] | v (bursty input, rate lambda(t)) +-----------+ | BUCKET | Capacity = B packets | [########]| (FIFO queue) +-----------+ | v (constant output, rate rho) [Network Interface] | v +-----+-----+ +-------------+ | Bucket | ----> | Transmitted | | FULL ? | No | packets | +-----+-----+ +-------------+ | Yes v +-------------+ | DROPPED | (congestion control signal) +-------------+ ``` **Conclusion:** By enforcing a constant output rate, the Leaky Bucket **decouples the application's burstiness from the network's offered load**, ensuring predictable performance and bounded delay. --- **(b)** A host uses a Leaky Bucket with **capacity B = 4 packets** and **leak rate ρ = 1 packet per second**. The following packet groups arrive (assume one tick = 1 s): | Tick (s) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Arrivals | 2 | 1 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 3 | 2 | Compute the **bucket content**, the **number of packets transmitted**, and the **number of packets dropped** at every tick. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Step by Step:** We apply the recurrence: $$ b[t+1] = \max\bigl(0, \; \min(B, b[t] + a[t]) - d\bigr) $$ with $B = 4$, $d = 1$ (1 packet leaked per tick). **Tick 1:** $b_{\text{before}}=0$, $a=2$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = \min(4, 0+2) = 2$, drop = 0. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 2 - 1 = 1$. Transmitted so far = 1. **Tick 2:** $b_{\text{before}}=1$, $a=1$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = \min(4, 1+1) = 2$, drop = 0. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 2 - 1 = 1$. Transmitted so far = 2. **Tick 3:** $b_{\text{before}}=1$, $a=3$. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = \min(4, 1+3) = 4$, drop = 0 (exact fit). - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 4 - 1 = 3$. Transmitted so far = 3. **Tick 4:** $b_{\text{before}}=3$, $a=2$. - Free slots = $4 - 3 = 1$. Accepted = 1, **dropped = 1** ❌. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 3 + 1 = 4$. - $b_{\text{after-leak}} = 4 - 1 = 3$. Transmitted so far = 4. **Tick 5:** $b_{\text{before}}=3$, $a=1$. - Accepted = 1, drop = 0. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 4$, after-leak = 3. Transmitted so far = 5. **Tick 6:** $b_{\text{before}}=3$, $a=3$. - Free slots = 1. Accepted = 1, **dropped = 2** ❌. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 4$, after-leak = 3. Transmitted so far = 6. **Tick 7:** $b_{\text{before}}=3$, $a=2$. - Free slots = 1. Accepted = 1, **dropped = 1** ❌. - $b_{\text{after-arrival}} = 4$, after-leak = 3. Transmitted so far = 7. **Final Summary Table:** | Tick | b_before | a | accepted | **dropped** | b_after_arr | b_after_leak | transmitted | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 0 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 1 | | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 2 | | 3 | 1 | 3 | 3 | 0 | 4 | 3 | 3 | | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | **1** | 4 | 3 | 4 | | 5 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 4 | 3 | 5 | | 6 | 3 | 3 | 1 | **2** | 4 | 3 | 6 | | 7 | 3 | 2 | 1 | **1** | 4 | 3 | 7 | - **Total arrived:** 14 - **Total accepted & transmitted:** 10 - **Total dropped:** 4 - **Steady-state bucket content:** 3 packets (drains to 1 only when input ceases). > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Order of operations matters.** A common mistake is to *leak before arrival* instead of *after arrival*. The KTU convention (and most textbooks, e.g., Tanenbaum) is **arrival → drop check → leak**. Reversing this can change the drop count. > 2. **Forgetting to clamp at zero.** If a tick has no arrivals but `b < d`, the bucket must be clamped at 0, not allowed to go negative. State `b[t] = max(0, ...)`. > 3. **Misinterpreting "drop" as "transmit"**. Drops must be **counted but not queued**. Only accepted packets ever get transmitted. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **(a)** Compare and contrast the **two implementations** of the Leaky Bucket algorithm — namely the **meter-based** and the **queue-based** versions. State, with justification, which one is more suitable for **traffic shaping**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Meter-Based | Queue-Based | |---|---|---| | Operation | Counts tokens; drops if full | Enqueues packets; transmits at constant rate | | Output shape | Not directly shaped — input allowed if token exists | **Perfectly smooth output** at rate ρ | | Primary use | **Policing** (drop excess) | **Shaping** (smooth output) | | Buffer cost | None / constant | O(B) memory for the queue | | Suitable for | Policing at the network edge | Source-end traffic shaping | **For traffic shaping, the queue-based version is more suitable** because shaping requires a *predictable* output rate. The meter version simply enforces an upper bound on arrivals, but the *output* of a meter can still be bursty (the arriving packets go straight through if a token is present). The queue version explicitly drains at rate ρ, guaranteeing that the wire sees a constant packet rate. --- **(b)** Consider a server transmitting a video stream using a Leaky Bucket with **B = 10 packets** and **ρ = 5 packets/second**. The video encoder produces packets in **bursts of 8** at the start of every second (i.e., 8 packets at t = 0, 1, 2, … s). Show that the bucket is **stable** or **unstable** and compute the **steady-state bucket occupancy** and the **drop rate**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Average input rate: $$ \lambda_{\text{avg}} = 8 \text{ pkts} \times \frac{1 \text{ burst}}{1 \text{ s}} = 8 \text{ pkts/s} $$ Compare to leak rate $\rho = 5$ pkts/s: $$ \lambda_{\text{avg}} = 8 > 5 = \rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{UNSTABLE} $$ **Per-second accounting** (leak happens *after* arrival each second): - Arrival: 8 packets. - Free slots in bucket: $B - b_{\text{before}}$. - Accepted: $\min(8, 10 - b_{\text{before}})$. - Dropped: $8 - \text{accepted}$. - After leak: $b_{\text{after-arrival}} - 5$. | t (s) | b_before | a | accepted | dropped | b_after_arr | leaked | b_after_leak | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 0 | 8 | 8 | 0 | 8 | 5 | 3 | | 1 | 3 | 8 | 8 | 0 | 11 → clamp to 10 | 5 | 5 | | 2 | 5 | 8 | 5 | **3** | 10 | 5 | 5 | | 3 | 5 | 8 | 5 | **3** | 10 | 5 | 5 | | … | 5 | 8 | 5 | **3** | 10 | 5 | 5 | **Steady-state:** - $b_{\text{ss}} = 5$ packets. - **Drop rate = 3 packets per second = 37.5 % drop probability.** - Effective throughput out of the bucket = $\rho = 5$ packets/s. **Conclusion:** Since the encoder outpaces the bucket leak rate, **3 out of every 8 packets are dropped** in the long run. To avoid drops, the encoder must reduce its average rate to $\le 5$ pkts/s, or the bucket capacity must be increased to absorb the bursts (note: increasing $B$ only delays — not eliminates — the drops). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PCERL407 - Module 2:** - 🪣 **Leaky Bucket = traffic shaper** that converts bursty input into **constant-rate output** $\rho$. - 📦 **Bucket capacity $B$** → controls the maximum *absorbable burst size* and the *worst-case queuing delay* $D_{\max} = B/\rho$. - ⏱️ **Leak rate $\rho$** → constant output rate; equals the **long-run maximum sustainable input rate**. - 🔁 **Recurrence:** $b[t+1] = \min(B,\; b[t] + a[t] - d)$. - ❌ **Drop rule:** $\text{drop}[t] = \max(0,\; a[t] - (B - b_{\text{before}}[t]))$. - ⚖️ **Stability:** System stable $\iff$ $\mathbb{E}[\lambda_{\text{in}}] \le \rho$. - 🆚 **Leaky vs Token Bucket:** Leaky = *no credit for idle*; Token = *credit accumulates* → Token allows controlled bursts. - 🧠 **Two implementations:** *Meter (policer)* and *Queue (shaper)*. KTU lab = queue version. - 💡 **Time complexity of the algorithm:** $O(n)$ where $n$ is the number of input events. - 🛠️ **Lab implementation language:** C / C++ / Java / Python — must tabulate **(i)** bucket content, **(ii)** transmitted, **(iii)** dropped at every tick. - 📈 **Real-world use:** ATM policing, Frame Relay (CIR/Bc), Linux `tc tbf`, streaming-server rate limiting, QoS routers. - ⚠️ **Common exam traps:** - Confusing **"input rate"** with **"output rate"** — they are *different* by design. - Forgetting to **clamp at zero** after leaking. - Off-by-one errors in tick indexing (start at $t = 0$ or $t = 1$ — be consistent). - Reporting **"received = transmitted"** when in fact some packets are dropped — *received includes dropped*; *transmitted = received − dropped*. > **Final Pro Tip for KTU 2024 Lab Exam:** Always include a **clean tabular event log** and a **neat block diagram** in your answer sheet. Examiners reward *clarity* — a well-labelled table and diagram can easily fetch 2–3 bonus marks even if the numerical result has a small arithmetic slip.

topics.title: Study of NS2 simulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of NS2 simulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Simulator 2 (NS2)** is an **object-oriented, discrete-event simulator** specifically engineered for networking research and pedagogical analysis. It is built using two integrated languages: **C++** for the high-performance backend (packet processing, protocol core) and **OTcl (Object-oriented Tool Command Language)** for the frontend simulation scripting, configuration, and topology assembly.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, NS2 is classified as a **Discrete Event Network Simulator (DENS)** that allows students to:
- Model wired and wireless network topologies.
- Simulate transport-layer protocols (TCP, UDP, SCTP).
- Measure performance metrics such as **Throughput**, **End-to-End Delay**, **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)**, and **Jitter**.
- Visualize packet flow through the **Network Animator (NAM)** tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU lab manual treats NS2 as the *de facto* standard simulator for studying congestion control, routing protocols (DSDV, AODV, DSR), and traffic engineering in a controlled, reproducible environment — without requiring physical hardware.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **civil engineer designing a road network** before laying a single brick of asphalt. You cannot afford to build, demolish, and rebuild real highways to test traffic flow. Instead, you build a **miniature scale model city** on a computer, drive toy cars through it, count collisions, measure travel times, and tweak signal timings. NS2 is exactly that — but for **data packets travelling through routers, links, and protocols**.

| Real World Concept | NS2 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Miniature model city | Simulator (Tcl script + object library) |
| Toy cars on roads | Data packets travelling through agents |
| Traffic signals at junctions | Network queues (e.g., DropTail, RED) |
| CCTV cameras observing traffic | **NAM (Network Animator)** visualizer |
| Logbook of every car's movement | **Trace files (.tr)** |

## 1.3 Key Physical / Software Constants

- **Default packet size** in many CBR examples: **500 bytes**
- **Default simulation step granularity**: **event-driven** (not fixed time-step)
- **Standard trace file format**: **WireShark-compatible ASCII** with `<`, `>`, `r`, `d`, `f`, `+`, `-` event tags
- **NAM extension**: **`.nam`** (Network Animator playback file)

> [!NOTE]
> NS2 is officially a *Unix/Linux-native* tool. On Windows, it is conventionally run inside **Cygwin** or within a virtualized **Ubuntu** image (as mandated by the KTU 2024 lab environment).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of NS2

NS2 follows a **Two-Language Hybrid Architecture**. The motivation behind this design is to balance two competing engineering demands:

1. **Execution Speed** → required for fast packet processing at runtime.
2. **Flexibility / Re-configurability** → required for rapidly changing topologies without recompilation.

The solution: implement protocol *internals* in **C++**, but expose them to users as **OTcl objects** that can be assembled and reconfigured without touching compiled code.

### 2.1.1 Layered Component Breakdown

- **Layer 1 — Event Scheduler (C++)**  
  Maintains the **simulation clock** as a sorted priority queue of upcoming events. Only the event at the head of the queue is executed at any given virtual time.

- **Layer 2 — Network Component Objects (C++ + OTcl mirror)**  
  Includes `Node`, `Link`, `Agent`, `Queue`, `Classifier`. These objects are *mirrored* — each C++ class has an OTcl counterpart created via the **TclCL** glue library.

- **Layer 3 — TclCL (Tcl C Language linkage)**  
  The bridge library that performs **method binding** between OTcl scripts and C++ compiled classes. It allows an OTcl command like `$ns duplex-link` to invoke a corresponding C++ function.

- **Layer 4 — OTcl Script (Frontend)**  
  The user-written `.tcl` file defining the topology, agents, traffic sources, and event scheduling.

- **Layer 5 — Output Tools**  
  - **NAM (Network Animator)** → visual playback (`.nam` file).  
  - **Trace Analyzer** → numerical extraction from `.tr` file (using `awk`, `grep`, `Xgraph`, or `Tracegraph`).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the C++ / OTcl Split

| Aspect | C++ Backend | OTcl Frontend |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Heavy protocol logic, packet fields, timers | Scenario scripting, topology assembly |
| Execution Speed | **Fast** (compiled native code) | **Slower** (interpreted) |
| Re-compilation needed? | Yes, for every protocol change | **No**, fully runtime-configurable |
| Role in Packet Path | Per-packet execution (~millions/sec) | Setup-time execution only |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Memory Trick:** "**C** for **C**ore speed, **O**Tc**l** for **O**uter flexibility."

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Command & Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> NS2 is command-driven rather than formula-driven. The following table serves as the **High-Yield Reference Sheet** equivalent for the lab exam.

| Category | Command / Element | Function | Typical Usage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simulator | `set ns [new Simulator]` | Creates event scheduler instance | Mandatory first line |
| Trace | `$ns trace-all $tf` | Writes per-event records into `.tr` | Performance analysis |
| Trace | `$ns namtrace-all $nf` | Records events for NAM visualization | Visual verification |
| Node | `set n0 [$ns node]` | Creates a network node object | Topology construction |
| Link | `$ns duplex-link $n0 $n1 1Mb 10ms DropTail` | Creates bidirectional link | Bandwidth $= 1$ Mb/s, Delay $= 10$ ms |
| Agent | `[new Agent/TCP]` or `[new Agent/UDP]` | Creates a transport agent | End-point protocol |
| Application | `[new Application/Traffic/CBR]` | Constant Bit Rate traffic source | Generate synthetic load |
| Sink | `[new Agent/Null]` or `[new Agent/TCPSink]` | Packet receiver / terminator | For UDP or TCP respectively |
| Connection | `$ns connect $udp0 $null0` | Wires source agent to sink | Logical binding |
| Scheduling | `$ns at 0.5 "$cbr0 start"` | Schedules event at virtual time 0.5 s | Timeline control |
| Termination | `proc finish {} { ... exec nam out.nam & exit 0 }` | Cleanup and launch NAM | Mandatory for trace close |
| Execution | `$ns run` | Starts event scheduler | Final line of every script |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Although NS2 has been gradually superseded by **NS3** in active research, it remains a **gold-standard pedagogical simulator** in universities worldwide — including KTU-affiliated colleges — because it exposes students to the **raw mechanics of discrete-event simulation**. Skills acquired transfer directly to:
- **NS3** (successor simulator, C++-only).
- **OMNeT++** (industry discrete-event simulator).
- **Mininet / NS3-DCE** for Software-Defined Networking testbeds.
- **Network performance testing** in real 5G/LTE stacks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: Anatomy of an NS2 Script

## 3.1 Canonical NS2 Script (2-Node UDP/CBR Topology)

Below is the **reference 2-node script** that KTU examiners frequently test. Each block is dissected step-by-step.

```tcl
# ============================================
# Step 0 — Create Event Scheduler
# ============================================
set ns [new Simulator]

# ============================================
# Step 1 — Open Trace Files
# ============================================
set nf [open out.nam w]
$ns namtrace-all $nf
set tf [open out.tr w]
$ns trace-all $tf

# ============================================
# Step 2 — Define Termination Procedure
# ============================================
proc finish {} {
    global ns nf tf
    $ns flush-trace
    close $nf
    close $tf
    exec nam out.nam &
    exit 0
}

# ============================================
# Step 3 — Create Nodes
# ============================================
set n0 [$ns node]
set n1 [$ns node]

# ============================================
# Step 4 — Create Link Between Nodes
# ============================================
$ns duplex-link $n0 $n1 1Mb 10ms DropTail

# ============================================
# Step 5 — Create UDP Agent at n0 and Null Sink at n1
# ============================================
set udp0 [new Agent/UDP]
$ns attach-agent $n0 $udp0

set null0 [new Agent/Null]
$ns attach-agent $n1 $null0

# ============================================
# Step 6 — Connect Agents Logically
# ============================================
$ns connect $udp0 $null0

# ============================================
# Step 7 — Attach CBR Traffic Source
# ============================================
set cbr0 [new Application/Traffic/CBR]
$cbr0 attach-agent $udp0
$cbr0 set packetSize_ 500
$cbr0 set interval_ 0.005

# ============================================
# Step 8 — Schedule Events
# ============================================
$ns at 0.5  "$cbr0 start"
$ns at 4.5  "$cbr0 stop"
$ns at 5.0  "finish"

# ============================================
# Step 9 — Start the Simulation
# ============================================
$ns run
```

## 3.2 Line-by-Line Logical Reasoning

### Block A — Simulator Initialization (Step 0)

```tcl
set ns [new Simulator]
```

- `new Simulator` invokes the **C++ `Simulator` class constructor** through TclCL linkage.
- Returns a *handle* stored in the OTcl variable `ns`.
- This handle is the **entry point** to all subsequent API calls.

### Block B — Trace File Management (Step 1)

The script opens **two** trace streams:

1. **`.tr` (Trace File)** — used for **quantitative analysis** (throughput, delay).
2. **`.nam` (NAM File)** — used for **visual playback** in the Network Animator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always close trace files using `$ns flush-trace` inside the `finish` procedure. Failure to do so results in a **corrupted `.nam` file** — a common reason students see blank NAM windows.

### Block C — Termination Procedure (Step 2)

The `finish` procedure is **not executed immediately**. It is *passed as a callback string* to `$ns at`. When virtual time reaches 5.0 s, NS2 invokes it to:
- Flush trace buffers to disk.
- Close file pointers.
- Launch NAM as a background process.
- Terminate the Tcl interpreter.

### Block D — Topology Construction (Steps 3 & 4)

```tcl
set n0 [$ns node]
set n1 [$ns node]
$ns duplex-link $n0 $n1 1Mb 10ms DropTail
```

The duplex link parameters are interpreted as:

$$\text{duplex-link} \; n_i \; n_j \; \mid \; B \; \mid \; D \; \mid \; Q$$

where

- $B$ = Bandwidth (Mb/s) → here $\mathbf{1 \, Mb/s}$
- $D$ = Propagation Delay (ms) → here $\mathbf{10 \, ms}$
- $Q$ = Queue discipline → here $\mathbf{DropTail}$ (FIFO with tail-drop)

The associated **propagation delay equation** for the link is:

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{D_{\text{link}}}{2} \times 10^{-3} \; \text{seconds}$$

$$t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{10}{2} \times 10^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{s} = 5 \; \text{ms (per direction)}$$

### Block E — Agent Creation (Step 5)

```tcl
set udp0 [new Agent/UDP]
$ns attach-agent $n0 $udp0
```

- `Agent/UDP` is an OTcl class mirrored from the C++ `Agent/UDP` class.
- `attach-agent` binds the agent **to the node's port classifier**, enabling it to send/receive packets through that node.

### Block F — Logical Connection (Step 6)

```tcl
$ns connect $udp0 $null0
```

This creates a **logical pipe** between two agents, populating each agent's `dst_` and `target_` fields. After this, packets generated by UDP at $n_0$ will automatically be routed (via the link) to the Null sink at $n_1$.

### Block G — Traffic Generation (Step 7)

```tcl
set cbr0 [new Application/Traffic/CBR]
$cbr0 attach-agent $udp0
$cbr0 set packetSize_ 500
$cbr0 set interval_ 0.005
```

The **traffic generation rate** is derivable as:

$$R_{\text{CBR}} = \frac{\text{packetSize}}{\text{interval}} \text{ bytes/second}$$

$$R_{\text{CBR}} = \frac{500}{0.005} = 100{,}000 \; \text{bytes/s} = 800{,}000 \; \text{bits/s} = 0.8 \; \text{Mb/s}$$

Since $R_{\text{CBR}} = 0.8$ Mb/s $< B_{\text{link}} = 1$ Mb/s, the link is **not congested**, and no packets will be dropped.

### Block H — Event Scheduling (Step 8)

```tcl
$ns at 0.5  "$cbr0 start"
$ns at 4.5  "$cbr0 stop"
$ns at 5.0  "finish"
```

- At $t = 0.5$ s, `cbr0` begins generating packets every 5 ms.
- At $t = 4.5$ s, CBR traffic halts → 4 seconds of active traffic.
- Total packets generated:

$$N_{\text{packets}} = \frac{T_{\text{active}}}{\text{interval}} = \frac{4.0}{0.005} = 800 \; \text{packets}$$

### Block I — Execution (Step 9)

```tcl
$ns run
```

This invokes the **event scheduler loop**, which runs until the queue is empty or `exit` is called inside `finish`.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Trace File Event Tags

The `.tr` file contains **11 fields per line**. The first field identifies the event type:

| Tag | Meaning | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| `+` | Packet **enqueued** at queue | — |
| `-` | Packet **dequeued** from queue | — |
| `r` | Packet **received** at destination | — |
| `d` | Packet **dropped** | — |
| `f` | Packet **forwarded** | — |
| `<` | Trace at **source** (entry to link) | `n0 → n1` |
| `>` | Trace at **destination** (exit from link) | `n0 → n1` |

> [!TIP]
> For throughput calculation, **filter only `r` (received) events** at the destination node using `awk`:
> `awk '$1=="r" && $4=="1" { count++ } END { print count }' out.tr`

## 3.4 Numerical Performance Verification

Given $N_{\text{packets}} = 800$ and each packet size $L = 500$ bytes, total data transferred:

$$D_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{packets}} \times L = 800 \times 500 = 400{,}000 \; \text{bytes} = 3.2 \; \text{Mb}$$

Effective throughput over the active window $T_{\text{active}} = 4$ s:

$$\eta = \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{active}}} = \frac{3.2 \, \text{Mb}}{4 \, \text{s}} = 0.8 \; \text{Mb/s}$$

This **exactly matches** $R_{\text{CBR}}$ because no drops occurred — confirming the link was not saturated.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NS2 Two-Language Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USERLAYER["USER LAYER - Frontend"]
        A1["TCL Script File .tcl"]
        A2["Event Scheduling at t0 t1 t2"]
        A3["Topology Configuration"]
    end

    subgraph GLUE["MIDDLEWARE LAYER"]
        B1["TclCL Library - Method Binding"]
        B2["OTcl Interpreter"]
        B3["Class Mirroring Mechanism"]
    end

    subgraph CORELAYER["COMPILATION LAYER - Backend"]
        C1["C Plus Plus Class Library"]
        C2["Event Scheduler Priority Queue"]
        C3["Packet Headers and Protocol Logic"]
        C4["Queue Implementations DropTail RED"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTLAYER["OUTPUT AND VISUALIZATION"]
        D1["Trace File out.tr"]
        D2["NAM Trace File out.nam"]
        D3["Network Animator Visualizer"]
        D4["Xgraph or Tracegraph Plots"]
    end

    A1 --> A3
    A3 --> B2
    A2 --> B2
    B2 --> B1
    B1 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> D3
```

## 4.2 NS2 Simulation Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Start TCL Interpreter"]
    S1["new Simulator - Initialize Event Scheduler"]
    S2["Open Trace and NAM Files"]
    S3["Create Nodes and Links"]
    S4["Attach Agents and Applications"]
    S5["Connect Agents and Configure Traffic"]
    S6["Schedule Events via ns at commands"]
    S7["Call ns run - Enter Event Loop"]
    S8{"Is Event Queue Empty?"}
    S9["Pop Head Event from Queue"]
    S10["Advance Simulation Clock"]
    S11["Execute Event Handler C Plus Plus"]
    S12["Generate New Future Events"]
    S13["Insert Events into Sorted Queue"]
    S14["Check if exit was Called"]
    S15["Flush and Close Trace Files"]
    S16["Launch NAM Visualizer"]
    S17["End of Simulation"]

    S0 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8
    S8 -- No --> S9 --> S10 --> S11 --> S12 --> S13 --> S14
    S14 -- Continue Loop --> S8
    S14 -- Yes --> S15 --> S16 --> S17
    S8 -- Yes --> S17
```

## 4.3 Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0["Node n0 - Source"] -- "UDP Agent" --> L1["Duplex Link 1 Mb per s, 10 ms"]
    L1 -- "Null Sink Agent" --> N1["Node n1 - Destination"]
    N0 -- "CBR Application 500 bytes every 5 ms" --> L1
    L1 -.-> TF["out.tr Trace File"]
    L1 -.-> NF["out.nam Visualization File"]
    TF --> AWK["AWK or GREP Filters"]
    NF --> NAM["NAM Animation Window"]
    AWK --> XG["Xgraph Plots"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is Network Simulator 2 (NS2)? Mention its two key languages.**

**Model Answer:**
Network Simulator 2 (NS2) is an **object-oriented, discrete-event simulator** widely used for modeling and analyzing wired and wireless network protocols. It is built using **two languages**:
1. **C++** — used for the backend, where protocol internals and packet processing logic are implemented for performance.
2. **OTcl (Object-oriented Tool Command Language)** — used for the frontend scripting to assemble topologies, attach agents, and schedule simulation events without recompilation.

The two are linked via the **TclCL** glue library, which provides class mirroring between OTcl and C++.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between the `.tr` trace file and the `.nam` file in NS2.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `.tr` Trace File | `.nam` File |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Event Trace File | Network Animator File |
| **Format** | ASCII, 11 fields per line | Custom binary-like NAM format |
| **Content** | Quantitative per-packet events (enqueue, dequeue, drop, receive) | Visual playback data for animation |
| **Tool for Analysis** | `awk`, `grep`, `Xgraph`, `Tracegraph` | NAM visualizer only |
| **Purpose** | Throughput, delay, PDR computation | Visual verification of topology and packet flow |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule (ESE):** *Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each carries 14 marks split across sub-parts.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Write and explain an NS2 OTcl simulation script to create a simple two-node topology connected by a duplex link, transmitting UDP/CBR traffic. Also explain the resulting trace file format with an example.**

#### **Part (a) — Write the OTcl Script (7 Marks)**

**Model Script:**

```tcl
set ns [new Simulator]

set nf [open out.nam w]
$ns namtrace-all $nf

set tf [open out.tr w]
$ns trace-all $tf

proc finish {} {
    global ns nf tf
    $ns flush-trace
    close $nf
    close $tf
    exec nam out.nam &
    exit 0
}

set n0 [$ns node]
set n1 [$ns node]

$ns duplex-link $n0 $n1 1Mb 10ms DropTail

set udp0 [new Agent/UDP]
$ns attach-agent $n0 $udp0

set null0 [new Agent/Null]
$ns attach-agent $n1 $null0

$ns connect $udp0 $null0

set cbr0 [new Application/Traffic/CBR]
$cbr0 attach-agent $udp0
$cbr0 set packetSize_ 500
$cbr0 set interval_ 0.005

$ns at 0.5 "$cbr0 start"
$ns at 4.5 "$cbr0 stop"
$ns at 5.0 "finish"

$ns run
```

**Explanation:**
- `new Simulator` initializes the **event scheduler** (1 mark).
- `trace-all` and `namtrace-all` open the **trace files** (1 mark).
- Two nodes `$n0`, `$n1` are created using `$ns node` (1 mark).
- `duplex-link` with bandwidth $1$ Mb/s and delay $10$ ms, DropTail queue (1 mark).
- UDP agent at `$n0`, Null sink at `$n1`, connected logically (1 mark).
- CBR application with packet size $500$ bytes and interval $0.005$ s generates traffic at $0.8$ Mb/s (1 mark).
- Event scheduling with `$ns at` and final `$ns run` (1 mark).

#### **Part (b) — Trace File Format & Sample Output (7 Marks)**

A trace file line has **11 fields**:

$$\text{E} \; \mid \; t \; \mid \; n_s \; \mid \; n_d \; \mid \; \text{NT} \; \mid \; \text{PT} \; \mid \; S \; \mid \; \text{pkt\_id} \; \mid \; \text{ttl} \; \mid \; \text{src\_addr} \; \mid \; \text{dst\_addr}$$

**Sample Trace Line:**
```
+ 0.502 0 1 cbr 500 ------- 0 0.0 1.0 0 1
r 0.512 1 1 cbr 500 ------- 0 0.0 1.0 0 1
```

**Interpretation of First Line (`+`):**
- `+` → packet **enqueued** at the link's queue (1 mark).
- `0.502` → virtual simulation time in seconds (1 mark).
- `0` → source node ID; `1` → destination node ID (1 mark).
- `cbr` → packet type (CBR traffic) (1 mark).
- `500` → packet size in bytes (1 mark).

**Interpretation of Second Line (`r`):**
- `r` → packet **received** at the destination node (1 mark).
- `0.512` → arrival time → **End-to-End Delay** can be computed (1 mark).

**Delay Calculation:**
$$\Delta t = t_{\text{recv}} - t_{\text{send}} = 0.512 - 0.502 = 0.010 \; \text{s} = 10 \; \text{ms}$$

This $10$ ms corresponds to the **propagation delay** of the link, confirming the script is operating as designed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the architecture of NS2 in detail. Discuss the role of OTcl, C++, and TclCL, and describe the function of NAM.**

#### **Part (a) — NS2 Architecture and Two-Language Split (7 Marks)**

NS2 follows a **hybrid two-language architecture**:

1. **C++ Backend (3 Marks)**  
   - Implements the **core simulation engine**: event scheduler, packet structure, protocol headers.  
   - Executes per-packet processing logic, where **speed is critical**.  
   - Compiled into a single binary `ns` executable located in `~/ns-allinone-2.35/ns-2.35/ns`.

2. **OTcl Frontend (2 Marks)**  
   - Interpreted language used to **assemble simulation scenarios**.  
   - The user writes a `.tcl` script describing topology, agents, applications, and event timings.  
   - Slow compared to C++, but offers **unmatched flexibility** — entire topologies can be reconfigured without recompilation.

3. **TclCL Glue Layer (2 Marks)**  
   - Stands for **Tcl/C++ Language binding**.  
   - Implements **class mirroring**: every C++ class has a corresponding OTcl class with the same name.  
   - When OTcl invokes `$ns duplex-link`, TclCL dispatches the call into the C++ `CMUTrace` and `DelayLink` classes.

**Architectural Flow:**  
Tcl Script → OTcl Interpreter → TclCL Method Dispatch → C++ Object → Event Scheduler Execution → Trace Output

#### **Part (b) — Role of NAM and Trace Files (7 Marks)**

**Network Animator (NAM) — 4 Marks:**
- NAM is a **Tcl/Tk-based animation tool** bundled with NS2.
- It reads the `.nam` file (generated by `$ns namtrace-all`) and plays back a **time-scaled visualization** of:
  - Node placement and topology.
  - Packet movement along links.
  - Queue buildup and drops.
- Launched from inside the `finish` procedure using `exec nam out.nam &`.
- Useful for **visual verification** of correct simulation setup.

**Trace Files — 3 Marks:**
- The `.tr` file records every packet event with 11 fields as described in Section 3.3.
- It is the **primary source for quantitative metrics**:
  - **Throughput:** count received bits over active window.
  - **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR):** received packets ÷ sent packets.
  - **Average End-to-End Delay:** mean of $(\text{recv\_time} - \text{send\_time})$ over all received packets.
- Parsed using `awk`, `grep`, and plotted using `Xgraph` or `Tracegraph`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting `$ns flush-trace`** before `close $nf` → corrupted `.nam` file → -2 marks.
> 2. **Missing `$ns connect`** between source and sink agents → packets will not flow → entire simulation is invalid → -3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong variable scope in `finish` procedure** → must declare `global ns nf tf`; otherwise files remain open → -1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing C++ with OTcl** when asked about implementation details → say "C++ implements packet headers internally, OTcl sets packetSize_ externally."
> 5. **Mixing up event tags** (`+`, `-`, `r`, `d`) in the trace file interpretation → memorize: `+`=enqueue, `-`=dequeue, `r`=receive, `d`=drop.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NS2** is a **discrete-event simulator** using **C++ (backend) + OTcl (frontend)** glued by **TclCL**.
- Every NS2 script must begin with `set ns [new Simulator]` and end with `$ns run`.
- **Two trace files**: `.tr` for numerical analysis, `.nam` for visual playback.
- The `finish` procedure must `flush-trace`, `close`, then `exec nam out.nam &`.
- **CBR traffic rate formula**: $R = \dfrac{\text{packetSize}}{\text{interval}}$ — must remain $\leq$ link bandwidth to avoid drops.
- **Duplex link syntax**: `duplex-link $\;n_i\;$ $\;n_j\;$ $\;B\;$ $\;D\;$ $\;Q$`.
- **Event tags** in `.tr`: `+`, `-`, `r`, `d`, `f`, `<`, `>`.
- **NAM** = visualization; **Trace file** = analysis.
- **TclCL** = linkage between OTcl and C++ class hierarchies.
- **Throughput** = (received packets × packet size) / active window time.
- **End-to-end delay** = $\sum (t_{\text{recv}} - t_{\text{send}}) / N_{\text{received}}$.
- **PDR** = $N_{\text{received}} / N_{\text{sent}}$.
- Always run NS2 on **Linux/Cygwin** — Windows native execution is **not supported**.
- Verify any script by **first** watching NAM animation; only then trust the trace file numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and configure a network with multiple subnets with wired and wireless LANs using required network devices and configure commonly used services in the network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design and configure a network with multiple subnets with wired and wireless LANs using required network devices and configure commonly used services in the network # 🛰️ Module Experiment 2 — Multi-Subnet Wired/Wireless LAN Design & Service Configuration ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **multi-subnet enterprise network** is an internetwork in which a single administrative domain is logically partitioned into two or more **IP subnets** using **subnet masking (CIDR/VLSM)**, interconnected through Layer-3 devices (**Routers**), and populated with both **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet/Wired LAN)** and **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi/Wireless LAN)** segments. **Commonly used network services** — such as **DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol, UDP 67/68)**, **DNS (Domain Name System, UDP 53)**, **HTTP/HTTPS (TCP 80/443)**, **FTP (TCP 20/21)**, and **Telnet/SSH (TCP 23/22)** — are configured on dedicated servers or integrated network devices to enable automated host configuration, name resolution, web access, file transfer, and remote administration respectively. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus Mapping (PCERL407):** This experiment satisfies **CO2** — *"Configure network devices and services to build a functional enterprise LAN with multiple subnets."* It is a **mandatory continuous evaluation experiment** assessed during the internal lab viva and end-semester practical examination. ### 🌆 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Analogy" Think of a multi-subnet network as a **large corporate building**: | Network Element | Real-World Building Analogy | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | **Router** | The main **security gate** at the building entrance | Directs traffic between different floors (subnets) | | **Switch** | The **corridor inside each floor** | Connects all rooms (PCs) on the same floor | | **Access Point (AP)** | A **Wi-Fi router mounted in the ceiling** | Provides wireless connectivity within the floor | | **Subnet** | An **entire floor** of the building | Each floor has its own room-numbering scheme (subnet mask) | | **DHCP Server** | The **reception desk** handing out numbered keys | Automatically assigns IP addresses to new devices | | **DNS Server** | The **building directory board** | Converts "HR Department" → "Floor 4, Room 12" | | **Web/HTTP Server** | The **company intranet portal** | Hosts the internal website employees visit | | **FTP Server** | The **central document archive room** | Where files are stored and shared | > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU lab exams, students are expected to **simulate this network in Cisco Packet Tracer (v8.x+)** or perform the same configuration on **real Cisco/Edge-core switches and routers**. Understanding the **logical flow** of packets (PC → Switch → Router → Switch → Server) is more important than memorizing CLI syntax. ### 🎯 Physical & Protocol Constants to Remember | Constant / Standard | Value / Port | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Default Gateway IP** | First usable host in subnet | Router's LAN interface IP | | **Subnet Mask (Class C)** | **255.255.255.0** or **/24** | Default for small LANs | | **DHCP Port** | **UDP 67 (server) / UDP 68 (client)** | Automatic IP assignment | | **DNS Port** | **UDP 53** (queries), TCP 53 (zone transfer) | Name resolution | | **HTTP / HTTPS** | **TCP 80 / TCP 443** | Web services | | **FTP Control / Data** | **TCP 21 / TCP 20** | File transfer | | **Telnet / SSH** | **TCP 23 / TCP 22** | Remote CLI access | | **Wireless Standard** | **IEEE 802.11n/ac/ax** | Wi-Fi 4/5/6 | | **Wired Standard** | **IEEE 802.3u (100 Mbps) / 802.3ab (1 Gbps)** | Ethernet | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧠 Conceptual Breakdown — The "Why" Behind Every Step ### 🔷 Step 1 — Why Subnet a Network? A single large broadcast domain suffers from **broadcast storms**, **MAC table overflow**, and **security leaks**. Subnetting divides one large network into smaller broadcast domains using **variable-length subnet masking (VLSM)** or **Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR)**. Each subnet then needs its own **default gateway** (router interface) to communicate outside its boundary. ### 🔷 Step 2 — Why a Router in the Middle? Routers operate at **Layer 3 (Network Layer)** of the **OSI model** and use **routing tables** to forward packets between different subnets. A **directly connected route** is automatically created for every active interface. For multi-router topologies, **static routes** or **dynamic routing protocols (RIP, OSPF, EIGRP)** must be configured. ### 🔷 Step 3 — Why an Access Point Alongside a Switch? A **Layer-2 switch** only serves **802.3 Ethernet** devices. To extend the same subnet to **Wi-Fi clients (laptops, smartphones, IoT)**, an **Access Point (AP)** is connected to the switch. The AP acts as a **Layer-2 bridge** between the 802.3 wired segment and the 802.11 wireless segment — clients in both segments share the **same IP subnet and default gateway**. ### 🔷 Step 4 — Why Configure Services Manually? While a single PC can use a static IP, an enterprise network with **tens or hundreds of hosts** needs automation. **DHCP** eliminates manual IP configuration, **DNS** decouples human-friendly names from IP addresses, **HTTP/FTP** provide application-layer services, and **Telnet/SSH** allow remote management from a central location. ## 📐 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Subnetting Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The following formulas are **mathematically grounded** and used in the **University Lab Viva & ESE Practical** for verifying IP designs. For the lab exam, you are not asked to perform full subnet math on paper, but examiners **frequently quiz** on the 3 calculations below. | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Number of usable hosts per subnet** | $H = 2^{n} - 2$ | $n$ = number of **host bits**; subtract 2 for **network address** and **broadcast address** | | 2 | **Number of subnets from borrowed bits** | $S = 2^{s}$ | $s$ = number of **bits borrowed** from host portion | | 3 | **Subnet mask from prefix length** | $M = 32 - n$ | $M$ = mask bits in **1s**; $n$ = host bits | | 4 | **Subnet increment (block size)** | $B = 2^{8 - h_{\text{octet}}}$ | $h_{\text{octet}}$ = host bits in the **rightmost non-/24 octet** | | 5 | **CIDR aggregate route formula** | $\text{Supernet mask} = \text{Common prefix length}$ | Used when summarizing multiple subnets | | 6 | **AND operation (Network ID)** | $\text{Network ID} = \text{IP} \;\textbf{AND}\; \text{Mask}$ | Bitwise AND yields the subnet address | | 7 | **Broadcast address** | $\text{Broadcast} = \text{Network ID} + (2^{n} - 1)$ | Last address in the subnet | | 8 | **First usable host** | $\text{Gateway} = \text{Network ID} + 1$ | Conventionally assigned to the router | | 9 | **Last usable host** | $\text{Last host} = \text{Broadcast} - 1$ | Highest assignable host address | | 10 | **AP coverage (free-space path loss)** | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz — only for viva theory | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vertical Bar Replacement Rule:** In markdown tables, never write $\vert x \vert$ — always use $\lvert x \rvert$ to avoid breaking the table parser. ## 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Enterprise Campuses:** Universities, hospitals, and IT parks use exactly this architecture — multiple VLANs/subnets per department, centralized DHCP/DNS, wireless APs in every hallway. - **Small Office / Home Office (SOHO):** A single home router internally performs all the roles — routing, DHCP, DNS relay, NAT, and Wi-Fi AP — exactly the services you will configure in this experiment. - **Industrial IoT:** Modern factories use **separated subnets for OT (Operational Technology) and IT (Information Technology)** networks for security, connected via industrial routers/firewalls. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🔬 Worked Example 1 — Designing a 4-Subnet Network from a /24 Block **Given:** A network administrator is allocated the block **192.168.10.0/24** and must create **4 equal subnets** for: **Admin (wired)**, **Faculty (wired)**, **Students (wireless)**, and **Servers**. ### 🧮 Derivation — Step-by-Step **Step 1:** Identify the default class and starting prefix. The block 192.168.10.0/24 belongs to **Class C**, with **24 network bits** and **8 host bits** in the fourth octet. **Step 2:** Calculate the number of subnet bits required. $$ \begin{aligned} S &= 2^{s} \geq 4 \\ 2^{s} &\geq 4 \\ s &\geq \log_{2}(4) = 2 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, **borrow 2 bits** from the host portion. The new prefix becomes **/26**. **Step 3:** Recalculate host bits per subnet. $$ \begin{aligned} n_{\text{host}} &= 32 - 26 = 6 \text{ host bits} \\ H_{\text{usable}} &= 2^{6} - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 \text{ hosts per subnet} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Calculate the block size (subnet increment). $$ \begin{aligned} B &= 2^{8 - 6} = 2^{2} = 4 \text{ (in the last octet)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Enumerate the four subnets. $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Subnet 1 (Admin):} &\quad 192.168.10.0/26 \quad \to \quad \text{Range: } .0 \to .63 \\ \textbf{Subnet 2 (Faculty):} &\quad 192.168.10.64/26 \quad \to \quad \text{Range: } .64 \to .127 \\ \textbf{Subnet 3 (Students):} &\quad 192.168.10.128/26 \quad \to \quad \text{Range: } .128 \to .191 \\ \textbf{Subnet 4 (Servers):} &\quad 192.168.10.192/26 \quad \to \quad \text{Range: } .192 \to .255 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Verify the **AND operation** for the Faculty subnet to extract its network ID. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IP:} \quad & 192.168.10.75 \\ \text{Mask:}\quad & 255.255.255.192 \;(/26) \\ \text{AND:}\quad & \\ 192.168.10.75 &= 11000000.10101000.00001010.01001011 \\ 255.255.255.192 &= 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000 \\ \hline \text{Result:}\quad & 11000000.10101000.00001010.01000000 \\ &= \mathbf{192.168.10.64} \;\; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ This confirms **192.168.10.75** belongs to the **Faculty subnet** (Subnet 2). **Step 7:** Assign the **default gateway** for each subnet (the router's LAN interface). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Admin Gateway:} &\quad 192.168.10.1/26 \\ \text{Faculty Gateway:} &\quad 192.168.10.65/26 \\ \text{Students Gateway:} &\quad 192.168.10.129/26 \\ \text{Servers Gateway:} &\quad 192.168.10.193/26 \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 🔬 Worked Example 2 — Verifying Inter-Subnet Communication Cost When a host in **Subnet 1 (192.168.10.10)** wants to ping a host in **Subnet 3 (192.168.10.150)**, the packet crosses **1 router hop**. The **TTL (Time-To-Live)** field in the IP header is decremented by **1** at each router. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TTL}_{\text{arrival}} &= \text{TTL}_{\text{initial}} - (\text{Hop Count}) \\ &= 128 - 1 = 127 \end{aligned} $$ This is the value a `ping` reply will show on a Windows client — a **favourite viva question**. --- ## 🔬 Worked Example 3 — AP Channel Selection Math (Wireless Segment) The **2.4 GHz Wi-Fi band** offers **11–13 overlapping channels** in India, but only **3 non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11)** exist. If your college has **4 APs** in adjacent corridors, you assign them as follows: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AP}_1 &\to \text{Channel 1 (2412 MHz)} \\ \text{AP}_2 &\to \text{Channel 6 (2437 MHz)} \\ \text{AP}_3 &\to \text{Channel 11 (2462 MHz)} \\ \text{AP}_4 &\to \text{Channel 1 (reused, non-adjacent coverage)} \end{aligned} $$ Channel spacing between 1 → 6 → 11 is exactly **5 channels × 5 MHz = 25 MHz**, which prevents co-channel interference. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 📊 Diagram 1 — Full Multi-Subnet Enterprise Topology ```mermaid graph TD internetCloud[("INTERNET / ISP")] router1["ROUTER 2911
Serial0/0/0: 10.0.0.1/30
Gig0/0: 192.168.10.1/26
Gig0/1: 192.168.10.65/26
Gig0/2: 192.168.10.129/26"] internetCloud --- router1 router1 ---|"Gig0/0 Wired"| switch1["SW1 2960
Admin Subnet"] router1 ---|"Gig0/1 Wired"| switch2["SW2 2960
Faculty Subnet"] router1 ---|"Gig0/2 Wired + AP uplink"| switch3["SW3 2960
Students Subnet"] switch1 --- pc1["PC-Admin-1
192.168.10.2"] switch1 --- pc2["PC-Admin-2
192.168.10.3"] switch1 --- printer1["Network Printer
192.168.10.4"] switch2 --- pc3["PC-Faculty-1
192.168.10.66"] switch2 --- pc4["PC-Faculty-2
192.168.10.67"] switch3 --- ap1["AccessPoint-Lab1
SSID: KTU-Students
Channel 1
192.168.10.130"] switch3 --- ap2["AccessPoint-Lab2
SSID: KTU-Students
Channel 6
192.168.10.131"] ap1 -.-> laptop1["Laptop-Student
DHCP Assigned"] ap2 -.-> mobile1["Mobile-Student
DHCP Assigned"] router1 ---|"Gig0/3 Server Farm"| switch4["SW4 2960
Servers Subnet"] switch4 --- dhcpSrv["DHCP Server
192.168.10.194"] switch4 --- dnsSrv["DNS Server
192.168.10.195"] switch4 --- httpSrv["HTTP Web Server
192.168.10.196"] switch4 --- ftpSrv["FTP Server
192.168.10.197"] ``` ## 📊 Diagram 2 — DHCP Four-Handshake (DORA Process) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant Client as Client PC participant Server as DHCP Server Client->>Server: 1. DHCP DISCOVER (Broadcast 255.255.255.255) Note over Server: Server reserves an IP from its pool Server-->>Client: 2. DHCP OFFER (Offers IP + Mask + Gateway + DNS) Note over Client: Client accepts the offer Client->>Server: 3. DHCP REQUEST (Broadcast - formally requests) Server-->>Client: 4. DHCP ACK (Acknowledges lease - 8 days default) Note over Client: IP is now bound to MAC for lease duration ``` ## 📊 Diagram 3 — DNS Resolution Flow (Recursive vs Iterative) ```mermaid graph LR user["User PC
Types: www.ktu.edu.in"] localDNS["Local DNS Resolver
192.168.10.195"] rootDNS["Root DNS Server
."] tldDNS["TLD DNS Server
.in"] authDNS["Authoritative DNS
ktu.edu.in"] webSrv["Web Server
192.168.10.196"] user -->|"1. Query www.ktu.edu.in"| localDNS localDNS -->|"2. Query Root"| rootDNS rootDNS -->|"3. Referral to .in TLD"| localDNS localDNS -->|"4. Query .in TLD"| tldDNS tldDNS -->|"5. Referral to ktu.edu.in"| localDNS localDNS -->|"6. Query Authoritative"| authDNS authDNS -->|"7. Returns A Record 192.168.10.196"| localDNS localDNS -->|"8. Final Answer to User"| user user -->|"9. HTTP GET to 192.168.10.196"| webSrv webSrv -->|"10. HTML Page"| user ``` ## 📊 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Configuration Workflow) ```mermaid graph TD A["STEP 1: Physical Topology Design
Place Router, Switches, APs, PCs, Servers"] B["STEP 2: Cable Connections
Copper Straight for PC-SW
Serial DCE/DTE for WAN
Console for management"] C["STEP 3: IP Addressing Plan
Assign subnets and gateway IPs"] D["STEP 4: Router Configuration
hostname, interface IPs, no shutdown"] E["STEP 5: Switch Configuration
hostname, VLANs if any, mgmt IP"] F["STEP 6: AP Configuration
SSID, security WPA2-PSK, channel"] G["STEP 7: Static Server IPs
DHCP, DNS, HTTP, FTP"] H["STEP 8: DHCP Pool Creation
default-router, dns-server, lease time"] I["STEP 9: DNS Records
A, CNAME, PTR entries"] J["STEP 10: Service Activation
http server, ftp server, ssh"] K["STEP 11: Verification
ping, ipconfig, nslookup, browser test"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📚 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Question 1]** > **Q1. Differentiate between a Router, a Switch, and an Access Point in terms of OSI layer, function, and typical port count.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Device | OSI Layer | Primary Function | Typical Port Count | |---|---|---|---| | **Router** | **Layer 3 (Network)** | Forwards packets between **different IP subnets** using routing tables | 2–4 LAN + 1–2 WAN (serial) | | **Switch** | **Layer 2 (Data Link)** | Forwards frames within the **same subnet** using MAC tables | 8 / 24 / 48 Gigabit ports | | **Access Point** | **Layer 2 (bridges Layer 1)** | Converts **wired Ethernet to wireless Wi-Fi (802.11)** | 1 uplink + multiple wireless clients | > *(1 mark each for the three differentiating parameters)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Question 2]** > **Q2.
