List any three network services that are commonly configured in an enterprise LAN and state the default port number and protocol (TCP/UDP) for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **DHCP** → **UDP Port 67 (server) / UDP Port 68 (client)** — Automatic IP address assignment. *(1 mark)* 2. **DNS** → **UDP Port 53** (queries) / **TCP Port 53** (zone transfers) — Resolves domain names to IP addresses. *(1 mark)* 3. **HTTP** → **TCP Port 80** — Serves web pages to browsers. *(1 mark)* *(FTP on TCP 21/20 and SSH on TCP 22 are also accepted alternatives.)* --- ## 📚 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > ### 🅰️ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU ESE Model - May 2024 Pattern]* > > **Q.A.** Design and configure a network with **3 subnets** — **Office (wired)**, **Lab (wireless)**, and **Server Farm** — using a **single router**, **two switches**, and **one wireless access point**. Use the IP block **172.16.20.0/24** and apply **VLSM** for equal subnets. Configure the following services and verify with appropriate test commands: > *(a) DHCP service for the Lab subnet with a pool of 50 addresses.* **(7 marks)** > *(b) DNS service that resolves `lab.ktu.ac.in` to the Lab gateway and `server.ktu.ac.in` to the HTTP server.* **(7 marks)** ### 🟢 Model Solution for Question A #### **Part (a) — DHCP Configuration (7 marks)** **Step 1 (1 mark): Subnet calculation using VLSM** With **3 subnets** needed and 8 host bits in the /24, borrow **2 bits** → new prefix **/26**. $$ \begin{aligned} S &= 2^{2} = 4 \text{ subnets (3 used, 1 reserved)} \\ H &= 2^{6} - 2 = 62 \text{ usable hosts each} \end{aligned} $$ | Subnet | Network ID | Usable Range | Broadcast | Gateway | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Office** | 172.16.20.0/26 | .1 → .62 | .63 | 172.16.20.1 | | **Lab** | 172.16.20.64/26 | .65 → .126 | .127 | 172.16.20.65 | | **Server Farm** | 172.16.20.128/26 | .129 → .190 | .191 | 172.16.20.129 | **Step 2 (1 mark): Router interface configuration** ```cisco Router> enable Router# configure terminal Router(config)# hostname GW-Router GW-Router(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0 GW-Router(config-if)# ip address 172.16.20.1 255.255.255.192 GW-Router(config-if)# no shutdown GW-Router(config-if)# exit GW-Router(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/1 GW-Router(config-if)# ip address 172.16.20.65 255.255.255.192 GW-Router(config-if)# no shutdown GW-Router(config-if)# exit GW-Router(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/2 GW-Router(config-if)# ip address 172.16.20.129 255.255.255.192 GW-Router(config-if)# no shutdown ``` **Step 3 (2 marks): Access Point uplink & SSID configuration** On AP GUI: Set SSID as **KTU-LAB**, Security as **WPA2-PSK**, Channel as **1 or 6 or 11**, and uplink IP to **172.16.20.66/26** (optional static) with gateway **172.16.20.65**. **Step 4 (3 marks): DHCP pool creation on DHCP Server (172.16.20.130)** Using Cisco Packet Tracer **Services → DHCP** tab: | Field | Value | |---|---| | **Pool Name** | KTU-LAB-POOL | | **Default Gateway** | 172.16.20.65 | | **DNS Server** | 172.16.20.131 | | **Starting IP** | 172.16.20.70 | | **Subnet Mask** | 255.255.255.192 | | **Max Users** | 50 | | **Lease Time** | 8 days (default) | **Step 5 (verification — marks awarded on output):** On a wireless client laptop: ```cisco C:\> ipconfig /release C:\> ipconfig /renew C:\> ipconfig IPv4 Address. . . . . . . . : 172.16.20.70 Subnet Mask . . . . . . . . : 255.255.255.192 Default Gateway . . . . . . : 172.16.20.65 DNS Server . . . . . . . . : 172.16.20.131 C:\> ping 172.16.20.130 ← Pings DHCP server (should succeed) C:\> ping 172.16.20.1 ← Pings Office gateway (inter-subnet, should succeed) ``` --- #### **Part (b) — DNS Configuration (7 marks)** **Step 1 (1 mark): Assign static IPs to DNS & HTTP servers** - **DNS Server:** 172.16.20.131/26, Gateway 172.16.20.129 - **HTTP Server:** 172.16.20.132/26, Gateway 172.16.20.129 **Step 2 (3 marks): DNS A-Record configuration on DNS Server** In Packet Tracer, navigate to **Services → DNS**: | Name | Type | Address (A Record) | TTL | |---|---|---|---| | `lab.ktu.ac.in` | A Record | **172.16.20.65** | 86400 (1 day) | | `server.ktu.ac.in` | A Record | **172.16.20.132** | 86400 | | `www.ktu.ac.in` | CNAME | → `server.ktu.ac.in` | 86400 | > *(Note: The lab gateway is the AP's uplink IP — a clever trick to demonstrate that a name can resolve to a network device, not just a server.)* **Step 3 (1 mark): Enable DNS Service toggle** to **ON** and save. **Step 4 (2 marks): Configure all DHCP clients to use 172.16.20.131 as DNS server** (done in part (a) — examiner verifies via `ipconfig /all`). **Step 5 (verification — marks for command output):** ```cisco C:\> nslookup lab.ktu.ac.in Server: 172.16.20.131 Address: 172.16.20.131 Name: lab.ktu.ac.in Address: 172.16.20.65 ← Correct resolution C:\> nslookup server.ktu.ac.in Name: server.ktu.ac.in Address: 172.168.20.132 ← Correct resolution C:\> ping lab.ktu.ac.in ← Should resolve and ping C:\> http://server.ktu.ac.in ← Browser should display HTTP page ``` --- > ### 🅱️ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > > **Q.B.** Design a network for a **2-floor office building** using: > - **Floor 1 (Wired LAN — 30 PCs)** > - **Floor 2 (Wireless LAN — 25 laptops)** > - **Common Services (DHCP, HTTP, FTP)** > > Use IP block **10.10.10.0/24**, apply **VLSM**, and configure services such that: > *(a) The HTTP server hosts a webpage visible to both floors; FTP allows a user to upload and download a test file.* **(7 marks)** > *(b) Wireless clients on Floor 2 obtain IPs automatically and can access the HTTP server by hostname `intranet.ktu.edu`.* **(7 marks)** ### 🟢 Model Solution Sketch for Question B #### **Part (a) — VLSM + HTTP + FTP (7 marks)** **Subnetting (1 mark):** Since Floor 1 needs **30 hosts**, use **/27** (30 usable hosts). Since Floor 2 needs **25 hosts**, also use **/27** (30 usable hosts, 25 used). Server farm gets **/27** (30 usable hosts). The WAN link between router and ISP gets **/30** (2 usable hosts). | Subnet | CIDR | Network | Usable Range | Gateway | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Floor 1 | /27 | 10.10.10.0 | .1 → .30 | 10.10.10.1 | Wired LAN | | Floor 2 | /27 | 10.10.10.32 | .33 → .62 | 10.10.10.33 | Wireless LAN | | Servers | /27 | 10.10.10.64 | .65 → .94 | 10.10.10.65 | DHCP/DNS/HTTP/FTP | | WAN Link | /30 | 10.10.10.96 | .97 → .98 | 10.10.10.97 | Router ↔ ISP | **HTTP Setup (2 marks):** On HTTP server **10.10.10.66**, enable HTTP service, edit `index.html` to a custom KTU page. **FTP Setup (2 marks):** On FTP server **10.10.10.67**, enable FTP service, create user `student` with password `ktu@123`, add a test file `notes.txt`. **Verification (2 marks):** ```cisco C:\> ftp 10.10.10.67 User: student Password: ktu@123 ftp> put testfile.txt ftp> get notes.txt ftp> bye C:\> http://10.10.10.66 ← Browser shows KTU custom page ``` #### **Part (b) — Wireless DHCP + DNS-Based Access (7 marks)** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Configure AP SSID `KTU-Floor2`, security WPA2-PSK, channel 6, uplink to switch. **Step 2 (3 marks):** Create DHCP pool on DHCP server **10.10.10.65**: - Pool name: `FLOOR2-WIFI` - Gateway: 10.10.10.33 - DNS: 10.10.10.68 (DNS server) - Start IP: 10.10.10.35 - Max users: 25 - Lease: 8 days **Step 3 (2 marks):** On DNS server **10.10.10.68**, add A-record: `intranet.ktu.edu` → **10.10.10.66** **Verification on a wireless laptop:** ```cisco C:\> ipconfig /renew IPv4 Address. . . . . . : 10.10.10.40 DNS Server . . . . . . : 10.10.10.68 C:\> ping intranet.ktu.edu ← Should resolve to 10.10.10.66 C:\> start http://intranet.ktu.edu ← Browser shows KTU page ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting `no shutdown` on router interfaces** → Interface stays administratively down, all pings fail. *(−2 marks in KTU valuation)* > 2. **Wrong default gateway on PCs** → Inter-subnet communication fails silently. *(−1 mark)* > 3. **DHCP pool exclusion not added** → Gateway IP gets handed to a client, causing intermittent outages. *(−1 mark)* — **Always exclude the gateway!** > 4. **DNS A-Record mistyped** (e.g., `intranet.ktu,edu` with a comma) → Resolution fails. *(−1 mark)* — **Use dots, not commas.** > 5. **Forgetting to enable the HTTP/FTP service toggle** in Packet Tracer → Service is configured but not running. *(−2 marks)* — **Always click the `ON` button.** > 6. **AP not bridged to the correct VLAN/subnet** → Wireless clients get IPs from the wrong pool or fail to get any IP. *(−3 marks — fatal topology error)* > 7. **Not saving running-config to startup-config** → On reload, all configuration is lost. *(−1 mark in practicals)* > 8. **Mixing up serial cable DCE/DTE ends** → WAN link stays down. *(−2 marks)* — **DCE side needs `clock rate 64000`.** --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🎯 **Subnetting is mandatory first step** — always allocate subnets using **VLSM** based on actual host requirements, not equally. - 🎯 **Router interfaces become default gateways** — one router interface per subnet; always `no shutdown`. - 🎯 **Switches are Layer 2 only** — they forward frames within a subnet, never between subnets. - 🎯 **Access Points are Layer 2 bridges** — they do NOT route; they simply extend the same subnet wirelessly. - 🎯 **DHCP = UDP 67/68**; **DNS = UDP 53**; **HTTP = TCP 80**; **FTP = TCP 21/20**; **SSH = TCP 22**. - 🎯 **DHCP four-step process: D-O-R-A** (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge). - 🎯 **DNS resolves names → IPs** using A-records; CNAMEs are aliases; PTR is reverse lookup. - 🎯 **WPA2-PSK** is the minimum security standard for enterprise Wi-Fi in 2024 (avoid WEP/Open). - 🎯 **Channels 1, 6, 11** are the only non-overlapping channels in the 2.4 GHz band. - 🎯 **Always verify with `ping`, `ipconfig`, `nslookup`, and a browser** — a configuration that has not been tested earns zero marks in KTU practicals. - 🎯 **Save your work**: `copy running-config startup-config` on Cisco IOS; in Packet Tracer, save the `.pkt` file before exiting the lab. - 🎯 **Exam mantra**: *"Plan → Cable → IP → Configure Services → Verify."*

topics.title: Measurement of current, voltage, frequency and phase shift of signal in a RC network using oscilloscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of current, voltage, frequency and phase shift of signal in a RC network using oscilloscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **RC Network** is a fundamental first-order linear passive electrical circuit consisting of a **Resistor (R)** and a **Capacitor (C)** connected in either series or parallel topology. When excited by a sinusoidal alternating current (AC) source, the RC network exhibits a characteristic frequency-dependent behavior governed by the differential equation of charge storage and dissipation.

**Oscilloscope-based measurement** of an RC network refers to the experimental procedure of visualizing time-domain waveforms on a Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) or Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) to quantify four primary electrical parameters:

1. **Voltage** — Instantaneous and peak-to-peak potential differences across R and C.
2. **Current** — Deduced indirectly via Ohm's law using the voltage drop across the known resistor.
3. **Frequency** — Determined from the time period of the displayed waveform.
4. **Phase Shift** — The temporal angular displacement between the input source voltage and the output voltage across the capacitor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The phase shift in a series RC low-pass filter is **45° at the cutoff frequency** $f_c$. This is the single most frequently tested numerical value in board examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an RC circuit like a **water storage tank connected to a pipe with friction**:

- The **Resistor (R)** is the narrow pipe — it restricts the steady flow of water (current).
- The **Capacitor (C)** is the storage tank — it takes time to fill and empty, never allowing instant changes in water level (voltage).
- When you push water in sinusoidally (AC source), the tank's water level (capacitor voltage) cannot keep up with your pushing rhythm — it **lags behind**. That lag, measured in degrees of the pushing cycle, is the **phase shift**.
- The **oscilloscope** is like a high-speed camera that captures both your pushing motion (input) and the water level rising (output) on the same screen, allowing you to measure how out-of-sync they are.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Rule:** In a series RC circuit driven by AC, the **current always leads the source voltage by an angle $\phi$**, while the **voltage across the capacitor always lags the source voltage by the same angle $\phi$**. The voltage across the resistor, however, is **in phase** with the current.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Angular frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ (radians/second)
- **Time constant:** $\tau = RC$ (seconds)
- **Capacitive reactance:** $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ (ohms)
- **Cutoff frequency (-3 dB):** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (hertz)
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (used implicitly for oscilloscope probe calibration)
- **Standard input impedance of oscilloscope:** **1 MΩ** parallel with **20 pF** (board exam favourite)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A series RC network connected to a sinusoidal source $V_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ obeys Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):

**Step 1 — KVL Application:**
The source voltage must equal the sum of the resistor drop and the capacitor drop at every instant.

$$V_s(t) = V_R(t) + V_C(t)$$

**Step 2 — Constitutive Relations:**
The resistor obeys Ohm's law instantaneously ($V_R = iR$), but the capacitor stores charge ($i = C \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$), introducing a time-derivative relationship.

$$V_R(t) = i(t) \cdot R \qquad \text{and} \qquad i(t) = C \frac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Phasor Domain Conversion:**
In steady-state AC analysis, all sinusoidal quantities are represented as rotating phasors. The resistor's impedance is real ($Z_R = R$), while the capacitor's impedance is purely imaginary and negative ($Z_C = \dfrac{-j}{\omega C}$).

$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C} = -\frac{j}{\omega C}$$

**Step 4 — Total Impedance Calculation:**
Impedances in series add as complex numbers. The total series impedance is:

$$Z_{total} = R + Z_C = R - \frac{j}{\omega C}$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude and Phase Extraction:**
The magnitude $|Z_{total}|$ determines the current amplitude (Ohm's law for AC), and the argument $\angle Z_{total}$ determines the phase shift.

$$|Z_{total}| = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_C}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Frequency Response Interpretation:**
- At **very low frequencies** ($\omega \to 0$): $X_C \to \infty$, so the capacitor blocks DC-like signals and $\phi \to -90°$.
- At **cutoff frequency** ($\omega = \omega_c = 1/RC$): $X_C = R$, so $\phi = -45°$ (the classic board exam value).
- At **very high frequencies** ($\omega \to \infty$): $X_C \to 0$, so the capacitor acts like a short circuit and $\phi \to 0°$.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why phasors?** Converting time-differential equations into algebraic complex-number equations makes AC analysis dramatically simpler — no calculus required.
- **Why does voltage across C lag?** Because charge accumulation on a capacitor requires time; the capacitor cannot instantaneously follow a rapidly changing input, producing a temporal delay.
- **Why measure $V_C$ for phase?** $V_C$ and $V_R$ are always 90° apart in a series RC circuit, so observing one of them relative to the source reveals the phase angle directly.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitive Reactance | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Ω ($\Omega$) | $X_C \to \infty$ as $f \to 0$ |
| Series Impedance Magnitude | $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2}$ | Ω | Dominated by R at high f |
| Series Impedance Argument | $\phi = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{X_C}{R}\right)$ | degrees / rad | $0°$ at $f \to \infty$, $-90°$ at $f \to 0$ |
| Cutoff Frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | $\phi = -45°$ exactly here |
| Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | seconds | 63.2% charge time |
| AC Current (RMS) | $I_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{\vert Z \vert}$ | Ampere | Ohm's law, AC form |
| Power Factor | $\cos \phi = \dfrac{R}{\vert Z \vert}$ | dimensionless | Always $\leq 1$ |
| Lissajous Phase | $\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{B}{A}\right)$ | degrees | A = max X-deflection, B = Y-intercept |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Substitution Note:** The table above uses $\vert Z \vert$ instead of $\vert Z \vert$ to prevent markdown table-breaking. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Crossovers:** RC networks separate bass/treble frequencies in loudspeakers.
- **Smoothing Filters:** RC low-pass filters remove high-frequency noise from DC power supplies.
- **Timer Circuits (555 Timer IC):** The RC time constant sets precise timing intervals in millions of devices.
- **Signal Conditioning in Networking:** Before analog signals enter ADC converters in network interface cards, RC anti-aliasing filters shape the bandwidth — directly linking this experiment to **PCERL407** Computer Networking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Phase Shift in Series RC Circuit

**Starting Premise:** A sinusoidal voltage source $V_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is applied across a series combination of resistor R and capacitor C.

**Step 1:** Express the KVL equation in the time domain.

$$V_m \sin(\omega t) = i(t) R + \frac{1}{C}\int i(t)\, dt$$

*Logic:* The resistor drop is instantaneous, while the capacitor drop is the integral of current (since $V_C = \dfrac{Q}{C}$ and $i = \dfrac{dQ}{dt}$).

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to time to eliminate the integral.

$$V_m \omega \cos(\omega t) = R \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{i(t)}{C}$$

*Logic:* Differentiating removes the integral and applies $\dfrac{d}{dt}\sin(\omega t) = \omega\cos(\omega t)$.

**Step 3:** Assume a steady-state sinusoidal current solution of the form $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \theta)$, where $\theta$ is the unknown phase angle by which current leads the source voltage.

**Step 4:** Substitute into the differentiated equation and match coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$.

$$V_m \omega \cos(\omega t) = R I_m \omega \cos(\omega t + \theta) + \frac{I_m}{C}\sin(\omega t + \theta)$$

**Step 5:** Expand using trigonometric identities and separate sine and cosine components.

After simplification (grouping like terms), the tangent of the phase angle emerges as the ratio of reactive to resistive components:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{X_C}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega RC}$$

**Step 6:** Take the inverse tangent to obtain the phase angle in radians (convert to degrees by multiplying by $180/\pi$).

$$\boxed{\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi f RC}\right)}$$

*Logic:* This is the angle by which current **leads** the source voltage, equivalently the angle by which the capacitor voltage **lags** the source voltage.

### 3.2 Derivation of Lissajous Phase Measurement Formula

**Starting Premise:** Two sinusoidal signals of equal frequency but different phase are applied to the X (horizontal) and Y (vertical) inputs of an oscilloscope set in **X-Y mode**. The resulting figure is an ellipse.

**Step 1:** Write the parametric equations of the two signals.

$$x(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$$
$$y(t) = B \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

*Logic:* A is the amplitude on the X-channel, B is the amplitude on the Y-channel, $\phi$ is the unknown phase difference.

**Step 2:** Find the value of $y$ when $x = 0$. Since $x = 0$ occurs at $\omega t = 0$, substitute $t = 0$ into the Y equation.

$$y(0) = B \sin(\phi)$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that the maximum X-deflection equals A (when $\sin(\omega t) = 1$).

$$\text{Max X-deflection} = A$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio of the Y-intercept to the maximum X-deflection. This ratio equals $\sin(\phi)$.

$$\frac{B \sin(\phi)}{A} = \frac{y\text{-intercept}}{\text{max X}}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the phase angle $\phi$.

$$\boxed{\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{Y\text{-intercept}}{\text{max X-deflection}}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{B'}{A'}\right)}$$

*Logic:* Where $B'$ is the vertical distance from the X-axis to the ellipse's intersection on the Y-axis, and $A'$ is the total horizontal width of the ellipse. This is the **Lissajous Figure Method**, the standard technique taught in KTU labs.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Given:** A series RC circuit has $R = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\text{ \mu F}$, driven by a 1 kHz sinusoidal source of 5 V peak.

**Step 1:** Compute the angular frequency.

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 1000 = 6283.19 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute capacitive reactance.

$$X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C} = \frac{1}{6283.19 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1591.55 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Compute total impedance magnitude.

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2} = \sqrt{(1000)^2 + (1591.55)^2} = 1878.55 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Compute phase shift.

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{X_C}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1591.55}{1000}\right) = 57.86°$$

**Step 5:** Compute RMS current.

$$I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{5/\sqrt{2}}{1878.55} = 1.882 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 6:** Compute cutoff frequency for verification.

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1591.55 \text{ Hz}$$

*Logic:* Since the source frequency (1000 Hz) is less than $f_c$ (1591 Hz), the circuit is operating below cutoff — confirming that significant phase shift occurs (here $\approx 58°$, between the 0° and -90° extremes).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FG[Function Generator\nSinusoidal Source]
    RC[Series RC Network\nR = 1kOhm, C = 0.1uF]
    CH1[Oscilloscope Channel 1\nSource Voltage Vs]
    CH2[Oscilloscope Channel 2\nCapacitor Voltage Vc]
    VR[Voltmeter Across R\nIndirect Current Measurement]

    FG -->|Vs at f kHz| RC
    RC -->|Vc| CH2
    FG -->|Direct connection| CH1
    RC -->|Voltage across R| VR

    CH1 --- CH2

    style FG fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RC fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CH1 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CH2 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VR fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Circuit Schematic of Series RC Network with Measurement Probes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRC[AC Source Function Generator]
        A1((+))
        A2((-))
    end

    subgraph RCTOP[Series RC Network]
        R1[/Resistor R/]
        C1[/Capacitor C/]
    end

    subgraph OSC[Dual Channel Oscilloscope]
        P1[Probe CH1\nMeasures Vs]
        P2[Probe CH2\nMeasures Vc]
        GND[Common Ground]
    end

    A1 --> R1
    R1 --> C1
    C1 --> A2
    A2 --> GND
    A1 --> P1
    C1 --> P2
    P2 --> GND

    style SRC fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style RCTOP fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460
    style OSC fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

### 4.3 Lissajous Figure Pattern Recognition Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Set Oscilloscope to XY Mode]) --> OBSERVE{What Shape on Screen?}
    OBSERVE -->|Straight Line Tilted| LINE[Phase = 0 deg or 180 deg\nSignals In Phase]
    OBSERVE -->|Circle| CIRC[Phase = 90 deg or 270 deg\nSignals in Quadrature]
    OBSERVE -->|Ellipse| ELL[Phase between 0 and 90 deg\nUse Formula]
    ELL --> FORM[phi equals arcsine of B over A]
    FORM --> END([Record Phase Shift Value])

    LINE --> END
    CIRC --> END

    style START fill:#3498db,color:#fff
    style OBSERVE fill:#f39c12
    style LINE fill:#2ecc71
    style CIRC fill:#2ecc71
    style ELL fill:#2ecc71
    style FORM fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff
    style END fill:#9b59b6,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above use a **block-level functional architecture** approach as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol. They map signal flow and probe connections rather than attempting literal circuit symbol rendering, which is the optimal schematic strategy for documentation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**

Define the term **time constant** of an RC circuit. For a series RC network with $R = 2\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.5\text{ \mu F}$, calculate the time constant and the cutoff frequency.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **time constant** $\tau$ of an RC circuit is the time required for the capacitor voltage to reach 63.2% of its final steady-state value during charging, or equivalently, the product of resistance and capacitance.

$$\tau = RC = (2 \times 10^3) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Cutoff frequency:**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} = 159.15 \text{ Hz} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

The time constant represents how quickly the circuit responds to changes. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]**

What is the **Lissajous figure method** for phase measurement? Mention the formula and the significance of using X-Y mode on an oscilloscope.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Lissajous figure method** is a technique used to measure the phase difference between two sinusoidal signals of equal frequency by displaying one on the horizontal (X) input and the other on the vertical (Y) input of an oscilloscope operating in **X-Y mode**. **(1 mark)**

The resulting pattern is an ellipse whose shape depends on the phase angle. The phase is computed using:

$$\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{B}{A}\right)$$

where $A$ is the maximum horizontal deflection and $B$ is the vertical intercept at $x = 0$. **(1 mark)**

**Significance:** This method provides an accurate visual phase measurement without requiring dual-trace synchronization, and is widely used in lab experiments where precise phase relations must be verified. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Adapted from 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the **phase shift** between the source voltage and the voltage across the capacitor in a series RC network driven by a sinusoidal source. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a series RC circuit with $R = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\text{ \mu F}$, calculate: **(7 marks)**

- (i) The cutoff frequency $f_c$
- (ii) The phase shift when the input frequency is twice the cutoff frequency
- (iii) The capacitive reactance at the input frequency of $2f_c$
- (iv) The total impedance magnitude at $2f_c$

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Apply KVL around the series loop:

$$V_s(t) = i(t)R + \frac{1}{C}\int i(t)\, dt$$

Convert to phasor form. In phasor domain, $V_s$, $I$, $V_R$, $V_C$ are complex numbers with magnitudes and angles. The impedances are $Z_R = R$ (real) and $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ (imaginary). **(2 marks)**

Total impedance:

$$Z = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = R - \frac{j}{\omega C}$$

Magnitude and phase:

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

$$\phi = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)$$

The current leads source voltage by $\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)$, and since $V_C = I \cdot Z_C$, the capacitor voltage **lags** the source voltage by exactly:

$$\phi_C = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right) \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

Given: $R = 4.7 \times 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$

**(i) Cutoff frequency:**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}} = 3386.27 \text{ Hz} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Phase shift at $f = 2f_c$:**

At $f = 2f_c$, $\omega = 2\omega_c = \dfrac{2}{RC}$, so $\omega RC = 2$.

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = 26.57° \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Capacitive reactance at $2f_c$:**

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi (2f_c) C} = \frac{1}{2 \times 2\pi f_c C} = \frac{R}{2} = \frac{4700}{2} = 2350 \text{ }\Omega \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iv) Total impedance at $2f_c$:**

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2} = \sqrt{(4700)^2 + (2350)^2} = \sqrt{22.09 \times 10^6 + 5.5225 \times 10^6} = 5255.71 \text{ }\Omega \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Internal Choice Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **Lissajous figure method** for measuring phase difference. How would you distinguish between a phase shift of $30°$ and a phase shift of $150°$ using the ellipse orientation? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series RC low-pass filter has $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\text{ nF}$. A sinusoidal signal of 10 V peak at 50 kHz is applied. Calculate: **(7 marks)**

- (i) The output voltage magnitude across the capacitor
- (ii) The phase lag of output with respect to input
- (iii) The current drawn from the source (peak value)
- (iv) The power dissipated in the resistor

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lissajous Figure Method (7 marks):**

When two sinusoidal signals of equal frequency but different phase are applied to the X and Y inputs of an oscilloscope in X-Y mode, the screen displays an ellipse (or line, depending on phase). The parametric equations are:

$$x(t) = A \sin(\omega t), \qquad y(t) = B \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

**(2 marks)** for equations.

The phase is extracted from:

$$\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{B_{\text{intercept}}}{A_{\text{max}}}\right)$$

**(2 marks)** for formula.

**Distinguishing 30° from 150° (3 marks):** Both produce ellipses, but the **major axis tilt direction differs**:
- For $\phi < 90°$: the ellipse is tilted with positive slope (rising from lower-left to upper-right).
- For $\phi > 90°$: the ellipse is tilted with negative slope (descending from upper-left to lower-right).
- Additionally, when the Y signal **leads** the X signal, the ellipse opens in one direction; when it **lags**, the ellipse opens in the opposite direction. By temporarily connecting the same signal to both channels and observing which way the line tilts (as a reference), the examiner can determine whether the phase is acute (30°) or obtuse (150°).

---

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

Given: $R = 10^4 \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 10^{-9} \text{ F}$, $V_m = 10 \text{ V}$, $f = 50 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}$

**(i) Output voltage magnitude across C (transfer function magnitude):**

$$\frac{V_C}{V_s} = \frac{X_C}{\sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(\omega RC)^2 + 1}}$$

Compute $\omega RC$:

$$\omega RC = 2\pi \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-9} = 2\pi \times 0.5 = 3.1416$$

$$\frac{V_C}{V_s} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(3.1416)^2 + 1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10.87}} = 0.3033$$

$$V_C = 10 \times 0.3033 = 3.033 \text{ V (peak)} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Phase lag:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1}{3.1416}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.3183) = 17.66° \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Peak current:**

$$\vert Z \vert = \frac{R}{\cos \phi} \cdot \frac{\cos \phi}{1} = \sqrt{R^2 + X_C^2} = \sqrt{(10^4)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{2\pi \times 5\times10^4 \times 10^{-9}}\right)^2}$$

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-9}} = 3183.10 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{10^8 + 1.0132 \times 10^7} = \sqrt{1.1013 \times 10^8} = 10494.29 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$I_m = \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{10}{10494.29} = 0.9529 \text{ mA (peak)} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iv) Power dissipated in R (average power using RMS values):**

$$I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{0.9529 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.6738 \text{ mA}$$

$$P_R = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R = (0.6738 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 10^4 = 4.54 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 4.54 \text{ \mu W} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing lead and lag:** In a series RC circuit, students often state that voltage **leads** current. The correct statement is the **current leads the source voltage** by angle $\phi$, and **capacitor voltage lags** by $\phi$. This 2-mark concept error appears in nearly every batch.
> 2. **Wrong Lissajous sign:** The formula $\phi = \sin^{-1}(B/A)$ gives only the **acute angle**. For obtuse phases, you must determine the sign from the ellipse orientation by toggling the reference. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert peak to RMS:** When asked for "current" or "voltage" without specification, board answers require RMS values. Peak-to-peak and peak are different by factors of $\sqrt{2}$ and $2$.
> 4. **Unit of capacitive reactance:** $X_C$ is in **ohms (Ω)**, not farads. Students confuse capacitance with reactance.
> 5. **$\omega RC$ at cutoff = 1:** A classic trap. Many students incorrectly write $X_C = R$ at cutoff instead of $\omega RC = 1$. Both are equivalent but the second form is what examiners want for algebraic manipulation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RC Time Constant:** $\tau = RC$ — sets the speed of capacitor charging.
- **Cutoff Frequency:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — frequency where $X_C = R$ and phase = 45°.
- **Capacitive Reactance:** $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ — frequency-dependent opposition to AC current.
- **Series Impedance:** $Z = R - j/(\omega C)$ — complex-valued, with real part $R$ and imaginary part $-1/(\omega C)$.
- **Phase Shift (Series RC):** $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)$ — current leads source by this angle.
- **Lissajous Phase Formula:** $\phi = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{B}{A}\right)$ — visual method using ellipse intercept.
- **Transfer Function (Low-Pass):** $\dfrac{V_C}{V_s} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ — amplitude ratio.
- **Power Factor:** $\cos \phi = \dfrac{R}{\vert Z \vert}$ — always $\leq 1$.
- **Measurement Tip:** Use **oscilloscope in dual-channel mode** for time-domain phase; switch to **X-Y mode** for Lissajous verification.
- **Probe Calibration:** Always compensate the 10:1 probe before lab measurement to avoid amplitude distortion errors.
- **Lab Safety:** Never connect the oscilloscope ground clip to a non-grounded point in the circuit — this creates a short and can damage the function generator.
- **Real-World Link to PCERL407:** RC filters form the basis of **anti-aliasing filters** in ADC stages of network interface hardware, directly tying this experiment to computer networking signal integrity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectifier circuits with and without C filter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Rectifier circuits with and without C filter ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **rectifier circuit** is an electronic circuit that converts an alternating quantity (AC voltage or current) into a unidirectional (DC) quantity. A rectifier essentially allows current to flow in only one direction, blocking the reverse polarity half-cycles of the input AC waveform. The process of conversion from AC to pulsating DC is called **rectification**, and the resulting waveform is referred to as a **pulsating DC waveform**. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Basic Electrical and Electronics Engineering Laboratory, the experiment focuses on three canonical rectifier topologies: 1. **Half-Wave Rectifier (HWR)** – Conducts during only one half of the AC cycle. 2. **Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier (FWCT)** – Uses a center-tapped transformer and two diodes; conducts during both half-cycles. 3. **Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier (FWB)** – Uses four diodes in a bridge configuration; conducts during both half-cycles without a center-tapped transformer. A **C filter (Capacitor Filter)** is a shunt-connected reservoir capacitor placed across the load to smoothen the pulsating DC output by storing energy during the conduction peaks and releasing it during the valleys, thereby reducing the **AC ripple component** superimposed on the DC. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The experiment explicitly requires students to plot and compare the output waveforms of the rectifier **without filter** (pure pulsating DC) and **with C filter** (smoothed DC), and to compute the **Ripple Factor (γ)** and **Rectification Efficiency (η)** in both cases. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water pump** that pushes water through a pipe in alternating directions every second (this is AC). A **rectifier acts like a one-way check valve** that only allows water to flow in one direction — when the pump pushes in the permitted direction, water gushes out; when the pump reverses, the valve slams shut and no water flows. - A **half-wave rectifier** is like a single check valve — water only flows half the time. - A **full-wave rectifier** uses two check valves and a clever pipe arrangement so that water flows in the *same useful direction* during *both* halves of the pump cycle, doubling the output flow. - A **capacitor filter** is like a **small overhead water tank** placed at the output. When water flows strongly, the tank fills up. When the flow stops, the tank slowly drains through the consumer tap, ensuring a nearly continuous trickle. The bigger the tank (capacitor value), the smoother the water supply (less ripple). ### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold for Emphasis) The quality of a rectifier is judged by the following standard metrics, all of which are **KTU high-yield exam topics**: - **Ripple Factor (γ)** — A measure of the remaining AC content in the output (lower is better). - **Rectification Efficiency (η)** — Ratio of DC output power to AC input power (higher is better). - **Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)** — Maximum reverse voltage a diode must withstand without breakdown. - **Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF)** — How effectively the transformer is used. - **Peak-to-Peak Ripple Voltage (Vr(pp))** — Difference between maximum and minimum of the filtered output. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Values (no filter):** > - Half-Wave Rectifier: γ = **1.21**, η = **40.6 %**, PIV = **Vm** > - Full-Wave Rectifier: γ = **0.482**, η = **81.2 %**, PIV = **2Vm** > - Bridge Rectifier: γ = **0.482**, η = **81.2 %**, PIV = **Vm** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Half-Wave Rectifier — Operational Logic A half-wave rectifier uses a **single diode** in series with the load. The diode conducts only when it is **forward biased** (i.e., during the positive half-cycle of the input AC). **Step-by-step logic:** - During the **positive half-cycle** of input Vi = Vm sin(ωt), the diode D is forward biased and acts as a closed switch. The output across the load is approximately Vo ≈ Vm sin(ωt) (minus the diode drop VD ≈ 0.7 V for Si). - During the **negative half-cycle**, the diode is reverse biased and acts as an open switch. The output is **zero**, and the entire input voltage appears across the diode as reverse bias — this defines the **PIV** of the diode. - The resulting output is a series of positive half-sine pulses separated by gaps of equal duration — this is **pulsating DC**. ### 2.2 Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier — Operational Logic A full-wave center-tapped rectifier uses a **center-tapped transformer** and **two diodes** D1 and D2. - During the **positive half-cycle**, D1 conducts (its anode is at +Vm/2 relative to center tap) and D2 is reverse biased. Current flows through the upper half of the secondary and D1 to the load. - During the **negative half-cycle**, D2 conducts and D1 is reverse biased. Current flows through the lower half of the secondary and D2 to the load. - Because current always flows through the load in the **same direction**, the output is a series of positive half-sine pulses **with no gaps** between them — the **fundamental frequency is doubled** (2f instead of f). - The PIV of each diode is **2Vm** because when one diode is conducting, the entire secondary voltage appears across the other diode in reverse. ### 2.3 Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier — Operational Logic A bridge rectifier uses **four diodes** arranged in a diamond/bridge configuration and does **not** require a center-tapped transformer. - During the **positive half-cycle**, diodes D1 and D3 conduct; D2 and D4 are reverse biased. Current flows from the positive terminal of the source, through D1, through the load, through D3, and back to the source. - During the **negative half-cycle**, the polarity reverses, and D2 and D4 conduct. Current still flows through the load in the **same direction**. - The PIV of each diode is only **Vm** (half that of the center-tapped configuration), making the bridge the most widely used topology in practice. ### 2.4 Capacitor (C) Filter — Operational Logic The output of any rectifier without a filter is **pulsating DC** — a series of half-sine pulses. To obtain a smoother DC, a **capacitor** is connected in **parallel** with the load resistor RL. **Charging phase (Conduction Interval):** - When the rectifier output rises above the capacitor voltage, the diode becomes forward biased and the capacitor **charges rapidly** through the diode (which has very low forward resistance). - The capacitor voltage follows the rising portion of the rectified pulse closely, reaching approximately the **peak value Vm** at the crest. **Discharging phase (Non-Conduction Interval):** - Once the rectifier output falls below the capacitor voltage, the diode becomes reverse biased and disconnects the source from the load. - The capacitor now **discharges slowly** through the load resistor RL, following the exponential decay: $$v_c(t) = V_m \cdot e^{-t / (R_L C)}$$ - This discharge continues until the next rectifier pulse rises above the capacitor voltage again, at which point the diode turns ON and **recharges** the capacitor. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **time constant τ = RL C** must be much greater than the period of the rectified waveform (T = 1/f for HWR and T = 1/(2f) for FWR) for effective filtering. A larger C → smaller ripple → smoother DC. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Half-Wave Rectifier | Full-Wave (CT or Bridge) Rectifier | With C Filter (FWR) | Unit | |---|----------|---------------------|------------------------------------|---------------------|------| | 1 | DC Output Voltage, VDC | Vm / π | 2Vm / π | Vm − Vr(pp)/2 | V | | 2 | RMS Output Voltage, VRMS | Vm / 2 | Vm / √2 | ≈ VDC (for small ripple) | V | | 3 | Ripple Frequency, fr | f (input frequency) | 2f (double input frequency) | 2f | Hz | | 4 | Ripple Factor, γ | 1.21 | 0.482 | 1 / (4√3 f RL C) | — | | 5 | Rectification Efficiency, η | 40.6 % | 81.2 % | ≈ 100 % (ideal, very large C) | % | | 6 | Peak Inverse Voltage, PIV | Vm | 2Vm (CT) or Vm (Bridge) | Same as unfiltered | V | | 7 | Peak-to-Peak Ripple Voltage, Vr(pp) | — | — | Vm / (2 f RL C) | V | | 8 | RMS Ripple Voltage, Vr(rms) | — | — | Vr(pp) / (2√3) | V | | 9 | Transformer Utilization Factor, TUF | 0.2865 | 0.6928 (CT), 0.8106 (Bridge) | — | — | > **Boundary Condition:** All the above formulas assume **ideal diodes** (zero forward drop, infinite reverse resistance) and a **purely resistive load** RL. The diode forward drop VD ≈ 0.7 V (silicon) introduces a small correction in practical measurements. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Rectifier circuits with C filters are the **front-end of virtually every DC power supply** in the world — from the phone charger on your desk to the SMPS powering a server rack, the rectifier-filter combination is the first stage that converts mains AC to low-ripple DC. They are also used in: - **Battery chargers** (single-phase HWR for low-power, FWR for fast charging). - **DC motor drives** in small appliances. - **Signal demodulation** in AM radio envelope detectors. - **Welding rectifiers** and **electroplating** power supplies. - **HVDC transmission** systems use three-phase bridge rectifiers at gigawatt scale. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Average (DC) Output Voltage for Half-Wave Rectifier The output of a half-wave rectifier over one full period T = 2π/ω is given by: $$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} V_m \sin(\omega t), & 0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi \\ 0, & \pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi \end{cases}$$ The average (DC) value is defined as: $$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$ Since vo = 0 from π to 2π, the integration only runs from 0 to π: $$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$ Pulling the constant Vm outside and evaluating: $$V_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{0}^{\pi}$$ $$V_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\pi) - (-\cos(0)) \Big]$$ $$V_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -(-1) + 1 \Big] = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2$$ $$\boxed{V_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.318 \, V_m}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of Average (DC) Output Voltage for Full-Wave Rectifier In a full-wave rectifier, the output is positive for the **entire** period because both half-cycles are inverted and rectified. Thus: $$v_o(t) = V_m \vert \sin(\omega t) \vert, \quad 0 \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi$$ By symmetry, the average over the full period is twice the average over half a period: $$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$ $$V_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{0}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2$$ $$\boxed{V_{DC} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.636 \, V_m}$$ This is **exactly double** the half-wave value, which is a key KTU expected result. ### 3.3 Derivation of RMS Output Voltage for Half-Wave Rectifier $$V_{RMS}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$ Using the identity sin²(x) = (1 − cos(2x))/2: $$V_{RMS}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \Big[ \omega t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \Big]_{0}^{\pi}$$ $$V_{RMS}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \cdot \pi = \frac{V_m^2}{4}$$ $$\boxed{V_{RMS} = \frac{V_m}{2} = 0.5 \, V_m}$$ ### 3.4 Derivation of RMS Output Voltage for Full-Wave Rectifier By symmetry, integrating over the full period gives twice the contribution of one half: $$V_{RMS}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^2}{2}$$ $$\boxed{V_{RMS} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \, V_m}$$ ### 3.5 Derivation of Ripple Factor (γ) The **ripple factor** is defined as: $$\gamma = \frac{V_{ac(rms)}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{RMS}^2 - V_{DC}^2}}{V_{DC}}$$ **For Half-Wave Rectifier:** $$V_{ac(rms)} = \sqrt{V_{RMS}^2 - V_{DC}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_m}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{V_m}{\pi}\right)^2}$$ $$= V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{\pi^2}} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 4}{4\pi^2}}$$ $$\gamma_{HWR} = \frac{V_{ac(rms)}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{V_m \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 4}{4\pi^2}}}{\frac{V_m}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 4}{4}}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_{HWR} = 1.21}$$ **For Full-Wave Rectifier:** $$V_{ac(rms)} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\right)^2} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2} - \frac{4}{\pi^2}}$$ $$\gamma_{FWR} = \frac{V_{ac(rms)}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{2} - \frac{4}{\pi^2}}}{\frac{2V_m}{\pi}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{2} - 4}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_{FWR} = 0.482}$$ ### 3.6 Derivation of Rectification Efficiency (η) $$\eta = \frac{P_{DC}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{DC}^2 / R_L}{V_{RMS}^2 / R_L} = \frac{V_{DC}^2}{V_{RMS}^2} \times 100\%$$ **For Half-Wave Rectifier:** $$\eta_{HWR} = \frac{(V_m/\pi)^2}{(V_m/2)^2} = \frac{4}{\pi^2} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{HWR} = 40.6\%}$$ **For Full-Wave Rectifier:** $$\eta_{FWR} = \frac{(2V_m/\pi)^2}{(V_m/\sqrt{2})^2} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{FWR} = 81.2\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > The maximum possible efficiency of any rectifier is **100 %**, but this is approached only when the output is pure DC (i.e., when the ripple factor → 0, which requires an infinitely large C filter). ### 3.7 Derivation of Ripple Voltage with C Filter (Full-Wave Rectifier) During the discharge interval, the capacitor discharges exponentially through RL. If the ripple is small (τ ≫ T), the exponential can be linearized to a straight line (the **piecewise-linear approximation** used in KTU textbooks). The discharge time is approximately equal to half the rectified period: $$T_{discharge} \approx \frac{T}{2} = \frac{1}{2f}$$ The discharge slope is Vm / (RL C). The peak-to-peak ripple voltage is: $$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{V_m}{R_L C} \cdot \frac{1}{2f}$$ $$\boxed{V_{r(pp)} = \frac{V_m}{2 f R_L C}}$$ The RMS ripple voltage (assuming a triangular waveform): $$V_{r(rms)} = \frac{V_{r(pp)}}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{V_m}{4\sqrt{3} \, f R_L C}$$ The ripple factor with C filter: $$\boxed{\gamma = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{3} \, f R_L C} = \frac{0.144}{f R_L C}}$$ ### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table | Parameter | HWR (No Filter) | FWR (No Filter) | FWR + C Filter | |-----------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------| | Frequency of Ripple | f | 2f | 2f | | VDC | Vm/π | 2Vm/π | ≈ Vm | | Ripple Factor | 1.21 | 0.482 | 1 / (4√3 f RL C) | | Efficiency | 40.6 % | 81.2 % | Approaches 100 % | | PIV | Vm | 2Vm (CT) / Vm (Bridge) | Same as unfiltered | | Output Smoothness | Poor (large gaps) | Better (no gaps) | Excellent (almost pure DC) | | Transformer Required | Single secondary | Center-tapped (for CT) | None (for Bridge) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DC Power Supply This block diagram shows the complete signal flow from mains AC to regulated DC, with the **Rectifier + C Filter** stage being the focus of the experiment. ```mermaid flowchart LR sourceAC[Mains AC Supply
230V 50Hz] --> transformer[Step Down
Transformer] transformer --> rectifier[Rectifier Circuit
HWR or FWR] rectifier --> filterStage[Capacitor Filter
C in shunt with R Load] filterStage --> rawDC[Filtered DC Output
Low Ripple] rawDC --> regulator[Voltage Regulator
IC 7805 or 7812] regulator --> regulatedDC[Regulated DC
Pure DC Output] regulator --> load[DC Load] ``` ### 4.2 Half-Wave Rectifier Circuit Topology ```mermaid flowchart LR acIn[AC Source
V sub m sin omega t] --> diode1[Diode D1
Forward Biased in positive half] diode1 --> rLoad[Load Resistor R sub L] rLoad --> backToSource[Return to AC Source] rLoad -.Output.-> vOut[Output V sub o
Pulsating DC] diode1 -.Reverse Bias.-> pivMark[Reverse Voltage across D1
PIV equals V sub m] ``` ### 4.3 Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier Topology ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph bridgeBlock [Bridge Rectifier] d1[Diode D1] --- d2[Diode D2] d3[Diode D3] --- d4[Diode D4] d1 --- d3 d2 --- d4 end acSrc[AC Source] --> bridgeBlock bridgeBlock --> rLoad[Load Resistor R sub L] rLoad --> groundNode[Circuit Ground] bridgeBlock -.PIV.-> pivInfo[Each Diode PIV equals V sub m] ``` ### 4.4 Capacitor Filter Charging/Discharging Waveform Timing ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph cycleA [Conduction Phase] ch1[Diode ON] --> ch2[Capacitor Charges to V sub m] ch2 --> ch3[Output follows input peak] end subgraph cycleB [Non Conduction Phase] dch1[Diode OFF] --> dch2[Capacitor Discharges through R sub L] dch2 --> dch3[Exponential decay v sub c equals V sub m e power minus t by R sub L C] end cycleA --> cycleB cycleB --> cycleA ``` ### 4.5 Comparative Output Waveform Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph inputWave [Input AC] ac[Sinusoidal AC
V sub m sin omega t] end subgraph hwrWave [HWR Output No Filter] hwr[Pulsating DC
Positive half cycles only
Gaps between pulses] end subgraph fwrWave [FWR Output No Filter] fwr[Continuous Pulsating DC
Both half cycles rectified
No gaps doubled frequency] end subgraph fwrCWave [FWR Output With C Filter] fwrC[Smoothed DC
Capacitor charges to peak
Discharges slowly between peaks
Small ripple V sub r pp] end inputWave --> hwr inputWave --> fwr fwr --> fwrC ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of rectifier circuits and their output waveforms. In an actual KTU lab examination, students are expected to **draw the circuit schematic by hand** on graph paper using standard symbols and then sketch the corresponding input and output waveforms on the same time axis. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define ripple factor of a rectifier. What is its value for an ideal rectifier? **Model Answer:** Ripple factor (γ) is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of the AC component (ripple) present in the output to the average (DC) value of the output. $$\gamma = \frac{V_{ac(rms)}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{RMS}^2 - V_{DC}^2}}{V_{DC}}$$ For an **ideal rectifier**, the output is pure DC with no AC component, so Vac(rms) = 0, giving **γ = 0**. In practice, γ is never zero but is made very small using large-value filter capacitors. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)? State its value for a bridge rectifier. **Model Answer:** PIV is the **maximum reverse-bias voltage** that a diode in a rectifier circuit can withstand without breaking down. It is an important rating used while selecting a diode for a given application. For a **full-wave bridge rectifier**, each diode conducts for half a cycle, and during the other half, it is reverse biased by the full secondary voltage. Therefore, **PIV = Vm** (peak value of the transformer secondary voltage). This is half the PIV required in a center-tapped full-wave rectifier (where PIV = 2Vm). (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **full-wave center-tapped rectifier** with a **capacitor filter**. Draw the input and output waveforms. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** A full-wave center-tapped rectifier uses a **center-tapped step-down transformer**, two diodes D1 and D2, and a **shunt capacitor C** across the load RL. **Operation during positive half-cycle:** - The upper half of the secondary winding is positive with respect to the center tap. - D1 is forward biased and conducts; D2 is reverse biased and OFF. - The capacitor C charges rapidly through D1 to the peak voltage Vm. **Operation during negative half-cycle:** - The lower half of the secondary winding is positive with respect to the center tap. - D2 conducts; D1 is reverse biased. - The capacitor again charges to Vm (with the polarity maintained across the load). **Capacitor Action:** - Between successive peaks, the diode is reverse biased and the capacitor **discharges exponentially** through RL with time constant τ = RLC. - The output waveform is a **nearly flat DC with small triangular ripples** of frequency 2f. **Waveform sketch description (to be drawn on graph paper):** - **Input:** Sinusoidal AC Vi = Vm sin(ωt). - **Output (no filter):** Continuous series of positive half-sine pulses at frequency 2f. - **Output (with C filter):** Approximately a flat DC line at Vm with small exponential dips between charging peaks. **Key results:** $$V_{DC} \approx V_m - \frac{V_m}{4 f R_L C} = V_m \left(1 - \frac{1}{4 f R_L C}\right)$$ $$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{V_m}{2 f R_L C}, \quad \gamma = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{3} f R_L C}$$ **(7 marks — circuit 2, working 3, waveforms 2)** --- **(b)** For a full-wave rectifier with C filter, the secondary voltage of the transformer is **Vrms = 12 V**, **f = 50 Hz**, **RL = 100 Ω**, and **C = 1000 μF**. Compute the **DC output voltage**, **peak-to-peak ripple voltage**, and **ripple factor**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** Vrms = 12 V, f = 50 Hz, RL = 100 Ω, C = 1000 μF = 1000 × 10⁻⁶ F. **Step 1: Compute peak voltage Vm** $$V_m = V_{rms} \times \sqrt{2} = 12 \times \sqrt{2} = 16.97 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2: Compute peak-to-peak ripple voltage** $$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{V_m}{2 f R_L C} = \frac{16.97}{2 \times 50 \times 100 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{16.97}{10} = 1.697 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3: Compute DC output voltage** $$V_{DC} = V_m - \frac{V_{r(pp)}}{2} = 16.97 - \frac{1.697}{2} = 16.97 - 0.849$$ $$V_{DC} = 16.12 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4: Compute ripple factor** $$\gamma = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{3} \, f R_L C} = \frac{1}{4 \times 1.732 \times 50 \times 100 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\gamma = \frac{1}{4 \times 1.732 \times 5} = \frac{1}{34.64} = 0.0289$$ **Final Answers:** VDC ≈ **16.12 V**, Vr(pp) ≈ **1.70 V**, γ ≈ **0.0289 (2.89 %)**. **(7 marks — each step ~1.5 marks, final answer with units 1 mark)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** Derive the expressions for **rectification efficiency** and **ripple factor** of a **half-wave rectifier** with a resistive load. State the standard numerical values. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Average (DC) Output Voltage:** The output of a HWR is Vo = Vm sin(ωt) for 0 ≤ ωt ≤ π, and 0 otherwise. $$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{\pi}$$ **RMS Output Voltage:** $$V_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)} = \frac{V_m}{2}$$ **Rectification Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{DC}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{DC}^2 / R_L}{V_{RMS}^2 / R_L} = \frac{V_{DC}^2}{V_{RMS}^2} = \frac{(V_m/\pi)^2}{(V_m/2)^2} = \frac{4}{\pi^2}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{HWR} = \frac{4}{\pi^2} \times 100\% = 40.6\%}$$ **Ripple Factor:** $$V_{ac(rms)} = \sqrt{V_{RMS}^2 - V_{DC}^2} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{4} - \frac{V_m^2}{\pi^2}} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 4}{4\pi^2}}$$ $$\gamma = \frac{V_{ac(rms)}}{V_{DC}} = \frac{V_m \sqrt{(\pi^2 - 4)/(4\pi^2)}}{V_m/\pi} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 4}{4}}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_{HWR} = 1.21}$$ **(7 marks — derivations 5, standard values 2)** --- **(b)** Compare the performance of **half-wave, full-wave center-tapped, and bridge rectifiers** without filter in terms of (i) DC output voltage, (ii) ripple factor, (iii) rectification efficiency, and (iv) PIV. Mention one **practical application** of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Half-Wave (HWR) | Full-Wave CT (FWCT) | Bridge (FWB) | |-----------|-----------------|---------------------|--------------| | VDC | Vm/π = 0.318 Vm | 2Vm/π = 0.636 Vm | 2Vm/π = 0.636 Vm | | Ripple Factor (γ) | 1.21 | 0.482 | 0.482 | | Efficiency (η) | 40.6 % | 81.2 % | 81.2 % | | PIV | Vm | 2Vm | Vm | | Ripple Frequency | f | 2f | 2f | | Transformer TUF | 0.2865 | 0.6928 | 0.8106 | **Practical Applications:** - **HWR:** Low-power battery chargers, signal demodulators (AM envelope detector), simple DC supplies for toys. - **FWCT:** Audio amplifiers, laboratory DC power supplies where a center-tapped transformer is available. - **FWB:** General-purpose DC power supplies, mobile chargers, computer SMPS front-end, UPS systems. (Bridge is preferred because it does not need a center-tapped transformer and has lower PIV rating per diode.) **(7 marks — table 4, applications 3)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the DC output of FWR** — Students often write VDC = Vm/π for full-wave as well. This costs **2 marks** directly. > 2. **Mixing up PIV** — Confusing PIV = Vm for bridge with PIV = 2Vm for center-tapped is a classic error. Always draw the conducting and non-conducting diodes and trace the voltage loop. > 3. **Sign error in C-filter formula** — VDC = Vm − Vr(pp)/2, **not** Vm + Vr(pp)/2. The capacitor voltage sits *below* the peak. > 4. **Units inconsistency** — Always convert μF to F, kHz to Hz, kΩ to Ω before substituting into the formula. Marks are deducted for unit mismatches. > 5. **Skipping the waveform sketch** — KTU strictly requires the input and output waveforms. Omitting them costs **2 to 3 marks** even if the calculation is perfect. > 6. **Forgetting "ideality assumptions"** — Always state the assumptions (ideal diode, no source resistance) at the start of a derivation question. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - **Rectifier types:** HWR (1 diode), FWCT (2 diodes + center-tapped transformer), FWB (4 diodes, no center tap). - **HWR key values:** VDC = Vm/π, VRMS = Vm/2, η = 40.6 %, γ = 1.21, PIV = Vm, fripple = f. - **FWR key values:** VDC = 2Vm/π, VRMS = Vm/√2, η = 81.2 %, γ = 0.482, PIV = 2Vm (CT) / Vm (Bridge), fripple = 2f. - **C filter function:** Charges to Vm during conduction, discharges through RL during non-conduction → smooths the output. - **C filter key formulas:** Vr(pp) = Vm / (2 f RL C), VDC = Vm − Vr(pp)/2, γ = 1 / (4√3 f RL C). - **Bigger C → Smaller ripple → Smoother DC** (provided RL is fixed). - **Bridge rectifier is the industry standard** because it needs no center tap, has the lowest PIV per diode, and the highest TUF. - **Always draw waveforms** — input sinusoidal AC, unfiltered pulsating DC, and filtered smoothed DC. - **Units check:** Capacitance in Farads, frequency in Hertz, resistance in Ohms, voltages in Volts. - **Practical tip:** In the lab, measure VDC using a **DC voltmeter** (or DMM on DC mode) and VRMS using an **AC voltmeter** (or DMM on AC mode) across the load. - **Safety note:** Always use an **isolation transformer** between mains and the experimental circuit to avoid electric shock; keep the load resistor's wattage rating above the expected dissipation. - **Exam mantra:** "Half-wave is half-useful, full-wave is fully useful, capacitor filter makes both beautiful." Remember the canonical numbers **1.21, 40.6, 0.482, 81.2** — they appear in almost every KTU question paper on this topic.

topics.title: Clipping and clamping circuits using diodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Clipping and clamping circuits using diodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wave-Shaping Circuits: The Two Pillars

In Analog Electronics, a **Wave-Shaping Circuit** is a combination of linear and non-linear elements (diodes, transistors, op-amps) designed to alter the shape of an input signal without causing excessive distortion of its fundamental information. The two principal branches of passive wave-shaping are **Clippers** and **Clampers** — both relying on the non-linear, one-way conduction property of the **p-n junction diode**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Analog Electronics Track):**
> The clipping and clamping experiment is a mandatory lab item in the Analog Electronics / Electronic Circuits Lab. The diode here acts as a *voltage-controlled switch* — OFF (open) during reverse bias, ON (closed ≈ 0.7 V drop) during forward bias.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Clipper

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clipper:** A clipper (also called a *limiter* or *voltage selector*) is a two-terminal network that transmits the input waveform to the output without alteration over a certain amplitude range, and *removes* (clips off) the portion of the waveform that lies outside this range. The diode is the controlling element, and a resistor sets the current path.

**Mathematical form of an ideal clipper transfer characteristic:**

$$v_{out} = \begin{cases} v_{in} & \text{for } v_{in} \leq V_{ref} \\ V_{ref} & \text{for } v_{in} > V_{ref} \end{cases}$$

where $V_{ref}$ is the clipping reference (0 V for an unbiased clipper, a bias voltage for a biased clipper).

## 1.3 Formal Definition of a Clamper

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clamper:** A clamper (also called a *DC restorer* or *level shifter*) is a network comprising a diode, capacitor, and resistor that shifts the entire input waveform either upward or downward along the voltage axis by a desired DC level, *without changing the peak-to-peak amplitude or shape* of the signal.

**Mathematical form of an ideal clamper transfer characteristic:**

$$v_{out}(t) = v_{in}(t) + V_{DC,shift}$$

where $V_{DC,shift}$ is the DC offset introduced by the clamper.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### The Clipper → "Hedge-Trimmer for Voltage"

Imagine a long, rolling hill of grass (your sine wave) and a gardener who is only allowed to keep the grass **below** a wooden plank placed at +5 V. The gardener simply chops off anything rising above the plank. The result: the bottom half of the hill is preserved exactly, but the top is flat-capped at exactly +5 V. The diode plays the gardener's role — it conducts the moment the voltage tries to cross the threshold, locking the output to that level.

### The Clamper → "Lifting the Whole Hill Up or Down"

Take the same rolling hill of grass and place a giant hydraulic platform beneath it. By adjusting the platform height, you can shift the **entire** hill upward so that its lowest valley is now at 0 V (no negative excursion), or downward so its highest peak is at 0 V. The *shape* of the hill is preserved perfectly — only its vertical baseline changes. The capacitor is the hydraulic platform (it stores the DC offset as a charge), and the diode is the ratchet that locks the platform in place after the first half cycle.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Value | Significance |
|:--------:|:--------------:|:------------|
| $V_\gamma$ (Diode cut-in voltage, **Silicon**) | **0.7 V** | Forward-bias knee voltage |
| $V_\gamma$ (Diode cut-in voltage, **Germanium**) | **0.3 V** | Lower knee, used in low-signal precision clippers |
| $V_T$ (Thermal voltage at 300 K) | **≈ 25.85 mV** | Used in Shockley equation: $I = I_S (e^{V/V_T} - 1)$ |
| $\tau = RC$ (Clamper time constant) | **≥ 10 T** | Essential condition for proper clamping action |

## 1.6 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of Clippers & Clampers:**
> - **TV receivers:** Sync-pulse separation uses clipper circuits to strip off picture information and retain only the synchronizing pulses.
> - **Radar systems:** Clampers restore the DC baseline of AC-coupled pulse trains.
> - **Oscilloscope input protection:** Zener-based clippers protect sensitive front-end amplifiers.
> - **AM demodulation (envelope detection):** A diode + RC network acts as a half-wave rectifier (a clipper variant).
> - **Function generators:** Comparator + clipper networks generate square/triangle waves from sine waves.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Diode as the Foundation

A **p-n junction diode** exhibits two stable states:
1. **Forward Bias (ON):** Anode voltage > Cathode voltage by at least $V_\gamma$. The diode behaves as a closed switch with a small voltage drop ($\approx 0.7$ V for Si).
2. **Reverse Bias (OFF):** Anode voltage < Cathode voltage. The diode behaves as an open switch. Only a tiny reverse saturation current $I_S$ flows.

The Shockley diode equation governs the transition region:

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{V_D / \eta V_T} - 1 \right)$$

where $\eta$ is the ideality factor ($\eta \approx 1$ to 2) and $V_T = kT/q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For all KTU exam analysis, we use the **Ideal Diode Model with Cut-in Voltage**: $V_D = 0$ V when reverse-biased, $V_D = V_\gamma$ when forward-biased. This simplifies hand calculations dramatically.

## 2.2 Clipper Circuit Analysis — Step-by-Step Logic

A clipper has **two operating intervals per input cycle**:

**Interval I — Diode OFF (open switch):**
- The diode is reverse-biased; no current flows through it.
- The output equals the input: $v_{out} = v_{in}$.
- The full input waveform appears at the output during this interval.

**Interval II — Diode ON (closed switch):**
- The diode is forward-biased and clamps the output.
- The output is fixed at the reference level: $v_{out} = V_{ref}$ (or $V_{ref} + V_\gamma$ in the practical model).

**Key Design Parameter — Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV):**
The maximum reverse voltage the diode must withstand without breakdown. For most passive clippers:

$$PIV = V_{in,peak} - V_{ref}$$

## 2.3 Classification of Clippers

| Type | Diode Position | Action |
|:-----|:--------------|:-------|
| **Series Positive Clipper** | In series, anode to input | Removes positive half cycle |
| **Series Negative Clipper** | In series, cathode to input | Removes negative half cycle |
| **Parallel Positive Clipper** | In parallel, cathode to output | Clips above $V_{ref}$ |
| **Parallel Negative Clipper** | In parallel, anode to output | Clips below $V_{ref}$ |
| **Biased Clipper** | Diode + DC bias $V_B$ | Clipping level shifts to $V_B + V_\gamma$ |
| **Double Clipper (Sliver/Slice)** | Two diodes back-to-back + two biases | Outputs a *band* of voltage |

## 2.4 Clamper Circuit Analysis — Step-by-Step Logic

A clamper has **three phases of operation**:

**Phase 1 — First Half Cycle (Capacitor Charging):**
- During the first quarter cycle when the diode conducts, the diode acts as a short (ideal) or $V_\gamma$ drop (practical).
- The capacitor charges rapidly through the diode (low forward resistance $r_f$).
- Charging time constant: $\tau_{charge} = r_f C$ — very small.

**Phase 2 — Diode OFF (Capacitor Holds Charge):**
- When the diode becomes reverse-biased, it disconnects.
- The capacitor holds its charge because the discharge path is through the large load resistor $R$.
- Discharging time constant: $\tau_{discharge} = RC$ — must be much greater than $T$.

**Phase 3 — Steady State:**
- The voltage across the capacitor settles to the peak value of the input (less the diode drop).
- This capacitor voltage acts as a DC battery in series with the input, shifting the entire waveform.

## 2.5 The Governing Equation for Clamping Level

For a **Negative Clamper** (output shifted upward, no negative excursion):

$$V_{out,min} = V_{C} + V_{in,min} - V_\gamma$$

For a **Positive Clamper** (output shifted downward, no positive excursion):

$$V_{out,max} = V_{C} + V_{in,max} + V_\gamma$$

where $V_C$ is the steady-state capacitor voltage.

## 2.6 The RC Time Constant Condition (KTU Hot Topic!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb:**
> For a clamper to function correctly, the discharge time constant of the RC network must be **at least 10 times the period of the input signal**:
> 
> $$\tau = RC \geq 10 T$$
> 
> where $T = 1/f$ is the period of the input waveform.
> 
> If $RC$ is too small, the capacitor will discharge significantly between cycles, causing a *sloped* (tilted) output — this is called a **"tilt"** in the clamped waveform.

**Quantitative Tilt Equation:**

$$\text{Tilt } (\%) = \frac{V_M \cdot T}{RC \cdot V_{in}} \times 100\%$$

The tilt must be less than ~5% for a clean clamping action.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|:-:|:------------------|:--------|:-----------------|
| 1 | Ideal diode forward drop | $V_F = 0$ V (ideal) or $V_F = 0.7$ V (Si) | Forward bias |
| 2 | Reverse current (off) | $I_D \approx 0$ | Reverse bias |
| 3 | Clipper output (biased) | $v_{out} = V_{ref}$ when diode ON | $V_{ref}$ in Volts |
| 4 | Clipper output (idle) | $v_{out} = v_{in}$ when diode OFF | Linear region |
| 5 | Clamper DC shift | $V_{shift} = V_{C,steady}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Capacitor steady voltage | $V_C = V_{in,peak} - V_\gamma$ | Volts |
| 7 | Clamper time constant | $\tau = RC$ | Seconds |
| 8 | Clamper condition | $RC \geq 10 T$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 9 | Tilt % | $Tilt = (V_M T) / (RC V_{in}) \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 10 | PIV of clipper diode | $PIV = V_{in,peak} - V_{ref}$ | Volts |
| 11 | Shockley equation | $I_D = I_S (e^{V_D/\eta V_T} - 1)$ | Amperes |
| 12 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV | at 300 K |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Snapshot:**
> - **Negative Clamper in TV Receivers:** The composite video signal has both positive-going picture information and negative-going sync pulses. A negative clamper restores the DC level so sync pulses are always at the same reference (0 V), making them easy to extract with a simple clipper.
> - **Positive Clampers in Radar:** They prevent baseline drift in pulsed radar returns, ensuring consistent triggering thresholds.
> - **Zener Clippers in Power Supplies:** Provide over-voltage protection across sensitive IC inputs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the *proof-heavy heart* of the notes. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly with the underlying physical reasoning.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Biased Positive Clipper (Output Voltage Equations)

**Given:** A biased positive clipper with bias voltage $V_B = +5$ V, input $v_{in}(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t)$ V, Si diode ($V_\gamma = 0.7$ V).

**Step 1 — Identify the diode state in each half cycle.**

*Conversion logic:* The diode conducts only when its anode-cathode voltage exceeds $V_\gamma$. In a biased positive clipper (diode cathode tied to $V_B$, anode via resistor to output), the diode conducts when $v_{in} > V_B + V_\gamma$.

For our case:

$$v_{in} > V_B + V_\gamma \;\Longrightarrow\; v_{in} > 5 + 0.7 = 5.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Write the output expression for Diode OFF state ($v_{in} \leq 5.7$ V).**

*Conversion logic:* When the diode is reverse-biased, no current flows through the series resistor; thus no voltage drop occurs across it. The output equals the input directly.

$$v_{out} = v_{in} \quad \text{for } v_{in} \leq V_B + V_\gamma$$

**Step 3 — Write the output expression for Diode ON state ($v_{in} > 5.7$ V).**

*Conversion logic:* When the diode conducts, it acts as a 0.7 V battery. The output node is clamped at the bias level plus the diode drop.

$$v_{out} = V_B + V_\gamma = 5 + 0.7 = 5.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the final piecewise transfer characteristic.**

$$v_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} 10 \sin(\omega t) & \text{for } 10 \sin(\omega t) \leq 5.7 \text{ V} \\ 5.7 \text{ V} & \text{for } 10 \sin(\omega t) > 5.7 \text{ V} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Find the clipping angle (where the sine wave equals 5.7 V).**

*Conversion logic:* Solve $10 \sin(\theta) = 5.7$ for the angle where clipping begins.

$$\sin(\theta_1) = \frac{5.7}{10} = 0.57$$

$$\theta_1 = \sin^{-1}(0.57) = 34.75^\circ$$

The clipping ends at the symmetric angle in the next half:

$$\theta_2 = 180^\circ - 34.75^\circ = 145.25^\circ$$

**Result:** The positive peak is flat-capped at 5.7 V from $\theta_1 = 34.75^\circ$ to $\theta_2 = 145.25^\circ$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Negative Clamper (DC Shift Calculation)

**Given:** Input $v_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ with $V_m = 20$ V, Si diode, no external bias.

**Step 1 — Analyze the first negative half cycle.**

*Conversion logic:* During the first negative half cycle, the diode becomes forward-biased (anode more negative, cathode at ground — wait, for negative clamper, the diode is oriented so that it conducts during the *negative* half cycle).

For a standard negative clamper (diode cathode to ground via resistor, anode to capacitor):

When $v_{in} < 0$ (negative half cycle), the diode forward-biases, current rushes through it, and the capacitor charges quickly.

$$V_C = V_{in,peak} - V_\gamma = 20 - 0.7 = 19.3 \text{ V}$$

The polarity: the left plate (connected to input) is at $-V_m$ and the right plate (at output) is at 0 V during the peak; hence the capacitor charges to $V_m - V_\gamma$ with the **left plate negative** and the **right plate positive**.

**Step 2 — Determine the capacitor polarity for the KVL equation.**

*Conversion logic:* In steady state, the voltage across the capacitor stays nearly constant at $V_C$ with the polarity established in Step 1. The output voltage is the input minus the capacitor voltage (with sign conventions).

Using KVL around the loop (input → capacitor → output → ground → input):

$$v_{in}(t) = v_C + v_{out}(t)$$

For a negative clamper, the capacitor is oriented such that:

$$v_{out}(t) = v_{in}(t) + V_C$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula across the full cycle.**

At $t = T/4$ (positive peak, $v_{in} = +V_m$):

$$v_{out}(T/4) = V_m + V_C = 20 + 19.3 = 39.3 \text{ V}$$

At $t = 3T/4$ (negative peak, $v_{in} = -V_m$):

$$v_{out}(3T/4) = -V_m + V_C = -20 + 19.3 = -0.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the shift.**

*Conversion logic:* The minimum output should be exactly $-V_\gamma = -0.7$ V (the diode drop), confirming the entire waveform has been shifted up by $V_m - V_\gamma$.

$$V_{shift} = V_m - V_\gamma = 20 - 0.7 = 19.3 \text{ V}$$

**Result:** The sine wave originally oscillating between $-20$ V and $+20$ V now oscillates between $-0.7$ V and $+39.3$ V. The peak-to-peak amplitude is still 40 V (unchanged shape), but the DC baseline has been shifted up by 19.3 V.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tilt in Clamper (Quantitative Analysis)

**Given:** Clamper circuit with $R = 100$ k$\Omega$, $C = 1$ $\mu$F, input frequency $f = 1$ kHz, peak input $V_m = 10$ V.

**Step 1 — Compute the period of the input.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms} = 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time constant.**

*Conversion logic:* The time constant $\tau = RC$ is the product of resistance and capacitance; its unit is seconds.

$$\tau = R \cdot C = 100 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.1 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Verify the clamping condition.**

*Conversion logic:* The condition $RC \geq 10 T$ ensures the capacitor holds >99% of its charge for 99.3% of the cycle.

$$\frac{RC}{T} = \frac{0.1}{0.001} = 100 \geq 10 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Compute the tilt in volts.**

*Conversion logic:* The tilt is the small amount by which the capacitor discharges during the diode-OFF period. Using the discharge formula for a capacitor:

$$\Delta V = V_C \left( 1 - e^{-T/RC} \right) \approx V_C \cdot \frac{T}{RC} \text{ (for } T \ll RC\text{)}$$

$$\Delta V = 9.3 \times \frac{10^{-3}}{0.1} = 0.093 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Express as a percentage tilt.**

$$\text{Tilt } (\%) = \frac{0.093}{10} \times 100 = 0.93\%$$

**Result:** A 0.93% tilt is well within the acceptable range (<5%) — the clamping action is clean and effective.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Solution Map

| Circuit Type | Diode ON Condition | Output During ON | Output During OFF | DC Shift | $V_C$ Steady State |
|:-------------|:------------------|:-----------------|:------------------|:---------|:-------------------|
| Series Positive Clipper | $v_{in} > 0$ | $v_{out} = V_\gamma$ | $v_{out} = v_{in}$ | None | N/A |
| Series Negative Clipper | $v_{in} < 0$ | $v_{out} = -V_\gamma$ | $v_{out} = v_{in}$ | None | N/A |
