| Parallel Positive Clipper | $v_{in} > V_B$ | $v_{out} = V_B + V_\gamma$ | $v_{out} = v_{in}$ | None | N/A |
| Negative Clamper | First $v_{in} < 0$ | $v_{out} = -V_\gamma$ | $v_{out} = v_{in} + V_C$ | $+V_m - V_\gamma$ | $V_m - V_\gamma$ |
| Positive Clamper | First $v_{in} > 0$ | $v_{out} = +V_\gamma$ | $v_{out} = v_{in} - V_C$ | $-(V_m - V_\gamma)$ | $V_m - V_\gamma$ |
| Biased Negative Clamper | First $v_{in} < V_B$ | $v_{out} = V_B + V_\gamma$ | $v_{out} = v_{in} + V_C$ | Depends on $V_B$ | $V_m - V_B - V_\gamma$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid block diagrams illustrate the functional architecture, classification, and waveform-flow relationships of diode-based clipping and clamping circuits. They serve as block-level functional substitutes for hand-drawn circuit schematics.

## 4.1 Master Classification of Wave-Shaping Circuits

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootNode[Wave Shaping Circuits using Diodes]
    rootNode --> clipperNode[Clipper Limiter]
    rootNode --> clamperNode[Clamper DC Restorer]

    clipperNode --> seriesNode[Series Clipper]
    clipperNode --> parallelNode[Parallel Clipper]
    clipperNode --> biasedNode[Biased Clipper]
    clipperNode --> doubleNode[Double Diode Clipper]

    seriesNode --> serPos[Series Positive]
    seriesNode --> serNeg[Series Negative]
    parallelNode --> parPos[Parallel Positive]
    parallelNode --> parNeg[Parallel Negative]
    biasedNode --> biPos[Biased Positive]
    biasedNode --> biNeg[Biased Negative]
    doubleNode --> sliverNode[Sliver Circuit]

    clamperNode --> posClamp[Positive Clamper]
    clamperNode --> negClamp[Negative Clamper]
    clamperNode --> biClamp[Biased Clamper]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of a Clipper Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inVin[Input Sine Wave v_in t]
    inVin --> biasNode{Diode State Decision}
    biasNode --> offState[Diode OFF Reverse Bias]
    biasNode --> onState[Diode ON Forward Bias]
    offState --> outPassthrough[v_out equals v_in Linear Pass]
    onState --> outClamp[v_out equals V_ref plus V_gamma Clamped Flat]
    outPassthrough --> outTerminal[Final Output Waveform with Clipped Region]
    outClamp --> outTerminal
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow of a Clamper Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    acIn[AC Input Sine Wave]
    acIn --> phase1[Phase 1: Diode Conducts Capacitor Charges Rapidly]
    phase1 --> phase2[Phase 2: Diode OFF Capacitor Holds Charge]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3: Steady State DC Offset Established]
    phase3 --> shiftedOut[Output: Same Shape, New DC Level]
    shiftedOut --> upShift[Negative Clamper: Shifts Upward]
    shiftedOut --> downShift[Positive Clamper: Shifts Downward]
```

## 4.4 Ideal Transfer Characteristic of a Biased Positive Clipper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Region 1: v_in less than V_ref]
    p2[Region 2: v_in greater than V_ref]
    p1 --> q1[Output follows Input linearly slope 1]
    p2 --> q2[Output constant at V_ref Flat Clipped Region]
    q1 --> r1[Slope 1 line in V_out vs V_in plot]
    q2 --> r2[Horizontal line at V_out equals V_ref]
```

## 4.5 Clamper Steady-State Waveform Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inWave[Input: Sine from minus V_m to plus V_m]
    inWave --> clamperBlock[Negative Clamper Circuit Diode plus Capacitor plus R]
    clamperBlock --> outWave[Output: Sine from minus V_gamma to plus 2V_m minus V_gamma]
    outWave --> annot[Same peak to peak amplitude, DC baseline shifted up by V_m minus V_gamma]
```

## 4.6 Decision Logic for Clipper vs. Clamper Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Need to Modify a Waveform]
    startNode --> q1{Goal: Remove a portion OR Shift the baseline?}
    q1 -->|Remove portion| clipperPath[Use Clipper]
    q1 -->|Shift baseline| clamperPath[Use Clamper]
    clipperPath --> cq1{Clip above or below a level?}
    cq1 -->|Above| biPosClip[Biased Positive Clipper]
    cq1 -->|Below| biNegClip[Biased Negative Clipper]
    clamperPath --> clq1{Make waveform positive-only or negative-only?}
    clq1 -->|Positive only| negClampOut[Negative Clamper]
    clq1 -->|Negative only| posClampOut[Positive Clamper]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Lab Viva:**
> Memorize the three-phase clamper cycle (charge → hold → discharge) and the two-interval clipper behavior (ON clip / OFF pass). Examiners often ask: *"What happens in the first half cycle vs. the steady state?"* — these flowcharts give you the answer template.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Apr 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### Q1. Define a clipper circuit. List any four types of clippers.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **clipper circuit** is a wave-shaping circuit that limits the amplitude of an input signal by removing (clipping) the portion of the waveform that exceeds a specified reference voltage level, while passing the remainder of the signal unchanged. It uses the non-linear V-I characteristic of a diode along with a resistor.

**Four types of clippers:**
1. **Series Positive Clipper** — removes the positive half cycle.
2. **Series Negative Clipper** — removes the negative half cycle.
3. **Parallel (Shunt) Positive Clipper** — clips the signal above a reference level.
4. **Parallel (Shunt) Negative Clipper** — clips the signal below a reference level.

(Biased and double-diode variants are also acceptable.)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

### Q2. State and explain the condition for proper clamping action in a clamper circuit.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For proper clamping action, the **discharge time constant $\tau = RC$ of the clamper circuit must be at least 10 times the period $T$ of the input signal**, i.e.,

$$RC \geq 10 T$$

This ensures that during the diode-OFF interval, the capacitor retains nearly all of its charge (loss < 1%), preventing the output from drooping. If $RC$ is too small, the output exhibits a **tilt** — a noticeable exponential decay between successive peaks — which corrupts the desired DC-restored waveform.

$$\text{Tilt } (\%) = \frac{V_M \cdot T}{RC \cdot V_{in}} \times 100\% \quad \text{(must be } < 5\%\text{)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, End Semester Pattern with Internal Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram and input-output waveforms, explain the operation of a *biased positive clipper* with a bias voltage of $V_B = +4$ V. Assume silicon diode ($V_\gamma = 0.7$ V) and a 20 V peak sine input.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:** A resistor $R$ is connected in series with the input. The diode's anode is connected to the junction of $R$ and the output, and the diode's cathode is tied to the DC bias $V_B = +4$ V. The output is taken across the diode-bias combination (parallel to the output terminals).

**Diode Conduction Condition:**

*Conversion logic:* The diode conducts when its anode voltage exceeds its cathode voltage by at least $V_\gamma$. Here, anode voltage = $v_{in}$ and cathode voltage = $V_B$. So:

$$v_{in} - V_B \geq V_\gamma \;\Longrightarrow\; v_{in} \geq V_B + V_\gamma = 4 + 0.7 = 4.7 \text{ V}$$

**Piecewise Output Equation:**

$$v_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} v_{in}(t) = 20 \sin(\omega t) & \text{for } 20 \sin(\omega t) \leq 4.7 \text{ V} \\ V_B + V_\gamma = 4.7 \text{ V} & \text{for } 20 \sin(\omega t) > 4.7 \text{ V} \end{cases}$$

**Clipping Angles:**

$$\sin(\theta_1) = \frac{4.7}{20} = 0.235 \;\Longrightarrow\; \theta_1 = 13.6^\circ$$

$$\theta_2 = 180^\circ - 13.6^\circ = 166.4^\circ$$

**Result:** The output is an undistorted sine wave from $-20$ V to $+4.7$ V, and a flat-topped line at $4.7$ V between $\theta_1 = 13.6^\circ$ and $\theta_2 = 166.4^\circ$.

**PIV of the diode:**

$$PIV = V_{in,peak} - V_B = 20 - 4 = 16 \text{ V}$$

#### (b) [7 Marks] For the circuit in part (a), calculate the **average value** of the clipped output over one full cycle.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the integration limits.**

*Conversion logic:* The output is a sine wave from $-\pi$ to $\theta_1$ and from $\theta_2$ to $\pi$ (in radians), and a constant $4.7$ V between $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

Convert angles to radians: $\theta_1 = 13.6^\circ \times \pi/180 = 0.237$ rad, $\theta_2 = 166.4^\circ \times \pi/180 = 2.904$ rad.

**Step 2 — Set up the average-value integral.**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{-\pi}^{\theta_1} 20 \sin(\theta) \, d\theta + \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} 4.7 \, d\theta + \int_{\theta_2}^{\pi} 20 \sin(\theta) \, d\theta \right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral.**

$$\int_{-\pi}^{0.237} 20 \sin(\theta) \, d\theta = 20 \left[ -\cos(\theta) \right]_{-\pi}^{0.237} = 20 \left( -\cos(0.237) + \cos(-\pi) \right)$$

$$= 20 \left( -0.972 + (-1) \right) = 20 \times (-1.972) = -39.44$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second integral (the clipped region).**

$$\int_{0.237}^{2.904} 4.7 \, d\theta = 4.7 \times (2.904 - 0.237) = 4.7 \times 2.667 = 12.53$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the third integral.**

$$\int_{2.904}^{\pi} 20 \sin(\theta) \, d\theta = 20 \left[ -\cos(\theta) \right]_{2.904}^{\pi} = 20 \left( -\cos(\pi) + \cos(2.904) \right)$$

$$= 20 \left( -(-1) + (-0.972) \right) = 20 \times (1 - 0.972) = 20 \times 0.028 = 0.56$$

**Step 6 — Sum and divide by $2\pi$.**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{-39.44 + 12.53 + 0.56}{2\pi} = \frac{-26.35}{6.283} = -4.19 \text{ V}$$

**Result:** The DC (average) value of the clipped waveform is approximately $-4.19$ V.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, input and output waveforms, explain the operation of a *negative clamper* with $V_B = +5$ V bias. Assume input is 20 V peak sine wave, $R = 100$ k$\Omega$, $C = 1$ $\mu$F, Si diode.

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:** A capacitor $C$ is in series between input and output. A diode (cathode to output, anode to ground via $V_B$ bias) and a parallel resistor $R$ complete the network.

**Step 1 — Verify the clamping condition.**

*Conversion logic:* Compute period and time constant.

$$f = 1 \text{ kHz} \;\Longrightarrow\; T = 1 \text{ ms}$$

$$RC = 100 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.1 \text{ s}$$

$$RC/T = 0.1 / 0.001 = 100 \geq 10 \quad \checkmark \text{ (proper clamping)}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the steady-state capacitor voltage.**

*Conversion logic:* During the first negative half cycle, the diode conducts, and the capacitor charges to the difference between the input peak and the bias and diode drop.

$$V_C = V_{in,peak} - V_B - V_\gamma = 20 - 5 - 0.7 = 14.3 \text{ V}$$

The capacitor is charged with the **left plate negative** and **right plate positive**.

**Step 3 — Write the output equation.**

*Conversion logic:* Once steady state is reached, the capacitor acts as a DC battery. The output is the input shifted by the capacitor voltage minus the bias.

$$v_{out}(t) = v_{in}(t) + V_C + V_B = 20 \sin(\omega t) + 14.3 + 5 = 20 \sin(\omega t) + 19.3 \text{ V}$$

Wait — careful: for a biased clamper, the shift includes both $V_C$ and the influence of $V_B$ on the conduction threshold. Re-deriving:

$$V_{out,min} = V_B + V_\gamma = 5 + 0.7 = 5.7 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Determine peak values.**

$$V_{out,max} = V_{in,peak} + V_C = 20 + 14.3 = 34.3 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{out,min} = V_B + V_\gamma = 5.7 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{pp} = 34.3 - 5.7 = 28.6 \text{ V} \quad \text{(matches } 2 V_m - 2 V_B - 2 V_\gamma = 40 - 10 - 1.4 = 28.6 \text{ V)} \quad \checkmark$$

**Result:** The 20 V peak sine wave (originally between $-20$ V and $+20$ V) is shifted to oscillate between $+5.7$ V and $+34.3$ V. The minimum is fixed at $V_B + V_\gamma = 5.7$ V, and the DC shift is $V_C + V_B - V_\gamma = 14.3 + 5 - 0.7$ — actually the net shift is $+25.7$ V (so the new minimum is at $+5.7$ V).

#### (b) [7 Marks] Calculate the **tilt** in the clamped output and comment on whether the clamping is satisfactory.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the tilt formula.**

*Conversion logic:* Tilt is the percentage drop in capacitor voltage over one full discharge period.

$$\Delta V = V_C \cdot \frac{T}{RC} = 14.3 \times \frac{10^{-3}}{0.1} = 0.143 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Express as a percentage of peak input.**

$$\text{Tilt } (\%) = \frac{\Delta V}{V_{in,peak}} \times 100 = \frac{0.143}{20} \times 100 = 0.715\%$$

**Step 3 — Comment on acceptability.**

*Conversion logic:* A tilt of less than 5% is considered acceptable for clean clamping action.

$$0.715\% < 5\% \quad \therefore \text{Clamping is SATISFACTORY.}$$

The output waveform will be virtually flat between successive negative peaks, with negligible droop.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read This Before Your Exam!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the diode cut-in voltage $V_\gamma$:** Many students assume an ideal diode ($V_F = 0$ V). The KTU answer key often awards the $V_\gamma = 0.7$ V step separately. Always state: *"Assuming silicon diode with $V_\gamma = 0.7$ V."*
>
> 2. **Confusing series and parallel clippers:** A series clipper has the diode *in series* with the load; a parallel (shunt) clipper has the diode *in parallel* with the output. Drawing the wrong topology = 2 to 3 marks lost immediately.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to check the $RC \geq 10T$ condition:** In clamper problems, KTU examiners specifically test whether you verified the condition. Skipping this step costs 1 to 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Wrong sign of the DC shift in clampers:** A *negative clamper shifts the waveform UP* (so that the minimum is at $-V_\gamma$, not the maximum). A *positive clamper shifts the waveform DOWN*. Mixing these up is the most common conceptual error.
>
> 5. **Not drawing the steady-state waveform separately from the first-cycle transient:** Examiners want to see the *first cycle* (capacitor charging) and the *steady state* (after stabilization). Drawing only one is a 1-mark penalty.
>
> 6. **Clipping-angle calculation error:** When asked for the angle at which clipping begins, students often write $90^\circ$ by default. Always solve $V_m \sin(\theta) = V_{ref} + V_\gamma$ explicitly.
>
> 7. **PIV not specified:** Every clipper question expects the Peak Inverse Voltage. Always calculate and state it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **clipper** *removes* a portion of the input waveform above or below a reference level; a **clamper** *shifts* the entire waveform up or down without changing its shape.
- The diode is the controlling element: it acts as a **closed switch** ($V_F = V_\gamma$) when forward-biased and an **open switch** ($I = 0$) when reverse-biased.
- A **series clipper** places the diode in the signal path; a **parallel clipper** places the diode across the output.
- A **positive clipper** removes the positive half cycle (or portion above a positive reference); a **negative clipper** removes the negative half cycle.
- A **biased clipper** uses an external DC source $V_B$ to shift the clipping threshold to $V_B + V_\gamma$.
- A **negative clamper** shifts the waveform **upward** by $V_m - V_\gamma$ so the minimum is at $-V_\gamma$ (or $V_B + V_\gamma$ if biased).
- A **positive clamper** shifts the waveform **downward** by $V_m - V_\gamma$ so the maximum is at $+V_\gamma$ (or $V_B - V_\gamma$ if biased).
- The **clamping condition** is $RC \geq 10T$, where $T$ is the input period. Violating this causes a visible **tilt** in the output.
- **Tilt formula:** $\text{Tilt }(\%) = (V_C \cdot T)/(RC \cdot V_{in}) \times 100$; must be $< 5\%$.
- **PIV of clipper diode:** $PIV = V_{in,peak} - V_{ref}$ (always state this in your answer).
- The **steady-state capacitor voltage** in a clamper is $V_C = V_{in,peak} - V_\gamma$ (or $V_m - V_B - V_\gamma$ for biased clampers).
- For a **clipped waveform**, the average (DC) value is computed by integrating the piecewise output over one full cycle and dividing by $2\pi$.
- The **peak-to-peak amplitude** of a clamped waveform is **preserved** ($= 2V_m$ for unbiased, reduced by $2V_B$ for biased clampers).
- **Thermal voltage** at 300 K is $V_T \approx 25.85$ mV — used in the Shockley equation for precise diode analysis.
- For KTU lab work, **always sketch** the input waveform, the clipped/clamped output, the diode current waveform, and the capacitor voltage waveform separately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra (Mnemonic — "C-R-A-M-P"):**
> **C** — Capacitor stores the DC shift in a clamper.
> **R** — Resistor sets the discharge time constant ($RC \geq 10T$).
> **A** — Always include $V_\gamma = 0.7$ V for silicon diodes.
> **M** — Mark the clipping angle by solving $V_m \sin(\theta) = V_{ref} + V_\gamma$.
> **P** — PIV must be calculated and stated for every clipper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC coupled amplifier using BJT in CE Configuration - Measurement of gain, BW and plotting of frequency response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RC coupled amplifier using BJT in CE Configuration - Measurement of gain, BW and plotting of frequency response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **RC Coupled Amplifier** is a multi-stage amplifier circuit in which a resistor-capacitor (RC) network is used to couple the output of one amplifying stage to the input of the next stage. When this topology is implemented using a **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** biased in the **Common Emitter (CE) configuration**, the circuit is referred to as an *RC coupled CE amplifier*. It is the most widely used small-signal audio-frequency amplifier due to its excellent frequency response, low cost, and high gain per stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCERL407 - Module 2):**
> The experiment focuses on three measurable outcomes:
> 1. **Mid-band Voltage Gain** ($A_{V(mid)}$)
> 2. **Bandwidth (BW)** of the amplifier
> 3. **Frequency Response Curve** plotted on a semi-logarithmic graph between gain (dB) and frequency (Hz)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Bucket Brigade of Sound"**
> Imagine a row of people standing in a line, each holding an empty bucket. A whispered message (small input signal) reaches the first person, who shouts it louder into the next person's bucket, and so on. By the end of the line, the message has become a roar. The **BJT in CE** is the "shouter," the **RC coupling network** is the "bucket" that passes the amplified shout to the next stage while blocking any DC bias (so each person doesn't get "burned" by the previous person's emotional/DC state). The **frequency response** tells us *how well* this shouting works for bass voices (low frequencies) versus soprano voices (high frequencies).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Standard test input signal | **1 kHz sine wave, ~10–50 mV (rms)** |
| Standard supply voltage ($V_{CC}$) | **+12 V DC** |
| Mid-band frequency range | **1 kHz to 50 kHz (typically)** |
| Bandwidth (BW) | **$f_H - f_L$** in **Hz** |
| Gain expression | Expressed in **dB**: $A_V(dB) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_V\vert$ |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU lab evaluations, always state the **unit of gain** explicitly — examiners will deduct marks if you write "gain = 100" without clarifying whether it is **linear (unitless)** or **40 dB**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology and Working Principle

The single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier consists of the following sub-blocks:

1. **Biasing Network** — A potential divider ($R_1$, $R_2$) and emitter resistor $R_E$ to fix the Q-point (operating point) of the BJT. $R_E$ is bypassed by a large electrolytic capacitor $C_E$ to prevent AC negative feedback.
2. **Input Coupling Capacitor ($C_{in}$)** — Blocks the DC bias of the source from reaching the base, but allows the AC signal to pass.
3. **Transistor (BJT in CE mode)** — Provides the current/voltage amplification.
4. **Output Coupling Capacitor ($C_{out}$)** — Blocks the DC collector voltage from reaching the load, but couples the amplified AC signal to the next stage or load $R_L$.
5. **Collector Resistor ($R_C$)** — Converts the collector current variations into a voltage output.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1: DC Biasing:** $V_{CC}$ is applied through $R_1$ and $R_2$, setting the base voltage $V_B$ such that the BJT operates in the **active region** (Q-point at the middle of the DC load line for maximum symmetrical swing).
- **Step 2: AC Signal Injection:** The small sinusoidal input $v_{in}$ is applied at the base through $C_{in}$. Since $C_{in}$ has very low reactance at signal frequencies, the entire AC signal reaches the base–emitter junction.
- **Step 3: Amplification:** The BJT's collector current $i_c$ becomes $\beta \cdot i_b$. This varying $i_c$ flows through $R_C$, producing a large voltage drop variation, which is the amplified output.
- **Step 4: Output Coupling:** $C_{out}$ removes the DC component ($V_C \approx V_{CC}/2$) and delivers only the amplified AC signal to the load.
- **Step 5: Frequency Limitation:**
  - At **low frequencies**, the reactance of coupling and bypass capacitors ($X_C = 1/2\pi f C$) becomes large, attenuating the signal.
  - At **high frequencies**, the internal junction capacitances ($C_{bc}$, $C_{be}$) of the BJT (combined into the **Miller capacitance**) shunt the signal to ground, again causing attenuation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Gain (CE, mid-band) | $A_V = -\dfrac{\beta \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_e + R_E^{(ac)}}$ | Negative sign = **180° phase inversion** |
| 2 | Simplified Mid-band Gain (with bypassed $R_E$) | $A_V \approx -\dfrac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e}$ | $r_e = 26\,\text{mV} / I_E$ |
| 3 | Gain in decibels | $A_V(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_V\vert$ | dB |
| 4 | Lower Cutoff Frequency | $f_L \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} + R_{in(next)}) C_{out}}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Upper Cutoff Frequency (Miller) | $f_H \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{Miller}}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_L \ll f_H$) | Hz |
| 7 | Transistor Input Resistance | $r_\pi = \beta \cdot r_e$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Emitter Dynamic Resistance | $r_e = \dfrac{26\,\text{mV}}{I_E(\text{mA})}$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** RC coupled amplifiers form the **front-end audio block** of nearly every consumer electronic device — radios, televisions, mobile phones, hearing aids, and public-address systems — because of their flat response over the human audible band (**20 Hz – 20 kHz**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Mid-Band Voltage Gain

In the mid-band region, all coupling and bypass capacitors behave as **AC short circuits**, and internal transistor capacitances behave as **AC open circuits**. The small-signal AC equivalent circuit of the CE stage reduces to a simple transconductance model.

**Step 1:** The input voltage at the base is
$$v_{be} = v_{in}$$

**Step 2:** The collector AC current is controlled by the transconductance $g_m$ of the BJT:
$$i_c = g_m \cdot v_{be}$$

**Step 3:** The output voltage developed across the effective load $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$ is
$$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $i_c$ from Step 2 into Step 3:
$$v_{out} = -g_m \cdot v_{in} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the voltage gain becomes
$$A_V = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $g_m = \beta / r_\pi = 1/r_e$ (for $r_e \ll r_\pi$):
$$A_V = -\frac{(R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_e}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Style)

**Given:** A single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier uses a BC547 BJT with $\beta = 100$, $I_E = 2\,\text{mA}$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, and $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1: Compute dynamic emitter resistance**
$$r_e = \frac{26\,\text{mV}}{I_E(\text{mA})} = \frac{26}{2} = 13\,\Omega$$

**Step 2: Compute effective AC load**
$$R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10}\,\text{k}\Omega$$
$$\begin{aligned}
R_{ac} &= \frac{47}{14.7}\,\text{k}\Omega \\
       &= 3.197\,\text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Compute linear voltage gain**
$$A_V = -\frac{R_{ac}}{r_e} = -\frac{3197}{13} = -245.9$$

**Step 4: Convert to dB**
$$A_V(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert -245.9 \vert = 20 \times 2.3907 = 47.81\,\text{dB}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Negative sign** in $A_V$ indicates a **180° phase shift** between input and output. In the KTU answer script, you may report either the **magnitude** $\vert A_V \vert$ or include the negative sign with a note about phase inversion.

### 3.3 Derivation of Cutoff Frequencies and Bandwidth

The frequency response of an RC coupled amplifier is governed by **two dominant poles**:

**Lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$):** Dominated by the output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$ seeing the series combination of $R_C$ and $R_L$ (or the source resistance $R_S$ and $R_{in}$ for $C_{in}$). The general form is
$$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi \, (R_{source} + R_{load}) \, C_{coupling}}$$

**Upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$):** Dominated by the **Miller effect**, where the feedback capacitance $C_{bc}$ appears at the input as
$$C_{Miller} = C_{bc} \cdot (1 + \vert A_V\vert)$$

This huge effective capacitance at the base node gives
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \, R_{eq} \, C_{Miller}}$$

**Bandwidth:**
$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H \quad \text{(since } f_L \ll f_H\text{)}$$

### 3.4 Standard Frequency Response Plot (Bode Plot)

| Region | Frequency Range | Behaviour | Slope |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-frequency** | $f < f_L$ | Gain rolls off; coupling caps dominate | **+20 dB/decade** rise |
| **Mid-band** | $f_L \le f \le f_H$ | Gain is constant and maximum | **0 dB/decade** (flat) |
| **High-frequency** | $f > f_H$ | Gain rolls off; Miller capacitance dominates | **−20 dB/decade** fall |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU lab record, the frequency response is plotted on **semi-log paper** with **frequency (Hz) on the log scale (x-axis)** and **gain (dB) on the linear scale (y-axis)**. The two corner points are $f_L$ at **−3 dB** below mid-band gain and $f_H$ at **−3 dB** below mid-band gain. The **−3 dB point** is the universally accepted cutoff definition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Single-Stage RC Coupled CE Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Input Signal Source vs] --> nodeB[Input Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Bias Network R1 R2]
    nodeC --> nodeD[BJT in CE Configuration Q1]
    nodeE[Emitter Bypass Capacitor CE] -.-> nodeD
    nodeF[DC Supply VCC 12V] --> nodeG[Collector Resistor RC]
    nodeG --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeH[Output Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Load Resistor RL]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Output Signal Vout]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Functional Map)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT_STAGE [Input Coupling Stage]
        src1[AC Source Vs] --> cin1[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    end

    subgraph BIAS_STAGE [DC Biasing Network]
        vcc1[+12V VCC] --> r1[R1 Base Resistor]
        r1 --> q1base[Base of Q1 BJT]
        vcc1 --> r2[R2 Base Resistor]
        r2 --> gnd1[Ground]
        vcc1 --> rc1[Collector Resistor RC]
        rc1 --> q1coll[Collector of Q1]
        vcc1 --> re1[Emitter Resistor RE]
        re1 --> q1emit[Emitter of Q1]
        q1emit --> gnd1
        ce1[Bypass Capacitor CE] -.-> re1
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_STAGE [Output Coupling Stage]
        q1coll --> cout1[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
        cout1 --> rl1[Load Resistor RL]
        rl1 --> gnd1
        rl1 --> vout1[Amplified Output Vout]
    end
```

### 4.3 Frequency Response Characteristic (Bode Plot — Idealized)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pA[Gain dB 0 dB] --> pB[Mid band Flat region +20 to +50 dB]
    pB --> pC[Corner at fL 3 dB drop]
    pC --> pD[Low frequency roll off +20 dB per decade]
    pB --> pE[Corner at fH 3 dB drop]
    pE --> pF[High frequency roll off minus 20 dB per decade]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Mapping Note:** The above diagrams represent the **functional flow** of signal and DC power, since Mermaid cannot directly render a transistor's physical pin-out. In the KTU lab record, you must paste the **actual circuit diagram** drawn using standard BJT symbol conventions (circle with arrow on emitter, NPN type for BC547/2N2222).

### 4.4 Pin Diagram Reference (BC547 NPN BJT)

| Pin No. | Label | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Emitter | Current leaves; connected to $R_E$ (bypassed) |
| 2 | Base | Signal input via $C_{in}$; bias via $R_1, R_2$ |
| 3 | Collector | Output taken via $R_C$ and $C_{out}$ |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 1]**
*Define the term "bandwidth" of an RC coupled amplifier. Why is it an important parameter in audio-frequency amplifier design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bandwidth (BW) of an RC coupled amplifier is defined as the range of frequencies over which the amplifier delivers at least **70.7% (−3 dB)** of the maximum mid-band gain. Mathematically,
$$BW = f_H - f_L$$
where $f_H$ is the upper cutoff frequency (limited by the Miller capacitance of the BJT) and $f_L$ is the lower cutoff frequency (limited by the coupling and bypass capacitors).
**Importance (1 mark):** In audio applications, the amplifier must faithfully reproduce signals in the human audible range (20 Hz – 20 kHz). If the BW is narrower than this range, low-bass or high-treble components get attenuated, degrading sound quality.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
*Why is the emitter resistor $R_E$ in a CE amplifier bypassed by a capacitor $C_E$? What happens if $C_E$ is removed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$R_E$ is used for **DC bias stabilization** by providing negative feedback for the DC operating point. However, $R_E$ also introduces AC negative feedback, which drastically reduces the AC voltage gain.
To eliminate this, a large electrolytic capacitor $C_E$ is connected in **parallel** with $R_E$. At signal frequencies, $C_E$ acts as an **AC short circuit** across $R_E$, removing the AC feedback and restoring the high gain.
**Consequence of removal (1 mark):** If $C_E$ is removed (open), the gain drops to
$$A_V = -\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e + R_E} \approx -\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{R_E}$$
which is typically less than **1**, making the amplifier practically useless for voltage amplification.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier using an NPN BJT. Identify the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the standard single-stage circuit showing $V_{CC}$, biasing resistors $R_1, R_2$, emitter resistor $R_E$ (bypassed by $C_E$), collector resistor $R_C$, coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$, BJT Q1 (NPN, e.g., BC547), and load $R_L$.

2. **Component Functions (3 marks):**
   - $R_1, R_2$: Potential divider to fix base DC bias voltage.
   - $R_E$: Stabilizes the Q-point against temperature variations.
   - $C_E$: Bypasses $R_E$ for AC to maintain high gain.
   - $R_C$: Converts collector current variation into output voltage.
   - $C_{in}, C_{out}$: Block DC and pass AC signal between stages.
   - Q1: Active device providing current gain $\beta$.

3. **Working (2 marks):** The AC input modulates the base current, which is amplified by $\beta$ at the collector. The amplified voltage appears across $R_C$, is decoupled by $C_{out}$, and delivered to $R_L$ as a 180° phase-inverted, enlarged version of the input.

**(b)** Derive the expression for the mid-band voltage gain. A CE amplifier has $R_C = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 150$, and $I_E = 1.5\,\text{mA}$. Calculate the voltage gain in dB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3 marks):**
In mid-band, all capacitors are AC short. The small-signal model yields:
$$A_V = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e}$$
where $r_e = 26\,\text{mV} / I_E$.

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Compute $r_e$
$$r_e = \frac{26}{1.5} = 17.33\,\Omega$$

Step 2: Compute effective load
$$R_{ac} = \frac{2.2 \times 4.7}{2.2 + 4.7} = \frac{10.34}{6.9} = 1.498\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Step 3: Compute linear gain
$$A_V = -\frac{1498}{17.33} = -86.44$$

Step 4: Convert to dB
$$A_V(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(86.44) = 20 \times 1.9368 = 38.74\,\text{dB}$$

**Final Answer: $|A_V| = 86.44$ (linear), $A_V = 38.74$ dB**

---

**Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Cycle 2 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the frequency response of an RC coupled amplifier. Sketch the response and mark the cutoff frequencies, mid-band gain, and bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** The frequency response is a plot of gain (dB) versus frequency (Hz) on a semi-log scale, showing how the amplifier behaves across the audio spectrum.

2. **Three Regions (3 marks):**
   - **Low-frequency region** ($f < f_L$): Gain falls at +20 dB/decade due to high reactance of $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$.
   - **Mid-band region** ($f_L \le f \le f_H$): Gain is constant and maximum ($A_{V\text{ mid}}$). All coupling caps are short; junction caps are open.
   - **High-frequency region** ($f > f_H$): Gain falls at −20 dB/decade due to the Miller effect of $C_{bc}$.

3. **Sketch and Labels (2 marks):** Plot the ideal trapezoidal Bode curve; mark $f_L$ and $f_H$ at −3 dB points; indicate mid-band gain $A_{V\text{ mid}}$ (in dB) on the y-axis; bandwidth $BW = f_H - f_L$ on the x-axis.

**(b)** An RC coupled amplifier has $f_L = 50\,\text{Hz}$ and $f_H = 250\,\text{kHz}$. If the mid-band gain is **46 dB**, find (i) the gain in linear scale, (ii) the gain at $f_L$ and $f_H$ in dB, and (iii) the gain in linear scale at $f = 2f_H$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Linear gain (2 marks):**
$$A_V = 10^{46/20} = 10^{2.3} = 199.5 \approx 200$$

**(ii) Gain at cutoff (2 marks):**
At $f_L$ and $f_H$, the gain drops by 3 dB from the mid-band value:
$$A_V(f_L) = A_V(f_H) = 46 - 3 = 43\,\text{dB}$$
$$\text{Linear value} = 10^{43/20} = 141.25$$

**(iii) Gain at $2 f_H$ (3 marks):**
Above $f_H$, gain rolls off at −20 dB/decade. One octave above $f_H$ means the gain drops by an additional **6 dB** (since 20 dB/decade × log₁₀ 2 = 6.02 dB).
$$A_V(2 f_H) = 43 - 6 = 37\,\text{dB}$$
$$\text{Linear value} = 10^{37/20} = 70.79$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the −3 dB offset at cutoff:** Many students incorrectly report the gain at $f_L$ and $f_H$ as equal to the mid-band gain. The cutoff is *defined* as the −3 dB point — **−1 mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Confusing dB and linear values:** Always specify units. A gain of "100" is **40 dB**, not 100 dB.
> 3. **Ignoring the phase inversion:** The negative sign in $A_V = -R_C/r_e$ indicates **180° phase shift**, not a negative magnitude. Magnitude is $\vert A_V \vert$.
> 4. **Miller capacitance miscalculation:** $C_{Miller} = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_V\vert)$, **not** $C_{bc} \cdot \vert A_V\vert$. The "+1" is often dropped — **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Not showing the −20 dB/decade slope:** In the Bode plot, the high-frequency roll-off must explicitly show a slope of **−20 dB per decade**, not a generic downward line.
> 6. **Mixing up $f_L$ and $f_H$ formulas:** $f_L$ is governed by **large** coupling capacitors, $f_H$ by **small** internal capacitances. Wrong assignment leads to nonsensical bandwidth.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **RC coupled CE amplifier** uses an RC network to cascade amplifying stages; coupling caps block DC and pass AC.
- **Common Emitter (CE)** provides **high voltage gain** with **180° phase inversion** between input and output.
- **Mid-band voltage gain:** $A_V = -g_m(R_C \parallel R_L) = -(R_C \parallel R_L)/r_e$
- **Gain in dB:** $A_V(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_V\vert$
- **Dynamic emitter resistance:** $r_e = 26\,\text{mV}/I_E(\text{mA})$
- **Lower cutoff $f_L$** — limited by $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$. Gain rises at **+20 dB/decade** as frequency increases.
- **Upper cutoff $f_H$** — limited by the **Miller capacitance** $C_M = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_V\vert)$. Gain falls at **−20 dB/decade** above $f_H$.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_L \ll f_H$).
- **−3 dB point** = cutoff frequency definition; linear value is **0.707 × mid-band gain**.
- **Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$** is **essential** to maintain high AC gain.
- **Frequency response plot** uses a **semi-log** graph: log-frequency on x-axis, gain (dB) on y-axis.
- **Standard test conditions** in KTU lab: input = **1 kHz sine wave**, $V_{CC} = $ **+12 V DC**, BJT = **BC547 / 2N2222A**.
- **Phase relationship:** CE configuration **inverts** the signal; this is critical when cascading an odd number of stages (total 180° × n phase shift).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op-amp circuits - Design and set up of inverting and non-inverting amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Op-amp circuits - Design and set up of inverting and non-inverting amplifier # ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) – Formal Definition An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, DC-coupled electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and a single-ended output. In the context of analog signal conditioning, op-amps are configured using external passive components (resistors and capacitors) to realize precise mathematical operations such as amplification, integration, differentiation, summation, and filtering. The two foundational closed-loop configurations explored in this experiment are: - **Inverting Amplifier** – Output is amplified and inverted (180° phase shift) relative to the input. - **Non-Inverting Amplifier** – Output is amplified and in phase with the input. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** This experiment is mandatory under the Analog Electronics / Linear Integrated Circuits lab track. Students are expected to **design**, **set up on breadboard/PCB**, **measure**, and **verify** the theoretical gain using a function generator and CRO/DSO. ## 1.2 Symbol and Pin Configuration The standard 8-pin DIP package of the universally used **IC 741 Op-Amp** has the following pin map: | Pin No. | Function | Symbol | |---------|----------------------|--------| | 1 | Offset Null | OFF1 | | 2 | Inverting Input | V⁻ | | 3 | Non-Inverting Input | V⁺ | | 4 | V⁻ Supply (–VCC) | V– | | 5 | Offset Null | OFF2 | | 6 | Output | Vout | | 7 | V⁺ Supply (+VCC) | V+ | | 8 | NC | — | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – The "Lever and Fulcrum" Concept > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine an op-amp as a **sensitive seesaw (lever)** where the differential input voltage (V⁺ – V⁻) is the tiny push you apply, and the output voltage is the giant amplified response. - The op-amp itself has an **astronomically large internal gain** (A ≈ 2 × 10⁵ for the 741). Left uncontrolled, it would saturate the output for even a millivolt input. - By feeding back a controlled portion of the output to the inverting input (a technique called **Negative Feedback**), we trade raw amplification for **precision, linearity, and stability**. - Think of negative feedback like a thermostat: when the room gets too hot, it slightly reduces the heater output to maintain the exact desired temperature. --- ## 1.4 Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions (Foundation for All Derivations) | Parameter | Ideal Value | Practical 741 Value | |-----------|-------------|---------------------| | Open-Loop Gain (A) | ∞ | 2 × 10⁵ | | Input Impedance (Zin) | ∞ | 2 MΩ | | Output Impedance (Zout) | 0 | 75 Ω | | Bandwidth (BW) | ∞ | 1 MHz | | Input Offset Voltage | 0 | 2 mV (typ) | | Slew Rate | ∞ | 0.5 V/μs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Golden Two Rules" of an Ideal Op-Amp:** > 1. **Rule 1 (Virtual Short):** V⁺ = V⁻ (because A is infinite, the differential input is forced to zero). > 2. **Rule 2 (Virtual Open):** I⁺ = I⁻ = 0 (because input impedance is infinite, no current flows into the input terminals). # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Concept of Virtual Ground and Virtual Short **Virtual Short:** Because the open-loop gain A is practically infinite, any finite output voltage would require an infinitesimally small differential input. Hence the two input terminals are at the **same potential**, even though they are not physically connected. **Virtual Ground:** In the inverting amplifier configuration, the non-inverting input is tied to ground (0 V). By the virtual short principle, the inverting input is also at 0 V potential—but it is *not* physically grounded. This node is said to be at "virtual ground." > [!NOTE] > **Why is this important?** Virtual ground simplifies nodal analysis dramatically. We can apply KCL at the inverting node as if it were physically grounded, leading to quick derivations. ## 2.2 The Negative Feedback Principle Negative feedback is the cornerstone of all practical op-amp circuits. It works by: 1. Sampling a fraction β of the output voltage. 2. Subtracting this from the input at the inverting terminal. 3. The error signal (V⁺ – V⁻) is multiplied by A, the open-loop gain. 4. The result is the new output. The **closed-loop gain** is given by the canonical feedback equation: $$A_{CL} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ For A → ∞, this simplifies to the **Golden Result**: $$A_{CL} \approx \frac{1}{\beta}$$ This means the closed-loop gain depends **only on the external passive components** (the feedback network β) and is independent of the op-amp's intrinsic parameters. This is what makes op-amp circuits **predictable and accurate**. ## 2.3 Configuration Topologies at a Glance ### A. Inverting Amplifier Topology - Input signal applied to the **inverting terminal (V⁻)** via resistor R1. - Feedback resistor Rf connects output to the inverting terminal. - Non-inverting terminal (V⁺) is grounded. ### B. Non-Inverting Amplifier Topology - Input signal applied directly to the **non-inverting terminal (V⁺)**. - A resistor R1 connects the inverting terminal to ground. - Feedback resistor Rf connects output back to the inverting terminal. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always use \vert or \mid inside tables for absolute value to prevent markdown syntax breakage.** | Parameter | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier | |-----------|---------------------|--------------------------| | Closed-Loop Voltage Gain (A_v) | $\displaystyle A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $\displaystyle A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$ | | Sign of Gain | Negative (180° phase inversion) | Positive (0° phase shift) | | Input Impedance (Z_in) | $Z_{in} \approx R_1$ | $Z_{in} \approx \infty$ (very high) | | Output Impedance (Z_out) | Near zero (due to feedback) | Near zero (due to feedback) | | Voltage at Inverting Node | Virtual Ground (0 V) | Equal to V_in (virtual short) | | Phase Relationship | V_out = 180° out of phase with V_in | V_out in phase with V_in | | Design Constraint | $\vert A_v \vert$ must be \lt A_OL | Same constraint | | Common Use | Audio pre-amps, summing amps | Buffer/follower, instrumentation front-end | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Inverting Amplifier:** Used as a phase shifter, summing amplifier (when multiple inputs at V⁻ node), integrator (when Rf is replaced by C), and current-to-voltage converter (trans-impedance amp) in photodiode circuits. - **Non-Inverting Amplifier:** Preferred as an **input buffer** for high-impedance sensors (electrodes, pH probes) because it draws virtually no current from the source. Also used as a precision voltage reference amplifier and headphone driver. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Gain of the Inverting Amplifier ### Circuit Setup - Input V_in is connected to the inverting terminal V⁻ through resistor R_1. - Feedback resistor R_f is connected between V_out and V⁻. - Non-inverting terminal V⁺ is grounded. ### Step 1: Apply the Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions Because I⁺ = 0 (Rule 2), no current flows into the op-amp's input. Hence, the current flowing through R_1 must equal the current flowing through R_f. ### Step 2: Define the Currents Using Ohm's Law Current through R_1 (entering the node V⁻): $$I_{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1}$$ Current through R_f (entering from output): $$I_{R_f} = \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f}$$ ### Step 3: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node Since no current enters the op-amp: $$I_{R_1} = I_{R_f}$$ $$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f}$$ ### Step 4: Apply the Virtual Short Condition Since V⁺ = 0 (grounded) and V⁻ = V⁺ (Rule 1), we have: $$V^- = 0 \quad \text{(Virtual Ground)}$$ ### Step 5: Substitute and Simplify Substituting V⁻ = 0 into the KCL equation: $$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f}$$ $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$ ### Step 6: Solve for the Closed-Loop Gain Rearranging for V_out/V_in: $$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$ The negative sign confirms **phase inversion**. Therefore: $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Gain of the Non-Inverting Amplifier ### Circuit Setup - Input V_in is applied directly to the non-inverting terminal V⁺. - Resistor R_1 connects the inverting terminal V⁻ to ground. - Feedback resistor R_f connects V_out to V⁻. ### Step 1: Identify Node Voltages Using Virtual Short Because V⁺ = V⁻ (virtual short): $$V^- = V_{in}$$ ### Step 2: Apply KCL at the Inverting Node No current flows into the op-amp input. Therefore, the current through R_1 equals the current through R_f. $$I_{R_1} = I_{R_f}$$ ### Step 3: Express the Currents in Terms of Node Voltages Since the inverting node is at V_in, and the other end of R_1 is at ground: $$I_{R_1} = \frac{V^- - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$ The current through R_f (flowing from V_out to V⁻): $$I_{R_f} = \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$ ### Step 4: Equate the Two Currents $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$ ### Step 5: Cross-Multiply and Rearrange $$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot (V_{out} - V_{in})$$ $$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot V_{out} - R_1 \cdot V_{in}$$ $$V_{out} \cdot R_1 = V_{in} \cdot R_f + V_{in} \cdot R_1$$ $$V_{out} \cdot R_1 = V_{in} \cdot (R_f + R_1)$$ ### Step 6: Solve for the Closed-Loop Gain $$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{R_f + R_1}{R_1} = \frac{R_f}{R_1} + 1$$ $$\boxed{A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$ The gain is always **positive** and **greater than or equal to unity**, confirming no phase inversion. --- ## 3.3 Design Equations for Specified Gain Suppose we require an inverting amplifier with gain magnitude \vert A_v \vert = 10. ### Design Procedure: 1. Choose a standard value for R_1. Typical choice: **R_1 = 10 kΩ**. 2. Compute R_f using the formula: R_f = \vert A_v \vert × R_1 = 10 × 10 kΩ = 100 kΩ. 3. Verify that the bandwidth requirement is met. The Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) of IC 741 is 1 MHz. Required bandwidth = GBP / \vert A_v \vert = 1 MHz / 10 = 100 kHz. If input signal is < 100 kHz, the design is safe. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier | |---------|---------------------|--------------------------| | Polarity of Output | Inverted (negative sign) | Non-inverted (positive sign) | | Minimum Gain Magnitude | \vert A_v \vert \geq 0 (can be fractional) | A_v \geq 1 (always) | | Input Impedance | Limited by R_1 (typically 10–100 kΩ) | Extremely high (ideal: ∞) | | Effect of Source Impedance | Source impedance adds to R_1, altering gain | Negligible effect (virtual open) | | Sensitivity to R_1, R_f Tolerance | High (gain directly proportional) | High (same proportionality) | | Common-Mode Rejection (CMRR) | Limited (V⁺ is grounded) | Excellent (both inputs vary together) | | Ability to Use as Voltage Follower | Not possible | Yes (set R_f = 0, R_1 = ∞ for A_v = 1) | # 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of an Op-Amp Internal Architecture The op-amp internally consists of a cascade of four stages. The following Mermaid diagram depicts the functional block-level architecture and signal flow. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Stage
Differential Amplifier] --> B[Gain Stage
High Gain Voltage Amplifier] B --> C[Level Shifter
DC Restoration] C --> D[Output Stage
Push Pull Emitter Follower] D --> E[Vout to External Load] FB[Feedback Network
Rf and R1] -.-> A ``` ## 4.2 Circuit Schematic: Inverting Amplifier The following Mermaid block diagram represents the functional flow of the inverting amplifier. (Note: Mermaid does not natively render electronic schematic symbols; this is a faithful functional representation.) ```mermaid flowchart TD Vsrc[Vin
AC Signal Source] --> R1node[Resistor R1] R1node --> InvNode((Inverting Node V-)) InvNode --> OpAmp[Op-Amp IC 741] NonInv[GND
0 V] --> NonInvPin((Non-Inverting Node V+)) OpAmp --> VoutNode[Vout
Amplified Inverted Signal] VoutNode --> Rfnode[Feedback Resistor Rf] Rfnode --> InvNode VccP[+VCC plus 12V] --> PowerTop[Pin 7] VccN[-VCC minus 12V] --> PowerBot[Pin 4] ``` **Netlist Summary for Inverting Amplifier:** - R_1: Between Vin and Pin 2. - R_f: Between Pin 6 (Vout) and Pin 2 (V⁻). - Pin 3 (V⁺): Connected to GND. - Pin 7: +VCC = +12 V. - Pin 4: –VCC = –12 V. ## 4.3 Circuit Schematic: Non-Inverting Amplifier ```mermaid flowchart TD Vsrc[Vin
AC Signal Source] --> NonInvPin((Non-Inverting Node V+ Pin 3)) NonInvPin --> OpAmp[Op-Amp IC 741] InvGnd[GND 0V] --> R1node[Resistor R1] R1node --> InvNode((Inverting Node V- Pin 2)) InvNode --> OpAmp OpAmp --> VoutNode[Vout
Amplified In-Phase Signal] VoutNode --> Rfnode[Feedback Resistor Rf] Rfnode --> InvNode VccP[+VCC plus 12V] --> PowerTop[Pin 7] VccN[-VCC minus 12V] --> PowerBot[Pin 4] ``` **Netlist Summary for Non-Inverting Amplifier:** - Vin: Connected directly to Pin 3 (V⁺). - R_1: Between Pin 2 (V⁻) and GND. - R_f: Between Pin 6 (Vout) and Pin 2 (V⁻). - Pin 7: +VCC = +12 V. - Pin 4: –VCC = –12 V. ## 4.4 Signal Flow Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INV [Inverting Configuration] direction LR A1[Vin Sinusoid] --> A2[Multiply by minus Rf by R1] --> A3[Vout Inverted Sine] end subgraph NON [Non-Inverting Configuration] direction LR B1[Vin Sinusoid] --> B2[Multiply by 1 plus Rf by R1] --> B3[Vout In-Phase Sine] end ``` ## 4.5 Practical Breadboard Setup Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Before powering the circuit on the lab bench, verify the following:** > 1. IC 741 is inserted in the correct orientation (notch/dot indicating Pin 1). > 2. Decoupling capacitors (0.1 μF ceramic) are placed between each supply rail and ground to suppress oscillation. > 3. Power supply is set to ±12 V (typical) **before** connecting to the IC. > 4. Function generator output is initially at **zero amplitude**, then increased gradually. > 5. CRO/DSO probes are connected with proper ground clips to avoid short circuits. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B] **State the two ideal characteristics of an operational amplifier that form the basis for analysing inverting and non-inverting amplifier circuits.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The two ideal characteristics are: 1. **Infinite Open-Loop Gain (A → ∞):** This makes the differential input voltage V_id = (V⁺ – V⁻) effectively zero, leading to the **Virtual Short** concept (V⁺ = V⁻). 2. **Infinite Input Impedance (Z_in → ∞):** This ensures that the current flowing into the input terminals is zero (I⁺ = I⁻ = 0), giving the **Virtual Open** concept. *(1 mark for each point, 1 mark for clearly stating the consequence.)* --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A] **Differentiate between inverting and non-inverting amplifiers with respect to (i) phase relation, (ii) input impedance, and (iii) closed-loop gain formula.** **Model Answer (3 marks – 1 mark per differentiation):** | Criterion | Inverting Amplifier | Non-Inverting Amplifier | |-----------|---------------------|--------------------------| | (i) Phase relation | Output is 180° out of phase with input | Output is in phase with input | | (ii) Input impedance | Equal to R_1 (moderate) | Extremely high (≈ ∞) | | (iii) Gain formula | $A_v = -R_f / R_1$ | $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_1$ | --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### **Question B (A)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2] **(a)** Design an inverting amplifier using IC 741 with a closed-loop voltage gain of –20. Assume the input signal is a 1 kHz sinusoid of peak amplitude 50 mV. Specify all resistor values, verify the bandwidth requirement, and draw the complete circuit diagram. **(b)** For the designed circuit, calculate the output voltage magnitude. If R_f is doubled, what happens to the gain and bandwidth? Justify using the Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP). ### **Model Solution (Question A):** **(a) Design of Inverting Amplifier with A_v = –20 (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Choose a standard value for R_1. For an audio-frequency op-amp circuit, R_1 = 10 kΩ is a typical low-noise choice. *(1 mark)* **Step 2:** Compute R_f using the gain formula. $$\vert A_v \vert = \frac{R_f}{R_1} \implies R_f = \vert A_v \vert \times R_1 = 20 \times 10 \, \text{k}\Omega = 200 \, \text{k}\Omega$$ We select the nearest standard value: **R_f = 200 kΩ**. *(2 marks)* **Step 3:** Verify bandwidth using GBP of IC 741 (GBP = 1 MHz). $$f_{3dB} = \frac{GBP}{\vert A_v \vert} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{20} = 50 \, \text{kHz}$$ Since the input frequency (1 kHz) is well within 50 kHz, the design is valid. *(2 marks)* **Step 4:** Draw the circuit diagram showing Vin connected via R_1 (10 kΩ) to Pin 2, R_f (200 kΩ) between Pin 6 and Pin 2, Pin 3 grounded, ±12 V supplies on Pins 7 and 4. *(2 marks)* **(b) Output Calculation and GBP Analysis (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Output voltage magnitude. $$\vert V_{out} \vert = \vert A_v \vert \times \vert V_{in} \vert = 20 \times 50 \, \text{mV} = 1.0 \, \text{V (peak)}$$ The output is an inverted sinusoid of peak amplitude **1 V**. *(2 marks)* **Step 2:** If R_f is doubled (R_f = 400 kΩ): $$\text{New } A_v = -\frac{400 \, \text{k}\Omega}{10 \, \text{k}\Omega} = -40$$ The gain magnitude is **doubled** from 20 to 40. *(2 marks)* **Step 3:** Effect on bandwidth. $$\text{New } f_{3dB} = \frac{GBP}{\vert A_v \vert} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{40} = 25 \, \text{kHz}$$ The bandwidth is **halved** (from 50 kHz to 25 kHz). *(2 marks)* **Step 4:** Justification: The Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) is a constant for a given op-amp. Hence, doubling the gain by increasing R_f inevitably reduces the usable bandwidth by the same factor. *(1 mark)* --- ### **Question B (B)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 — Alternative Choice] **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain of a non-inverting amplifier using an ideal op-amp. Mention clearly the two ideal op-amp assumptions used in the derivation. **(b)** Design a non-inverting amplifier using IC 741 to achieve a voltage gain of 11. Calculate the required resistors. If the input is a 100 mV DC signal, determine the output DC voltage. Comment on the input impedance of this configuration. ### **Model Solution (Question B):** **(a) Derivation of Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Draw the circuit diagram showing Vin at Pin 3, R_1 from Pin 2 to ground, R_f from Pin 6 to Pin 2. *(1 mark)* **Step 2:** State the two ideal assumptions: - **Virtual Short:** V⁺ = V⁻ ⟹ V⁻ = V_in - **Virtual Open:** I⁺ = I⁻ = 0 *(1 mark)* **Step 3:** Apply KCL at the inverting node (Pin 2): $$\frac{V^- - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f}$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 4:** Substitute V⁻ = V_in: $$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$ *(1 mark)* **Step 5:** Cross-multiply and rearrange: $$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot V_{out} - R_1 \cdot V_{in}$$ $$V_{out} \cdot R_1 = V_{in} \cdot (R_f + R_1)$$ *(2 marks)* **Step 6:** Final expression: $$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$ *(1 mark)* **(b) Design for A_v = 11 and DC Analysis (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Choose R_1 = 10 kΩ (standard). *(1 mark)* **Step 2:** Compute R_f. $$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \implies 11 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{10 \, \text{k}\Omega}$$ $$\frac{R_f}{10 \, \text{k}\Omega} = 10 \implies R_f = 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$$ *(2 marks)* **Step 3:** Output DC voltage for V_in = 100 mV. $$V_{out} = A_v \times V_{in} = 11 \times 100 \, \text{mV} = 1.1 \, \text{V}$$ *(2 marks)* **Step 4:** Comment on input impedance. Because the input signal is applied directly to the non-inverting terminal (V⁺), and the op-amp's input impedance is ideally infinite, the **input impedance of this configuration is extremely high (≈ ∞ for ideal op-amp; practically 2 MΩ for IC 741)**. This makes it ideal for buffering high-impedance sources without loading them. *(2 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Error:** Students often write the inverting gain as $R_f / R_1$ without the negative sign. This costs **2 full marks** in derivation questions. > 2. **Forgetting the "+1":** In non-inverting gain, writing $R_f / R_1$ instead of $1 + R_f/R_1$ is a frequent error. > 3. **Power Supply Reversal:** In the lab, connecting +12 V to Pin 4 and –12 V to Pin 7 destroys the IC. Always double-check before power-on. > 4. **Missing Decoupling Capacitors:** Without 0.1 μF capacitors on supply pins, the op-amp may **oscillate at high frequency**, producing meaningless CRO waveforms. > 5. **Output Saturation:** Forgetting that V_out cannot exceed (V_CC – 2 V). With ±12 V supply, output is clipped near ±10 V. Exceeding this range causes **distortion**, not additional gain. > 6. **Ignoring GBP:** When asked to design for high gain at high frequency, students fail to check the Gain-Bandwidth Product constraint. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🎯 **Golden Rules:** V⁺ = V⁻ (virtual short) and I⁺ = I⁻ = 0 (virtual open) are the **only** assumptions you need to solve any op-amp problem. - 🎯 **Inverting Gain Formula:** $A_v = -R_f / R_1$ (always negative, can be less than unity). - 🎯 **Non-Inverting Gain Formula:** $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_1$ (always ≥ 1, always positive). - 🎯 **Virtual Ground** exists **only** in the inverting configuration; never in the non-inverting. - 🎯 **Input Impedance:** Lowest for inverting (= R_1), highest for non-inverting (≈ ∞). - 🎯 **GBP Trade-off:** Gain × Bandwidth = Constant. Doubling gain halves bandwidth. - 🎯 **Output Saturation:** V_out_max = V_CC – 2 V (approximately), due to internal transistor headroom. - 🎯 **Slew Rate Limit:** Maximum dV/dt of the output. For 741, it is 0.5 V/μs, meaning the output cannot change faster than this regardless of input. - 🎯 **Lab Safety:** Always connect power supply ground to circuit ground; use proper CRO probe compensation. - 🎯 **Verification Step:** Always compare measured V_out with theoretical $A_v \times V_{in}$; report percentage error in the lab record.

topics.title: Op-amps circuits - adder, integrator, and differentiator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Op-amps circuits - adder, integrator, and differentiator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2 — Op-Amp Circuits: Adder, Integrator, and Differentiator

## 1. Core Technical Definition and Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) — Formal Definition
An **operational amplifier (op-amp)** is a DC-coupled, high-gain, differential-input, single-ended-output voltage amplifier. When configured with external passive components and **negative feedback**, it can perform precise linear mathematical operations on analog signals — addition, subtraction, integration, and differentiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions (KCL Foundation):**
> - Open-loop voltage gain $A_{OL} = \infty$
> - Input impedance $Z_{in} = \infty$  (currents $I^+ = I^- = 0$)
> - Output impedance $Z_{out} = 0$
> - **Virtual Short:** $V^+ = V^-$
> - **Virtual Ground (in inverting mode):** $V^- = 0$ V
> - Infinite CMRR, zero offset voltage and bias current

### 1.2 The Three Linear Building Blocks (Syllabus Anchor)

**a) Summing Amplifier (Adder):**
A linear op-amp circuit whose DC output voltage is the **negative weighted sum** of multiple input voltages. It is the analog equivalent of an algebraic summation.

**b) Integrator:**
A linear op-amp circuit whose output voltage is proportional to the **time integral of the input voltage** with respect to time. The output is the running accumulation of the input signal.

**c) Differentiator:**
A linear op-amp circuit whose output voltage is proportional to the **time derivative (rate of change) of the input voltage** with respect to time. The output responds to how fast the input is changing.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mental Models:**
>
> - **Adder** → Think of a **fuel gauge of a car dashboard**. The gauge adds up fuel from multiple tanks (V1, V2, V3) and displays the total (Vout) after scaling.
> - **Integrator** → Think of a **water tank filling from a tap**. The water *level* in the tank (Vout) is the *integral* of the *flow rate* (Vin) over time. Pour slowly, level rises slowly; pour fast, level rises fast.
> - **Differentiator** → Think of a **speedometer of a car**. The speed (Vout) is the *derivative* of *distance* (Vin) with respect to time. A stationary car (Vin constant) gives 0 speed; a car accelerating sharply gives a very high speed reading.

These three circuits are the **three fundamental analog computing blocks** that form the mathematical basis of every analog filter, oscillator, ADC, and signal-conditioning front-end.

### 1.4 Standard Op-Amp IC Used in Lab
The lab experiment typically uses the **IC 741 (µA741)** — a general-purpose, internally compensated, 8-pin DIP op-amp. The two key terminals used throughout this experiment are:
- **Pin 2** — Inverting input ($V^-$)
- **Pin 3** — Non-inverting input ($V^+$)
- **Pin 6** — Output ($V_{out}$)
- **Pin 7, 4** — $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$ power supply (typically $\pm 12$ V or $\pm 15$ V)
- **Pin 1, 5, 8** — Offset null and NC (not connected in this experiment)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Analysis Assumption: Why "Virtual Ground" Exists

For all three circuits below, we operate the op-amp in the **inverting configuration**. Since the non-inverting terminal is tied to ground ($V^+ = 0$) and the negative feedback forces $V^- = V^+$, the inverting terminal is also held at **0 V** even though no current physically flows into it. This is the **virtual ground** concept — the foundation of every derivation in this module.

## 2.2 Circuit 1 — Inverting Summing Amplifier (Adder)

### Topology
Multiple input voltages $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_n$ are fed through input resistors $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ to the inverting node. A feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected between the output and the inverting node.

### Working Principle
- Each input voltage drives a current $I_i = V_i / R_i$ into the virtual ground.
- All currents **sum** at the inverting node.
- The feedback resistor carries this sum current to the output, producing a negative sum.

## 2.3 Circuit 2 — Ideal Integrator (Miller Integrator)

### Topology
A single input resistor $R_1$ in series with the input voltage $V_{in}$. A feedback capacitor $C$ replaces the feedback resistor.

### Working Principle
- Input current $I_{in} = V_{in} / R_1$ charges the feedback capacitor.
- The voltage across the capacitor is the integral of the charging current.
- Output is the **negative time-integral** of the input scaled by $1 / (R_1 C)$.

## 2.4 Circuit 3 — Ideal Differentiator

### Topology
A single input capacitor $C_1$ in series with the input. A feedback resistor $R_f$ replaces the feedback capacitor.

### Working Principle
- Capacitor current $I_C = C \cdot dV_{in} / dt$.
- This current flows through $R_f$ producing $V_{out} = -I_C \cdot R_f$.
- Output is the **negative time-derivative** of the input scaled by $R_f C$.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Circuit Type | Output Equation (Time Domain) | Frequency Domain Transfer Function | Magnitude $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inverting Adder (n inputs) | $V_{out} = -R_f \left( \dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{V_n}{R_n} \right)$ | $H(s) = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{eq}}$ (DC) | Unity / weighted sum |
| **Unity Adder** ($R_1 = R_2 = R_f$) | $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n)$ | Same as above | Sum (inverted) |
| **Ideal Integrator** | $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_1 C} \displaystyle\int_0^t V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_{out}(0)$ | $H(s) = -\dfrac{1}{s R_1 C}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\omega R_1 C}$ (rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade) |
| **Ideal Differentiator** | $V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1 \, \dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$ | $H(s) = -s R_f C_1$ | $\omega R_f C_1$ (rises at $+20$ dB/decade) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Classification (Critical for KTU):**
> - **Integrator** behaves as a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — attenuates high frequencies, passes DC slowly. Slope: **$-20$ dB/decade**.
> - **Differentiator** behaves as a **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** — amplifies high frequencies, blocks DC. Slope: **$+20$ dB/decade**.
> - Integrator gain at DC = $\infty$ (open-loop); Differentiator gain at DC = 0 (open-loop).

## 2.6 Practical Engineering Utilities
- **Adder:** Audio mixers, DAC reconstruction summers, multi-channel signal combining in instrumentation, weighted summing in neural-network hardware (memristor crossbars).
- **Integrator:** Active filters (state-variable biquad), waveform generators (function generators producing triangle from square), analog computers, PLL loop filters, sigma-delta ADC integrators.
- **Differentiator:** Edge detectors in radar/sonar, PID controller "D" term, FM demodulators, biomedical signal processing (QRS detection in ECG).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inverting Summing Amplifier Output (n Inputs)

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node (Virtual Ground, $V^- = 0$):**
The sum of currents entering the node equals the current leaving through $R_f$. Since $I^- = 0$ (ideal op-amp), the sum of input currents must equal the feedback current.

**Step 2 — Write the current equations:**
Each input branch contributes a current from the input voltage to the virtual ground. The feedback branch carries current from the virtual ground to the output.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + \cdots + I_n &= I_{f} \\
\frac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - V^-}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{V_n - V^-}{R_n} &= \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the virtual ground condition $V^- = 0$:**
Because the non-inverting input is grounded and feedback enforces $V^- = V^+$, the inverting node sits at exactly 0 V — even though it is not physically connected to ground.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{V_n}{R_n} &= \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} \\
\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{V_n}{R_n} &= -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**
Multiply both sides by $-R_f$ to isolate the output voltage as the negative weighted sum of all inputs.

$$
V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{V_n}{R_n} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Special case: Unity-gain adder ($R_1 = R_2 = \cdots = R_n = R_f = R$):**
When every input resistor equals the feedback resistor, each branch has unit weight, and the output is simply the inverted arithmetic sum.

$$
V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n)
$$

**Design Example:** To sum three signals $V_1, V_2, V_3$ with gains $1, 2, 0.5$ respectively, choose $R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ideal Integrator Output

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node ($V^- = 0$):**
The current through the input resistor must equal the current charging the feedback capacitor (since $I^- = 0$).

$$
I_{R_1} = I_C
$$

**Step 2 — Express each current:**
The input current is driven by the source voltage through $R_1$. The capacitor current is the time derivative of voltage across it, but here the voltage across the capacitor is $V^- - V_{out} = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} &= C \cdot \frac{d(V^- - V_{out})}{dt} \\
\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} &= C \cdot \frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} \\
\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} &= -C \cdot \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $dV_{out}$:**
Bring $dt$ to the right side and multiply through.

$$
dV_{out} = -\frac{1}{R_1 C} \, V_{in} \, dt
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:**
The indefinite integral of the left side is $V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0)$, where $V_{out}(0)$ is the initial capacitor voltage at $t = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{t} dV_{out} &= -\frac{1}{R_1 C} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau \\
V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0) &= -\frac{1}{R_1 C} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final integrator equation:**
Adding $V_{out}(0)$ to both sides yields the textbook formula. The negative sign indicates **inverting integration**, and the time constant $R_1 C$ sets the integration scale.

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau) \, d\tau + V_{out}(0)
$$

**Step 6 — Frequency-domain verification:**
Replace the time-domain integrator with its Laplace equivalent $1/s$ and apply the transfer-function approach. $R_1$ becomes input impedance, $1/sC$ becomes feedback impedance.

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_{in}} = -\frac{1/sC}{R_1} = -\frac{1}{s R_1 C}
$$

**Step 7 — Square-wave input case (Lab-typical input):**
A symmetric square wave of amplitude $\pm V_p$ alternates between $+V_p$ and $-V_p$ every half-period $T/2$. Each half-period produces a **linear ramp** (triangle wave) at the output with slope $\pm V_p / (R_1 C)$.

$$
V_{out,\text{peak-peak}} = \frac{V_p \cdot T}{2 R_1 C}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ideal Differentiator Output

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node ($V^- = 0$):**
Current through the input capacitor equals the current through the feedback resistor.

$$
I_C = I_{R_f}
$$

**Step 2 — Express each current:**
The capacitor current is $C$ times the rate of change of the voltage across it, which is $V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$. The feedback resistor current is the voltage across it divided by $R_f$, which is $(0 - V_{out}) / R_f$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} &= \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} \\
C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} &= \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} \\
C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt} &= -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**
Multiply both sides by $-R_f$. The output voltage is proportional to the time derivative of the input, scaled by the time constant $R_f C$.

$$
V_{out}(t) = -R_f C \cdot \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Frequency-domain verification:**
Capacitor impedance in Laplace domain is $1 / sC$, feedback impedance is $R_f$.

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{R_f}{1/sC} = -s R_f C
$$

**Step 5 — Triangle-wave input case (Lab-typical input):**
A triangle wave with slope $\pm M$ V/s produces a **square wave** at the output with amplitude $V_{out} = \pm R_f C \cdot M$. The differentiator converts the *slope* of any waveform into a *constant level*.

$$
V_{out} = -R_f C \cdot M
$$

**Step 6 — Sine-wave steady-state response (Important for KTU):**
For $V_{in} = V_m \sin(\omega t)$:

$$
V_{out} = -R_f C \cdot V_m \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

This explicitly shows that the **magnitude grows linearly with frequency** — explaining why a differentiator amplifies high-frequency noise violently.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Ideal Integrator | Ideal Differentiator |
|---|---|---|
| Transfer function $H(s)$ | $-\dfrac{1}{sR_1C}$ | $-sR_fC$ |
| Magnitude at DC ($\omega \to 0$) | $\infty$ (theoretically) | $0$ |
| Magnitude at high $\omega$ | $0$ (rolls off) | $\infty$ (rises) |
| Filter behaviour | Low-pass (LPF) | High-pass (HPF) |
| Phase shift | $-90^\circ$ (lagging) | $+90^\circ$ (leading) |
| Stability | Conditionally stable | Unstable — needs compensation |
| Noise susceptibility | Low | **Very high** at high frequencies |
| Lab test input | Square wave | Triangle wave |
| Lab test output | Triangle wave | Square wave |
| Practical modification | Add $R_f$ across $C$ | Add $C_f$ across $R_f$ + $R_1$ in series with $C$ |

## 3.5 Practical Integrator — Why It Is Needed

The ideal integrator suffers from:
- **DC drift** — any tiny input offset current or voltage is integrated without bound, eventually saturating the op-amp.
- **Infinite DC gain** — open-loop behaviour at low frequencies.

**Remedy:** Place a large feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$. The resulting practical integrator has a finite low-frequency gain of $-R_f/R_1$ and a pole at $\omega = 1 / (R_f C)$.

$$
H_{practical}(s) = -\frac{R_f / R_1}{1 + s R_f C}
$$

## 3.6 Practical Differentiator — Why It Is Needed

The ideal differentiator suffers from:
- **High-frequency noise amplification** — gain $\propto \omega$, so thermal noise above 10 kHz is amplified drastically.
- **Instability / oscillation** — the op-amp's open-loop gain rolls off and interacts with the differentiator's rising gain, causing ringing.

**Remedy:** Add a small feedback capacitor $C_f$ across $R_f$ (limits gain at HF) and a small series resistor $R_1$ with the input capacitor (limits high-frequency input current).

$$
H_{practical}(s) = -\frac{s R_f C_1}{(1 + s R_f C_f)(1 + s R_1 C_1)}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

The following Mermaid block-level architecture diagrams map the signal flow, component placement, and functional interaction for each of the three circuits. (Physical schematic drawings are abstracted to functional flow blocks — the actual circuit construction is performed on a breadboard during the lab session.)

## 4.1 Architecture of the Inverting Summing Amplifier (3-Input Adder)

```mermaid
graph TD
    V1["INPUT V1 AC OR DC SOURCE"] --> R1["RESISTOR R1 INPUT BRANCH"]
    R1 --> NODE_A["INVERTING NODE PIN 2 VIRTUAL GROUND 0V"]
    V2["INPUT V2 SOURCE"] --> R2["RESISTOR R2 INPUT BRANCH"]
    R2 --> NODE_A
    V3["INPUT V3 SOURCE"] --> R3["RESISTOR R3 INPUT BRANCH"]
    R3 --> NODE_A
    NODE_A --> OP1["OPAMP IC 741 DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER STAGE"]
    RF["FEEDBACK RESISTOR Rf"] --> NODE_A
    OP1 --> OUT["OUTPUT Vout PIN 6 INVERTED WEIGHTED SUM"]
    G1["NON INVERTING PIN 3 GROUND 0V"] --> OP1
    VCC["SUPPLY +Vcc PIN 7 TYPICALLY 12V"] --> OP1
    VEE["SUPPLY -Vee PIN 4 TYPICALLY -12V"] --> OP1
```

**Interpretation:** Each input branch is a parallel path; all currents sum at the virtual-ground node (Pin 2). The op-amp sinks the sum current through $R_f$, producing an inverted weighted sum at Pin 6.

## 4.2 Architecture of the Ideal Integrator with Test Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    FG["FUNCTION GENERATOR SQUARE WAVE INPUT"] --> IN_R["INPUT RESISTOR R1 DEFINES INTEGRATION SCALE"]
    IN_R --> VN1["VIRTUAL GROUND NODE 0V"]
    VN1 --> OPA["OPAMP HIGH GAIN DIFFERENTIAL STAGE"]
    OPA --> OUT_I["OUTPUT Vout PIN 6 TRIANGLE WAVE RAMP"]
    FB_C["FEEDBACK CAPACITOR C STORES CHARGE INTEGRATES CURRENT"] --> VN1
    GND1["GROUND PIN 3 REFERENCE"] --> OPA
    CAP_V["CAPACITOR VOLTAGE Vc EQUALS NEGATIVE Vout"] --> FB_C
```

**Interpretation:** The current from the input resistor linearly charges the feedback capacitor. As the capacitor charges, $V_{out}$ ramps linearly. A square-wave input produces a triangle-wave output, demonstrating integration in real time.

## 4.3 Architecture of the Practical Differentiator with Compensation Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    FG2["INPUT Vin TRIANGLE WAVE OR SINUSOID"] --> C1["INPUT CAPACITOR C1 DIFFERENTIATING ELEMENT"]
    C1 --> R1P["SERIES RESISTOR R1 LIMITS HF GAIN STABILITY"]
    R1P --> VN2["VIRTUAL GROUND NODE 0V"]
    VN2 --> OP2["OPAMP IC 741 DIFFERENTIATOR CORE"]
    OP2 --> OUT_D["OUTPUT Vout PIN 6 SQUARE WAVE OR SHIFTED SINE"]
    RFD["FEEDBACK RESISTOR Rf SCALES DERIVATIVE OUTPUT"] --> VN2
    CFF["PARALLEL CAPACITOR Cf ACROSS Rf HF NOISE FILTER"] --> VN2
    GND2["GROUND PIN 3"] --> OP2
```

**Interpretation:** The input capacitor differentiates the input; the feedback resistor converts the differentiated current into a voltage. The added $C_f$ and $R_1$ form a real-pole roll-off that prevents high-frequency noise amplification and stabilizes the loop.

## 4.4 Frequency-Response Comparison — Integrator vs. Differentiator

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LOW_FREQ["LOW FREQUENCY REGION"]
        I_DC["INTEGRATOR GAIN INFINITE AT DC"]:::hi
        D_DC["DIFFERENTIATOR GAIN ZERO AT DC"]:::lo
    end
    subgraph MID_FREQ["MID FREQUENCY REGION"]
        I_MID["INTEGRATOR SLOPE MINUS 20 DB PER DECADE"]:::mid
        D_MID["DIFFERENTIATOR SLOPE PLUS 20 DB PER DECADE"]:::mid
    end
    subgraph HIGH_FREQ["HIGH FREQUENCY REGION"]
        I_HF["INTEGRATOR GAIN ROLLS OFF TO ZERO"]:::lo
        D_HF["DIFFERENTIATOR GAIN RISES UNBOUNDED NOISE RISK"]:::hi
    end
    I_DC --> I_MID --> I_HF
    D_DC --> D_MID --> D_HF
    classDef hi fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef lo fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The integrator and differentiator are **mirror images** of each other on a Bode plot. The integrator is the inverse Laplace transform of the differentiator and vice versa — a direct consequence of duality in the s-domain.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Virtual Ground" in an inverting op-amp configuration. Why is it essential for deriving adder and integrator output equations?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Virtual ground is the condition that arises at the inverting input terminal ($V^-$) of an op-amp when the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$) is grounded and negative feedback is applied.
