## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. What happens if the loop gain is less than unity?

**Model Answer:**

The **Barkhausen criterion** for sustained sinusoidal oscillations has two conditions:

1. The magnitude of the loop gain must be unity: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
2. The total phase shift around the loop must be zero (or an integer multiple of $2\pi$ rad / $360°$): $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } n \times 360°$

**If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$:** The oscillations will be **damped** and will not be sustained. The amplitude will decay exponentially until the output becomes zero. This is because the energy fed back to the input is insufficient to compensate for the losses in the circuit.

**If $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$:** The oscillations will **grow in amplitude** until non-linearities in the active device (gain compression) reduce the effective gain to satisfy $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, leading to amplitude stabilization with some distortion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Compare the frequency-determining networks used in phase shift and Wien's bridge oscillators.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Phase Shift Oscillator | Wien's Bridge Oscillator |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Network type** | 3-stage RC ladder (cascade) | Series RC + Parallel RC |
| **Phase shift at $f_o$** | $180°$ | $0°$ |
| **Components** | 3 Resistors + 3 Capacitors | 2 Resistors + 2 Capacitors |
| **Feedback factor at $f_o$** | $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$ | $\vert \beta \vert = 1/3$ |
| **Frequency formula** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Op-amp mode** | Inverting | Non-inverting |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Phase Shift Oscillator**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 2 / ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **phase shift oscillator using an Op-amp**. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a phase shift oscillator using an Op-amp to generate a **$1\text{ kHz}$ sinusoidal** waveform. Assume suitable values for the capacitors and determine the required resistor values and Op-amp gain. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Circuit Operation:**

The phase shift oscillator uses an Op-amp in inverting configuration with a 3-stage RC ladder network connected between the output and the inverting input. Each RC section provides a phase lead of $60°$, and the three together give a total phase lead of $180°$ at the frequency of oscillation.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 for the Mermaid schematic)*

**Working:**
1. Initial power-on produces a small noise signal at the Op-amp input.
2. The Op-amp amplifies it (inverting → $180°$ phase shift).
3. The amplified signal passes through the 3-stage RC network, which adds another $180°$ phase shift at $f_o$.
4. The total loop phase is $360°$, and the feedback is in phase with the input.
5. Regenerative feedback causes the oscillations to build up to a steady amplitude, sustained at $f_o$.

**Derivation:**

The transfer function of the loaded 3-stage RC network is:

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 - j(6\alpha - \alpha^3)}, \quad \text{where } \alpha = \frac{1}{\omega RC}
$$

For phase condition $\angle \beta = -180°$:

$$
6\alpha - \alpha^3 = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha^2 = 6 \Rightarrow \alpha = \sqrt{6}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}
$$

For magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

At $\alpha = \sqrt{6}$, $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$, so $\vert A \vert \geq 29$, giving:

$$
R_f \geq 29 R_i
$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $f_o = 1\text{ kHz} = 1000\text{ Hz}$.

**Assumption:** Choose $C = 0.1\,\mu F = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}\text{ F}$.

**Find R:**

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C \sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.449}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{1.5386 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 650\text{ }\Omega
$$

**Choose standard value:** $R = 680\text{ }\Omega$ (closest E12 standard).

**Refined $f_o$:**

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 680 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{6}} \approx 957\text{ Hz}
$$

**Op-amp gain setting:** Choose $R_i = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 30\text{ k}\Omega$ (so $\vert A \vert = 30 > 29$).

**Components List:**

| Component | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| R1, R2, R3 | $680\text{ }\Omega$ each |
| C1, C2, C3 | $0.1\,\mu F$ each |
| $R_i$ | $1\text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $R_f$ | $30\text{ k}\Omega$ |
| Op-amp | $\mu A 741$ or equivalent |
| Output $f_o$ | $\approx 957\text{ Hz}$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Wien's Bridge Oscillator**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 2 / ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **Wien's Bridge oscillator using an Op-amp** and explain its operation. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wien's Bridge oscillator is to be designed to generate a **$10\text{ kHz}$** sinusoid. If the capacitors used are $C = 1\text{ nF}$, find the value of resistors in the bridge network. Also determine the values of the Op-amp gain-setting resistors. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Circuit Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.4 for the Mermaid schematic)*

The Wien's Bridge oscillator uses an Op-amp in non-inverting configuration. The positive feedback is through the Wien's bridge (series arm Z₁ = R + 1/jωC, parallel arm Z₂ = R/(1 + jωRC)), and the negative feedback is through a resistor divider (R₁ and R_f).

**Working:**
1. The Wien's network provides a frequency-selective feedback.
2. At the null frequency, the network phase shift is zero, so the feedback is in phase with the input.
3. The non-inverting Op-amp adds zero phase shift, giving a total loop phase of zero.
4. The Op-amp compensates for the feedback loss ($\vert \beta \vert = 1/3$) by providing a gain of 3.

**Derivation:**

The feedback factor of the Wien's network is:

$$
\beta = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}
$$

For phase = 0:

$$
\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0 \Rightarrow \omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}
$$

For magnitude condition:

At $f_o$, $\vert \beta \vert = 1/3$, so $\vert A \vert \geq 3$.

$$
1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \Rightarrow \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2
$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $f_o = 10\text{ kHz} = 10{,}000\text{ Hz}$, $C = 1\text{ nF} = 1 \times 10^{-9}\text{ F}$.

**Find R:**

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10{,}000 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 15.92\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Choose standard value:** $R = 16\text{ k}\Omega$ (closest E12 standard).

**Op-amp gain resistors (need $R_f = 2R_1$):**

Choose $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 20\text{ k}\Omega$.

**Components List:**

| Component | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| R (bridge) | $16\text{ k}\Omega$ × 2 |
| C (bridge) | $1\text{ nF}$ × 2 |
| $R_1$ | $10\text{ k}\Omega$ |
| $R_f$ | $20\text{ k}\Omega$ |
| Op-amp | $\mu A 741$ or equivalent |
| Output $f_o$ | $\approx 9.95\text{ kHz}$ |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $\sqrt{6}$** in the phase shift oscillator formula. Many students write $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ for both oscillators — this is WRONG. The $\sqrt{6}$ arises from the cascade of 3 sections and is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Inverting vs. Non-inverting confusion:** Phase shift oscillator uses **inverting** (signal to inverting input), Wien's bridge uses **non-inverting** (signal to non-inverting input). Mixing these up leads to incorrect phase analysis — major deduction.
>
> 3. **Sign of feedback factor:** In phase shift oscillator, $\beta = -1/29$ (negative sign indicates $180°$ phase shift). Don't drop the sign.
>
> 4. **Unit errors:** Always convert kHz to Hz, $\mu F$ to F, before substituting in formulas. KTU examiners check this carefully.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning Barkhausen criterion:** Even if the question doesn't ask for it explicitly, the criterion forms the theoretical foundation. Always mention it before deriving any oscillator formula.
>
> 6. **Ignoring practical amplitude stabilization:** In a real circuit, $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ at startup and settles to 1 due to gain compression (non-linear amplitude limiting). Mention this in long-answer questions to score full marks on "explain the operation" type questions.
>
> 7. **Not drawing the circuit diagram:** A neat, labeled circuit diagram is worth 2-3 marks in a 14-mark question. Always include it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Barkhausen Criterion (Universal):**
- $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ (magnitude)
- $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$ (phase)

**Phase Shift Oscillator — Must Remember:**
- Uses **inverting** Op-amp configuration
- **3-stage RC ladder** provides $180°$ phase shift
- **Frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$
- **Required gain:** $\vert A \vert \geq 29$
- **Resistor condition:** $R_f \geq 29 R_i$
- **Feedback factor:** $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$
- 6 R + 3 C in feedback network

**Wien's Bridge Oscillator — Must Remember:**
- Uses **non-inverting** Op-amp configuration
- Wien's bridge: series RC + parallel RC
- **Frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (simpler, no $\sqrt{6}$)
- **Required gain:** $\vert A \vert \geq 3$
- **Resistor condition:** $R_f = 2 R_1$
- **Feedback factor:** $\vert \beta \vert = 1/3$
- 2 R + 2 C in feedback network (simpler design)

**Design Procedure (Universal for both):**
1. Choose $C$ based on practical availability.
2. Calculate $R$ from the frequency formula.
3. Choose nearest standard value of $R$.
4. Calculate Op-amp gain-setting resistors ($R_1$, $R_f$).
5. Verify that $\vert A \vert$ meets the minimum gain condition.
6. Use a ±15 V dual supply with a standard Op-amp (μA741, LM741, TL081, etc.).

**Comparison Memory Aid:**
- Phase Shift = **3 sections, $180°$, gain 29, inverting** → "PSO: Three-Six-29-Inverting"
- Wien's Bridge = **2 arms, $0°$, gain 3, non-inverting** → "WBO: Two-Zero-3-NonInverting"

**Real-World Applications:**
- **Phase Shift:** Simple audio tone generators, musical synthesizers.
- **Wien's Bridge:** Laboratory function generators, audio test equipment, hi-fi testing, biomedical instrumentation.

**Common KTU Exam Pitfalls (re-emphasized):**
- Forgetting $\sqrt{6}$ factor in PSO
- Inverting vs. non-inverting confusion
- Unit conversion errors
- Not drawing circuit diagrams
- Not mentioning Barkhausen criterion
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveform generation - Square, triangular and saw tooth waveform generation using Op-amps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Waveform generation - Square, triangular and saw tooth waveform generation using Op-amps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Function Generators using Op-Amps

A **function generator** is an electronic circuit that produces repetitive, non-sinusoidal waveforms such as **square waves, triangular waves, and sawtooth waves**. In analog electronics, these waveforms are predominantly generated using **operational amplifiers (op-amps)** configured in feedback topologies. The op-amp acts as the active building block that exploits its high open-loop gain, saturation behavior, and integrator/differentiator properties to synthesize precise periodic signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Waveform generators are classified as **non-linear signal conditioning circuits** under linear integrated circuits (ICs). The KTU lab manual lists three mandatory waveforms: **Square, Triangular, and Sawtooth** — all derived using the generic op-amps **µA741 or LM741**.

## 1.2 Square Wave Generator (Astable Multivibrator)

### Formal Definition
A **square wave generator using an op-amp** is an **astable multivibrator circuit** — a regenerative circuit with **no stable state** — that continuously switches between two opposite saturation levels $(+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat})$ of the op-amp, producing a symmetrical or asymmetrical square waveform at the output.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!TIP]
> **Think of a square wave generator like a ball rolling between two hills.**
> Imagine a ball placed exactly on a perfectly flat ridge between two valleys. The slightest push makes it fall into one valley. Once in the valley, an internal mechanism (gravity + spring analogy) lifts it back over the ridge, where the slightest imbalance tips it into the opposite valley. The continuous up-and-over motion creates the alternating high-low pattern of a square wave. The op-amp's comparator action provides the "ridge," while the **RC timing network** determines how long the ball stays in each valley (i.e., the time period).

## 1.3 Triangular Wave Generator

### Formal Definition
A **triangular wave generator** is a circuit that produces a **linearly rising and falling waveform** with equal rise and fall times, obtained by integrating a square wave using an op-amp integrator. The combination of a **Schmitt trigger (comparator with hysteresis)** followed by an **active integrator** forms the classical triangular wave generator.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!TIP]
> **Triangular wave generation is like filling and emptying a bucket at constant rate.**
> The Schmitt trigger acts like a tap that turns ON when the bucket is empty and OFF when the bucket is full. The integrator (the bucket) fills with water (charge) at a constant rate. When full, the tap reverses and the bucket empties at the same rate. The water level vs. time gives a perfect triangle.

## 1.4 Sawtooth Wave Generator

### Formal Definition
A **sawtooth wave generator** produces a waveform with a **linear ramp followed by a sharp (abrupt) drop or rise**. It is structurally similar to a triangular wave generator, but uses an **asymmetric charging/discharging mechanism** — typically a **current source** in place of a resistor in the integrator — to create unequal rise and fall times.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!TIP]
> **Sawtooth wave is like slowly drawing back the string of a bow and then releasing it instantly.**
> The slow drawing (ramp) takes most of the cycle, while the release (sudden drop) is nearly instantaneous — exactly like a sawtooth waveform where the gradual slope occupies ~95% of the period and the sharp edge occupies only ~5%.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Parameters to Remember:**
> - **Op-amp supply voltage:** Typically $\pm 12\text{ V}$ or $\pm 15\text{ V}$ (dual supply)
> - **Saturation voltage:** $V_{sat} \approx \pm V_{CC} - 1\text{ V} \approx \pm 13\text{ V}$ (for $\pm 15\text{ V}$ supply)
> - **Standard IC used:** **µA741 / LM741** (KTU lab standard)
> - **Threshold ratio for square wave:** $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Square Wave Generator — Operational Theory

The op-amp square wave generator is essentially a **regenerative comparator (Schmitt trigger)** with an **RC timing network** in the negative feedback path.

### Circuit Operation Logic

- The op-amp output $V_o$ is forced to either $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ (saturation mode).
- A **positive feedback** through the resistor divider $R_1, R_2$ sets the **threshold voltages** at the non-inverting input $(V_+)$.
- The **RC network** connected between output and inverting input $(V_-)$ charges/discharges toward $V_o$.
- Whenever $V_-$ crosses the threshold $V_+$, the output **switches state**.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Initial state:** Suppose $V_o = +V_{sat}$.
2. The capacitor $C$ charges through $R$ toward $+V_{sat}$.
3. The non-inverting input is held at $V_+ = +V_{sat} \cdot \beta$, where $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
4. When capacitor voltage $V_-$ exceeds $V_+$, the op-amp output **flips** to $-V_{sat}$.
5. Now $V_+ = -V_{sat} \cdot \beta$, and capacitor discharges toward $-V_{sat}$.
6. When $V_-$ falls below $V_+$, output flips back to $+V_{sat}$.
7. Cycle repeats indefinitely — **astable operation**.

## 2.2 Frequency Derivation Logic (Square Wave)

The time for the capacitor to charge from $-V_{sat} \cdot \beta$ to $+V_{sat} \cdot \beta$ (and vice versa) is **half the period** $(T/2)$.

The capacitor voltage during charging:
$$V_c(t) = V_{final} + (V_{initial} - V_{final}) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

At the switching instant $t = T/2$, $V_c(T/2) = \beta \cdot V_{sat}$.

Solving yields:
$$\frac{T}{2} = RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$$

## 2.3 Triangular Wave Generator — Block Architecture

It consists of **two cascaded op-amp stages**:

1. **Op-amp 1 — Schmitt Trigger (Comparator with Hysteresis):** Generates a square wave.
2. **Op-amp 2 — Active Integrator (Miller Integrator):** Integrates the square wave to produce a triangle wave.

The feedback from the integrator output back to the Schmitt trigger's input creates a **closed-loop regenerative system** that auto-oscillates.

## 2.4 Sawtooth Wave Generator — Modification Theory

A sawtooth wave is obtained by replacing the **input resistor $R$** of the integrator with a **constant current source** (or by using a transistor switch). This forces a **linear (constant-slope) charging current** rather than exponential, and the use of a **reset transistor** creates the sharp drop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Function generators are the **heart of test and measurement equipment** — used in laboratories to test amplifiers, filters, ADCs, communication circuits, and oscillators. They are also used in **PWM motor drives, sweep generators in CROs, audio synthesizers, and radar modulators**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Waveform** | **Frequency Formula** | **Time Period** | **Peak Amplitude** | **Key Components** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Square Wave | $f = \dfrac{1}{2RC \ln\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$ | $T = 2RC \ln\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$ | $\pm V_{sat}$ | Op-amp + $R_1, R_2$ + $R, C$ |
| Triangular Wave | $f = \dfrac{R_2}{4 R C R_1}$ (approx.) | $T = \dfrac{4 R C R_1}{R_2}$ | $\pm \dfrac{R_2 V_{sat}}{2 R_1}$ | Op-amp (Schmitt) + Op-amp (Integrator) |
| Sawtooth Wave | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_r + T_f}$ | $T_r \neq T_f$ (asymmetric) | Set by comparator thresholds | Comparator + Current source Integrator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention Used:** The vertical bar in $\vert \beta \vert$ and modulus expressions is written as `\vert` in LaTeX to preserve the markdown table structure.

### Critical Design Relationships

- **Feedback factor:** $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$
- **For symmetric square wave ($\beta = 0.473$):** $T = 2RC$
- **Integrator output slope:** $\dfrac{dV_o}{dt} = -\dfrac{V_{in}}{RC}$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Square Wave Frequency

### Given Conditions
- Op-amp output saturates at $\pm V_{sat}$
- Positive feedback ratio: $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$
- RC timing network: Resistor $R$, Capacitor $C$

### Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the general capacitor charging equation. The voltage across a capacitor charging from initial voltage $V_i$ toward final voltage $V_f$ through resistance $R$ is given by:

$$V_c(t) = V_f + (V_i - V_f) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 2:** Apply initial and final conditions for the first half cycle.

Suppose at $t = 0$, the output switches to $+V_{sat}$. The capacitor then charges from its previous value $- \beta V_{sat}$ toward the new final value $+V_{sat}$.

Substituting:
$$V_i = -\beta V_{sat}, \quad V_f = +V_{sat}, \quad V_c(T/2) = +\beta V_{sat}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute these into the capacitor equation.

$$+\beta V_{sat} = +V_{sat} + (-\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat}) \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the bracket term.

$$+\beta V_{sat} = +V_{sat} - (1+\beta) V_{sat} \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to isolate the exponential term.

$$(1+\beta) V_{sat} \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)} = V_{sat} - \beta V_{sat} = (1-\beta) V_{sat}$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $V_{sat}$ from both sides.

$$(1+\beta) \cdot e^{-T/(2RC)} = (1-\beta)$$

**Step 7:** Take natural logarithm on both sides.

$$-\frac{T}{2RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}\right)$$

**Step 8:** Multiply both sides by $-2RC$.

$$T = 2RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$$

**Step 9:** Frequency is the reciprocal of period.

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$$

### Special Case Analysis
For a **symmetric 50% duty cycle**, the standard choice is $\beta = 0.473$ (i.e., $R_1 = R_2$, ignoring load), which gives:

$$f = \frac{1}{2RC}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The $50\%$ duty cycle is achieved specifically when $\beta \approx 0.473$ because $\ln\left(\dfrac{1+0.473}{1-0.473}\right) = \ln(2.793) \approx 1.027 \approx 1$. This is the most-tested configuration.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Triangular Wave Amplitude and Frequency

### Circuit Setup
- **Op-amp A1:** Schmitt trigger with feedback resistors $R_1$ (inverting input to ground) and $R_2$ (from integrator output to inverting input).
- **Op-amp A2:** Integrator with input resistor $R$ and feedback capacitor $C$.

### Amplitude Derivation

**Step 1:** The Schmitt trigger output $V_{sq}$ switches between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$.

**Step 2:** At the inverting input of the Schmitt trigger, by virtual short principle (KCL):

$$\frac{0 - V_{sq}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{tri} - 0}{R_2} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the integrator output voltage.

$$V_{tri} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sq}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the two saturation states:

$$V_{tri, peak} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}$$

$$V_{tri, trough} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}$$

**Step 5:** Peak-to-peak amplitude of the triangular wave:

$$V_{tri, pp} = 2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}$$

### Frequency Derivation

**Step 1:** The slope of the integrator output is:

$$\frac{dV_{tri}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{sq}}{RC}$$

**Step 2:** During one quarter period, the triangular wave rises from $0$ to $+V_{tri, peak}$ with constant slope. The duration is:

$$\frac{T}{4} = \frac{V_{tri, peak}}{\vert dV_{tri}/dt \vert}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$\frac{T}{4} = \frac{(R_2/R_1) \cdot V_{sat}}{V_{sat}/RC} = \frac{R_2 \cdot RC}{R_1}$$

**Step 4:** Full period:

$$T = \frac{4 R_2 R C}{R_1}$$

**Step 5:** Frequency:

$$f = \frac{R_1}{4 R_2 R C}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Expected Outcomes

| **CO Mapping** | **Bloom's Level** | **Concept Tested** | **Mark Weightage** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO1: Understand op-amp waveform circuits | L2 – Understand | Identify circuit topology | 3 marks |
| CO2: Analyze frequency formulas | L4 – Analyze | Derive $f$ in terms of $R, C, \beta$ | 7 marks |
| CO3: Design function generator | L6 – Create | Component selection for given $f$ | 7 marks |
| CO4: Compare waveform outputs | L5 – Evaluate | Distinguish square vs. triangular vs. sawtooth | 3 marks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Bloom's Distribution:** Approximately 30% L2-L3 (remember/understand), 50% L4-L5 (analyze/evaluate), and 20% L6 (design/create). Always frame answers to demonstrate the relevant cognitive level.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Square Wave Generator — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[DC Supply ±Vcc] --> B[Op-Amp LM741]
    C[R1 Resistor] --> B
    D[R2 Resistor Feedback] --> B
    E[R Timing Resistor] --> F[Capacitor C]
    B -->|Square Wave Output| G[Output Vo ±Vsat]
    F --> B
    D --> B
    H[Non-inverting Input V+] --> B
    I[Inverting Input V-] --> F
```

> [!TIP]
> **Block Interpretation:**
> - The DC supply sets the saturation rails $\pm V_{sat}$.
> - $R_1$ and $R_2$ form the **positive feedback divider** that sets the threshold.
> - $R$ and $C$ form the **timing network** that determines the oscillation frequency.
> - The output is a **square wave** alternating between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$.

## 4.2 Triangular Wave Generator — Cascaded Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputNode[Reference Node Ground] --> A1[Op-Amp 1: Schmitt Trigger]
    A1 -->|Square Wave Vs| Rint[Input Resistor R]
    Rint --> NodeX[Inverting Input of Integrator]
    NodeX --> A2[Op-Amp 2: Active Integrator]
    CapFB[Feedback Capacitor C] --> NodeX
    A2 -->|Triangular Output Vtri| OutNode[Output]
    OutNode --> R2fb[Feedback Resistor R2]
    R2fb --> A1
    R1gnd[R1 to Ground] --> A1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cascade Working:** The Schmitt trigger output (square wave) drives the integrator. The integrator output (triangle wave) is fed back to the Schmitt trigger's inverting input. This **closed-loop** ensures continuous oscillation without external triggering.

## 4.3 Sawtooth Wave Generator — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC[DC Reference] --> A1b[Op-Amp Comparator]
    A1b -->|Pulse Output| Switch1[Transistor Reset Switch]
    Switch1 --> A2b[Op-Amp Integrator with Current Source]
    A2b -->|Ramp + Sharp Drop| OutSaw[Sawtooth Wave Output]
    OutSaw --> R2fb2[Feedback to Comparator]
    A1b --> OutSaw
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Sawtooth Distinguishing Feature:** The integrator uses a **current source** (instead of a resistor) for constant charging current, producing a **linear ramp**. The transistor switch rapidly discharges the capacitor to create the sharp drop, yielding the characteristic sawtooth shape.

## 4.4 Complete Functional Architecture — All Three Waveforms

```mermaid
graph TD
    StartBlock[Function Generator Core] --> Stage1[Square Wave Generator Stage]
    Stage1 --> OutputA[Output: Square Wave at Vo1]
    StartBlock --> Stage2[Triangular Wave Generator Stage]
    Stage2 --> Cascade1[Schmitt Trigger Subsystem]
    Cascade1 --> Cascade2[Integrator Subsystem]
    Cascade2 --> OutputB[Output: Triangular Wave at Vo2]
    StartBlock --> Stage3[Sawtooth Wave Generator Stage]
    Stage3 --> Sub3A[Comparator Subsystem]
    Sub3A --> Sub3B[Constant Current Integrator Subsystem]
    Sub3B --> Sub3C[Transistor Reset Subsystem]
    Sub3C --> OutputC[Output: Sawtooth Wave at Vo3]
```

### Component Summary Table

| **Waveform Type** | **Op-Amps Required** | **Key Passive Elements** | **Active Devices** | **Output Shape** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Square Wave | **1** | $R_1, R_2, R, C$ | None | Rectangular pulse train |
| Triangular Wave | **2** | $R_1, R_2, R, C$ | None | Linear rise and fall |
| Sawtooth Wave | **2** | $R, C$ | **1 Transistor** (2N2222 or BC547) | Ramp + abrupt drop |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Lab Pitfall (KTU 2024 Manual):** Students often use a single op-amp for triangular wave generation, which is **incorrect**. The triangular wave **strictly requires two op-amps** — one comparator and one integrator. A single op-amp can only produce a square wave.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an astable multivibrator using an op-amp. Why is it called "astable"?**

**Model Answer:**
An astable multivibrator is a regenerative op-amp circuit that has **no stable state** — its output continuously switches between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$ without any external triggering pulse. It is called "astable" (meaning "not stable") because the circuit has **two quasi-stable states**, and it keeps alternating between them at a rate determined by the RC time constant. The output is a **square wave**.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three differences between a triangular wave generator and a sawtooth wave generator.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Triangular Wave Generator** | **Sawtooth Wave Generator** |
|---|---|
| Uses **resistor** in integrator input | Uses **constant current source** in integrator input |
| Rise time **equals** fall time (symmetric) | Rise time **not equal** to fall time (asymmetric) |
| Two equal linear slopes per cycle | One long linear ramp + one sharp reset |
| No transistor reset switch required | **Transistor reset switch** is mandatory |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Internal Choice A]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **op-amp based square wave generator**. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a square wave generator to produce a **1 kHz signal** with **50% duty cycle** using an op-amp 741. Assume suitable component values. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Solution — Circuit and Operation

The op-amp square wave generator consists of:
- Op-amp 741 in **open-loop comparator mode**
- **Positive feedback** through $R_1$ and $R_2$
- **RC timing network** between output and inverting input

**Operation Sequence:**

1. Assume initial output $V_o = +V_{sat}$. Then the non-inverting input voltage is $V_+ = +\beta V_{sat}$, where $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
2. Capacitor $C$ charges through $R$ toward $+V_{sat}$.
3. When capacitor voltage $V_-$ just exceeds $V_+ = +\beta V_{sat}$, the differential input becomes negative and the op-amp output **switches to $-V_{sat}$**.
4. Now $V_+ = -\beta V_{sat}$, and capacitor begins discharging toward $-V_{sat}$.
5. When $V_-$ falls below $V_+ = -\beta V_{sat}$, output flips back to $+V_{sat}$.
6. The cycle continues, producing a **square wave** at the output.

**Frequency Derivation:**

Using the capacitor charging equation and applying the switching condition $V_-(T/2) = \beta V_{sat}$:

$$f = \frac{1}{2RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$$

For 50% duty cycle, $\beta = 0.473$, which simplifies to $f = 1/(2RC)$.

#### Part (b) Solution — Design

**Given:** $f = 1\text{ kHz}$, duty cycle = 50%

**Step 1:** For 50% duty cycle, choose $\beta = 0.473$, which means $R_1 = R_2$. Let $R_1 = R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 2:** Using the simplified formula:

$$RC = \frac{1}{2f} = \frac{1}{2 \times 1000} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\text{ s} = 500\text{ }\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Choose $C = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ (standard value). Then:

$$R = \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 5000\text{ }\Omega = 5\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Use $R = 5\text{ k}\Omega$ (standard), $C = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$.

**Final Component List:**
- Op-amp: **LM741**
- $R_1 = R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$
- $R = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$
- Supply: **$\pm 12\text{ V}$**
- Output: Square wave of **$\pm 11\text{ V}$** at **1 kHz**

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module Internal Choice B]`

**(a)** Explain the working of a **triangular wave generator** using two op-amps with a neat block diagram. Derive its frequency of oscillation and peak output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a circuit diagram, describe the generation of a **sawtooth waveform using an op-amp integrator**. Mention the role of the transistor in the circuit. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) Solution — Triangular Wave Generator

**Block Diagram Explanation:**

- **Op-amp A1 (Schmitt Trigger):** Compares the integrator output with reference and produces a square wave.
- **Op-amp A2 (Active Integrator):** Integrates the square wave output to produce a linear ramp.
- The triangular output is fed back to the inverting input of the Schmitt trigger via $R_2$, closing the loop.

**Frequency Derivation:**

Applying KCL at the inverting input of the Schmitt trigger:

$$V_{tri} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sq}$$

During one-quarter period, the integrator ramps from $0$ to peak value:

$$\frac{T}{4} = \frac{R_2 \cdot RC}{R_1}$$

Therefore:

$$T = \frac{4 R_2 RC}{R_1}, \quad f = \frac{R_1}{4 R_2 R C}$$

**Peak Output Voltage:**

$$V_{tri, peak} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}$$

#### Part (b) Solution — Sawtooth Wave Generator

**Circuit Description:**

- The comparator (op-amp) produces a square wave output.
- This drives the integrator, but the input **resistor is replaced by a constant current source** (implemented using a **P-channel JFET** or **transistor current mirror**).
- A **transistor switch** (typically a **BC547 NPN transistor**) is connected across the feedback capacitor of the integrator.
- During the ramp period, the transistor is **OFF**, and the capacitor charges linearly through the current source.
- When the comparator output switches, the transistor turns **ON** for a brief moment, **rapidly discharging** the capacitor to near zero, producing the **sharp drop** of the sawtooth.

**Role of the Transistor:**
The transistor acts as a **rapid reset switch** that dumps the charge on the capacitor in a very short duration, creating the characteristic **abrupt falling edge** of the sawtooth waveform.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "astable" with "monostable":** A square wave generator is **astable** (no stable state). A monostable has one stable state. **Do not mix these up.**
> 2. **Forgetting the natural log:** Many students write $T = 2RC$ for square wave without verifying the $\beta$ condition. Marks are deducted if the full expression $T = 2RC \ln[(1+\beta)/(1-\beta)]$ is not shown.
> 3. **Mixing triangular and sawtooth:** Triangular = **two equal slopes**. Sawtooth = **one ramp + one sharp drop**. Drawing a sawtooth and calling it triangular (or vice versa) is a **fatal error**.
> 4. **Wrong op-amp count:** Triangular needs **2 op-amps**. Students often write 1 — **this is a recurring 2-mark deduction**.
> 5. **Missing the transistor in sawtooth:** The reset transistor is a **mandatory active component** in a sawtooth generator. Omitting it makes the answer incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:**

- **Square wave generator** uses **1 op-amp** + $R_1, R_2$ (positive feedback) + $R, C$ (timing).
- **Triangular wave generator** uses **2 op-amps** (Schmitt + Integrator).
- **Sawtooth wave generator** uses **2 op-amps + 1 transistor** (reset switch).
- **Frequency of square wave:** $f = 1 / \left[2RC \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)\right]$
- **For 50% duty cycle:** $\beta = 0.473$ and $f = 1/(2RC)$.
- **Feedback factor:** $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
- **Frequency of triangular wave:** $f = R_1 / (4 R_2 R C)$.
- **Peak triangular amplitude:** $V_{tri, peak} = (R_2 / R_1) \cdot V_{sat}$.
- **Integrator output slope:** $dV_o/dt = -V_{in} / (RC)$.
- **Sawtooth = ramp + sharp drop** (asymmetric); **Triangular = ramp + ramp** (symmetric).
- **Standard IC:** **µA741 / LM741** (KTU lab standard).
- **Standard supply:** **$\pm 12\text{ V}$ or $\pm 15\text{ V}$** (dual polarity).
- **Saturation voltage:** $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1\text{ V}$ (approximately).
- **Op-amp 741 pin configuration:** Pin 2 = inverting input, Pin 3 = non-inverting input, Pin 6 = output, Pin 7 = $+V_{CC}$, Pin 4 = $-V_{EE}$.
- **Astable** = no stable state (continuous oscillation). **Monostable** = one stable state (one-shot). **Bistable** = two stable states (flip-flop).
- **Duty cycle** = $(T_{high} / T_{total}) \times 100\%$. Symmetric square wave = 50% duty cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic comparator and Schmitt trigger circuits using Op-amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Basic comparator and Schmitt trigger circuits using Op-amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Basic Voltage Comparator using Op-amp

A **voltage comparator** is an electronic circuit that compares two analog input voltages and produces a binary (digital) output indicating which input is larger. It is essentially an **op-amp operated in open-loop configuration** (without feedback), exploiting its extremely high open-loop gain (typically **A ≈ 10⁵ to 10⁶** for IC 741) to drive the output into saturation.

The output is mathematically expressed as:

$$
V_o = A_{OL} \cdot (V_{+} - V_{-})
$$

When the differential input is even a few microvolts, the enormous gain pushes the output into one of the two saturation levels: **+V_sat** (≈ +13.5 V for ±15 V supply) or **−V_sat** (≈ −13.5 V for ±15 V supply).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A comparator is NOT a linear amplifier. It is a **1-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — its output has only two valid states, making it the fundamental building block of decision-making circuits.

#### Real-World Analogy: The Elevator Button

Imagine a **plain comparator** as an elevator floor indicator:
- Press "Up" → display jumps to the next floor
- Press "Down" → display drops to the lower floor

Now imagine **noisy, trembling fingers** (representing input noise around the threshold). The elevator will jitter wildly between floors. This is exactly what happens in a basic comparator when the input signal hovers near the threshold — multiple spurious transitions occur.

---

### 1.2 Schmitt Trigger using Op-amp

A **Schmitt trigger** is a comparator with **positive (regenerative) feedback** that introduces **hysteresis** — two distinct threshold voltages (Upper Threshold Point, **V_UTP**, and Lower Threshold Point, **V_LTP**) — to suppress noise-induced multiple transitions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Hysteresis:** Hysteresis is the phenomenon where the output state depends not only on the current input but also on the **history (past state)** of the input. Mathematically, hysteresis width $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$.

#### Real-World Analogy: The Home Thermostat

A simple comparator = a thermostat that turns the heater ON at exactly 25°C and OFF at exactly 25°C. If the room temperature wobbles around 25°C, the heater constantly clicks ON/OFF (chattering), damaging the relay.

A **Schmitt trigger** = a smart thermostat:
- Turns heater **ON at 24°C** (V_LTP)
- Turns heater **OFF at 26°C** (V_UTP)
- The 2°C dead-band prevents rapid switching — this dead-band is **hysteresis**.

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Insight:** Hysteresis is **engineered immunity to noise**. The wider the hysteresis, the more noise-immune the circuit, but at the cost of reduced sensitivity to small input changes.

---

### 1.3 Classification Summary

| Feature | Basic Comparator | Schmitt Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback type | None (open loop) | Positive feedback |
| Threshold levels | Single ($V_{ref}$) | Two ($V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$) |
| Noise immunity | Poor | Excellent |
| Output vs Input | Single-valued function | Multi-valued (hysteretic) |
| Switching speed | Limited by slew rate | Faster (regenerative action) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles — Basic Comparator

**Configuration:** Op-amp with no feedback path. Two inputs applied to $V_{+}$ (non-inverting) and $V_{-}$ (inverting).

**Decision logic:**
1. If $V_{+} > V_{-}$ → Output saturates to $+V_{sat}$
2. If $V_{+} < V_{-}$ → Output saturates to $-V_{sat}$
3. Theoretically, when $V_{+} = V_{-}$, output is undefined (in reality, depends on offset)

**Transfer Characteristic:** A vertical jump at $V_{+} = V_{-}$ (slope = open-loop gain A).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students wrongly assume the output transitions through a linear region. In reality, the op-amp is ALWAYS in saturation — only the output level changes. The transition appears almost vertical only because of the very high gain.

### 2.2 Why Basic Comparators Fail — The Noise Problem

When the input signal contains noise riding on it, and the noise amplitude exceeds the few-microvolt threshold band, the comparator produces **multiple unwanted output transitions** (called *chattering* or *bouncing*). This is unacceptable in digital systems.

**Mathematical view:** The op-amp in open-loop has effective gain of ~10⁵. A noise of just **50 µV** at the input can swing the output by 5 V — enough to flip the state.

### 2.3 Operational Principles — Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative Comparator)

The introduction of **positive feedback** (output connected back to the non-inverting input) fundamentally changes the behavior:

1. **Two stable threshold points** exist — UTP and LTP
2. The output state "remembers" itself — switching requires the input to cross the *opposite* threshold
3. Once triggered, the positive feedback accelerates the transition (regenerative action), making switching **faster** than a basic comparator
4. Noise smaller than the hysteresis band is **ignored**

### 2.4 Circuit Configurations

**Inverting Schmitt Trigger:**
- Input signal $V_{in}$ applied to the **inverting terminal** ($V_{-}$)
- Reference (typically ground) at the **non-inverting terminal** via a resistor divider
- Feedback from output to $V_{+}$ through resistor $R_1$; $R_2$ from $V_{+}$ to ground

**Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger:**
- Input signal $V_{in}$ applied to the **non-inverting terminal** ($V_{+}$) through $R_2$
- Inverting terminal $V_{-}$ tied directly to ground
- Feedback from output to $V_{+}$ through $R_1$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready**. Memorize the thresholds for both configurations — they are the most frequently asked derivations in KTU ESE.

| Parameter | Inverting Schmitt Trigger | Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Output saturation levels | $\pm V_{sat}$ | $\pm V_{sat}$ |
| Voltage at $V_{+}$ (superposition) | $V_{+} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_o$ | $V_{+} = \dfrac{R_1 \cdot V_{in} + R_2 \cdot V_o}{R_1 + R_2}$ |
| Upper Threshold Point $V_{UTP}$ | $+\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $+\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}$ |
| Lower Threshold Point $V_{LTP}$ | $-\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $-\,\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}$ |
| Hysteresis Width $V_H$ | $V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \dfrac{2 R_2 V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = \dfrac{2 R_2 V_{sat}}{R_1}$ |
| Switching condition | $V_{in}$ crosses $\pm V_{T}$ | $V_{in}$ crosses $\pm V_{T}$ |
| Output in-phase with input? | No (inverting) | Yes (non-inverting) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Schmitt triggers are used in **signal conditioning** (squaring sine waves into square waves), **zero-crossing detectors with noise immunity**, **relaxation oscillators** (when combined with an RC network), **line receivers** in noisy industrial environments, and **switch debouncing** circuits for mechanical switches.

---

### 2.6 Threshold Voltage — Conceptual Meaning

The threshold voltage $V_T$ is the **critical input voltage at which the comparator changes state**. In a basic comparator, this is set by the reference voltage. In a Schmitt trigger, the threshold dynamically alternates between $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ based on the output state — this dual-threshold behavior **is** hysteresis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold Voltages

**Circuit Setup:**
- $V_{in}$ applied at $V_{-}$ terminal
- Resistor $R_1$ from output $V_o$ to $V_{+}$ terminal
- Resistor $R_2$ from $V_{+}$ terminal to ground
- $V_{ref} = 0$ (virtual ground reference)

**Step 1 — Express $V_{+}$ using superposition:**

Since the non-inverting terminal draws no current (ideal op-amp), $V_{+}$ is the voltage at the junction of $R_1$ and $R_2$, treated as a simple voltage divider between $V_o$ and ground:

$$
V_{+} = V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify switching condition:**

Switching occurs when $V_{+} = V_{-} = V_{in}$:

$$
V_{in} = V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Case 1 ($V_o = +V_{sat}$ — output currently HIGH):**

The input voltage needed to flip the output LOW is found by setting $V_o = +V_{sat}$:

$$
V_{in} = (+V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = +\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

This is the **Upper Threshold Point**, because the input must rise to this value (when output is HIGH) to trigger switching:

$$
\boxed{V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Case 2 ($V_o = -V_{sat}$ — output currently LOW):**

The input voltage needed to flip the output HIGH is found by setting $V_o = -V_{sat}$:

$$
V_{in} = (-V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = -\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

This is the **Lower Threshold Point**:

$$
\boxed{V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Hysteresis Width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} \\[6pt]
&= \left(+\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}\right) - \left(-\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}\right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2 \, R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_H = \frac{2 R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger Threshold Voltages

**Circuit Setup:**
- $V_{in}$ applied at $V_{+}$ terminal via $R_2$
- Resistor $R_1$ from output $V_o$ to $V_{+}$ terminal
- Inverting terminal $V_{-}$ directly grounded
- Therefore, $V_{-} = 0$

**Step 1 — Express $V_{+}$ using superposition (current summing at $V_{+}$ node):**

Since no current enters the op-amp input, KCL at $V_{+}$ gives:

$$
\frac{V_{in} - V_{+}}{R_2} + \frac{V_o - V_{+}}{R_1} = 0
$$

Multiplying through by $R_1 R_2$:

$$
R_1(V_{in} - V_{+}) + R_2(V_o - V_{+}) = 0
$$

Solving for $V_{+}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 V_{in} - R_1 V_{+} + R_2 V_o - R_2 V_{+} &= 0 \\[4pt]
V_{+}(R_1 + R_2) &= R_1 V_{in} + R_2 V_o \\[4pt]
V_{+} &= \frac{R_1 V_{in} + R_2 V_o}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Switching condition: $V_{+} = V_{-} = 0$**

$$
\frac{R_1 V_{in} + R_2 V_o}{R_1 + R_2} = 0
$$

$$
R_1 V_{in} + R_2 V_o = 0
$$

$$
V_{in} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_o
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Case 1 ($V_o = +V_{sat}$):**

$$
V_{in} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1}(+V_{sat}) = -\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1}
$$

This is the **Lower Threshold Point** (input must fall to this value when output is HIGH):

$$
\boxed{V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Case 2 ($V_o = -V_{sat}$):**

$$
V_{in} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1}(-V_{sat}) = +\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1}
$$

This is the **Upper Threshold Point**:

$$
\boxed{V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Hysteresis Width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_H &= V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} \\[4pt]
&= \left(+\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1}\right) - \left(-\frac{R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2 R_2 \, V_{sat}}{R_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_H = \frac{2 R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style Question)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> Design a non-inverting Schmitt trigger using op-amp 741 with $\pm 15$ V supply. Choose $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$. Assume $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5$ V. Calculate $V_{UTP}$, $V_{LTP}$, and hysteresis width $V_H$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify formula (Non-inverting Schmitt trigger):**

$$
V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}, \quad V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}, \quad V_H = \frac{2 R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{1\,\text{k}\Omega}{10\,\text{k}\Omega} = 0.1
$$

$$
V_{UTP} = +0.1 \times 13.5 = +1.35 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{LTP} = -0.1 \times 13.5 = -1.35 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_H = 2 \times 0.1 \times 13.5 = 2.7 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- When $V_{in}$ rises above **+1.35 V**, output switches from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$
- When $V_{in}$ falls below **−1.35 V**, output switches back from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$
- Any noise of amplitude less than **1.35 V peak** riding on the input will NOT cause unwanted switching — this is the noise-immunity advantage

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Design Parameter | Inverting ST | Non-Inverting ST |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold depends on $R_2$ alone? | No (ratio $R_2/(R_1+R_2)$) | Yes (ratio $R_2/R_1$) |
| Hysteresis controllable via | $R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ ratio | $R_2 / R_1$ ratio |
| Easy to set $V_{UTP} = 1$ V? | Requires ratio ≈ 0.074 | Requires ratio = 0.074 (with $V_{sat}=13.5$) |
| Output polarity when input rises | Output goes LOW ($-V_{sat}$) | Output goes HIGH ($+V_{sat}$) |
| Typical application | Zero-crossing detector | Square-wave generator, sine-to-square converter |

> [!TIP]
> **Designer Rule of Thumb:** To set a desired hysteresis voltage $V_H$ in a non-inverting Schmitt trigger, use $R_2/R_1 = V_H / (2 V_{sat})$. The choice of $R_1 + R_2$ (typically 10 kΩ to 100 kΩ) is then made for bias-current considerations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Basic Op-amp Comparator

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Inputs
        Vin1["V1 Input Signal"]
        Vin2["V2 Reference Voltage"]
    end
    subgraph OpAmpBlock["Open Loop Op-Amp Comparator"]
        OpAmpSymbol["Op-Amp IC 741"]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage["Digital Output"]
        VoutHi["Plus V_sat"]
        VoutLo["Minus V_sat"]
    end
    Vin1 -- Non-inverting Plus --> OpAmpSymbol
    Vin2 -- Inverting Minus --> OpAmpSymbol
    OpAmpSymbol -- V1 Greater Than V2 --> VoutHi
    OpAmpSymbol -- V1 Less Than V2 --> VoutLo
    style OpAmpBlock fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style OutputStage fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
```

**Reading the diagram:** Two analog voltages enter the op-amp. The op-amp, having no feedback, behaves as a high-gain decision device. The output is forced to one of the two saturation rails.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Inverting Schmitt Trigger

```mermaid
graph LR
    Vin["V in Input Signal"] --> InvTerm["Inverting Terminal Minus"]
    FBNet["Feedback Divider R1 R2"] --> NonInvTerm["Non-Inverting Terminal Plus"]
    Vout["V out Plus or Minus V_sat"] --> FBNet
    InvTerm --> OpCore["Op-Amp Core"]
    NonInvTerm --> OpCore
    OpCore --> Vout
    style OpCore fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    style FBNet fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#c2185b
```

**Key observation:** The feedback path from $V_{out}$ feeds back to the **non-inverting** input through $R_1$–$R_2$ — this is the *positive* feedback that creates the bistable (two-threshold) behavior.

---

### 4.3 State Transition Flow — Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateLow
    StateLow: Output Equals Minus V_sat
    StateHigh: Output Equals Plus V_sat
    StateLow --> StateHigh : V in rises above V UTP
    StateHigh --> StateLow : V in falls below V LTP
    StateLow --> StateLow : V in stays between V LTP and V UTP
    StateHigh --> StateHigh : V in stays between V LTP and V UTP
```

**Reading the diagram:** The output remains in either $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ state until the input crosses the *opposite* threshold. The dead-band (the gap between the two thresholds) is the hysteresis window.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Hysteresis Works

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["Input Signal plus Noise"] --> CompareStage["Comparator Core with Plus Feedback"]
    ThresholdLogic["Dynamic Threshold Selector based on Output State"] --> CompareStage
    OutputStage["Saturated Output Plus V_sat or Minus V_sat"] --> ThresholdLogic
    CompareStage --> OutputStage
    OutputStage --> Load["Digital Load or Next Stage"]
    style Input fill:#e1f5fe
    style CompareStage fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style ThresholdLogic fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457
    style OutputStage fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Architectural insight:** The output stage feeds back to dynamically choose the threshold. This creates a **memory effect** — the circuit "remembers" whether it was last high or low, and uses the appropriate threshold to switch.

---

### 4.5 Component-Level Wiring Reference (IC 741 Pin Map)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph IC741["IC 741 Op-Amp Package"]
        Pin1["Pin 1 Offset Null"]
        Pin2["Pin 2 Inverting Input"]
        Pin3["Pin 3 Non-Inverting Input"]
        Pin4["Pin 4 V minus 15 V Supply"]
        Pin5["Pin 5 Offset Null"]
        Pin6["Pin 6 Output"]
        Pin7["Pin 7 V plus 15 V Supply"]
        Pin8["Pin 8 NC"]
    end
    InSig["V in Signal Source"] --> Pin2
    Pin3 --> GNDref["Ground Reference via R2"]
    Pin6 --> FBPath["Feedback Resistor R1"]
    FBPath --> Pin3
    Pin6 --> OutTerm["V out to CRO or Load"]
    Pin4 --> Vneg["Minus 15 V DC"]
    Pin7 --> Vpos["Plus 15 V DC"]
    style IC741 fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00,stroke-width:2px
    style FBPath fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Lab Tip:** Always connect pins 1 and 5 (offset null) to a 10 kΩ potentiometer wiper with ends to $V_{-}$ supply for IC 741 to null the input offset voltage, ensuring accurate threshold calculations. Pin 8 is No Connection (NC) on the 8-pin DIP package.

---

### 4.6 Transfer Characteristic Visualization (Inverting Schmitt Trigger)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Inverting Schmitt Trigger Transfer Characteristic"
    x-axis "V in Volts" [-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3]
    y-axis "V out Volts" [-15, -10, -5, 0, 5, 10, 15]
    line [-13.5, -13.5, -13.5, 13.5, 13.5, 13.5, 13.5]
```

**Interpretation of the curve:**
- For $V_{in} > V_{UTP}$ (right of threshold) → $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ (LOW)
- For $V_{in} < V_{LTP}$ (left of threshold) → $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ (HIGH)
- The vertical jump in the middle represents the rapid regenerative switching at the threshold
- The **direction is inverted**: rising input causes falling output — hence "inverting"

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always label axes properly in the KTU exam. A transfer characteristic without axis labels is marked down by at least 1 mark out of 7.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define a voltage comparator. Why is it called a "1-bit ADC"?**

**Model Answer:**
A voltage comparator is an op-amp circuit operated in open-loop configuration that compares two analog input voltages ($V_{+}$ and $V_{-}$) and produces a binary output indicating which input is larger. The output saturates to $+V_{sat}$ when $V_{+} > V_{-}$ and to $-V_{sat}$ when $V_{+} < V_{-}$.

It is called a **1-bit ADC** because it converts a continuously varying analog signal into a single-bit digital representation: a HIGH (1) or LOW (0) output. *[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for output states, 1 for 1-bit ADC justification]*

---

**Q2. What is hysteresis in a Schmitt trigger? Why is it essential for noisy input signals?**

**Model Answer:**
Hysteresis is the property by which the switching threshold of a circuit depends on the direction (history) of the input change. In a Schmitt trigger, this is implemented by having two threshold voltages — $V_{UTP}$ and $V_{LTP}$ — separated by the hysteresis width $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$.

It is essential because, in a basic comparator, noise near the threshold causes **multiple unwanted output transitions (chattering)**. The hysteresis dead-band ensures that noise smaller than $V_H/2$ does not flip the output, providing **noise immunity**. *[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula $V_H$, 1 for noise-immunity explanation]*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer ONE full question (with both sub-parts).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**(a) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a Schmitt trigger using an op-amp. Derive the expressions for the Upper Threshold Point (UTP) and Lower Threshold Point (LTP) of an inverting Schmitt trigger.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:** Op-amp 741 with $V_{in}$ at inverting terminal, $R_1$ from output to non-inverting terminal, $R_2$ from non-inverting terminal to ground. *(1 mark for diagram)*

**Operation Explanation:**

The circuit uses **positive feedback** through the resistor divider $R_1$–$R_2$. The voltage at the non-inverting terminal is:

$$
V_{+} = V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

Depending on whether $V_{in}$ is greater or less than $V_{+}$, the op-amp output saturates to $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$. The output, in turn, changes $V_{+}$, creating a self-reinforcing (regenerative) switching action. *(2 marks for operation)*

**Derivation of Thresholds:**

Switching occurs when $V_{in} = V_{+}$:

$$
V_{in} = V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**When $V_o = +V_{sat}$:**

$$
V_{in} = +\frac{R_2 V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{UTP}
$$

**When $V_o = -V_{sat}$:**

$$
V_{in} = -\frac{R_2 V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{LTP}
$$

*(3 marks for derivation; 1 mark for final boxed expressions)*

---

**(b) Design an inverting Schmitt trigger using op-amp 741 to obtain a hysteresis width of 4 V. Assume the supply voltages are ±15 V and $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5$ V. Also calculate the values of UTP and LTP.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Choose a convenient value of $R_1$.**

Let $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (standard value, also limits bias current). *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Use hysteresis formula to find $R_2$:**

$$
V_H = \frac{2 R_2 V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

Substituting $V_H = 4$ V, $V_{sat} = 13.5$ V, $R_1 = 10$ kΩ:

$$
4 = \frac{2 \cdot R_2 \cdot 13.5}{10\,\text{k}\Omega + R_2}
$$

$$
4(10\,\text{k}\Omega + R_2) = 27 R_2
$$

$$
40\,\text{k}\Omega + 4 R_2 = 27 R_2
$$

$$
40\,\text{k}\Omega = 23 R_2
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{40\,\text{k}\Omega}{23} \approx 1.74\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

Choose standard value: $R_2 = 1.8\,\text{k}\Omega$. *(2 marks for derivation)*

**Step 3 — Calculate thresholds:**

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1.8}{10 + 1.8} = \frac{1.8}{11.8} \approx 0.1525
$$

$$
V_{UTP} = +0.1525 \times 13.5 \approx +2.06 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{LTP} = -0.1525 \times 13.5 \approx -2.06 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} \approx 4.12 \text{ V} \approx 4 \text{ V (verified)}
$$

*(3 marks for numerical evaluation; 1 mark for design summary statement)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]

**(a) Explain the operation of a non-inverting Schmitt trigger. Derive the expressions for UTP and LTP.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** $V_{in}$ applied to the non-inverting terminal via $R_2$, $R_1$ from output to non-inverting terminal, inverting terminal grounded.

**Operation:**

Using superposition at $V_{+}$ (no current into op-amp):

$$
V_{+} = \frac{R_1 V_{in} + R_2 V_o}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

Switching occurs when $V_{+} = V_{-} = 0$:

$$
R_1 V_{in} + R_2 V_o = 0 \implies V_{in} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_o
$$

**When $V_o = +V_{sat}$:**

$$
V_{in} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat} = V_{LTP}
$$

**When $V_o = -V_{sat}$:**

$$
V_{in} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat} = V_{UTP}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{V_{UTP} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}, \quad V_{LTP} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}}
$$

*(3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for boxed results)*

---

**(b) For the non-inverting Schmitt trigger of part (a), given $R_1 = 15\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{sat} = \pm 14$ V, draw the transfer characteristic and explain the operation with a triangular input of amplitude 5 V.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute thresholds:**

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{4.7}{15} = 0.3133
$$

$$
V_{UTP} = +0.3133 \times 14 = +4.39 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{LTP} = -0.3133 \times 14 = -4.39 \text{ V}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 2 — Analyze input behavior:**

The triangular input of amplitude 5 V has peaks at $+5$ V and $-5$ V.

- When input **rises past +4.39 V**, output switches from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$
- When input **falls past −4.39 V**, output switches back to $-V_{sat}$
- Between $-4.39$ V and $+4.39$ V, the output **retains its previous state** (hysteresis) *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Transfer characteristic sketch:**

The transfer characteristic is a **rectangular hysteresis loop**:
- Right branch (rising $V_{in}$): vertical jump at $V_{in} = +4.39$ V from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$
- Left branch (falling $V_{in}$): vertical jump at $V_{in} = -4.39$ V from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$
- Output stays constant at $+V_{sat}$ for all $V_{in}$ less than $+4.39$ V on the descending path
- Output stays constant at $-V_{sat}$ for all $V_{in}$ greater than $-4.39$ V on the ascending path *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Conversion to square wave:**

The triangular input is converted into a square wave with amplitude $V_{sat}$ (HIGH for ~half cycle, LOW for ~half cycle). The transition points are at $\pm 4.39$ V, **not** at 0 V — this asymmetry/delay is the hysteresis in action. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing inverting vs non-inverting formulas** — The most common error. Inverting ST: $R_2/(R_1+R_2)$. Non-inverting ST: $R_2/R_1$. Mixing these yields 0 marks for derivation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the superposition step** — Writing $V_{+} = V_o$ directly is a fatal mistake. The voltage divider ratio MUST be shown explicitly.
>
> 3. **Using $V_{CC}$ instead of $V_{sat}$** — The op-amp output cannot reach $V_{CC}$ (typically ±15 V); it saturates at ~±13.5 V for IC 741. Using 15 V in threshold calculations is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Missing the transfer characteristic sketch** — In design problems, ALWAYS include a labelled transfer characteristic. Missing this costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 5. **Not specifying input-output relationship in words** — "When $V_{in}$ exceeds $V_{UTP}$, the output saturates to ... " — this verbal explanation carries at least 2 marks in the operation part.
>
> 6. **Wrong sign convention for UTP/LTP** — UTP is the more positive threshold; LTP is the more negative one. Swapping them is a deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Schmitt Trigger & Comparator (KTU Module 2)**

- [x] A **basic comparator** is an op-amp in **open-loop**; output is always $\pm V_{sat}$.
- [x] A **Schmitt trigger** uses **positive feedback** to create **two thresholds**.
- [x] **Hysteresis width** $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$ determines noise immunity.
- [x] **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** thresholds: $V_{UTP, LTP} = \pm \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$
- [x] **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger** thresholds: $V_{UTP, LTP} = \pm \dfrac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}$
- [x] **Inverting ST** gives $V_H = \dfrac{2 R_2 V_{sat}}{R_1 + R_2}$
- [x] **Non-Inverting ST** gives $V_H = \dfrac{2 R_2 V_{sat}}{R_1}$
- [x] Switching condition is always: $V_{in} = V_{+}$ (inverting) or $V_{+} = 0$ (non-inverting).
- [x] **Positive feedback** accelerates switching → faster than basic comparator.
- [x] **Design rule:** To set hysteresis to $V_H$, solve for the resistor ratio first, then pick a total resistance (typically 10 kΩ–100 kΩ) to limit bias current.
- [x] **$V_{sat} \neq V_{CC}$**: For IC 741 with ±15 V supply, use $V_{sat} = \pm 13.5$ V (not ±15 V).
- [x] **Transfer characteristic** of inverting ST is an **inverted hysteresis loop**; of non-inverting ST is a **normal hysteresis loop**.
- [x] Applications: signal squaring, zero-crossing detector, relaxation oscillator, switch debouncing, line receiver, ADC front-end.
- [x] **Noise immunity** of Schmitt trigger is proportional to $V_H$; wider hysteresis → more immunity but less sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable circuits using 555 IC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable circuits using 555 IC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: Astable and Monostable Circuits using 555 IC

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The 555 Timer IC — Formal Definition

The **NE555 / SE555 Timer IC** is a highly stable, monolithic timing circuit capable of producing accurate time delays or oscillation. It is an **8-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** device that can operate in three fundamental modes: *Astable* (free-running oscillator), *Monostable* (one-shot), and *Bistable* (flip-flop). It was designed in 1971 by Hans R. Camenzind and remains one of the most popular analog ICs ever manufactured — over **1 billion units** are produced annually.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 555 IC is treated as a universal building block for waveform generation. Students must master its internal comparators, SR flip-flop, discharge transistor, and output stage to excel in lab viva-voce questions.

### 1.2 Internal Architecture (Block-Level)

The 555 consists of:
- A **voltage divider** (three 5 kΩ resistors — hence the name "555")
- **Two comparators** (Threshold and Trigger)
- An **SR Flip-Flop** (latch)
- A **Discharge transistor** (open-collector NPN)
- An **Output buffer/driver** (totem-pole)

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water-Bucket Logic"

Imagine two buckets representing the **upper threshold (2/3 Vcc)** and **lower threshold (1/3 Vcc)** of a water tank:

- **Astable Mode** → Imagine a tap that automatically fills and empties the tank *continuously without any external push*. Each time water reaches the upper bucket, a siphon empties it; when it falls to the lower bucket, the tap refills it. The tank oscillates **forever** like a heartbeat.
- **Monostable Mode** → Imagine the tank stays *empty (stable state)* until you press a button (trigger pulse). It then fills up once to the upper level and drains back, returning to its empty stable state. It waits for the **next button press**.

### 1.4 Pin Configuration — The 8 Sacred Pins

| Pin # | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | GND | Ground reference (**0 V**) |
| 2 | TRIG | Trigger input (active LOW; switches below 1/3 Vcc) |
| 3 | OUT | Output (push-pull; sources/sinks up to **200 mA**) |
| 4 | RESET | Active LOW reset (forces output LOW) |
| 5 | CTRL | Control voltage (2/3 Vcc internal reference) |
| 6 | THR | Threshold input (compares with 2/3 Vcc) |
| 7 | DIS | Discharge pin (open-collector NPN emitter) |
| 8 | Vcc | Supply voltage (**+4.5 V to +15 V**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Operating Voltage:** Most lab experiments use **+5 V** or **+9 V**. The control voltage pin (Pin 5) is usually bypassed to ground via a **0.01 µF** decoupling capacitor to prevent noise injection.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mode I — Astable Multivibrator (Free-Running Oscillator)

In astable mode, the 555 has **no stable state**. It continuously switches between HIGH and LOW, generating a **square wave** without any external trigger.

#### Operational Phases:

**Phase A — Charging (Output HIGH):**
- The discharge transistor (Pin 7) is **OFF**.
- Capacitor **C** charges through the series combination of **R_A** and **R_B** toward Vcc.
- The voltage across C rises exponentially.
- Charging continues until V_C reaches **2/3 Vcc** (upper threshold).
- At this point, the threshold comparator resets the internal flip-flop → Output goes **LOW**.

**Phase B — Discharging (Output LOW):**
- The discharge transistor is now **ON**.
- Capacitor **C** discharges through **R_B only** (R_A is bypassed because discharge transistor shorts Pin 7 to ground).
- The voltage across C falls exponentially.
- Discharging continues until V_C reaches **1/3 Vcc** (lower threshold).
- At this point, the trigger comparator sets the flip-flop → Output goes **HIGH**, and the cycle repeats.

#### KTU Formula Sheet — Astable Mode

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Charging Time (t_c) | $0.693 \times (R_A + R_B) \times C$ | seconds | Time output remains HIGH |
| Discharging Time (t_d) | $0.693 \times R_B \times C$ | seconds | Time output remains LOW |
| Total Time Period (T) | $t_c + t_d = 0.693 \times (R_A + 2R_B) \times C$ | seconds | One complete cycle |
| Frequency (f) | $\dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \times C}$ | Hz (Hertz) | Oscillation frequency |
| Duty Cycle (D) | $\dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | percent | HIGH time / Total time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight — The "1.44" Factor:** This magic constant comes from $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$ multiplied by 2. Since $\ln(2) \times 2 = 1.386 \approx 1.44$, this approximation is memorized for KTU lab calculations and viva.

#### Real-World Applications of Astable Mode:
- **LED flashers and blinkers** (decorative lighting, indicators)
- **Pulse generation** for digital logic and microcontrollers
- **Tone generation** in sirens and alarms
- **Clock pulse generator** for sequential logic circuits
- **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** for motor speed control
- **Function generator** building block

---

### 2.2 Mode II — Monostable Multivibrator (One-Shot)

In monostable mode, the 555 has **one stable state** (Output LOW) and **one unstable/temporary state** (Output HIGH). It produces a **single pulse** of fixed width when triggered.

#### Operational Phases:

**Stable State (Resting):**
- The discharge transistor (Pin 7) is **ON**, keeping capacitor C shorted to ground.
- Output is **LOW** (stable, resting condition).
- Circuit waits indefinitely for a trigger.

**Triggered State:**
- A **negative-going trigger pulse** (< 1/3 Vcc) is applied to Pin 2.
- This sets the internal flip-flop → Output goes **HIGH**, discharge transistor turns **OFF**.
- Capacitor C now charges through external resistor **R** toward Vcc.
- Charging continues until V_C reaches **2/3 Vcc**.
- At this point, threshold comparator resets the flip-flop → Output returns to **LOW**, discharge transistor turns ON, C rapidly discharges.
- Circuit returns to stable state and awaits next trigger.

#### KTU Formula Sheet — Monostable Mode

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pulse Width (T) | $1.1 \times R \times C$ | seconds | Duration of output HIGH |
| Trigger Pulse Width | Should be < T | seconds | Negative pulse on Pin 2 |
| Trigger Voltage Level | $< \dfrac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ | volts | Threshold to initiate pulse |
| Charging Voltage Range | $\dfrac{1}{3} V_{cc} \text{ to } \dfrac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ | volts | Capacitor voltage sweep |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "1.1" Factor in Monostable:** This constant arises from $\ln(3) \approx 1.098 \approx 1.1$. Since the capacitor charges from 1/3 Vcc to 2/3 Vcc (not 0 to 2/3 Vcc), the natural logarithm factor is $\ln(3)$, not $\ln(2)$.

#### Real-World Applications of Monostable Mode:
- **Missing pulse detection** (heartbeat monitors, watchdog timers)
- **Debouncing mechanical switches** and pushbuttons
- **Frequency division** (divide-by-N counters)
- **Time delay circuits** for relay activation
- **Pulse stretching** for narrow input signals
- **Analog-to-digital conversion timing**

---

### 2.3 Comparative Analysis — Astable vs Monostable

| Feature | Astable | Monostable |
|---|---|---|
| Stable States | **Zero** (no stable state) | **One** (Output LOW) |
| Trigger Required? | **No** (self-triggered) | **Yes** (external trigger on Pin 2) |
| Output Waveform | Continuous square wave | Single pulse per trigger |
| External Components | R_A, R_B, C | R, C |
| Control Voltage Effect | Frequency modulation | Pulse width modulation |
| Typical Use | Oscillator, clock generator | Timer, delay circuit |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Astable Time Period (T)

#### Step 1 — Capacitor Charging Equation
The voltage across a charging RC circuit follows the standard exponential form:

$$V_C(t) = V_{cc} - (V_{cc} - V_0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

where:
- $V_0$ = initial voltage on capacitor
- $\tau = R \cdot C$ = time constant

#### Step 2 — Apply Initial Condition
At the start of charging (just after discharge transistor turns OFF), the capacitor voltage is:

$$V_0 = \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$$

At the end of charging (when threshold comparator trips), the capacitor voltage reaches:

$$V_C(t_c) = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$$

The charging path is through **R_A + R_B**, so:

$$\tau_c = (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute and Solve
Substituting into the charging equation:

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \left(V_{cc} - \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}\right) \cdot e^{-t_c / [(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_c / [(R_A + R_B)C]}$$

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_c / [(R_A + R_B)C]} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$$

$$e^{-t_c / [(R_A + R_B)C]} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot V_{cc} \cdot \frac{3}{2 V_{cc}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$-\frac{t_c}{(R_A + R_B)C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)$$

$$t_c = (R_A + R_B) \cdot C \cdot \ln(2)$$

#### Step 4 — Numerical Approximation
Using $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$:

$$\boxed{t_c = 0.693 \times (R_A + R_B) \times C}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Astable Discharge Time (t_d)

#### Step 1 — Capacitor Discharging Equation
For a discharging RC circuit:

$$V_C(t) = V_i \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Initial Condition
At the start of discharge, $V_i = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$.
At the end, $V_C(t_d) = \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$.
The discharge path is through **R_B only** (Pin 7 to ground via transistor):

$$\tau_d = R_B \cdot C$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute and Solve
$$\frac{1}{3} V_{cc} = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t_d / (R_B C)}$$

$$e^{-t_d / (R_B C)} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot V_{cc} \cdot \frac{3}{2 V_{cc}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$t_d = R_B \cdot C \cdot \ln(2)$$

#### Step 4 — Numerical Approximation

$$\boxed{t_d = 0.693 \times R_B \times C}$$

---

### 3.3 Total Period and Frequency

The total time period is the sum:

$$T = t_c + t_d = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C + 0.693 R_B C$$

$$T = 0.693(R_A + R_B + R_B)C = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$$

Therefore, frequency:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}}$$

The duty cycle (fraction of time output is HIGH):

$$\boxed{D = \frac{t_c}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Monostable Pulse Width (T)

#### Step 1 — Charging Equation
The capacitor charges through resistor **R** from initial voltage $\frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ toward $V_{cc}$, and the pulse ends when $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$:

$$V_C(t) = V_{cc} - \left(V_{cc} - \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}\right) e^{-t/(RC)}$$

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-T/(RC)}$$

#### Step 2 — Solve for T
$$e^{-T/(RC)} = \frac{1}{3} \cdot V_{cc} \cdot \frac{3}{2 V_{cc}} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Wait — let me re-derive carefully:

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-T/(RC)} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$$

$$e^{-T/(RC)} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{cc}}{V_{cc}} \cdot \text{wait...}$$

Let me recompute:

$$e^{-T/(RC)} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Hmm, let me redo this carefully:

Starting equation:
$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-T/(RC)}$$

Subtract $\frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ from both sides after moving:
$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-T/(RC)} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$$

$$e^{-T/(RC)} = \frac{1/3 \cdot V_{cc}}{2/3 \cdot V_{cc}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

So $T = RC \cdot \ln(2)$... wait, that gives 0.693, not 1.1.

Let me reconsider. The standard formula for 555 monostable is:

$$T = R C \ln\left(\frac{V_{cc} - 0}{V_{cc} - V_{cc}}\right) \text{ -- no}$$

Actually the correct derivation considers charging from $\frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$ to $\frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$:

$$V_C(t) = V_{cc}(1 - e^{-t/RC}) + \frac{1}{3} V_{cc} \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

This equals: $V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} e^{-t/RC}$

When $V_C = \frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$:

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} e^{-T/(RC)}$$

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} e^{-T/(RC)} = V_{cc} - \frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = \frac{1}{3} V_{cc}$$

$$e^{-T/(RC)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$T = RC \ln(2) = 0.693 RC$$

Hmm, but the standard textbook formula is $T = 1.1 RC$. Let me check this.

Actually, looking at the data books (e.g., Texas Instruments NE555 datasheet), the monostable pulse width is:

$$T = R C \ln(3) \approx 1.098 RC \approx 1.1 RC$$

The discrepancy comes from how the threshold and trigger levels interact. In the actual 555 circuit, the trigger sets the flip-flop and the threshold resets it. The capacitor charges from **0 V** (because discharge transistor was ON, shorting it to ground), not from 1/3 Vcc.

Let me redo with this correct understanding:

**Phase A — Stable State:** Discharge transistor is ON, so capacitor C is shorted to ground. $V_C = 0$ V.

**Phase B — Triggered:** Transistor OFF, capacitor charges from 0 V through R toward $V_{cc}$.

When does the output go LOW? When $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2}{3} V_{cc}$ (threshold level).

$$V_C(t) = V_{cc}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$$

$$\frac{2}{3} V_{cc} = V_{cc}(1 - e^{-T/(RC)})$$

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/(RC)}$$

$$e^{-T/(RC)} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$T = RC \ln(3)$$

#### Step 3 — Final Numerical Result
Using $\ln(3) \approx 1.098 \approx 1.1$:

$$\boxed{T = 1.1 \times R \times C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction & Clarification:** In monostable mode, the capacitor discharges completely to 0 V during the stable state (because Pin 7 discharge transistor is ON and shorts the capacitor to ground). The pulse width formula $T = 1.1 RC$ comes from $\ln(3) \approx 1.098$.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Astable Design

**Problem:** Design an astable 555 circuit to generate a **1 kHz square wave** with **60% duty cycle** using $V_{cc} = 12$ V.

**Given:** $f = 1000$ Hz, $D = 60\%$, $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$

**Step 1:** Solve frequency equation for $R_A + 2R_B$:

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C}$$

$$R_A + 2R_B = \frac{1.44}{f \cdot C} = \frac{1.44}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.44}{10^{-4}} = 14400 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2:** Solve duty cycle equation:

$$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = 0.60$$

$$R_A + R_B = 0.60 \times 14400 = 8640 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3:** Solve simultaneous equations:

$$R_A + 2R_B = 14400$$
$$R_A + R_B = 8640$$

Subtracting: $R_B = 14400 - 8640 = 5760 \, \Omega \approx 5.6 \, k\Omega$

Then: $R_A = 8640 - 5760 = 2880 \, \Omega \approx 2.8 \, k\Omega$

**Verification:** $R_A + 2R_B = 2880 + 11520 = 14400 \, \Omega$ ✓

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1.44}{14400 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.44}{1.44 \times 10^{-3}} = 1000 \, \text{Hz} \checkmark}$$

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example — Monostable Design

**Problem:** Design a monostable 555 circuit to produce a pulse of width **T = 2 milliseconds**.

**Given:** $T = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ s, choose $C = 1 \, \mu F$

**Solution:**

$$T = 1.1 \times R \times C$$

$$R = \frac{T}{1.1 \times C} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1818 \, \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 1.8 \, k\Omega}$$
