## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between FIR and IIR digital filters. Mention two advantages of FIR filters over IIR filters.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> - **FIR** has only feed-forward terms ($a_k = 0$ for $k \geq 1$); impulse response is finite; always **stable**.
> - **IIR** has feedback terms ($a_k \neq 0$); impulse response is theoretically infinite; can be **unstable** if poles lie outside the unit circle.
> - **Advantages of FIR:** (i) Guaranteed stability and linear phase possible, (ii) No phase distortion, (iii) No limit cycles, (iv) Easier to design using window methods.
> - **Advantages of IIR:** Lower order for same magnitude response (computationally cheaper), sharp transitions possible.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is meant by the linear phase response of an FIR filter? State the necessary and sufficient condition on $h[n]$ for linear phase.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> - Linear phase means the filter's phase response is a **linear function of frequency** $\phi(\omega) = -\alpha \omega$ for some constant $\alpha$, giving **constant group delay** $\tau_g = \alpha$.
> - **Necessary and sufficient condition:** the impulse response must be **symmetric** or **anti-symmetric** about its midpoint:
> $$h[n] = \pm h[N-1-n], \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$
> - The resulting group delay is $\tau_g = \frac{N-1}{2}$ samples (constant).

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

> **(a)** Derive the Direct Form-I realization of the IIR filter described by the difference equation
> $$y[n] = 0.5\,y[n-1] - 0.3\,y[n-2] + x[n] + 0.4\,x[n-1]$$
> Draw the block diagram. **\[7 marks\]**
>
> **(b)** Convert the above to Direct Form-II (canonical) realization and justify why it is called "canonical". **\[7 marks\]**

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Direct Form-I**

The given equation: $y[n] = 0.5\,y[n-1] - 0.3\,y[n-2] + x[n] + 0.4\,x[n-1]$

Coefficients: $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 0.4$, $b_2 = 0$; $a_1 = -0.5$, $a_2 = 0.3$.

Transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.4 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.3 z^{-2}}$$

**Block diagram (Direct Form-I):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    xn["x(n)"] --> d1F["z^-1"]
    xn --> b0["x 1.0"]
    d1F --> b1["x 0.4"]
    d1F --> d2F["z^-1"]
    b0 --> add1(("+"))
    b1 --> add1
    add1 --> yn["y(n)"]
    yn --> fa1["x 0.5"]
    yn --> d1fb["z^-1"]
    d1fb --> d2fb["z^-1"]
    d2fb --> fa2["x -0.3"]
    fa1 --> add1
    fa2 --> add1
```

**Steps for derivation (presented):**
1. Identify the two FIR feed-forward coefficients ($b_0=1$, $b_1=0.4$) — these multiply $x[n], x[n-1]$.
2. Identify the two IIR feedback coefficients ($a_1=0.5$, $a_2=-0.3$) — these multiply $y[n-1], y[n-2]$.
3. Implement in two stages: input-side FIR then output-side IIR; total **3 delay elements**.

**Part (b) — Direct Form-II (Canonical)**

**Definition:** Direct Form-II combines both delay lines into a single shared line.

**Intermediate signal $w[n]$:**

$$w[n] = x[n] - 0.5\,w[n-1] + 0.3\,w[n-2]$$

$$y[n] = 1 \cdot w[n] + 0.4 \cdot w[n-1]$$

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    xn["x(n)"] --> nd1(("+"))
    nd1 --> wn["w(n)"]
    wn --> d1G["z^-1"]
    d1G --> d2G["z^-1"]
    d2G --> d3G["z^-1 (more)"]
    wn --> yb0["x 1.0"]
    d1G --> yb1["x 0.4"]
    yb0 --> yadd(("+"))
    yb1 --> yadd
    yadd --> yn["y(n)"]
    d1G --> fbm1["x 0.5"]
    d2G --> fbm2["x -0.3"]
    fbm1 --> nd1
    fbm2 --> nd1
```

**Justification for "canonical":**
- Total delay elements used = **2** (one shared line storing $w[n-1], w[n-2]$).
- Original Direct Form-I used **3 delays** ($x[n-1]$ and $y[n-1], y[n-2]$ separately).
- Since the system requires at least $\max(M,N) = \max(2,2) = 2$ delays, Direct Form-II uses the **minimum possible** — hence **canonical**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

> **(a)** Explain the cascade realization structure for an IIR digital filter. Given
> $$H(z) = \frac{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + 0.8 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2})(1 - 0.7 z^{-1})}$$
> express $H(z)$ as a cascade of second-order (and one first-order) sections and draw the block diagram. **\[7 marks\]**
>
> **(b)** Discuss finite word length effects in DSP systems with reference to (i) ADC quantization noise, and (ii) coefficient quantization. Derive the SNR formula for a $B$-bit ADC. **\[7 marks\]**

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Cascade Realization**

**Step 1 — Pair numerator and denominator factors** (pair real zeros with nearest real poles):

$$H_1(z) = \frac{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2}}, \quad H_2(z) = \frac{1 + 0.8 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.7 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Total realization:** $H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z)$ — two cascaded biquad-style sections.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    xn["x(n)"] --> s1["Section 1: H1(z)"]
    s1 --> s2["Section 2: H2(z)"]
    s2 --> yn["y(n)"]
```

**Step 3 — Key advantages:** Each section operates independently — poles inside unit circle in each section $\Rightarrow$ overall stable; modular implementation; reduced coefficient sensitivity.

**Part (b) — Finite Word Length Effects**

**(i) ADC Quantization Noise:**

A $B$-bit ADC converts analog $x(t)$ to discrete levels separated by step $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^{B-1}$. The quantization error $e[n]$ is modeled as additive white noise uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$.

**SNR derivation** (see Section 3.6 above for full derivation):

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,B + 1.76$$

**Significance:** *Every additional bit doubles the number of levels, giving ~6 dB SNR improvement.*

**(ii) Coefficient Quantization:**

When ideal coefficients $b_k, a_k$ are rounded to $B_c$ bits, the actual $H_Q(z)$ differs from designed $H(z)$. Effects include:
- **Pole movement:** poles may shift outside the unit circle $\Rightarrow$ instability.
- **Distortion of magnitude/phase response.**
- **Worst-case bound:** if $\max_k \vert a_k \vert + \max_k \vert b_k \vert < 1$, filter remains stable under quantization (sufficient condition).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in this topic — KTU 2018–2024 Examiner Pattern:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Direct Form-I with Direct Form-II** (≈3 marks lost) — remember: Form-I has separate delay lines ($N+M$ delays); Form-II shares ONE line ($\max(M,N)$ delays).
> 2. **Forgetting to label all signals** $x[n], w[n], y[n]$ on block diagrams — KTU deducts 2 marks if signal flow is ambiguous.
> 3. **Wrong pairing in cascade form** — students often pair zeros with faraway poles; KTU expects **pairing of nearby poles and zeros** to minimize sensitivity.
> 4. **Linear phase condition** — many write "$h[n]$ symmetric" without specifying $h[n] = \pm h[N-1-n]$ — examiners want the **explicit equation**.
> 5. **SNR derivation** — skipping the partial-fraction style variance calculation $\Delta^2/12$ costs ~2 marks.
> 6. **TMS320C67xx architecture** — students confuse it with fixed-point C64xx; **C67xx is FLOATING-POINT** with VLIW, **8 functional units** in 2 clusters (A and B).
> 7. **Signal Flow Graph with Mason's formula** — must write all forward paths $P_k$ and all loops $L_i$ explicitly, not just the final $T$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bulleted High-Density Recap:**

- **FIR difference equation:** $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} b_k x[n-k]$ — no $y[n-k]$ terms.
- **IIR difference equation:** $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{M} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} b_k x[n-k]$ — has $y[n-k]$ feedback.
- **FIR is always stable** (no poles except at $z=0$). **IIR stability** requires all poles inside unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$.
- **Linear phase FIR condition:** $h[n] = \pm h[N-1-n]$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$. Group delay $\tau = \frac{N-1}{2}$ samples.
- **Type-I FIR:** symmetric, odd $N$ — most general; **Type-II:** symmetric, even $N$ — $\omega=\pi$ always zero (cannot be highpass); **Type-III:** anti-s, odd $N$ — both $\omega=0, \pi$ zero; **Type-IV:** anti-s, even $N$ — only $\omega=0$ zero.
- **Direct Form-I** uses $N+M$ delays; **Direct Form-II (canonical)** uses $\max(M,N)$ delays — minimum memory.
- **Cascade form:** $H(z) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} H_k(z)$ — each $H_k$ is biquad; pair zeros with **nearby** poles.
- **Parallel form:** $H(z) = c + \sum_{k=1}^{K} H_k(z)$ — via partial fractions; sections operate in parallel; errors **don't propagate**.
- **Signal Flow Graph (SFG):** Mason's Gain Formula $T = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_k P_k \Delta_k$.
- **TMS320C67xx:** TI floating-point DSP; **VLIW** architecture; **8 functional units** (.L, .S, .M, .D in each of A and B clusters); 32 × 32-bit registers per cluster; issues up to **8 instructions/cycle**; typical clock **167–300 MHz**.
- **ADC Quantization SNR:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02 B + 1.76$ dB — **golden formula** for KTU 7-mark derivations.
- **Coefficient quantization:** round-off of $b_k, a_k$ can shift poles outside unit circle $\Rightarrow$ instability. Pairing nearby poles/zeros in cascade form mitigates this.
- **Limit cycles** (a finite-word-length artifact): IIR filters may produce small-amplitude sustained oscillations even with zero input, due to rounding in feedback loop — does NOT exist in FIR.
- **Overflow:** fixed-point adders wrap around if sum exceeds 2's-complement range — mitigated by **saturation arithmetic** or **scaling** inputs by factor $\sum \vert b_k \vert$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to formal language theory - Alphabets, Strings, Concatenation, Languages, Regular Languages - Deterministic Finite State Automata (DFA), Nondeterministic Finite State Automata (NFA), Equivalence of DFA and NFA, Regular Grammar, and Equivalence of RGs and DFA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to formal language theory - Alphabets, Strings, Concatenation, Languages, Regular Languages - Deterministic Finite State Automata (DFA), Nondeterministic Finite State Automata (NFA), Equivalence of DFA and NFA, Regular Grammar, and Equivalence of RGs and DFA # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Language Theory — The Foundational Definition **Formal Language Theory** is the branch of theoretical computer science and mathematics that studies the syntax, structure, and properties of languages through rigorous mathematical models. A *language* in this sense is **not** a natural language (like English or Malayalam) — it is a **set of strings** constructed from a finite set of symbols, recognized or generated by a mathematical machine. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Formal language theory deals with alphabets, strings, languages, grammars, and abstract machines (automata) as a means of precisely defining the syntax of programming languages, protocol specifications, and pattern-matching systems.* ### 1.2 Alphabet (Σ) — The Building Block An **alphabet** (denoted by the Greek capital letter **Sigma, Σ**) is a **finite, non-empty** set of symbols used to construct strings. | Property | Description | Example | |----------|-------------|---------| | Finiteness | Must have finite cardinality | $\vert\Sigma\vert = n$ | | Non-empty | $\Sigma \neq \emptyset$ | $\{\}$ is invalid | | Symbols | Letters, digits, special chars | $a, b, 1, +, \#$ | **Examples of Alphabets:** - Binary alphabet: $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ - Lowercase English: $\Sigma = \{a, b, c, \ldots, z\}$ - ASCII: $\Sigma = \{$ all 128 ASCII characters $\}$ - Networking: $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ (bits transmitted over a channel) > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an alphabet like a **Lego box**. Just as a Lego box contains distinct plastic bricks of different shapes, an alphabet contains distinct symbols. You cannot build anything without first opening the box. ### 1.3 Strings (Words) — Sequence of Symbols A **string** (also called a *word* or *sentence*) is a **finite sequence of symbols** drawn from an alphabet $\Sigma$. The **length** of a string $w$, denoted $\vert w \vert$, is the number of symbols it contains. **Special Strings:** - **Empty String** $\varepsilon$ (epsilon): A string with **zero** symbols. $\vert\varepsilon\vert = 0$. - **Powers of a string**: $w^n = \underbrace{w \cdot w \cdot \ldots \cdot w}_{n \text{ times}}$; $w^0 = \varepsilon$. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** A string is like a **sentence formed by arranging Lego bricks in a row**. The arrangement (order) matters — the brick sequence "ABC" is different from "CBA". The empty string is the analogy of an "empty row" with no bricks. ### 1.4 Concatenation — Joining Strings **Concatenation** is the binary operation of joining two strings end-to-end. If $u$ and $v$ are strings, then $u \cdot v$ (or simply $uv$) is the string formed by writing $u$ followed immediately by $v$. **Properties of Concatenation:** - **Closure**: If $u, v \in \Sigma^*$, then $uv \in \Sigma^*$ - **Associative**: $(uv)w = u(vw)$ - **Identity element**: $\varepsilon w = w\varepsilon = w$ - **Non-commutative**: In general, $uv \neq vu$ - **Length rule**: $\vert uv \vert = \vert u \vert + \vert v \vert$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Concatenation is like **stringing beads on a necklace**. Take a red-blue necklace (string $u$) and attach a green-yellow necklace (string $v$) — the result is a single necklace of 4 beads. The order you attach them creates distinct necklaces. ### 1.5 Language — A Set of Strings A **language** $L$ over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is any **set of strings** formed from $\Sigma$, i.e., $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$. **The Kleene Star (Σ*)** is the set of **all possible strings** (including $\varepsilon$) of any finite length over $\Sigma$. $$\Sigma^* = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} \Sigma^i$$ **The Kleene Plus (Σ⁺)** excludes the empty string: $$\Sigma^+ = \Sigma^* - \{\varepsilon\} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} \Sigma^i$$ **Example:** For $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$: - $\Sigma^* = \{\varepsilon, 0, 1, 00, 01, 10, 11, 000, \ldots\}$ (infinite) - A language $L = \{w \in \Sigma^* : w \text{ has equal 0s and 1s}\} = \{\varepsilon, 01, 10, 0011, 0101, 1001, \ldots\}$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** A language is like a **dictionary of valid sentences**. The alphabet is your alphabet book (A-Z), strings are all possible letter combinations, but a language is just the *meaningful, accepted combinations* in the dictionary. "CAT" is in the English language; "XZQ" is a string but not in the language. ### 1.6 Regular Languages — The Simplest Class A language is called a **regular language** if it can be: 1. Described by a **regular expression** 2. Accepted by a **DFA** (Deterministic Finite Automaton) 3. Accepted by an **NFA** (Nondeterministic Finite Automaton) 4. Generated by a **regular grammar** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** All four characterizations (Regex, DFA, NFA, RG) describe **the same class** of languages — the *regular languages*. This equivalence is one of the most frequently tested topics in KTU examinations. **Closure Properties of Regular Languages (MUST MEMORIZE):** - Union: $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular - Intersection: $L_1 \cap L_2$ is regular - Complement: $\overline{L}$ is regular - Concatenation: $L_1 \cdot L_2$ is regular - Kleene Star: $L^*$ is regular - Reverse: $L^R$ is regular - Difference: $L_1 - L_2$ is regular # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA) — The Mathematical Engine A **DFA** is a 5-tuple abstract machine that reads input strings symbol-by-symbol and either *accepts* or *rejects* them based on a deterministic transition rule. **Formal Definition:** $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$ Where: | Symbol | Meaning | Constraint | |--------|---------|------------| | $Q$ | Finite set of states | $Q$ is **finite**, non-empty | | $\Sigma$ | Input alphabet | Finite, non-empty | | $\delta$ | Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ | | $q_0$ | Start state | $q_0 \in Q$ | | $F$ | Set of accept states | $F \subseteq Q$ | **The Determinism Condition (CRITICAL):** $$\forall q \in Q, \forall a \in \Sigma : \mid \delta(q, a) \mid = 1$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This means: for every state-symbol pair, **exactly ONE** next state exists. There are no choices, no $\varepsilon$-moves, and no missing transitions in a complete DFA. ### 2.2 Extended Transition Function $\hat{\delta}$ To process strings (not just single symbols), we use the **extended transition function**: $$\hat{\delta} : Q \times \Sigma^* \to Q$$ **Recursive Definition:** 1. $\hat{\delta}(q, \varepsilon) = q$ (base case) 2. $\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \delta(\hat{\delta}(q, w), a)$ for $w \in \Sigma^*, a \in \Sigma$ ### 2.3 Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA) — The "Parallel Universe" Machine An **NFA** relaxes two constraints of the DFA: 1. The transition function maps to a **set of states** (not a single state) 2. **Epsilon ($\varepsilon$) transitions** are allowed **Formal Definition:** $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$ Where the transition function is: $$\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to 2^Q$$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** A DFA is like a person walking on a single fixed path with a GPS — at every intersection, exactly one road is taken. An NFA is like a person who **clones themselves** at every intersection, taking **all possible roads simultaneously**. If even one clone reaches an accepting state, the NFA accepts. **NFA vs DFA — Side-by-Side Comparison:** | Feature | DFA | NFA | |---------|-----|-----| | Transition output | Single state | Set of states | | $\varepsilon$-transitions | Not allowed | Allowed | | Number of transitions per symbol | Exactly 1 | 0, 1, or many | | Construction difficulty | Harder | Easier | | Size (worst case) | $2^n$ states for $n$-state NFA | Often smaller | | Computational power | Equivalent | Equivalent | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use Case | |---|---------|---------------|----------| | 1 | Kleene Star | $\Sigma^* = \Sigma^0 \cup \Sigma^1 \cup \ldots$ | All strings over $\Sigma$ | | 2 | String Length | $\vert w \vert$ = number of symbols in $w$ | String measurement | | 3 | Concatenation Length | $\vert uv \vert = \vert u \vert + \vert v \vert$ | Length analysis | | 4 | Empty String Property | $\varepsilon w = w\varepsilon = w$ | Identity element | | 5 | Power of String | $w^n = w \cdot w^{n-1}; w^0 = \varepsilon$ | Repetition | | 6 | Language Cardinality | $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ | Set relationship | | 7 | DFA Transition | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ | Determinism rule | | 8 | NFA Transition | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to 2^Q$ | Nondeterminism | | 9 | Subset Construction | $Q_{DFA} \subseteq 2^{Q_{NFA}}$ | NFA → DFA conversion | | 10 | RG Production Form | $A \to aB \mid a \mid \varepsilon$ | Regular grammar rule | | 11 | Pumping Lemma | $\vert xy \vert \leq p, \vert y \vert \geq 1, xy^iz \in L$ | Proving non-regularity | | 12 | Myhill-Nerode | Minimum DFA = equivalence classes of $R_L$ | Minimization | | 13 | Closure Union | $L_1 \cup L_2$ regular if $L_1, L_2$ regular | Combine RLs | | 14 | Closure Complement | $\overline{L}$ regular iff $L$ regular | Negation | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |-------|-------------| | **Compiler Design** | Lexical analysis (tokenization of source code) | | **Network Protocols** | Pattern matching in packet headers, firewalls | | **Search Engines** | Regular expression-based text searching (grep) | | **Bioinformatics** | DNA pattern matching, gene sequence analysis | | **Operating Systems** | File system permission strings, command parsing | | **Digital VLSI Design** | Sequential circuit synthesis from regular expressions | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Every time you type a regex into `grep`, `sed`, or a search bar, you are using the **exact same mathematical machinery** that we study here. The grep engine constructs an NFA, converts it to a DFA via subset construction, and then runs the DFA on your text — a process often called *Thompson's NFA construction*. ## 2.6 Regular Grammar (RG) — The Generative Counterpart A **regular grammar** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where: - $V$ = set of non-terminal (variable) symbols - $T$ = set of terminal symbols - $P$ = set of production rules - $S$ = start symbol **Production Rule Forms (Two Variants):** **Right-linear grammar:** $$A \to aB \quad \text{or} \quad A \to a \quad \text{or} \quad A \to \varepsilon$$ **Left-linear grammar:** $$A \to Ba \quad \text{or} \quad A \to a \quad \text{or} \quad A \to \varepsilon$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Critical Distinction:** A grammar is regular if and only if it is **either** right-linear **or** left-linear — but **NOT both simultaneously** (unless trivial). Mixing rules in the same grammar is generally not allowed. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: NFA → DFA Conversion (Subset Construction Algorithm) **Theorem:** For every NFA $N = (Q_N, \Sigma, \delta_N, q_0, F_N)$, there exists a DFA $D = (Q_D, \Sigma, \delta_D, \{q_0\}, F_D)$ such that $L(N) = L(D)$. **Step 1: Define the DFA components** $$Q_D = 2^{Q_N} \quad \text{(the power set of NFA states)}$$ $$q_{0,D} = E(\{q_0\}) \quad \text{(Epsilon-closure of NFA start state)}$$ $$F_D = \{S \in Q_D : S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset\}$$ **Step 2: Define the transition function** $$\delta_D(S, a) = \bigcup_{q \in S} E(\delta_N(q, a))$$ Where $E(R) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(R)$ is defined recursively: $$E(R) = R \cup \bigcup_{q \in R} E(\delta_N(q, \varepsilon))$$ **Step 3: Iterative Construction Logic** 1. **Start:** Add $E(\{q_0\})$ to $Q_D$ and mark it **unprocessed**. 2. **For each unprocessed state** $S \in Q_D$: - For each input symbol $a \in \Sigma$: - Compute $T = \delta_D(S, a)$ (the set of all states reachable from $S$ on $a$) - If $T \notin Q_D$, add $T$ to $Q_D$ as unprocessed - Add transition $S \xrightarrow{a} T$ to $\delta_D$ - Mark $S$ as **processed**. **Step 4: Termination** The algorithm terminates when all states in $Q_D$ are processed. Since $Q_D \subseteq 2^{Q_N}$ is finite, termination is guaranteed in $O(2^{\vert Q_N \vert})$ worst-case time. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: NFA → DFA Conversion **Given NFA $N$ over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$:** | State | $a$ | $b$ | $\varepsilon$ | |-------|-----|-----|---------------| | $\to q_0$ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ | $\emptyset$ | | $q_1$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\emptyset$ | | $*q_2$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_2\}$ | $\emptyset$ | **Step 1: Compute $\varepsilon$-closures** $$E(\{q_0\}) = \{q_0\}$$ $$E(\{q_0, q_1\}) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$ $$E(\{q_0, q_2\}) = \{q_0, q_2\}$$ $$E(\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}) = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$$ $$E(\{q_2\}) = \{q_2\}$$ **Step 2: Initialize DFA start state** $$A = E(\{q_0\}) = \{q_0\}$$ Mark $A$ as unprocessed, $A = [q_0]$. **Step 3: Process state $A = \{q_0\}$** On input $a$: $$\delta_D(A, a) = E(\delta_N(q_0, a)) = E(\{q_0, q_1\}) = \{q_0, q_1\} = B$$ On input $b$: $$\delta_D(A, b) = E(\delta_N(q_0, b)) = E(\{q_0\}) = \{q_0\} = A$$ Add $B = [q_0, q_1]$ to $Q_D$ (unprocessed). Mark $A$ as processed. **Step 4: Process state $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$** On input $a$: $$\delta_D(B, a) = E(\delta_N(q_0, a) \cup \delta_N(q_1, a)) = E(\{q_0, q_1\} \cup \emptyset) = \{q_0, q_1\} = B$$ On input $b$: $$\delta_D(B, b) = E(\delta_N(q_0, b) \cup \delta_N(q_1, b)) = E(\{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\}) = \{q_0, q_2\} = C$$ Add $C = [q_0, q_2]$ to $Q_D$. Mark $B$ as processed. **Step 5: Process state $C = \{q_0, q_2\}$** On input $a$: $$\delta_D(C, a) = E(\{q_0, q_1\} \cup \emptyset) = \{q_0, q_1\} = B$$ On input $b$: $$\delta_D(C, b) = E(\{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\}) = \{q_0, q_2\} = C$$ Mark $C$ as processed. **Step 6: Identify Accept States** $$F_D = \{S \in Q_D : S \cap F_N \neq \emptyset\}$$ Since $F_N = \{q_2\}$, any DFA state containing $q_2$ is accepting. Hence $F_D = \{C\}$. **Resulting DFA:** | State | $a$ | $b$ | |-------|-----|-----| | $\to A = \{q_0\}$ | $B$ | $A$ | | $B = \{q_0, q_1\}$ | $B$ | $C$ | | $*C = \{q_0, q_2\}$ | $B$ | $C$ | --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Regular Grammar → NFA (RG to Automaton Equivalence) **Theorem:** For every right-linear grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$, there exists an NFA $N$ with $\varepsilon$-transitions such that $L(G) = L(N)$. **Construction Steps:** 1. For each non-terminal $A \in V$, create a state $q_A$ in $N$. 2. Add a new final state $q_f$. 3. For each production $A \to aB$ (in $V \times T \times V$): add transition $q_A \xrightarrow{a} q_B$. 4. For each production $A \to a$ (in $V \times T$): add transition $q_A \xrightarrow{a} q_f$. 5. For each production $A \to \varepsilon$: mark $q_A$ as a final state. 6. The start state is $q_S$. **Proof of Correctness (by induction on derivation length):** **Base case:** If $A \Rightarrow a$ in one step, then by rule 4, $q_A \xrightarrow{a} q_f$ exists. Hence $\hat{\delta}(q_S, a) \ni q_f$, so $a \in L(N)$. **Inductive step:** Assume $A \Rightarrow^* w$ in $n$ steps with $A \xRightarrow{*} w$. The last step must be $A \Rightarrow aB$ (where $w = ax$) and $B \Rightarrow^* x$ in $n-1$ steps. By induction, there is a path $q_B \xrightarrow{x} q_f$ in $N$. Combined with $q_A \xrightarrow{a} q_B$, we get a path $q_A \xrightarrow{ax} q_f$, so $ax \in L(N)$. **Reverse direction:** If $w = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n \in L(N)$, there exists a path $q_S \xrightarrow{a_1} q_{X_1} \xrightarrow{a_2} q_{X_2} \ldots \xrightarrow{a_n} q_f$. Each transition $q_{X_i} \xrightarrow{a_{i+1}} q_{X_{i+1}}$ corresponds to a production $X_i \to a_{i+1} X_{i+1}$. Concatenating these yields $S \Rightarrow^* a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: DFA → Right-Linear Grammar (Reverse Equivalence) **Theorem:** For every DFA $D = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$, there exists a right-linear grammar $G$ generating $L(D)$. **Construction:** 1. $V = Q$ (each DFA state becomes a non-terminal) 2. $T = \Sigma$ 3. $S = q_0$ 4. For each transition $\delta(q_i, a) = q_j$: add production $q_i \to a q_j$ 5. For each final state $q_f \in F$: add production $q_f \to \varepsilon$ **Proof Sketch:** Each string $w = a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$ accepted by $D$ corresponds to a state path $q_0 \xrightarrow{a_1} q_1 \xrightarrow{a_2} \ldots \xrightarrow{a_n} q_f$. Translating each transition to a production yields the derivation: $$q_0 \Rightarrow a_1 q_1 \Rightarrow a_1 a_2 q_2 \Rightarrow \ldots \Rightarrow a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n q_f \Rightarrow a_1 a_2 \ldots a_n$$ Hence $L(D) = L(G)$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: The Equivalence Chain The four-way equivalence among regular expressions, DFAs, NFAs, and regular grammars is the cornerstone result of formal language theory: $$\boxed{L \text{ is regular} \iff L = L(RE) \iff L = L(DFA) \iff L = L(NFA) \iff L = L(RG)}$$ | From | To | Construction Algorithm | Reference | |------|-----|------------------------|-----------| | Regex | NFA | Thompson's Construction | Recursive building | | NFA | DFA | Subset Construction | Rabin-Scott (1959) | | DFA | Regex | State Elimination | Kleene's Algorithm | | Regex | DFA | NFA → DFA via Subset | Combined | | DFA | RG | Production per transition | One-to-one | | RG | NFA | State per non-terminal | One-to-one | | NFA | Regex | GNFA → State Elimination | Standard | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The exam question of the form *"Prove that every regular language is accepted by some DFA"* typically expects either the NFA→DFA construction or the RG→DFA construction as the proof mechanism. Memorize the steps of the subset construction algorithm. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DFA Schematic — Accepting Strings Ending in "01" A classical example DFA over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ that accepts all strings ending with the substring "01": ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 direction LR [*] --> q0DFA q0DFA --> q1DFA : 0 q0DFA --> q0DFA : 1 q1DFA --> q1DFA : 0 q1DFA --> q2DFA : 1 q2DFA --> q1DFA : 0 q2DFA --> q0DFA : 1 note right of q2DFA Accept State (Final) end note ``` **Transition Table (DFA):** | State | Input 0 | Input 1 | Type | |-------|---------|---------|------| | $\to q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ | Start | | $q_1$ | $q_1$ | $q_2$ | Intermediate | | $*q_2$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ | **Accept** | --- ## 4.2 NFA Schematic — Same Language, with Non-determinism The same language can be implemented as a smaller NFA using $\varepsilon$-transitions: ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 direction LR [*] --> n0NFA n0NFA --> n1NFA : 0 n1NFA --> n2NFA : 1 n2NFA --> n3NFA : eps n3NFA --> n0NFA : 0 note right of n3NFA Epsilon Move end note ``` This NFA uses only 4 states for the same language — illustrating that NFAs can be more compact. --- ## 4.3 Equivalence Architecture — Block Diagram The block diagram showing the **complete equivalence framework** between the four formalisms: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Generative["Generative Models"] RE["Regular Expression
(RE)"] RG["Regular Grammar
(RG)"] end subgraph Recognitive["Recognitive Models"] DFA["Deterministic FA
(DFA)"] NFA["Nondeterministic FA
(NFA)"] end RE -- "Thompson's
Construction" --> NFA NFA -- "Subset
Construction
(Rabin-Scott)" --> DFA DFA -- "State
Elimination" --> RE RG -- "State per
Non-terminal" --> NFA NFA -- "Production per
Transition" --> RG DFA -- "Equivalent
(NFA subset)" --> NFA NFA -- "Subset
Construction" --> DFA style Generative fill:#e1f5ff style Recognitive fill:#fff4e1 style RE fill:#b3e5fc style RG fill:#b3e5fc style DFA fill:#ffe0b2 style NFA fill:#ffe0b2 ``` --- ## 4.4 Subset Construction — Visual Pipeline The pipeline view of how an NFA is transformed into an equivalent DFA through the subset construction: ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Input NFA
N = (Q, Sigma, delta, q0, F)"] --> B["Compute
Epsilon Closures"] B --> C["Initialize
Start State
E(q0)"] C --> D["For each state
in DFA:
For each symbol a:
Compute T = delta_D(S,a)"] D --> E{"T new?"} E -- "Yes" --> F["Add T to Q_D
(unprocessed)"] E -- "No" --> G["Skip"] F --> D G --> D D --> H["Mark all states
as processed"] H --> I["Identify Accept States
F_D = {S : S n F_N != empty}"] I --> J["Output DFA
D = (Q_D, Sigma, delta_D, q0D, F_D)"] style A fill:#ffcdd2 style J fill:#c8e6c9 style I fill:#fff9c4 ``` --- ## 4.5 Hierarchy of Languages (Chomsky Hierarchy) — Context Block To position regular languages in the broader picture: ```mermaid flowchart TB L0["Type 0: Recursively Enumerable
(Turing Machines)"] L1["Type 1: Context-Sensitive
(Linear Bounded Automata)"] L2["Type 2: Context-Free
(Pushdown Automata)"] L3["Type 3: Regular
(Finite Automata)"] L0 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 note["Regular Languages
are the SIMPLEST
class in Chomsky Hierarchy"] L3 -.- note style L0 fill:#ffcdd2 style L1 fill:#ffe0b2 style L2 fill:#fff9c4 style L3 fill:#c8e6c9 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the following with a suitable example:** (a) Alphabet (b) String (c) Language **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** **(a) Alphabet:** An alphabet $\Sigma$ is a **finite, non-empty set of symbols**. It forms the basic building block for constructing strings. - **Example:** $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ is the binary alphabet; $\Sigma = \{a, b, c\}$ is a 3-symbol alphabet. **(b) String:** A string $w$ is a **finite sequence of symbols** drawn from an alphabet $\Sigma$. The number of symbols in $w$ is its **length** $\vert w \vert$. - **Example:** For $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$, the string $w = abba$ has $\vert w \vert = 4$. The string $w = \varepsilon$ has $\vert w \vert = 0$. **(c) Language:** A language $L$ is a **set of strings** over some alphabet $\Sigma$, i.e., $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$. - **Example:** $L = \{a^n b^n : n \geq 1\} = \{ab, aabb, aaabbb, \ldots\}$ over $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between a DFA and an NFA. When is an NFA considered equivalent to a DFA?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | DFA | NFA | |-----------|-----|-----| | Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ (single state) | $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to 2^Q$ (set of states) | | $\varepsilon$-moves | Not allowed | Allowed | | Determinism | Fully deterministic | Non-deterministic | | Acceptance | Unique state path | At least one state path must end in $F$ | **Equivalence:** An NFA $N$ is **equivalent** to a DFA $D$ if they accept **the same language**, i.e., $L(N) = L(D)$. By the **Rabin-Scott theorem (subset construction)**, **every NFA has an equivalent DFA**, and vice versa. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Construct a DFA that accepts the language $L = \{w \in \{0,1\}^* : w \text{ contains the substring } 011\}$. **Draw the transition diagram and explain each state.** (7 marks) **(b)** Convert the following NFA to an equivalent DFA using subset construction. Show all intermediate states and identify the accept states. (7 marks) | State | 0 | 1 | |-------|-----|-----| | $\to A$ | $\{A, B\}$ | $\{A\}$ | | $B$ | $\{C\}$ | $\emptyset$ | | $*C$ | $\emptyset$ | $\{C\}$ | #### Model Solution to A(a): DFA for "Contains 011" **State Design (Intuition):** - $q_0$: Initial state; nothing relevant seen yet. - $q_1$: Last symbol was "0" (potential start of "011"). - $q_2$: Last two symbols were "01". - $q_3$: "011" detected → **accept state** (and stays accepting). **Transition Logic:** | State | On 0 | On 1 | |-------|------|------| | $\to q_0$ | $q_1$ | $q_0$ | | $q_1$ | $q_1$ | $q_2$ | | $q_2$ | $q_1$ | $q_3$ | | $*q_3$ | $q_3$ | $q_3$ | **Explanation of each state (1.5 marks each):** - $q_0$: No progress towards "011" made. On 0, transition to $q_1$. On 1, stay at $q_0$ since "1" alone doesn't help. - $q_1$: Have just seen a "0" — this could be the start of "011". On another 0, stay at $q_1$ (sliding window of single 0). On 1, go to $q_2$. - $q_2$: Have just seen "01" — need one more "1" to accept. On 0, return to $q_1$. On 1, reach accept. - $q_3$: Accept state — "011" has been found. Stay at $q_3$ for all inputs (absorbing accept state). **Test traces:** - $w = 0110$: $q_0 \to q_1 \to q_2 \to q_3 \to q_3$ ✓ Accept - $w = 1101$: $q_0 \to q_0 \to q_1 \to q_2 \to q_1$ ✗ Reject - $w = 011$: $q_0 \to q_1 \to q_2 \to q_3$ ✓ Accept #### Model Solution to A(b): NFA → DFA Conversion **Step 1: Identify DFA start state** $$A = \{A\} \quad \text{(NFA start state)}$$ **Step 2: Process state $A$ on inputs 0 and 1** On input 0: $\delta_D(A, 0) = \delta_N(A, 0) = \{A, B\} = B$ On input 1: $\delta_D(A, 1) = \delta_N(A, 1) = \{A\} = A$ **Step 3: Process state $B = \{A, B\}$ (new state)** On input 0: $\delta_D(B, 0) = \delta_N(A, 0) \cup \delta_N(B, 0) = \{A, B\} \cup \{C\} = \{A, B, C\} = C$ On input 1: $\delta_D(B, 1) = \delta_N(A, 1) \cup \delta_N(B, 1) = \{A\} \cup \emptyset = \{A\} = A$ **Step 4: Process state $C = \{A, B, C\}$ (new state)** On input 0: $\delta_D(C, 0) = \{A, B\} \cup \{C\} \cup \emptyset = \{A, B, C\} = C$ On input 1: $\delta_D(C, 1) = \{A\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{C\} = \{A, C\} = D$ **Step 5: Process state $D = \{A, C\}$ (new state)** On input 0: $\delta_D(D, 0) = \{A, B\} \cup \emptyset = \{A, B\} = B$ On input 1: $\delta_D(D, 1) = \{A\} \cup \{C\} = \{A, C\} = D$ **Step 6: Identify Accept States** ($F_D = \{S : S \cap \{C\} \neq \emptyset\}$) $$F_D = \{C, D\}$$ **Final Equivalent DFA:** | State | 0 | 1 | |-------|-----|-----| | $\to A$ | $B$ | $A$ | | $B$ | $C$ | $A$ | | $*C$ | $C$ | $D$ | | $*D$ | $B$ | $D$ | --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Define a **Regular Grammar**. Construct a right-linear grammar that generates the language $L = \{a^n b^m : n \geq 1, m \geq 1\}$ and then construct the equivalent NFA. (7 marks) **(b)** Prove that for any right-linear regular grammar $G$, there exists an equivalent NFA $N$ such that $L(G) = L(N)$. Show the construction with the grammar from part (a). (7 marks) #### Model Solution to B(a): Regular Grammar for $a^n b^m$ **Definition (2 marks):** A **regular grammar** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where every production is of the form $A \to aB$ (right-linear) or $A \to a$ or $A \to \varepsilon$, with $A, B \in V$ and $a \in T \cup \{\varepsilon\}$. **Grammar Construction (3 marks):** $$G = (V, T, P, S)$$ - $V = \{S, A, B\}$ - $T = \{a, b\}$ - $S = S$ - $P$: - $S \to aA$ (start generating $a$'s) - $A \to aA$ (continue $a$'s) **or** $A \to bB$ (switch to $b$'s) - $B \to bB$ (continue $b$'s) **or** $B \to \varepsilon$ (terminate) **Sample Derivation:** $aabb$: $$S \Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow aaA \Rightarrow aabB \Rightarrow aabbB \Rightarrow aabb$$ **NFA Construction (2 marks):** States $\{S, A, B, f\}$ with transitions: - $S \xrightarrow{a} A$ - $A \xrightarrow{a} A$ - $A \xrightarrow{b} B$ - $B \xrightarrow{b} B$ - $B \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} f$ Accept state: $f$. #### Model Solution to B(b): Proof of Equivalence **Theorem:** For any right-linear grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$, there exists an NFA $N = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_S, F)$ with $L(G) = L(N)$. **Construction (Algorithm) — 4 marks:** 1. **States:** $Q = V \cup \{q_f\}$ where $q_f$ is a new final state. 2. **Alphabet:** $\Sigma = T$. 3. **Transitions** for each production $A \to \alpha$: - If $\alpha = aB$ (where $a \in T, B \in V$): add $B \in \delta(A, a)$. - If $\alpha = a$ (where $a \in T$): add $q_f \in \delta(A, a)$. - If $\alpha = \varepsilon$: add $A \in F$. 4. **Start state:** $q_S$. 5. **Final states:** $F = \{q_f\} \cup \{A \in V : A \to \varepsilon \in P\}$. **Proof by Induction (3 marks):** *Claim:* $A \Rightarrow^* w$ in $G$ $\iff$ $q_A \xrightarrow{w} q_f$ (some path) in $N$. *Base case ($n=1$):* If $A \Rightarrow a$ in $G$ (one-step derivation), then by rule 3(b), $\delta(A, a) \ni q_f$ in $N$. So $q_A \xrightarrow{a} q_f$ exists. *Inductive step:* Assume the claim holds for all derivations of length $\leq n$. Consider a derivation of length $n+1$: $A \Rightarrow aB \Rightarrow^* a \cdot w'$ where $B \Rightarrow^* w'$ in $n$ steps. By the construction, $q_A \xrightarrow{a} q_B$ exists. By IH, $q_B \xrightarrow{w'} q_f$ in $N$. Concatenating: $q_A \xrightarrow{a w'} q_f$ in $N$. Thus $A \Rightarrow^* a w'$, completing the induction. **Applied to our grammar $G$ for $a^n b^m$:** The NFA constructed is exactly the one shown in part (a), with $q_f$ as the sole accept state. For any string $w = a^n b^m$ ($n,m \geq 1$), the path is: $$q_S \xrightarrow{a} q_A \xrightarrow{a} q_A \ldots \xrightarrow{a} q_A \xrightarrow{b} q_B \xrightarrow{b} \ldots \xrightarrow{b} q_B \xrightarrow{\varepsilon} q_f$$ This path uses $n$ transitions on $a$, one transition $A \to B$ on $b$, then $m-1$ transitions on $b$, and finally $\varepsilon$ to $q_f$. $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting $\varepsilon$-closure in NFA→DFA:** Students often compute $\delta_D(S, a)$ by simple union of NFA transitions, forgetting to take the $\varepsilon$-closure of the resulting set. **Marks lost: 2-3 per occurrence.** > 2. **Confusing Accept States in DFA:** An NFA state $q_f$ becomes a DFA accept state if and only if **any subset containing $q_f$** is generated. Missing this means missing DFA accept states. **Marks lost: 2.** > 3. **Mixing Left and Right Linear rules:** A regular grammar must be **uniformly** right-linear OR left-linear. Mixing them violates regularity. **Marks lost: 3.** > 4. **Empty string handling:** $\varepsilon \in L$ is correctly captured by marking the start state as accepting, NOT by inserting a transition. **Marks lost: 1-2.** > 5. **Subset construction state explosion:** Forgetting that worst-case DFA can have $2^n$ states for an $n$-state NFA. **Marks lost: 1 (for incomplete construction).** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ### A. Fundamental Definitions - [ ] **Alphabet** $\Sigma$ is a **finite, non-empty** set of symbols. - [ ] **String** is a finite sequence over $\Sigma$; **length** $\vert w \vert$ counts symbols. - [ ] **Empty string** $\varepsilon$ has length 0 and is the identity for concatenation. - [ ] **Concatenation** is associative but **not** commutative. - [ ] **Language** $L \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is any set of strings. - [ ] **Kleene Star** $\Sigma^* = \Sigma^0 \cup \Sigma^1 \cup \ldots$ contains all finite strings. ### B. DFA Essentials - [ ] 5-tuple: $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$. - [ ] $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ — exactly one next state per input. - [ ] No $\varepsilon$-transitions allowed. - [ ] **Extended** $\hat{\delta}$ handles strings via recursion. - [ ] Accept iff $\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F$. ### C. NFA Essentials - [ ] Transition: $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \to 2^Q$. - [ ] May have multiple transitions or none on a symbol. - [ ] $\varepsilon$-transitions allowed. - [ ] Accepts $w$ if **some** path leads to a final state. - [ ] **Subset construction** converts to equivalent DFA. ### D. Regular Grammar Essentials - [ ] 4-tuple: $G = (V, T, P, S)$. - [ ] Productions: $A \to aB$ (right-linear) or $A \to Ba$ (left-linear) or $A \to a$ or $A \to \varepsilon$. - [ ] **Mixing** left and right linear in the same grammar is **forbidden**. - [ ] One non-terminal on the LHS only (no context-sensitive rules). - [ ] Equivalent to right-linear and to NFA (one-to-one correspondence). ### E. Equivalence Theorem (MUST MEMORIZE) - [ ] **Regular Expression ≡ DFA ≡ NFA ≡ Regular Grammar** (all generate/accept the same class — **regular languages**). - [ ] NFA → DFA: **Subset Construction** (Rabin-Scott, 1959). - [ ] DFA → Regex: **State Elimination** (Kleene's algorithm). - [ ] Regex → NFA: **Thompson's Construction**. - [ ] RG → NFA: **One state per non-terminal**. - [ ] NFA → RG: **One production per transition**. ### F. Closure Properties (Quick Recall) - [ ] Union ✓, Intersection ✓, Complement ✓ - [ ] Concatenation ✓, Kleene Star ✓, Reverse ✓ - [ ] Difference ✓, Homomorphism ✓ - [ ] **Intersection with Regular** is regular; **Intersection with CFL** is not necessarily regular. ### G. Pumping Lemma (for proving NON-regularity) - [ ] If $L$ is regular, $\exists p$ (pumping length) such that any $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \geq p$ can be written as $w = xyz$ with: - $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ - $\vert y \vert \geq 1$ - $xy^iz \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$ - [ ] Use **contradiction**: assume $L$ regular, find a string that cannot be pumped. ### H. Common Pitfalls to Avoid - [ ] Do not confuse $2^Q$ (power set) with $2 \cdot Q$ (scalar multiplication). - [ ] Do not forget to handle $\varepsilon$-closure in NFA-to-DFA conversion. - [ ] Do not mark the start state as accepting **just** because $\varepsilon \in L$ — verify with full construction. - [ ] Do not mix left-linear and right-linear rules in the same grammar. - [ ] Do not assume a DFA is minimal; apply **Hopcroft's algorithm** to minimize. ### I. Key Distinctions Table | Concept | Differentiator | |---------|----------------| | DFA vs NFA | Single state vs set of states per transition | | Right vs Left Linear | Non-terminal on right vs left of terminal | | $\Sigma^*$ vs $\Sigma^+$ | Includes $\varepsilon$ vs excludes $\varepsilon$ | | $L^R$ vs $L$ | Reversed strings vs original strings | | $\hat{\delta}$ vs $\delta$ | Strings vs single symbols | ### J. Real-World Applications (for viva/lab exam) - [ ] Lexical analyzers (compiler design) - [ ] Pattern matchers (`grep`, `awk`, `sed`) - [ ] Network packet filters - [ ] DNA sequence analysis - [ ] Digital circuit design - [ ] Text editors (find/replace) - [ ] Protocol verification (TCP/IP, HTTP headers) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For the 14-mark questions, always: > 1. State the formal definition (1 mark). > 2. Provide the algorithm/construction (4-5 marks). > 3. Apply it to the given problem step-by-step (4-5 marks). > 4. Draw the diagram/table and label all states (2-3 marks). > 5. Conclude with the language equivalence (1 mark).

topics.title: Regular Expression (RE), Equivalence of REs and DFA, Pumping Lemma for regular languages, Closure Properties of Regular Languages, DFA state minimization (Myhill-Nerode Theorem), and Applications of MNT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Regular Expression (RE), Equivalence of REs and DFA, Pumping Lemma for regular languages, Closure Properties of Regular Languages, DFA state minimization (Myhill-Nerode Theorem), and Applications of MNT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Regular Expression (RE) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Regular Expression (RE)** over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is a formal mathematical notation used to describe (generate) a regular language. It is built recursively from primitive symbols using three algebraic operations: **union (∪)**, **concatenation (·)**, and **Kleene star (∗)**.

Formally, the set $\mathbf{RE}$ of all regular expressions over $\Sigma$ is defined inductively:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) } & \varnothing \in \mathbf{RE} \quad \text{(describes the empty language)} \\
\text{(ii) } & \varepsilon \in \mathbf{RE} \quad \text{(describes the language } \{\varepsilon\}) \\
\text{(iii) } & \forall a \in \Sigma, \;\; a \in \mathbf{RE} \\
\text{(iv) } & R_1, R_2 \in \mathbf{RE} \;\Rightarrow\; (R_1 \cup R_2),\; (R_1 \cdot R_2),\; (R_1)^{*}\in \mathbf{RE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Operator Precedence (Highest → Lowest):** $\ast \;\;>\;\; \cdot \;\;>\;\; \cup$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Regular Expression** as a *search pattern* on steroids. Just like you use patterns in a search engine to filter text (e.g., searching filenames), an RE is the mathematical version of that idea. If you have ever typed `*.pdf` to find all PDF files, you have used a simple RE! The `*` in `*.pdf` is exactly the **Kleene star** — it means "zero or more of whatever came before."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Regular Expressions are the **declarative** way of saying what strings you want; **Finite Automata** (NFA/DFA) are the **imperative** machines that recognize them. They describe the **same class of languages** (regular languages), but from different angles.

---

## 1.2 Equivalence of REs and DFA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kleene's Theorem (1956):** A language $L$ is **regular** $\iff$ there exists a Regular Expression $R$ such that $L(R) = L$ $\iff$ there exists a DFA $M$ such that $L(M) = L$.

This means: **REs, NFAs, and DFAs all describe exactly the same class of languages — the Regular Languages.**

The equivalence is established via two constructive transformations:

| Direction | Construction | Algorithm Name |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{RE} \rightarrow \text{NFA}$ | Build NFA with $\varepsilon$-transitions from syntax tree of RE | **Thompson's Construction** |
| $\text{NFA} \rightarrow \text{DFA}$ | Group subsets of NFA states into single DFA states | **Subset Construction** |
| $\text{DFA} \rightarrow \text{RE}$ | Introduce new start/accept states, eliminate intermediate states one-by-one | **State Elimination (GNFA method)** |
| $\text{NFA} \rightarrow \text{RE}$ | Apply subset construction first, then state elimination | **Combined** |

---

## 1.3 Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Statement (Pumping Lemma):** If $L$ is a regular language, then $\exists$ a constant $p$ (called the **pumping length**) such that $\forall s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$, the string $s$ can be written as $s = xyz$ satisfying three conditions simultaneously:
> 1. $\vert xy \vert \leq p$
> 2. $\vert y \vert \geq 1$ (i.e., $y \neq \varepsilon$)
> 3. $\forall i \geq 0, \;\; xy^{i}z \in L$ (the "y" part can be pumped)

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **rubber band loop** representing a long string. If the language is regular, there is a short, repeating "pump" region inside any sufficiently long accepted string. You can stretch that region (pump up) or squash it (pump down) and the string still remains in the language — because a DFA has only finitely many states, and a long string must revisit a state, creating a loop that can be traversed any number of times.

> [!WARNING]
> The Pumping Lemma is used **only to prove that a language is NOT regular** (by contradiction). It cannot prove a language IS regular — for that, you must construct a DFA/RE/NFA.

---

## 1.4 Closure Properties of Regular Languages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A set of languages is **closed** under an operation if applying that operation to any language(s) in the set always produces another language still in the set.

The family of regular languages $\mathcal{R}$ is closed under a wide range of operations:

- **Boolean closures:** Union, Intersection, Complement
- **Algebraic closures:** Concatenation, Kleene star
- **String operations:** Reverse ($L^{R}$), Prefix, Suffix, Substring
- **Mappings:** Homomorphism, Inverse Homomorphism
- **Set operations:** Set difference $L_1 \setminus L_2$, Symmetric difference

---

## 1.5 DFA Minimization & Myhill-Nerode Theorem

> [!NOTE]
> **Myhill-Nerode Equivalence Relation:** For any language $L \subseteq \Sigma^{*}$, define the binary relation $\sim_{L}$ on $\Sigma^{*}$ as:
> $$x \sim_{L} y \iff \forall z \in \Sigma^{*},\; (xz \in L \Leftrightarrow yz \in L)$$

Two strings $x$ and $y$ are equivalent if no "future suffix" $z$ can distinguish them with respect to membership in $L$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Myhill-Nerode Theorem (1957–58):** A language $L$ is regular if and only if $\sim_{L}$ has a **finite number of equivalence classes**. Moreover, the number of such classes equals the **number of states in the unique minimal DFA** for $L$.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of $\sim_{L}$ as **"same destiny"**: two strings $x$ and $y$ are equivalent if, no matter what continuation $z$ you append, they either both end up accepted or both rejected. If you can distinguish them with some suffix, they must lead to different states in the DFA. Thus, the number of distinguishable strings = the number of states needed.

---

## 1.6 Applications of Myhill-Nerode Theorem

The MNT is not just a theoretical curiosity — it has powerful practical applications:

1. **Proving non-regularity** of languages (often more elegant than Pumping Lemma).
2. **Constructing the minimal DFA** algorithmically (via equivalence-class refinement).
3. **Establishing lower bounds** on the number of states required.
4. **Uniqueness of minimal DFA** — any two minimal DFAs for the same language are isomorphic.
5. **Compiler design** — DFA minimization optimizes pattern-matchers (e.g., `lex`/`flex`).
6. **Hardware circuit minimization** — sequential circuit synthesis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Regular Expression Algebra — Identity Rules

> [!NOTE]
> These identities are extremely useful for **simplifying REs** in KTU exam answers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(1) } & R \cup \varnothing = R & \text{(2) } R \cdot \varepsilon = R \\
\text{(3) } & R \cup R = R & \text{(4) } R \cdot \varnothing = \varnothing \\
\text{(5) } & R^{*} = \varepsilon \cup R \cdot R^{*} & \text{(6) } (R^{*})^{*} = R^{*} \\
\text{(7) } & \varnothing^{*} = \varepsilon & \text{(8) } \varepsilon^{*} = \varepsilon \\
\text{(9) } & R \cdot (S \cup T) = R \cdot S \cup R \cdot T & \text{(10) } (R \cup S) \cdot T = R \cdot T \cup S \cdot T
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 2.2 Why the Pumping Lemma Works — Logical Breakdown

The proof relies on the **pigeonhole principle** applied to the DFA:

1. Let $M$ be a DFA with $p$ states accepting $L$.
2. For any string $s = s_1 s_2 \ldots s_n$ with $n \geq p$, the path of states $q_0, q_1, \ldots, q_n$ has $n+1 > p$ states.
3. By pigeonhole, $\exists i < j$ such that $q_i = q_j$ (a state repeats).
4. The substring $y = s_{i+1} \ldots s_j$ corresponds to this loop and can be traversed $0, 1, 2, \ldots$ times.
5. Define $x = s_1 \ldots s_i$, $y = s_{i+1} \ldots s_j$, $z = s_{j+1} \ldots s_n$.
6. $\vert xy \vert = j \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert = j - i \geq 1$.

---

## 2.3 Closure Property Proof Strategies

| Property | Proof Strategy |
|---|---|
| Union $L_1 \cup L_2$ | Add new start state with $\varepsilon$-arrows to old start states (NFA construction) |
| Intersection $L_1 \cap L_2$ | **Product construction**: $Q = Q_1 \times Q_2$, $F = F_1 \times F_2$, $\delta((p,q),a) = (\delta_1(p,a), \delta_2(q,a))$ |
| Complement $\overline{L}$ | Same DFA structure, **swap** accepting and non-accepting states |
| Reverse $L^R$ | Reverse all transitions, swap start and accept states, then apply NFA→DFA |
| Kleene star $L^{*}$ | Add new start state that is also accepting; add $\varepsilon$-arrow from each old accept to old start |

---

## 2.4 Myhill-Nerode Theorem — Stepwise Logical Decomposition

**Proof Idea (Forward Direction):**

1. Suppose $L$ is regular. Then a minimal DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with $|Q| < \infty$ exists.
2. Define $x \sim_L y$ iff $\delta^{*}(q_0, x) = \delta^{*}(q_0, y)$.
3. There are at most $|Q|$ equivalence classes (one per state) — **finite**.

**Proof Idea (Reverse Direction):**

1. Suppose $\sim_L$ has $k$ finite equivalence classes $[w_1], [w_2], \ldots, [w_k]$.
2. Construct a DFA $M$ with states $Q = \{[w_1], \ldots, [w_k]\}$.
3. Set $q_0 = [\varepsilon]$, $F = \{[w] \mid w \in L\}$.
4. Define $\delta([w], a) = [wa]$.
5. Then $L(M) = L$, so $L$ is regular.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| Regular Expression | $R ::= \varnothing \mid \varepsilon \mid a \mid R \cup R \mid R \cdot R \mid R^{*}$ | Recursive definition |
| Kleene Star | $L(R^{*}) = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} L(R)^{i}$ | $L^{0} = \{\varepsilon\}$ |
| Myhill-Nerode Relation | $x \sim_{L} y \iff \forall z \in \Sigma^{*}, xz \in L \Leftrightarrow yz \in L$ | Equivalence relation |
| Pumping Lemma | $\exists p, \forall s \in L, \vert s \vert \geq p, s = xyz$ with 3 conditions | $y$ can be pumped |
| Pumping Condition 1 | $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ | Loop within first $p$ symbols |
| Pumping Condition 2 | $\vert y \vert \geq 1$ | Loop is non-trivial |
| Pumping Condition 3 | $\forall i \geq 0,\; xy^{i}z \in L$ | Loop repeatable |
| Number of States (MNT) | $\vert Q_{\min} \vert = $ number of $\sim_L$ classes | Uniqueness invariant |
| Subset Construction State | Represents set of NFA states | Powerset $2^{Q_{NFA}}$ |
| DFA Size Upper Bound | $\vert Q_{DFA} \vert \leq 2^{\vert Q_{NFA} \vert}$ | Worst-case exponential |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Lexical Analyzers** (Lex, Flex): Build minimal DFAs from RE patterns for token recognition.
- **Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS)**: Snort rules use RE for packet signature matching.
- **DNA Sequence Analysis in Bioinformatics**: REs describe biologically meaningful patterns.
- **Model Checking & Verification**: Hardware/software verifiers minimize state machines for tractability.
- **Search Engines (grep, awk)**: RE drives high-speed text searching — direct industrial application.
- **Routing Protocols**: Regular-language-theoretic models analyze protocol behavior.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Building a DFA from a Regular Expression

**Given RE:** $R = (a \cup b)^{*} abb$ (all strings over $\{a,b\}$ ending in `abb`).

### Step 1: Construct NFA via Thompson's Construction (Skeleton)

We start with NFAs for the atomic sub-expressions and combine them:
- $N_1$ for $a$: two states, transition on $a$.
- $N_2$ for $b$: two states, transition on $b$.
- $N_3$ for $a \cup b$: parallel paths with $\varepsilon$-merger.
- $N_4$ for $(a \cup b)^{*}$: add $\varepsilon$-loop from end back to start.
- $N_5$ for $abb$: three serial transitions.
- $N_6$ for full $R$: concatenation of $N_4$ and $N_5$.

### Step 2: Apply Subset Construction

Define DFA states as subsets of NFA states reachable from start:
- $A = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_7\}$ (start, includes $\varepsilon$-closures).
- $B = A \cup \{q_4\}$ (on input $a$).
- $C = A \cup \{q_5\}$ (on input $b$).
- $D = \{q_6, q_7, q_1, q_2, q_3\}$ (after reading `ab`).
- $E = \{q_8, q_7, q_1, q_2, q_3\}$ (after `abb`, accepting).

The DFA has **5 states** and recognizably tracks how many consecutive $b$'s we have seen (0, 1, or 2) at the suffix.

---

## 3.2 Pumping Lemma Proof: $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is NOT Regular

**Assumption (for contradiction):** $L$ is regular. Then by the Pumping Lemma, it has pumping length $p$.

### Step 1: Choose a target string

Pick $s = a^{p} b^{p} \in L$ with $\vert s \vert = 2p \geq p$.

### Step 2: Apply Pumping Lemma

By the lemma, $\exists$ decomposition $s = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and $\vert y \vert \geq 1$.

Since $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and the first $p$ symbols of $s$ are all $a$'s, both $x$ and $y$ consist entirely of $a$'s. So $y = a^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.

### Step 3: Pump down (i = 0)

$$xy^{0}z = xz = a^{p-k} b^{p}$$

The number of $a$'s is $p - k \neq p$ (since $k \geq 1$), but the number of $b$'s is still $p$. Hence $xz \notin L$.

### Step 4: Contradiction

This violates condition (3) of the Pumping Lemma. Therefore, our assumption that $L$ is regular is **false**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Closure Proof: Regular Languages Closed Under Complement

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem:** If $L$ is regular, then $\overline{L} = \Sigma^{*} \setminus L$ is also regular.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Since $L$ is regular, there exists a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ such that $L(M) = L$.

**Step 2:** Construct a new DFA $M' = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, Q \setminus F)$ by keeping the exact same components except the **accept set**.

**Step 3:** For any string $w \in \Sigma^{*}$:

$$
w \in L(M') \iff \delta^{*}(q_0, w) \in Q \setminus F \iff \delta^{*}(q_0, w) \notin F \iff w \notin L \iff w \in \overline{L}
$$

**Step 4:** Since $M'$ is a valid DFA, $\overline{L}$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical subtlety:** This trick works only with a **DFA**, not directly with an NFA! Because in an NFA, multiple paths can exist, and "no path accepts" ≠ "every path rejects" (this is the **subset construction** reason).

---

## 3.4 Myhill-Nerode Theorem: Counting States for $L = (ab)^{*}$

Let $L = (ab)^{*} = \{\varepsilon, ab, abab, ababab, \ldots\}$.

### Step 1: Find equivalence classes of $\sim_L$

| String $w$ | Distinguishing suffix? | Class |
|---|---|---|
| $\varepsilon$ | $ab \in L$, but $\varepsilon \cdot ab = ab \in L$; check $a$: $a \notin L$, $\varepsilon \cdot a = a \notin L$. So $a$ doesn't distinguish them. | $[\varepsilon]$ |
| $a$ | Test suffix $b$: $ab \in L$ but $a \cdot b = ab \in L$. Test suffix $a$: $aa \notin L$ but $a \cdot a = aa \notin L$. Actually $a$ is in no class with others. | $[a]$ |
| $ab$ | $ab \in L$; suffix $\varepsilon$: $ab \in L$, but $a \cdot \varepsilon = a \notin L$ — wait, we need $\forall z$, check $xz \in L \Leftrightarrow yz \in L$. | $[ab]$ |

Continuing systematically:
- $[\varepsilon]$ — represented by state $q_0$ (start, accepting)
- $[a]$ — represented by state $q_1$ (after reading $a$)
- $[ab]$ — represented by state $q_2$ (after reading $ab$, accepting, back to $q_0$)
- $[aba]$ — leads to $q_1$ again (loop)

### Step 2: Conclude

There are exactly **3 equivalence classes**, so the minimal DFA has **3 states**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= q_1 \\
\delta(q_0, b) &= q_{\text{trap}} \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= q_{\text{trap}} \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= q_2 \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= q_1 \\
\delta(q_2, b) &= q_{\text{trap}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 DFA Minimization: Table-Filling Algorithm

**Input:** DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ with states $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ where $F = \{4\}$.

**Step 1:** Initialize table. Mark all pairs $(p, q)$ where exactly one is in $F$ (these are **distinguishable**).

| | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | — | | | |
| 2 | | — | | |
| 3 | | | — | |
| 4 | X (since 1∉F, 4∈F) | X (2∉F, 4∈F) | X (3∉F, 4∈F) | — |

**Step 2:** Iteratively mark. For each unmarked pair $(p, q)$ and each $a \in \Sigma$, if $(\delta(p,a), \delta(q,a))$ is already marked, then mark $(p, q)$.

**Step 3:** Unmarked pairs at convergence are **equivalent** — merge them.

**Step 4:** Construct minimized DFA from equivalence classes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Language Classes

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    CFL -->|strict subset| CSL
    CSL -->|strict subset| RE
```

> **Note:** Regular Languages (describable by REs / DFAs) form the smallest class in the Chomsky Hierarchy.

## 4.2 Equivalence Mapping: RE ⇔ NFA ⇔ DFA ⇔ GNFA

```mermaid

    RE1[Regular Expression R]:::boxClass
    NFA1[epsilon-NFA via Thompson]:::boxClass
    DFA1[DFA via Subset Construction]:::boxClass
    MIN1[Minimal DFA via Myhill Nerode]:::boxClass
    GNFA1[GNFA for Reverse Engineering]:::boxClass

    RE1 -->|Thompson Construction| NFA1
    NFA1 -->|Subset Construction| DFA1
    DFA1 -->|State Minimization| MIN1
    DFA1 -->|Add start accept states| GNFA1
    GNFA1 -->|State Elimination| RE1

```

## 4.3 DFA State Minimization Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    classDef resultClass fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000

    STEP1[Step 1: Remove unreachable states BFS from q0]:::procClass
    STEP2[Step 2: Initialize table mark all accept vs non accept pairs]:::procClass

    STEP4[Step 4: Iterate until no new marks]:::procClass
    STEP5[Step 5: Unmarked pairs are equivalent]:::procClass
    STEP6[Step 6: Merge equivalent states into one]:::procClass
    END[Output Minimal DFA Mmin]:::resultClass

    START --> STEP1
    STEP1 --> STEP2
    STEP2 --> STEP3
    STEP3 --> STEP4
    STEP4 --> STEP5
    STEP5 --> STEP6
    STEP6 --> END
```

## 4.4 Pumping Lemma Decision Tree (Is L Regular?)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef decClass fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#a04000,color:#000
    classDef yesClass fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    classDef noClass fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#a93226,color:#000

    Q1{Is L finite?}:::decClass
    Q2{Can we construct DFA, NFA, or RE for L?}:::decClass
    Q3{Does L satisfy Pumping Lemma for all splits?}:::decClass
    Q4{Find adversary string s and split that fails Pumping Lemma?}:::decClass

    YES1[Yes: L is Regular trivially]:::yesClass
    YES2[Yes: L is Regular by construction]:::yesClass
    YES3[Yes: Likely Regular, try to construct DFA]:::yesClass
    NO1[No: Pumping Lemma violated, L is NOT Regular]:::noClass

    Q1 -->|Yes| YES1
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| YES2
    Q2 -->|No| Q3
    Q3 -->|Cannot violate for any split| YES3
    Q3 -->|Can find violating split| Q4
    Q4 -->|Found| NO1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Regular Expression. Construct an RE for the language $L = \{ w \in \{0,1\}^{*} \mid w \text{ contains the substring } 011\}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A Regular Expression is a formal notation for representing regular languages built from symbols of an alphabet $\Sigma$ using the operations union, concatenation, and Kleene star.

**Construction:**
Any string over $\{0,1\}$ that contains `011` as a substring can be described as: any prefix, then `011`, then any suffix.

$$
R = (0 \cup 1)^{*} \cdot 011 \cdot (0 \cup 1)^{*}
$$

**(1 mark)** for definition, **(1 mark)** for breaking the language structurally, **(1 mark)** for correct RE.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Pumping Lemma for regular languages. What is it used for?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement:** If $L$ is a regular language, then there exists a constant $p$ (pumping length) such that for every string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$, $s$ can be decomposed as $s = xyz$ where $\vert xy \vert \leq p$, $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, and $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.
>
> **Use:** The Pumping Lemma is used to **prove that a language is NOT regular** by contradiction — assuming it is regular, finding a string that cannot be pumped.

**(2 marks)** for the statement with all three conditions, **(1 mark)** for stating the application.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the **three conditions** of the Pumping Lemma simultaneously. Writing only one or two will result in **-2 to -3 marks** loss. Also, for closure property proofs, the **DFA construction must be explicitly written** — saying "we construct a DFA" without giving the tuple $M' = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F')$ loses marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Convert the following Regular Expression to a DFA using Thompson's Construction followed by Subset Construction: $R = (a \cup b)^{*} a$ (7 marks)

**Step 1: Build NFA via Thompson's Construction (3 marks)**

Construct NFAs for each sub-expression:
- $N_a$: states $\{p_0, p_1\}$, transition $p_0 \xrightarrow{a} p_1$
- $N_b$: states $\{p_2, p_3\}$, transition $p_2 \xrightarrow{b} p_3$
- $N_{a \cup b}$: states $\{p_4, p_5, p_6, p_7\}$, with $\varepsilon$-transitions $p_4 \to p_0$, $p_4 \to p_2$, $p_1 \to p_5$, $p_3 \to p_5$
- $N_{(a \cup b)^{*}}$: add new start $p_8$, accept $p_9$, $\varepsilon$-arrows $p_8 \to p_4$, $p_5 \to p_4$ (loop), $p_5 \to p_9$, $p_8 \to p_9$
- $N_a^{(2)}$: states $\{p_{10}, p_{11}\}$, transition $p_{10} \xrightarrow{a} p_{11}$
- Concatenate: $\varepsilon$-arrow from $p_9$ to $p_{10}$, accept state is $p_{11}$.

**Step 2: Apply Subset Construction (4 marks)**

Compute reachable subsets starting from start state with $\varepsilon$-closure:
- $A = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{p_8\}) = \{p_8, p_4, p_9, p_0, p_2\}$
- $\delta(A, a) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{p_1, p_{10}\}) = B = \{p_1, p_5, p_9, p_4, p_0, p_2, p_{10}\}$
- $\delta(A, b) = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{p_3\}) = C = \{p_3, p_5, p_9, p_4, p_0, p_2\}$
- $\delta(B, a) = B$ (after reading $a$, we are still in same conceptual state)
- $\delta(B, b) = C$
- $\delta(C, a) = B$, $\delta(C, b) = C$

**DFA Table:**

| State | $a$ | $b$ | Accept? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | No |
| $B$ | $B$ | $C$ | **Yes** (contains $p_{11}$ if reached) |
| $C$ | $B$ | $C$ | No |

> **Note:** Upon careful evaluation, the minimal DFA has only **2 states**: $q_0$ (no pending $a$) and $q_1$ (ends in $a$, accepting).

#### (b) Apply the Myhill-Nerode Theorem to find the minimum number of states for the language $L = \{ w \in \{0,1\}^{*} \mid w \text{ ends in } 01\}$ (7 marks)

**Step 1: Identify equivalence classes (3 marks)**

Test strings by their distinguishing suffixes:
- $[\varepsilon]$: doesn't end in `01`. Suffix `01` puts in $L$, any other non-empty doesn't.
- $[1]$: ends in `1`. Future `01` completes the pattern.
- $[0]$: ends in `0`. Future `1` completes the pattern.
- $[01]$: ends in `01`, accepting state.

**Step 2: Verify no further splits (2 marks)**

Check $[\varepsilon] \neq [1]$: $\varepsilon \cdot 1 = 1 \notin L$, but $1 \cdot 1 = 11 \notin L$. Try $\varepsilon \cdot 01 = 01 \in L$, $1 \cdot 01 = 101 \notin L$ — distinguished!

**Step 3: Conclude (2 marks)**

There are **3 equivalence classes**: $[\varepsilon]$, $[0]$, $[1]$ (where $[01]$ merges with $[\varepsilon]$ or another class). After minimization: **2 states suffice** — one for "ends in 0" (accepting if considering prefix of target) and "ends in 1 or empty" (with proper transitions).

> **Correct minimal DFA** has 2 states: $q_0$ (start, non-accepting), $q_1$ (accepting, on reading $0$ from $q_0$); $\delta(q_0, 0) = q_1$, $\delta(q_0, 1) = q_0$, $\delta(q_1, 0) = q_1$, $\delta(q_1, 1) = q_0$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) State and prove the Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages. Use it to show that $L = \{ a^{n} b^{n} \mid n \geq 0 \}$ is not regular. (7 marks)

**Statement (2 marks):** Already given in Section 1.3.

**Proof Idea (3 marks):** Using pigeonhole principle on a DFA with $p$ states — outlined in Section 2.2 above.

**Application to $L = \{a^n b^n\}$ (2 marks):**

1. Assume $L$ is regular with pumping length $p$.
2. Choose $s = a^{p} b^{p} \in L$ with $\vert s \vert = 2p \geq p$.
3. By Pumping Lemma: $s = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \leq p$, $\vert y \vert \geq 1$, and $xy^{i}z \in L$ for all $i \geq 0$.
4. Since $\vert xy \vert \leq p$ and the first $p$ symbols are all $a$'s, $y = a^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$.
5. Pump down: $xy^{0}z = a^{p-k} b^{p} \notin L$ (unequal counts).
6. **Contradiction.** Hence $L$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$

#### (b) Prove that the family of regular languages is closed under Union and Reversal. (7 marks)

**Closure Under Union (3.5 marks):**

Let $L_1$ and $L_2$ be regular with NFAs $M_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_1, F_1)$ and $M_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_2, F_2)$.

Construct NFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:
- $Q = Q_1 \cup Q_2 \cup \{q_0\}$ (new start state)
- $F = F_1 \cup F_2$
- $\delta(q_0, \varepsilon) = \{q_1, q_2\}$
- For all other $q \in Q_1$, $\delta(q, a) = \delta_1(q, a)$
- For all other $q \in Q_2$, $\delta(q, a) = \delta_2(q, a)$

A string is accepted by $M$ iff it leads from $q_0$ via $\varepsilon$ to either $M_1$ or $M_2$ and is accepted there, i.e., $w \in L_1 \cup L_2$. Hence $L_1 \cup L_2$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

**Closure Under Reversal (3.5 marks):**

Let $L$ be regular, accepted by DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$.

**Step 1:** Construct NFA $M' = (Q, \Sigma, \delta', F, \{q_0\})$ where $\delta'(q, a) = \{p \mid \delta(p, a) = q\}$ (reverse all transitions, swap start and accept).

**Step 2:** $M'$ accepts a string $w = w_1 w_2 \cdots w_n$ iff there is a path from some $f \in F$ through transitions $w_n, w_{n-1}, \ldots, w_1$ to $q_0$. This is equivalent to saying $w$ reversed, i.e., $w_n \cdots w_1$, is accepted by $M$. So $L(M') = L^{R}$.

**Step 3:** Since NFAs recognize regular languages, $L^R$ is regular. $\blacksquare$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ☐ **RE Definition:** Recursive — base cases ($\varnothing$, $\varepsilon$, $a$) + 3 operators ($\cup$, $\cdot$, $\ast$).
- ☐ **Kleene's Theorem:** REs, NFAs, DFAs all describe the same set: regular languages.
- ☐ **Thompson's Construction:** RE → NFA — adds $\varepsilon$-transitions; simple, mechanical.
- ☐ **Subset Construction:** NFA → DFA — state = set of NFA states; can be exponential.
- ☐ **Pumping Lemma — Three Conditions** (memorize exactly):
  - $\vert xy \vert \leq p$
  - $\vert y \vert \geq 1$
  - $\forall i \geq 0,\; xy^{i}z \in L$
- ☐ **Pumping Lemma Purpose:** ONLY for proving **non-regularity** by contradiction.
- ☐ **Closure Properties (Regular Languages closed under):** Union, Intersection, Complement, Concatenation, Kleene star, Reverse, Homomorphism, Set difference.
- ☐ **Complement Trick:** Only valid for **DFA**, not NFA — swap $F$ and $Q \setminus F$.
- ☐ **Myhill-Nerode Relation:** $x \sim_L y \iff \forall z, xz \in L \Leftrightarrow yz \in L$.
- ☐ **MNT Theorem:** $L$ is regular $\iff$ $\sim_L$ has **finitely many** equivalence classes.
- ☐ **Minimal DFA:** Number of states = Number of $\sim_L$ classes; **unique up to isomorphism**.
- ☐ **Minimization Algorithm:** Table-filling — mark distinguishable pairs, merge remaining.
- ☐ **Common Exam Trap:** Forgetting to show the DFA tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ explicitly in closure proofs.
- ☐ **Common Exam Trap 2:** Using Pumping Lemma to "prove" regularity — WRONG direction.
- ☐ **Standard Non-Regular Examples to remember:** $\{a^n b^n\}$, $\{ww \mid w \in \Sigma^*\}$, $\{a^{n^2}\}$, $\{a^p \mid p \text{ prime}\}$.
- ☐ **MNT vs Pumping Lemma:** MNT is **stronger** — can prove non-regularity AND give lower bound on states; Pumping Lemma only proves non-regularity.
- ☐ **Practical Applications:** Lexers, NIDS (Snort), grep/awk, hardware synthesis, model checking.
