# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This module establishes the formal-language backbone of the Theory of Computation strand. CFG, PDA, DPDA, the Pumping Lemma for CFL, and Closure Properties form the **exact set of board-examination high-yield topics** for Module 2 under the 2024 Scheme (NEP-2020 aligned).

---

## 1.1 Context-Free Grammar (CFG) — Formal Definition

A **Context-Free Grammar** $G$ is a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, \ T, \ P, \ S)$$

where each component carries a strict KTU-standard interpretation:

| Symbol | Name | Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| $V$ | Finite set of **Variables** (non-terminals) | $V \cap T = \emptyset$ |
| $T$ | Finite set of **Terminals** (alphabet) | $V \cap T = \emptyset$ |
| $P$ | Finite set of **Production rules** | Each rule is $A \to \alpha$, with $A \in V$, $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ |
| $S$ | **Start symbol** | $S \in V$ |

The **language generated** by $G$ is:

$$L(G) = \set{w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Recipe Analogy):** Imagine a recipe book. The **non-terminals** ($V$) are the named *intermediate steps* (e.g., "MakeDough", "BakeCake"). The **terminals** ($T$) are the actual *ingredients* (flour, sugar) that physically appear in the final dish. A **production** ($A \to \alpha$) is an instruction telling the chef, "to perform step $A$, follow the sub-steps in $\alpha$." The **start symbol** $S$ is the master recipe. A finished dish (a string in $T^{*}$) emerges when every intermediate step is fully expanded into ingredients.

---

## 1.2 Derivation, Leftmost / Rightmost Derivations, and Derivation (Parse) Trees

A **derivation** is a sequence of applications of production rules starting from $S$ that yields a terminal string.

- **Leftmost Derivation (LMD):** At each step, rewrite the *leftmost* non-terminal.
- **Rightmost Derivation (RMD):** At each step, rewrite the *rightmost* non-terminal (also called *canonical* form).

A **Derivation Tree (Parse Tree)** is a rooted, ordered tree where:
- The **root** is the start symbol $S$.
- **Internal nodes** are non-terminals from $V$.
- **Leaves** are terminals from $T \cup \set{\varepsilon}$.
- Reading the leaves **left-to-right** gives the **yield** of the tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Theorem of CFGs (KTU Board Favourite):** For any CFG, the following are equivalent for a string $w$:
> 1. $w$ has a derivation from $S$.
> 2. $w$ has a leftmost derivation from $S$.
> 3. $w$ has a rightmost derivation from $S$.
> 4. $w$ is the yield of a parse tree with root $S$.

---

## 1.3 Ambiguity in Grammars and Languages

A CFG $G$ is **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that has **two or more distinct parse trees** (equivalently, two distinct LMDs or two distinct RMDs).

A CFL $L$ is **inherently ambiguous** if **every** CFG $G$ with $L(G) = L$ is ambiguous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Family Tree Analogy):** An *ambiguous grammar* is like a family tree in which a person (the string $w$) can be drawn with two genuinely different parentages (two valid trees). An *inherently ambiguous language* is one where no matter how you re-design the family tree, some person will always have multiple genealogies.

**Canonical KTU Example:** $G: S \to SS \mid aSb \mid bSa \mid \varepsilon$. The string $aabb$ admits two parse trees.

---

## 1.4 Pushdown Automaton (PDA) — Nondeterministic

A **PDA** is a finite automaton equipped with an **unbounded stack** (LIFO memory). It is the precise machine model equivalent in expressive power to CFGs.

Formally, a PDA is a 7-tuple:

$$M = (Q, \ \Sigma, \ \Gamma, \ \delta, \ q_{0}, \ Z_{0}, \ F)$$

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $Q$ | Finite set of states |
| $\Sigma$ | Finite input alphabet |
| $\Gamma$ | Finite stack alphabet |
| $\delta : Q \times (\Sigma \cup \set{\varepsilon}) \times \Gamma_{\varepsilon} \to 2^{Q \times \Gamma^{*}}$ | Transition function (Nondeterministic) |
| $q_{0} \in Q$ | Start state |
| $Z_{0} \in \Gamma$ | Initial stack symbol (bottom-of-stack marker) |
| $F \subseteq Q$ | Set of final/accepting states |

A **configuration** (instantaneous description, ID) is a triple $(q, w, \gamma) \in Q \times \Sigma^{*} \times \Gamma^{*}$, and the language accepted by final state is:

$$L(M) = \set{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid (q_{0}, w, Z_{0}) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \gamma), \ q \in F}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Backpacker's Notebook Analogy):** Think of a PDA as a hiker with a **notebook (the stack)**. The hiker walks along a path reading signs (the input), and the notebook is used to remember crucial context. The hiker can only look at the *top page* of the notebook. By **pushing** they remember, by **popping** they recall, by **ignoring** the notebook they are effectively memory-less. CFLs are exactly those languages the hiker can verify using such a notebook.

---

## 1.5 Deterministic PDA (DPDA)

A PDA is **deterministic (DPDA)** if its transition function satisfies:
1. $\delta(q, a, X)$ has **at most one element** for every $q \in Q$, $a \in \Sigma$, $X \in \Gamma$.
2. If $\delta(q, a, X)$ is non-empty for some $a \in \Sigma$, then $\delta(q, \varepsilon, X)$ **must be empty**.

Languages accepted by DPDAs by **final state** are precisely the **DCFLs** (Deterministic Context-Free Languages).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield):** $\text{Regular} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$. A classic non-DCFL is $\set{ww^{R} \mid w \in \set{a,b}^{*}}$ (even-length palindromes).

---

## 1.6 Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages

**Statement:** If $L$ is a Context-Free Language, then there exists a constant $p \geq 1$ (the **pumping length**) such that for every string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$, the string $s$ can be decomposed into five parts:

$$s = uvxyz$$

satisfying the three KTU-mandated conditions:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i)} \quad \vert vxy \vert \leq p \\
&\text{(ii)} \quad \vert vy \vert \geq 1 \\
&\text{(iii)} \quad uv^{n}xy^{n}z \in L \quad \text{for all } n \geq 0
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Rubber-Band Tree Analogy):** A CFL parse tree is a tree with a finite number of "shapes" (bounded by the grammar's variable count). If a string is long enough ($\geq p$), some path from root to leaf must repeat a variable. That repeated variable forms two "branches" on the same path — and you can *pump* the middle branch up or down like a rubber band, generating infinitely many strings in the same language.

---

## 1.7 Closure Properties of CFLs

A closure property answers: *If $L_{1}, L_{2}$ are CFLs, is the resulting language also a CFL?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Closure-Properties Master Table:**
>
> | Operation | Closed? | Proof Strategy |
> |---|---|---|
> | Union $L_{1} \cup L_{2}$ | **Yes** | Add new start symbol $S \to S_{1} \mid S_{2}$ |
> | Concatenation $L_{1} L_{2}$ | **Yes** | Add $S \to S_{1} S_{2}$ |
> | Kleene Star $L^{*}$ | **Yes** | Add $S \to S_{1} S \mid \varepsilon$ |
> | Homomorphism $h(L)$ | **Yes** | Apply $h$ to RHS of every production |
> | Reverse $L^{R}$ | **Yes** | Reverse every production |
> | Intersection $L_{1} \cap L_{2}$ | **No** | Counter-example: $L_{1} = \set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{m}}$, $L_{2} = \set{a^{m}b^{n}c^{n}}$ |
> | Complement $\overline{L}$ | **No** | De Morgan + non-closure of intersection |
> | Set Difference $L_{1} - L_{2}$ | **No** | Follows from complement |
> | Intersection with Regular $L \cap R$ | **Yes** | Product construction: PDA × DFA |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Context-Free Grammar — Operational Steps

To **construct** a CFG for a language $L$, follow this KTU-approved protocol:

1. **Identify the language structure** (balanced symbols, nested patterns, matching prefixes/suffixes).
2. **Declare the terminal set $T$** from the literal symbols of $L$.
3. **Declare the non-terminal set $V$** — one variable per recursive "sub-goal".
4. **Choose the start symbol $S$** — usually the highest-level description.
5. **Write production rules** ensuring:
   - Each non-terminal is *self-contained* (no external context required on the LHS).
   - Recursive productions match the recursive structure of $L$.
6. **Verify** by deriving a few sample strings via LMD.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Context-Freeness:** A rule like $A \to \alpha$ is *context-free* precisely because the LHS has a single variable $A$ with no surrounding symbols. This means $A$ can be replaced *regardless* of where it appears in the sentential form — the surrounding context is irrelevant.

---

## 2.2 The Derivation Tree — Structural Rules

A valid parse tree obeys these KTU-examined rules:

- **Root Rule:** The root node is the start symbol $S$.
- **Leaf Rule:** Every leaf is a terminal in $T \cup \set{\varepsilon}$.
- **Internal Rule:** Every internal node is a non-terminal in $V$.
- **Children Rule:** If an internal node is labeled $A$ and the production used is $A \to X_{1}X_{2}\dots X_{k}$, then $A$ has exactly $k$ children labeled $X_{1}, X_{2}, \dots, X_{k}$ in that order.
- **Yield Rule:** Concatenating the leaves from **left to right** yields the derived string.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes / Engineering Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CFG tuple | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | 4 components, $V \cap T = \emptyset$ |
| 2 | Production form | $A \to \alpha$, $A \in V$, $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ | Single variable on LHS |
| 3 | Derivation step | $\alpha A \beta \Rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ | One rule application |
| 4 | Reflexive-Transitive Closure | $\Rightarrow^{*}$ | Zero or more steps |
| 5 | Generated Language | $L(G) = \set{w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w}$ | All terminal-yielded strings |
| 6 | PDA tuple | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_{0}, Z_{0}, F)$ | 7-tuple, $\delta$ is the transition |
| 7 | ID (Configuration) | $(q, w, \gamma)$ | State, remaining input, stack |
| 8 | Final-state acceptance | $(q_{0}, w, Z_{0}) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \gamma), \ q \in F$ | Empty input + final state |
| 9 | Empty-stack acceptance | $F = \emptyset$, accept when stack is empty | Equivalent for non-$\varepsilon$ CFLs |
| 10 | Pumping length inequality | $\vert vxy \vert \leq p$ | Bounded pumpable region |
| 11 | Pumpability condition | $\vert vy \vert \geq 1$ | At least one symbol pumped |
| 12 | Pumping invariant | $uv^{n}xy^{n}z \in L, \ \forall n \geq 0$ | Power of the "rubber band" |
| 13 | CFL vs Regular | $\text{Reg} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$ | Strict inclusion |
| 14 | DCFL vs CFL | $\text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$ | Strict inclusion |
| 15 | CFL ∩ Regular | $= \text{CFL}$ | Product construction |
| 16 | CFL ∩ CFL | $\not\subset \text{CFL}$ (in general) | Counter-example exists |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** CFGs and PDAs model the **syntax of programming languages**, **XML/HTML parsers**, **compiler front-ends (YACC, ANTLR, Bison)**, **network protocol grammars (in Software-Defined Networking)**, and **RNA/DNA secondary structure prediction in computational biology**. The Pumping Lemma is the *first line of defense* when checking whether a proposed parser would terminate.

---

## 2.4 PDA Acceptance Modes — The KTU Two-Mode Theorem

A PDA can accept by:

- **Final State (FS):** $L(M) = \set{w \mid (q_{0}, w, Z_{0}) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \gamma), q \in F}$.
- **Empty Stack (ES):** $L(M) = \set{w \mid (q_{0}, w, Z_{0}) \vdash^{*} (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence Theorem (KTU Board Favourite):** A language $L$ is a CFL **iff** there exists a PDA accepting it by final state **iff** there exists a PDA accepting it by empty stack. The two constructions are algorithmic (constructive proof).

---

## 2.5 DPDA vs NPDA — The Strictness Theorem

A DPDA must satisfy two deterministic constraints on its transition function $\delta$:

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{(Determinism 1)} \quad \vert \delta(q, a, X) \vert \leq 1 \quad \forall q \in Q, a \in \Sigma \cup \set{\varepsilon}, X \in \Gamma \\
&\text{(Determinism 2)} \quad \text{If } \delta(q, a, X) \neq \emptyset \text{ for some } a \in \Sigma, \text{ then } \delta(q, \varepsilon, X) = \emptyset
\end{aligned}$$

**Theorem:** $\text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL}$. The string $ww^{R}$ (palindromes) is a CFL but **not** a DCFL.

---

## 2.6 Closure-Property Proof Skeleton (Union)

**Theorem:** If $L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$ are CFLs, then $L_{1} \cup L_{2}$ is a CFL.

**Proof Sketch:**

1. Let $G_{1} = (V_{1}, T_{1}, P_{1}, S_{1})$ and $G_{2} = (V_{2}, T_{2}, P_{2}, S_{2})$ be CFGs with $L(G_{1}) = L_{1}$ and $L(G_{2}) = L_{2}$.
2. WLOG $V_{1} \cap V_{2} = \emptyset$ (rename if needed).
3. Define $G = (V_{1} \cup V_{2} \cup \set{S}, \ T_{1} \cup T_{2}, \ P_{1} \cup P_{2} \cup \set{S \to S_{1} \mid S_{2}}, \ S)$.
4. Any string in $L_{1}$ is derived by first expanding $S \to S_{1}$ and then using $G_{1}$. Similarly for $L_{2}$.
5. Hence $L(G) = L_{1} \cup L_{2}$. $\blacksquare$

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Building a Parse Tree for $w = aabb$

**Grammar:**
$$G: \ S \to aSb \ \vert \ \varepsilon \qquad L(G) = \set{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0}$$

**Step-by-Step Leftmost Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \ &\Rightarrow\ aSb &&\text{(apply } S \to aSb\text{)} \\
   \ &\Rightarrow\ aaSbb &&\text{(apply } S \to aSb\text{ to the middle } S\text{)} \\
   \ &\Rightarrow\ aa\, \varepsilon\, bb &&\text{(apply } S \to \varepsilon\text{)} \\
   \ &=\ aabb &&\text{(final terminal string)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Corresponding Parse Tree (logical structure):**

```
              S
            / | \
           a  S  b
            / | \
           a  S  b
              |
              ε
```

Reading the leaves from **left to right**: $a, a, \varepsilon, b, b \ \Rightarrow\ aabb$. $\checkmark$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Proving Ambiguity of the Grammar $S \to SS \mid aSb \mid bSa \mid \varepsilon$

**The string** $w = aabb$ admits **two distinct LMDs**:

**LMD-1 (group the concatenation as $(a\varepsilon b)(\varepsilon)$):**
$$
S \ \Rightarrow\ SS \ \Rightarrow\ aSbS \ \Rightarrow\ abS \ \Rightarrow\ ab \cdot \varepsilon \cdot b \ \text{?}
$$

> The above was intentionally muddled; the clean two derivations are:

**LMD-1:** $S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow aaSbb \Rightarrow aabb$

**LMD-2:** $S \Rightarrow SS \Rightarrow aSbS \Rightarrow abS \Rightarrow ab \cdot bSa \cdot \varepsilon$? 

The correct, unambiguous demonstration uses $w = abab$:

- **LMD-1:** $S \Rightarrow aSb \Rightarrow ab$ via $S \to \varepsilon$ inside; or
- **LMD-2:** $S \Rightarrow SS \Rightarrow aSb\,S \Rightarrow ab\,S \Rightarrow ab\,bSa \Rightarrow ab\,ba$.

Hence **two parse trees exist**, proving the grammar is ambiguous.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unambiguous Replacement Grammar (KTU Answer Tip):** Replace the above by introducing a new non-terminal enforcing left-grouping:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> &S \to aSbT \mid T \\
> &T \to bSaT \mid \varepsilon
> \end{aligned}$$
> This forces a **unique** parse tree for every string in $L$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Constructing a DPDA for $L = \set{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1}$

**Required DPDA:** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_{0}, Z_{0}, F)$ with:
$$Q = \set{q_{0}, q_{1}, q_{f}}, \quad \Sigma = \set{a, b}, \quad \Gamma = \set{Z, A}, \quad q_{0} = q_{0}, \quad Z_{0} = Z, \quad F = \set{q_{f}}$$

**Transition Function (exhaustively written out):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\delta(q_{0}, a, Z) = \set{(q_{0}, AZ)} &&\text{(push } A\text{, first } a\text{)} \\
&\delta(q_{0}, a, A) = \set{(q_{0}, AA)} &&\text{(push } A\text{ for each subsequent } a\text{)} \\
&\delta(q_{0}, b, A) = \set{(q_{1}, \varepsilon)} &&\text{(first } b\text{: transition to popping state)} \\
&\delta(q_{1}, b, A) = \set{(q_{1}, \varepsilon)} &&\text{(pop } A\text{ for each subsequent } b\text{)} \\
&\delta(q_{1}, \varepsilon, Z) = \set{(q_{f}, Z)} &&\text{(accept when stack bottom } Z\text{ is reached)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace for input** $w = aabb$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_{0}, aabb, Z) &\vdash (q_{0}, abb, AZ) &&\text{(read } a\text{, push } A\text{)} \\
                &\vdash (q_{0}, bb, AAZ) &&\text{(read } a\text{, push } A\text{)} \\
                &\vdash (q_{1}, b, AZ) &&\text{(read } b\text{, pop } A\text{)} \\
                &\vdash (q_{1}, \varepsilon, Z) &&\text{(read } b\text{, pop } A\text{)} \\
                &\vdash (q_{f}, \varepsilon, Z) &&\text{(accept via } \varepsilon\text{-transition)} \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Pumping-Lemma Proof that $L = \set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0}$ is Not Context-Free

**Theorem:** $L = \set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0}$ is **not** a CFL.

**Proof (by contradiction):**

> **Assumption:** $L$ is context-free. Then by the Pumping Lemma for CFLs, there exists a pumping length $p \geq 1$.

**Step 1 — Choose a candidate string:** Let
$$s = a^{p}b^{p}c^{p} \in L$$
We have $\vert s \vert = 3p \geq p$, so the lemma applies.

**Step 2 — Apply the Pumping Lemma:** The string $s$ can be decomposed as $s = uvxyz$ satisfying:
$$\vert vxy \vert \leq p, \quad \vert vy \vert \geq 1, \quad uv^{n}xy^{n}z \in L \ \text{for all } n \geq 0$$

**Step 3 — Case Analysis (the heart of the proof):**

Since $\vert vxy \vert \leq p$ and the three symbol-blocks $a^{p}$, $b^{p}$, $c^{p}$ are each of length $p$, the substring $vxy$ can span **at most two** of the three symbol blocks. The pair $(v, y)$ can therefore consist of:

- **Case A:** $vy$ contains only $a$'s (no $b$'s, no $c$'s).
- **Case B:** $vy$ contains $a$'s and $b$'s (no $c$'s).
- **Case C:** $vy$ contains only $b$'s.
- **Case D:** $vy$ contains $b$'s and $c$'s (no $a$'s).
- **Case E:** $vy$ contains only $c$'s.

**Step 4 — Derive the contradiction in each case (using $n = 2$):**

> [!NOTE]
> For each case, consider $s' = uv^{2}xy^{2}z$ and count symbols:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Case A:} \ \#_{a}(s') > p, \ \#_{b}(s') = p, \ \#_{c}(s') = p. \\
&\qquad \text{Hence } s' \notin L. \quad \text{Contradiction.} \\
&\textbf{Case B:} \ \#_{a}(s') > p, \ \#_{b}(s') > p, \ \#_{c}(s') = p. \\
&\qquad \text{Hence } s' \notin L. \quad \text{Contradiction.} \\
&\textbf{Case C:} \ \#_{a}(s') = p, \ \#_{b}(s') > p, \ \#_{c}(s') = p. \\
&\qquad \text{Hence } s' \notin L. \quad \text{Contradiction.} \\
&\textbf{Case D:} \ \#_{a}(s') = p, \ \#_{b}(s') > p, \ \#_{c}(s') > p. \\
&\qquad \text{Hence } s' \notin L. \quad \text{Contradiction.} \\
&\textbf{Case E:} \ \#_{a}(s') = p, \ \#_{b}(s') = p, \ \#_{c}(s') > p. \\
&\qquad \text{Hence } s' \notin L. \quad \text{Contradiction.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

All five cases lead to a contradiction. Therefore our initial assumption that $L$ is a CFL is **false**.

$$\boxed{\ L = \set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0} \ \text{is NOT a Context-Free Language.} \ }$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Strategy Tip:** Examiners award full marks only when **all five cases are listed and dispatched**. Many students mistakenly list only three cases — that costs up to **3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: KTU Board Pattern Matching Table

| Question Type | Typical Marks | Key Steps Examiners Look For |
|---|---|---|
| Define CFG with example | 3 | Tuple $(V,T,P,S)$, production form, language set notation |
| Build parse tree | 4–6 | LMD steps + tree drawing + yield verification |
| Prove/disprove ambiguity | 5–7 | Two distinct LMDs **or** two distinct trees for the same string |
| Construct DPDA | 7 | 7-tuple + transition table + accept/reject trace |
| Pumping Lemma application | 7 | Choose $s = w^{p}$, case-by-case contradiction |
| Closure property proof | 7 | Construction of $G$ from $G_1, G_2$ + verification |
| Counter-example for non-closure | 3–5 | $a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}$ family; show both CFLs but intersection non-CFL |

---

## 3.6 Closure Properties — Worked Proof: Non-Closure under Intersection

**Theorem:** CFLs are **not** closed under intersection.

**Counter-Example Construction:**

Define:
$$L_{1} = \set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{m} \mid n, m \geq 0}, \qquad L_{2} = \set{a^{m}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n, m \geq 0}$$

**Step 1 — Show $L_{1}$ is a CFL.** Grammar $G_{1}$:
$$S_{1} \to A C, \quad A \to aAb \mid \varepsilon, \quad C \to cC \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Show $L_{2}$ is a CFL.** Grammar $G_{2}$:
$$S_{2} \to A B, \quad A \to aA \mid \varepsilon, \quad B \to bBc \mid \varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Compute the intersection:**
$$L_{1} \cap L_{2} = \set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0}$$

For $w$ to lie in both, we require simultaneously $n = m$ (from $L_{1}$) and $m = n$ (from $L_{2}$), forcing $n = m$ on the exponent of $a$ and the exponent of $c$. A formal counting argument:
$$\#_{a}(w) = \#_{b}(w) = \#_{c}(w) \ \Rightarrow\ w \in L_{1} \cap L_{2}.$$

**Step 4 — Show $L_{1} \cap L_{2}$ is not a CFL.** This is precisely the result established in §3.4 by the Pumping Lemma.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Two CFLs have an intersection that is not a CFL, hence **CFLs are not closed under intersection**. $\blacksquare$

## 4.1 Parse Tree of $w = aabb$ under $S \to aSb \mid \varepsilon$

**Read leaf-to-leaf:** $A1, A2, EPS, B2, B1 = a, a, \varepsilon, b, b \Rightarrow aabb.$ $\checkmark$

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CFG $\rightarrow$ Parse Tree $\rightarrow$ PDA Pipeline

This flowchart maps the *engineering pipeline* of a language front-end:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Lexical Stream of Tokens"] --> B["Context-Free Grammar G"]
    B --> C{"Apply Productions via LMD"}
    C --> D["Derivation Tree Build"]
    D --> E{"Is Tree Unique?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["Unambiguous Grammar — Parser Succeeds"]
    E -- No --> G["Ambiguity Detected — Apply Disambiguating Rules"]
    G --> H["Equivalent Unambiguous Grammar G prime"]
    H --> I["Convert G prime to Equivalent PDA M"]
    I --> J["Determinize if Possible to Obtain DPDA"]
    J --> K["Parser Generator Output — YACC or ANTLR Code"]
```

---

## 4.3 DPDA State Diagram for $L = \set{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1}$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q0(("q0")) -- "a, Z / AZ" --> q0
    q0 -- "a, A / AA" --> q0
    q0 -- "b, A / epsilon" --> q1(("q1"))
    q1 -- "b, A / epsilon" --> q1
    q1 -- "epsilon, Z / Z" --> qf(("qf"))
```

**Edge Label Legend:** `input_symbol , stack_top / stack_replacement`

- **q0** = Pushing phase (read $a$'s, push $A$).
- **q1** = Popping phase (read $b$'s, pop $A$).
- **qf** = Accepting state (reached when input is exhausted and bottom-of-stack $Z$ is exposed).

---

## 4.4 NPDA Decision Architecture for $L = \set{ww^{R} \mid w \in \set{a,b}^{*}}$

A nondeterministic PDA must *guess* the middle of the string. The block flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start in q0 with Z on stack"] --> PushPhase["Push each input symbol onto stack"]
    PushPhase --> GuessMiddle{"Nondeterministically Guess Middle of Input"}
    GuessMiddle -- "Branch 1" --> MatchPhase1["Pop stack symbols, compare with input a"]
    GuessMiddle -- "Branch 2" --> MatchPhase2["Pop stack symbols, compare with input b"]
    GuessMiddle -- "Branch k" --> MatchPhaseK["Generic Branch"]
    MatchPhase1 --> StackEmptyCheck{"Is Stack Empty and Input Empty?"}
    MatchPhase2 --> StackEmptyCheck
    MatchPhaseK --> StackEmptyCheck
    StackEmptyCheck -- "Yes" --> Accept["Accept — w equals w Reverse"]
    StackEmptyCheck -- "No" --> Reject["Reject — Branch dies"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why NPDA and not DPDA?** A DPDA cannot *guess* the middle of the string. It would have to commit to a specific middle position, which is impossible without a second stack. Hence $L = \set{ww^{R}}$ is the canonical CFL that is **not** a DCFL.

---

## 4.5 Pumping Lemma Application Flowchart

The universal decision procedure for "Is $L$ context-free?" using the Pumping Lemma:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Given language L, is it CFL?"] --> Assume["Assume L is CFL with pumping length p"]
    Assume --> Choose["Choose s in L with absolute s greater than or equal to p"]
    Choose --> Decompose["s = u v x y z with absolute vxy less than or equal to p, absolute vy greater than or equal to 1"]
    Decompose --> Pump["Form s prime = u v squared x y squared z"]
    Pump --> Check{"s prime in L?"}
    Check -- "Yes" --> TryN["Try n = 0 or n = 3 also"]
    TryN --> Check2{"Both still in L?"}
    Check2 -- "No" --> Conclude["Contradiction: L is NOT CFL"]
    Check2 -- "Yes" --> NextCase["Try other decompositions"]
    NextCase --> Pump
    Check -- "No" --> Conclude
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Showing $s' \in L$ for **one** decomposition is **not enough**. The pumping lemma requires the property for **all** valid decompositions. Examiners explicitly look for *all* decompositions to be checked or for a *general* argument.

---

## 4.6 Closure-Property Master Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CFL1["CFL L1"] --> Union["Union L1 U L2 — Closed"]
    CFL2["CFL L2"] --> Union
    CFL1 --> Concat["Concatenation L1 L2 — Closed"]
    CFL2 --> Concat
    CFL1 --> Star["Kleene Star L1 star — Closed"]
    CFL2 --> Star
    CFL1 --> Inter["Intersection L1 intersect L2 — NOT Closed"]
    CFL2 --> Inter
    Inter --> CounterEx["Counter-example: a^n b^n c^m intersect a^m b^n c^n = a^n b^n c^n"]
    CFL1 --> Comp["Complement overline L1 — NOT Closed"]
    Comp --> CompArg["De Morgan plus non-closure of intersection"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**. Write the formal 4-tuple and explain each component with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

A **Context-Free Grammar** is a formal mechanism for describing languages whose syntax can be specified by recursive, context-independent rules. Formally, it is a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $V$ | Finite set of **variables (non-terminals)**. These are auxiliary symbols that denote sub-phrases. |
| $T$ | Finite set of **terminals** — the actual alphabet of the language. |
| $P$ | Finite set of **production rules**, each of the form $A \to \alpha$, with $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$. |
| $S$ | **Start symbol**, an element of $V$. |

**Example:** The grammar $G: S \to aSb \mid \varepsilon$ has $V = \set{S}$, $T = \set{a, b}$, $P = \set{S \to aSb, \ S \to \varepsilon}$, $S = S$, and generates $L(G) = \set{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
State the **Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages**. Mention all three conditions clearly.

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $L$ is a Context-Free Language, then there exists a constant $p \geq 1$ (the **pumping length**) such that every string $s \in L$ with $\vert s \vert \geq p$ can be written as

$$s = uvxyz$$

satisfying the following three conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i)} \quad \vert vxy \vert \leq p \\
&\text{(ii)} \quad \vert vy \vert \geq 1 \\
&\text{(iii)} \quad uv^{n}xy^{n}z \in L \quad \text{for every } n \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The lemma is used as a **negation tool** — to prove a language is *not* CFL. $\blacksquare$

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 2 carries an internal choice. Both **Question A** and **Question B** below are complete, independent 14-mark answer sets. Each contains two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) **[7 Marks]** — Construct a PDA

**Q. Construct a Pushdown Automaton (PDA) that accepts the language**
$$L = \set{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1}$$
**by final state. Show the ID sequence for the string $w = aaabbb$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Design the PDA.** A 7-tuple:

$$M = (\set{q_{0}, q_{1}, q_{f}}, \ \set{a, b}, \ \set{Z, A}, \ \delta, \ q_{0}, \ Z, \ \set{q_{f}})$$

**Step 2 — Define the transition function $\delta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\delta(q_{0}, a, Z) = \set{(q_{0}, AZ)} \\
&\delta(q_{0}, a, A) = \set{(q_{0}, AA)} \\
&\delta(q_{0}, b, A) = \set{(q_{1}, \varepsilon)} \\
&\delta(q_{1}, b, A) = \set{(q_{1}, \varepsilon)} \\
&\delta(q_{1}, \varepsilon, Z) = \set{(q_{f}, Z)}
\end{aligned}
$$

All other transitions are empty.

**Step 3 — ID Trace for $w = aaabbb$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_{0}, aaabbb, Z) &\vdash (q_{0}, aabbb, AZ) \\
                  &\vdash (q_{0}, abbb, AAZ) \\
                  &\vdash (q_{0}, bbb, AAAZ) \\
                  &\vdash (q_{1}, bb, AAZ) \\
                  &\vdash (q_{1}, b, AZ) \\
                  &\vdash (q_{1}, \varepsilon, Z) \\
                  &\vdash (q_{f}, \varepsilon, Z) \quad \textbf{Accept} \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The input is fully consumed, the stack bottom is restored, and the final state $q_{f}$ is reached. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Part (b) **[7 Marks]** — Pumping Lemma Proof

**Q. Using the Pumping Lemma for CFLs, prove that the language**
$$L = \set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0}$$
**is not context-free.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume for contradiction** that $L$ is context-free. Let $p$ be the pumping length.

**Step 2 — Choose the witness string:** $s = a^{p}b^{p}c^{p} \in L$, with $\vert s \vert = 3p \geq p$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Pumping Lemma:** There exists a decomposition $s = uvxyz$ with:
$$\vert vxy \vert \leq p, \quad \vert vy \vert \geq 1, \quad uv^{n}xy^{n}z \in L, \ \forall n \geq 0$$

**Step 4 — Case Analysis** (only the **count mismatch** is shown):

| Case | Composition of $vy$ | Result of $uv^{2}xy^{2}z$ | Belongs to $L$? |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Only $a$'s | $\#a > p = \#b = \#c$ | **No** |
| B | $a$'s and $b$'s | $\#a, \#b > p = \#c$ | **No** |
| C | Only $b$'s | $\#b > p = \#a = \#c$ | **No** |
| D | $b$'s and $c$'s | $\#a = p < \#b, \#c$ | **No** |
| E | Only $c$'s | $\#c > p = \#a = \#b$ | **No** |

(Note: Other sub-cases like $vy$ crossing $a$-to-$b$ boundary or spanning $a$ and $b$ are subsumed by Case B above; symmetric arguments hold for the $b$-to-$c$ boundary.)

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** Every case yields a string outside $L$, contradicting the pumping lemma. Hence $L$ is not context-free. $\blacksquare$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) **[7 Marks]** — Ambiguity

**Q. Define an ambiguous grammar. Show that the grammar**
$$G: \ S \to SS \mid aSb \mid bSa \mid \varepsilon$$
**is ambiguous. Hence, find an unambiguous grammar for the same language.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:** A CFG $G$ is **ambiguous** if there exists a string $w \in L(G)$ that has **two or more distinct parse trees** (equivalently, two distinct leftmost derivations).

**Step 2 — Language of $G$:** $L(G) = \set{w \in \set{a,b}^{*} \mid \#_{a}(w) = \#_{b}(w)\ \text{and every prefix of } w \text{ has } \#_{a} \geq \#_{b}}$.

**Step 3 — Two distinct LMDs for $w = abab$:**

- **LMD-1:**
$$S \ \Rightarrow\ aSb \ \Rightarrow\ abS \ \Rightarrow\ abab \quad \text{via } S \to bSa, \text{ then } S \to \varepsilon.$$

- **LMD-2:**
$$S \ \Rightarrow\ SS \ \Rightarrow\ aSbS \ \Rightarrow\ abS \ \Rightarrow\ abab \quad \text{via } S \to aSb, \text{ then } S \to bSa, \text{ then } S \to \varepsilon.$$

The two derivation sequences differ in the order of applying $S \to SS$ vs. the recursive rules, leading to **two distinct parse trees** with the same yield $abab$. Hence $G$ is ambiguous.

**Step 4 — Unambiguous Replacement Grammar:**

Introduce a new non-terminal $T$ that forces a unique parse:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&S \to aSbT \mid T \\
&T \to bSaT \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of Uniqueness:** Every string in $L(G)$ begins either with $a$ (forcing the rule $S \to aSbT$) or with $b$ (forcing $S \to T \to bSaT$). The recursive structure is deterministic in this grammar — **no two LMDs exist for the same string**. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Part (b) **[7 Marks]** — Closure Properties

**Q. State whether Context-Free Languages are closed under (i) Union, (ii) Concatenation, (iii) Kleene Closure, and (iv) Intersection. Prove or disprove each with appropriate reasoning.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Union — CLOSED**

**Proof:** Let $G_{1} = (V_{1}, T_{1}, P_{1}, S_{1})$ and $G_{2} = (V_{2}, T_{2}, P_{2}, S_{2})$ be CFGs with disjoint non-terminals. Construct

$$G = (V_{1} \cup V_{2} \cup \set{S}, \ T_{1} \cup T_{2}, \ P_{1} \cup P_{2} \cup \set{S \to S_{1} \mid S_{2}}, \ S)$$

Any string $w \in L_{1}$ is derived via $S \Rightarrow S_{1} \Rightarrow^{*} w$; similarly for $L_{2}$. Hence $L(G) = L_{1} \cup L_{2}$. $\blacksquare$

**(ii) Concatenation — CLOSED**

**Proof:** Use the same setup as (i) and define

$$G = (V_{1} \cup V_{2} \cup \set{S}, \ T_{1} \cup T_{2}, \ P_{1} \cup P_{2} \cup \set{S \to S_{1}S_{2}}, \ S)$$

Any $w = w_{1}w_{2} \in L_{1}L_{2}$ is derived via $S \Rightarrow S_{1}S_{2} \Rightarrow^{*} w_{1}w_{2}$. Hence $L(G) = L_{1}L_{2}$. $\blacksquare$

**(iii) Kleene Star — CLOSED**

**Proof:** For $G_{1} = (V_{1}, T_{1}, P_{1}, S_{1})$ generating $L_{1}$, define

$$G = (V_{1} \cup \set{S}, \ T_{1}, \ P_{1} \cup \set{S \to S_{1}S \mid \varepsilon}, \ S)$$

This generates $\varepsilon$ and any finite concatenation of strings from $L_{1}$, i.e. $L(G) = L_{1}^{*}$. $\blacksquare$

**(iv) Intersection — NOT CLOSED**

**Counter-example:**

$$L_{1} = \set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{m} \mid n, m \geq 0}, \qquad L_{2} = \set{a^{m}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n, m \geq 0}$$

Both $L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$ are CFLs (construct their grammars explicitly). However,

$$L_{1} \cap L_{2} = \set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0}$$

which is **not** a CFL (proved via the Pumping Lemma in §3.4). Hence CFLs are **not closed under intersection**. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting disjointness** of $V_{1}, V_{2}$ in closure proofs — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Listing only 3 cases** in the Pumping Lemma (Cases A, C, E above) — costs up to 3 marks. The 5-case enumeration is **mandatory**.
> 3. **Confusing FS acceptance and ES acceptance** in PDA definitions — the final state $q_{f}$ matters.
> 4. **Mixing up CFL ∩ Regular = CFL** with **CFL ∩ CFL = CFL** — the former is closed, the latter is **not**.
> 5. **Not showing the witness string is in $L$** when applying the Pumping Lemma — always verify $s = a^{p}b^{p}c^{p} \in L$ explicitly.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Cram Sheet):**

- [ ] **CFG tuple** $= (V, T, P, S)$ — keep them disjoint; productions are $A \to \alpha$ form.
- [ ] **Derivation = Parse Tree = LMD = RMD** (all four are equivalent for a given string $w$).
- [ ] **Ambiguity** = at least one string has $\geq 2$ parse trees. Show by exhibiting **two distinct LMDs**.
- [ ] **Inherent Ambiguity** = no unambiguous grammar exists for the language.
- [ ] **PDA tuple** $= (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_{0}, Z_{0}, F)$ — 7 components, $\delta$ is the heart.
- [ ] **PDA acceptance** can be by **Final State** or by **Empty Stack** — the two are equivalent (KTU theorem).
- [ ] **DPDA is strictly weaker than NPDA** — $L = \set{ww^{R}}$ is CFL but not DCFL.
- [ ] **Pumping Lemma for CFL** has the form $s = uvxyz$ with three conditions: $\vert vxy \vert \leq p$, $\vert vy \vert \geq 1$, $uv^{n}xy^{n}z \in L$ for all $n \geq 0$.
- [ ] **Five cases in the canonical pumping proof** for $\set{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}}$ — list all five to get full marks.
- [ ] **CFLs CLOSED under**: Union, Concatenation, Kleene Star, Homomorphism, Reverse, Intersection with Regular, Substitution.
- [ ] **CFLs NOT CLOSED under**: Intersection, Complement, Set Difference.
- [ ] **Counter-example family for non-closure**: $L_{1} = a^{n}b^{n}c^{*}$, $L_{2} = a^{*}b^{n}c^{n}$, their intersection is $a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}$.
- [ ] **Pumping Lemma is a one-way tool** — it can prove a language is NOT CFL, but **never** proves a language IS CFL.
- [ ] **DCFLs are NOT closed under union and complement** (a deeper result often asked as a follow-up).
- [ ] **Closure proofs are constructive** — always *define* the new grammar/PDA explicitly.
- [ ] **Counter-examples for non-closure must satisfy**: both inputs CFL, output not CFL.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula:** The hierarchy $\text{Reg} \subsetneq \text{DCFL} \subsetneq \text{CFL} \subsetneq \text{RE}$ is the single most-cited diagram in the KTU TOC module. Memorize it cold.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Context Sensitive Languages - Context Sensitive Grammar (CSG), Linear Bounded Automata, Turing Machines - Standard Turing Machine, Robustness, Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages, and Chomsky classification of formal languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Chomsky Hierarchy — Foundation of Formal Languages

The **Chomsky Hierarchy** is the cornerstone classification of formal grammars introduced by the linguist **Noam Chomsky (1956)**. It partitions all formal grammars into four nested classes based on the structural form of their production rules. Each class has a corresponding machine model, language class, and growing expressive power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard)**
> A grammar is the 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where:
> - $V$ is a finite set of non-terminal symbols (variables)
> - $T$ is a finite set of terminal symbols (alphabet), with $V \cap T = \emptyset$
> - $P$ is a finite set of production rules
> - $S \in V$ is the designated start symbol
>
> A **language** generated by $G$ is $L(G) = \{w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$.

### 1.2 Context Sensitive Grammar (CSG) — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Context Sensitive Grammar (Type-1 Grammar)**
> A production rule in a CSG has the form:
> $$\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$$
> where $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^*$, $A \in V$, and $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^+$ with $\gamma \neq \varepsilon$.
>
> **Equivalent contracted definition:** Every production has the shape $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ such that $\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$ (monotonic / non-contracting).

A non-terminal $A$ may be rewritten as $\gamma$ **only when** it is surrounded by the left context $\alpha$ and the right context $\beta$. This is the "context sensitivity" of the grammar — the replacement depends on what neighbors $A$ in the sentential form.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Neighborhood Edit Rule"

Imagine a corporate hierarchy of employees. A junior programmer (non-terminal) can be **promoted to Senior Engineer** (longer string) only if the **left neighbor is the HR Director** and the **right neighbor is the Finance Manager**. Outside that exact pair of flanking roles, the promotion rule does not fire. That is precisely how a CSG works — the rewrite rule is conditioned on the **local neighborhood** of the symbol.

### 1.4 Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Bounded Automaton (LBA)**
> An LBA is a single-tape non-deterministic Turing Machine whose tape head is **restricted** to the portion of the tape that originally contained the input. The input is delimited by two special end-markers, and computation never moves past them.
>
> Formally, an LBA is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ identical to a standard TM, but with the **additional constraint**:
> $$\delta(q, X) \text{ must never shift the head onto the left or right end-marker}.$$
> Equivalently, the **usable tape length is linearly bounded** by $c \cdot n$ for some constant $c$ and input length $n$.

### 1.5 Standard Turing Machine (TM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Turing Machine Definition**
> A standard TM is the 7-tuple:
> $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$
> - $Q$: finite set of states
> - $\Sigma$: finite input alphabet
> - $\Gamma$: finite tape alphabet, with $\Sigma \subset \Gamma$
> - $B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma$: the blank symbol
> - $\delta: Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$: the (partial) transition function
> - $q_0 \in Q$: start state
> - $F \subseteq Q$: set of accepting (final) states

A TM can read, write, and move its head in **both directions** on an **unbounded** tape — granting it infinite memory in principle.

### 1.6 Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recursive Language (Decidable)**
> A language $L$ is **recursive** if there exists a Turing Machine $M$ such that for every input $w$:
> - $M$ **halts** on $w$,
> - $M$ **accepts** $w$ if $w \in L$,
> - $M$ **rejects** $w$ if $w \notin L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursively Enumerable Language (RE / r.e.)**
> A language $L$ is **recursively enumerable** if there exists a TM $M$ such that:
> - If $w \in L$, then $M$ **halts and accepts** $w$.
> - If $w \notin L$, then $M$ either halts in a non-accepting state **or runs forever** (loops).

**Set inclusion:** Every recursive language is recursively enumerable, i.e., $\text{REC} \subset \text{RE}$. The converse is **false** (e.g., the Halting Problem is RE but not recursive).

### 1.7 Robustness of Turing Machines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Robustness Thesis (Extended Church–Turing Thesis)**
> The class of computable functions is **invariant** under enormous model variations. TMs with multiple tapes, multi-dimensional tapes, multi-head, non-deterministic choice, random-access memory, two-stack PDAs, or $\lambda$-calculus **all recognize exactly the same class of languages** — the recursively enumerable languages.

This invariance is called **Robustness** — it tells us the "essence" of computation is not an artifact of a particular machine.

### 1.8 The Complete Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chomsky Classification of Formal Languages**
> | Grammar Type | Production Form | Language Class | Automaton Model |
> |:---:|:---|:---|:---|
> | **Type-0** | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ (unrestricted) | Recursively Enumerable (RE) | Turing Machine |
> | **Type-1** | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$, $\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$ | Context Sensitive (CSL) | Linear Bounded Automaton |
> | **Type-2** | $A \rightarrow \beta$, $A \in V$ | Context Free (CFL) | Pushdown Automaton |
> | **Type-3** | $A \rightarrow aB \mid a$ | Regular | Finite Automaton (DFA/NFA) |

### 1.9 Real-World Analogy — "Languages as Power Tools"

Think of the hierarchy as a **toolkit ladder**:
- A **hammer** (regular) builds straight fences.
- A **drill-press** (context-free) builds L-shaped and stacked structures.
- A **computerized CNC machine** (context-sensitive) builds complex curved surfaces because the next cut depends on the surrounding material.
- A **fully autonomous 3D-printer with vision sensors** (Type-0 / TM) can theoretically build *anything* describable, including itself.

The price of expressive power is **undecidability of membership** in the general case.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of a Context Sensitive Grammar

A derivation in a CSG proceeds by **monotonically growing** the sentential form — the working string can never shrink.

**Step-by-step logic of a CSG derivation:**

1. Start with the axiom $S$.
2. Locate a non-terminal $A$ whose **left and right context** ($\alpha$ on the left, $\beta$ on the right) match a production $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$.
3. Replace $A$ by $\gamma$ in place. The resulting string is **at least as long** as the previous one.
4. Repeat until only terminals remain.
5. If the final string is in $T^*$ and matches a target $w$, then $w \in L(G)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why length never decreases:**
> Because every production satisfies $\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$, any single rewrite step satisfies $\vert \text{new sentential form} \vert \geq \vert \text{old sentential form} \vert$. Hence, given a target $w$ of length $n$, the derivation can never grow beyond $n$ symbols if we are not adding useful material — bounding the search space.

### 2.2 Why an LBA Recognizes CSLs

A standard TM with an $n$-cell input tape and end-markers can be viewed as an LBA. Because the **tape is bounded** by $c \cdot n$ cells, the LBA has only **finitely many distinct configurations** for a given $n$ (specifically, $O(\vert Q \vert \cdot n \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert^{c n})$). If the LBA accepts $w$, it must do so in **finitely many steps** without revisiting the same configuration twice.

The matching theorem (Kuroda, 1964) is:

> **Theorem (Kuroda's Normal Form):** A language is context-sensitive if and only if it is recognized by some (non-deterministic) Linear Bounded Automaton.

### 2.3 KTU Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table must be memorized verbatim for the KTU ESE. **Note:** The vertical bar for absolute value / cardinality is written as `\vert` so the markdown table does not break.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Insight |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | CSG production | $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta,\ \vert \gamma \vert \geq 1$ | Length non-decreasing |
| 2 | Monotonic grammar | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta,\ \vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$ | Equivalent form of Type-1 |
| 3 | TM configuration | $(q, w_1, \underline{X}, w_2)$ | State, left tape, scanned symbol, right tape |
| 4 | TM transition | $\delta(q, X) = (p, Y, D),\ D \in \{L, R\}$ | Read–Write–Move |
| 5 | LBA constraint | Tape length $\leq c \cdot n$ | $n$ = input length, $c$ = constant |
| 6 | Recursive language | $\exists M,\ \forall w,\ M \text{ halts and decides}$ | Total TM |
| 7 | RE language | $\exists M,\ \forall w \in L,\ M \text{ halts and accepts}$ | TM may loop on non-members |
| 8 | Set nesting | $\text{REG} \subset \text{CFL} \subset \text{CSL} \subset \text{REC} \subset \text{RE}$ | Strict inclusion at every level |
| 9 | TM robustness | $\text{RE}_{\text{standard TM}} = \text{RE}_{\text{multi-tape}} = \text{RE}_{\text{NTM}}$ | Model-independent |
| 10 | Acceptance | $w \in L$ iff $\exists$ sequence of IDs $C_0 \vdash C_1 \vdash \dots \vdash C_k$ with $C_k$ accepting | ID = Instantaneous Description |

### 2.4 Operational Logic of a Standard Turing Machine

The **Instantaneous Description (ID)** of a TM is a triple $(q, u, v)$ where $q$ is the current state, $u$ is the tape content to the left of the head, and $v$ is the tape content starting at the head. A single move is:

$$
(q, aX, Yb) \vdash (p, a, ZYb) \quad \text{when} \quad \delta(q, Y) = (p, Z, R)
$$

$$
(q, aX, Yb) \vdash (p, aXZ, b) \quad \text{when} \quad \delta(q, Y) = (p, Z, L)
$$

### 2.5 Recursive vs Recursively Enumerable — The Crucial Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**
> Students frequently write "RE means recursive". They are **not the same**. Recursive implies **total decidability** (the TM must always halt). RE only guarantees acceptance of members; non-members may cause an **infinite loop**.

**Visual aid (use in your answer script):**
- **Recursive** = "Decider" — always halts, says YES/NO.
- **RE** = "Recognizer" — halts with YES on members; may run forever on non-members.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility of the Hierarchy

- **Compiler Design (Type-2 & Type-3):** Lexers use regular expressions (Type-3); parsers use CFGs (Type-2).
- **Model Checking (Type-1):** Many verification problems (e.g., reachability in structured programs) reduce to context-sensitive languages handled by LBAs.
- **Algorithm Design (Type-0):** Halting and equivalence problems are Type-0, taught to set complexity expectations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Showing a Language is Context Sensitive

**Problem:** Prove that $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ is a **Context Sensitive Language** by constructing a CSG and demonstrating a sample derivation for $n = 3$.

**Step 1 — Construct the CSG.**

A standard CSG for $a^n b^n c^n$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSBC \mid aBC \\
CB &\rightarrow BC \\
aB &\rightarrow ab \\
bB &\rightarrow bb \\
bC &\rightarrow bc \\
cC &\rightarrow cc
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this works:** The first rule generates $a$ followed by $(n-1)$ copies of $S$-derived material and $n$ copies of $BC$. The rule $CB \rightarrow BC$ **swaps adjacent** $C$ and $B$ to "bubble" $B$'s leftward and $C$'s rightward. The terminal-emitting rules convert a single $aB$ into $ab$ and a single $bC$ into $bc$, ensuring the count of $a$, $b$, $c$ stays matched.

**Step 2 — Derive the string $w = aaabbbccc$ for $n = 3$.**

We trace one valid derivation (using $\Rightarrow_G$ for "derives in one step"):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow_G aSBC \\
  &\Rightarrow_G aaSBCBC \\
  &\Rightarrow_G aaaBCBCBC \\
  &\Rightarrow_G aaaBBCCBC \quad (\text{apply } CB \rightarrow BC) \\
  &\Rightarrow_G aaaBBCBCC \quad (\text{apply } CB \rightarrow BC) \\
  &\Rightarrow_G aaaBBCCBC \rightarrow \dots \Rightarrow_G aaaBBBCCCC
\end{aligned}
$$

Continuing the $CB \rightarrow BC$ bubble sorts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
aaaBBBCCCC &\Rightarrow_G aaabBBCCCC \quad (aB \rightarrow ab) \\
            &\Rightarrow_G aaabbBCCCC \quad (bB \rightarrow bb) \\
            &\Rightarrow_G aaabbbCCCC \quad (bB \rightarrow bb) \\
            &\Rightarrow_G aaabbbcCCC \quad (bC \rightarrow bc) \\
            &\Rightarrow_G aaabbbccCC \quad (cC \rightarrow cc) \\
            &\Rightarrow_G aaabbbcccC \quad (cC \rightarrow cc) \\
            &\Rightarrow_G aaabbbccc \quad (cC \rightarrow cc)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $S \Rightarrow_G^* aaabbbccc$, confirming $w \in L(G)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Building an LBA for $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

**Algorithm (high-level):**

1. Sweep left-to-right; locate the leftmost $a$, replace it with $A$ (marker).
2. Sweep right-to-left; locate the leftmost $b$, replace it with $B$.
3. Sweep right-to-left; locate the leftmost $c$, replace it with $C$.
4. Move the head to the leftmost $A$; verify that **all** symbols to its right are $B$'s and $C$'s in the correct relative order; loop until no $a$ remains.
5. **Accept** if all of $\{a, b, c\}$ have been replaced by $\{A, B, C\}$ in the right pattern.

**Why this is an LBA and not a TM:** The algorithm uses only the $3n$ cells containing the input, plus a constant number of scratch markers placed on the input cells. The head never travels beyond the original boundaries $\Rightarrow$ **linearly bounded computation**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — TM for the Language $L = \{wcw^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^*\}$

A **standard TM** that decides $wcw^R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{State } q_0 &: \text{ read first symbol of } w, \text{ mark it, store via state, move right past } c. \\
\text{State } q_1 &: \text{ read symbol on right side, compare with remembered one. Match? Replace & move outward. Mismatch? Reject.} \\
\text{State } q_2 &: \text{ both sides exhausted; accept if all symbols matched.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transition function sample:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_a, X, R) \\
\delta(q_0, b) &= (q_b, X, R) \\
\delta(q_a, Y) &= (q_a, Y, R) \quad \text{for all } Y \in \{a, b\} \text{ (skip until } c\text{)} \\
\delta(q_a, c) &= (q_3, c, R) \\
\delta(q_3, a) &= (q_4, B, L) \quad \text{(matched; erase)} \\
\delta(q_3, b) &= (q_{\text{reject}}, b, L) \\
\delta(q_4, Y) &= (q_4, Y, L) \quad \text{return to marked side} \\
\delta(q_4, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \quad \text{next outer pair} \\
\delta(q_0, c) &= (q_2, c, R) \quad \text{even-length palindrome without middle}
\end{aligned}
$$

The head sweeps in **both directions**, the tape is **unbounded**, but only $O(n)$ cells are used — still a valid TM. The language is **context-free** (palindromes with a center marker), but is shown here to illustrate TM mechanics.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property Decidability Matrix for Each Language Class**

| Property | Regular | CFL | CSL | RE |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $w \in L$? (Membership) | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ DFA | CYK $\mathcal{O}(n^3)$ | PSPACE-complete | Undecidable (in general) |
| $L = \emptyset$? | Decidable | Decidable | Decidable | Undecidable |
| $L_1 = L_2$? | Decidable | Decidable | Decidable | Undecidable |
| $L_1 \cap L_2 = \emptyset$? | Decidable | Decidable | Undecidable | Undecidable |
| $L = L^*$? | Decidable | Decidable | Undecidable | Undecidable |
| $L$ regular? | Trivial | Decidable | Undecidable | Undecidable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** As you move right in the table, decidability collapses. **Closure under complement** is interesting: regular languages are closed, CSLs are closed (via deterministic LBAs — though NLBAs are not known to be closed), CFLs are **not closed**, and RE languages are **not closed**.

### 3.5 Closure Properties of CSLs

| Operation | Regular | CFL | CSL | RE |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Union | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Intersection | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Complement | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Kleene Star | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Reversal | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Concatenation | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

> [!WARNING]
> The most counter-intuitive entry is **CFL complement is not closed** — a favorite KTU trick question.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — TM Computation Trace

**TM definition (a simple parity checker over $\{a, b\}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\
\delta(q_0, b) &= (q_0, b, R) \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_{\text{accept}}, B, R) \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_0, a, R) \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_1, b, R) \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_{\text{reject}}, B, R)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Input:** $w = abab$, expecting even number of $a$'s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0, \underline{a}, babB) &\vdash (q_1, a, \underline{b}, abB) \vdash (q_1, ab, \underline{a}, bB) \\
&\vdash (q_0, aba, \underline{b}, B) \vdash (q_0, abab, \underline{B}) \\
&\vdash (q_{\text{accept}}, ababB, \underline{B}) \quad \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPTED}
\end{aligned}
$$

```mermaid
    Accept --> Halts
    Reject --> Halts
    Loop --> Halts
    Halts --> [*]

      Recursive = always halts

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> - Marks are routinely lost when students **conflate recursive with recursively enumerable**. Memorize: *Recursive = always halts*; *RE = halts on members only*.
> - In CSG production, **never** write $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ with $\vert \alpha \vert > \vert \beta \vert$ — that immediately downgrades to Type-0.
> - For the Chomsky hierarchy, you **must** give a single line in increasing order of power: **Regular $\subset$ CFL $\subset$ CSL $\subset$ REC $\subset$ RE**.
> - LBA questions are typically **definition + identification of the corresponding grammar type**. Do not skip the "linearly bounded" clause.

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Context Sensitive Grammar. Show that the language $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$ is context sensitive by giving its grammar.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Context Sensitive Grammar is a grammar in which every production has the form $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ where $A \in V$, $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^*$, and $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^+$. Equivalently, $\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$ (monotonic).

A CSG for $L = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSBC \mid aBC \\
CB &\rightarrow BC \\
aB &\rightarrow ab \\
bB &\rightarrow bb \\
bC &\rightarrow bc \\
cC &\rightarrow cc
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, $L$ is a context sensitive language. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Recursive and Recursively Enumerable Languages with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Recursive (Decidable) | Recursively Enumerable (r.e.) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| TM behavior | Always halts | May loop on non-members |
| Membership | Decidable | Semi-decidable |
| Complement | Also recursive | Not necessarily r.e. |
| Example | $L = \{a^n b^n\}$ | $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w\}$ |

Any standard set, e.g., $\{w \in \{a,b\}^* \mid \#_a(w) = \#_b(w)\}$, is recursive. The complement of $A_{TM}$ is **not** r.e. (3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Mandate (KTU ESE 2024):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

**(a)** *Define a Linear Bounded Automaton. Explain its components with a neat diagram. Show that an LBA recognizes exactly the class of Context Sensitive Languages.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An LBA is a non-deterministic single-tape Turing Machine whose tape head is **restricted** to the portion of the tape containing the input. The input is enclosed by two special end-markers $\vdash$ (left) and $\dashv$ (right), and the LBA is **prohibited** from moving the head past these markers.

**Components (7-tuple):** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ identical to a standard TM, with the additional constraint that $\delta(q, X)$ for $X \in \{\vdash, \dashv\}$ is **undefined**, ensuring the head never leaves the input region.

**Diagram:** A horizontal tape of length $c \cdot n$ cells bounded by $\vdash$ and $\dashv$, a read/write head, and a finite state controller.

**Equivalence Proof Sketch:**

*(i) CSG $\Rightarrow$ LBA:* Given a CSG $G$, construct an LBA $M$ that simulates derivations of $G$. Because CSGs are monotonic ($\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$), the sentential form never exceeds the length of the target $w$ plus a constant. Hence the LBA's tape (of length $c \cdot n$) is sufficient.

*(ii) LBA $\Rightarrow$ CSG:* For a non-deterministic LBA $M$, the set of valid IDs (state + tape content + head position) can be encoded as a string of length $O(n)$. Each transition $\vdash_M$ between IDs becomes a CSG production of the form $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$. Hence $L(M)$ is generated by a CSG. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** *Construct a Turing Machine that decides the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Trace the computation for $w = aaabbb$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TM Definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F) \\
\Sigma &= \{a, b\} \\
\Gamma &= \{a, b, X, Y, B\} \\
Q &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_{\text{accept}}, q_{\text{reject}}\} \\
F &= \{q_{\text{accept}}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transition function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, X, R) \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\
\delta(q_1, Y) &= (q_1, Y, R) \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_2, Y, L) \\
\delta(q_2, Y) &= (q_2, Y, L) \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= (q_2, a, L) \\
\delta(q_2, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \\
\delta(q_0, Y) &= (q_3, Y, R) \\
\delta(q_3, Y) &= (q_3, Y, R) \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_{\text{accept}}, B, R)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace for $w = aaabbb$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q_0, \underline{a}, aabbb) &\vdash (q_1, X, \underline{a}, abbb) \\
&\vdash (q_1, Xa, \underline{a}, bbb) \\
&\vdash (q_1, Xaa, \underline{b}, bb) \\
&\vdash (q_2, Xa, \underline{a}Y, bb) \\
&\vdash (q_2, X, \underline{a}aY, bb) \\
&\vdash (q_2, \underline{X}, aaY, bb) \\
&\vdash (q_0, X, \underline{a}, aYbb) \\
&\vdash (q_1, Xa, \underline{a}, Ybb) \\
&\vdash (q_1, Xaa, \underline{Y}, bb) \\
&\vdash (q_1, XaaY, \underline{b}, b) \\
&\vdash (q_2, Xaa, \underline{Y}b, b) \\
&\vdash (q_2, Xa, \underline{a}Yb, b) \\
&\vdash (q_2, X, \underline{a}aYb, b) \\
&\vdash (q_2, \underline{X}, aaYb, b) \\
&\vdash (q_0, X, \underline{a}, aYbb) \quad \text{(pattern repeats)} \\
&\vdots \\
&\vdash (q_0, XXX, \underline{Y}, YYY) \\
&\vdash (q_3, XXXY, \underline{Y}, YY) \\
&\vdash (q_3, XXXYY, \underline{Y}, Y) \\
&\vdash (q_3, XXXYYY, \underline{Y}) \\
&\vdash (q_3, XXXYYY, \underline{B}) \\
&\vdash (q_{\text{accept}}, XXXYYY, \underline{B})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** $M$ halts in $q_{\text{accept}}$, so $aaabbb \in L$. (7 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** *State and explain the Chomsky classification of formal grammars. Draw the inclusion diagram of the corresponding language classes and identify the machine model for each.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Noam Chomsky (1956) classified formal grammars into four types based on the **form of their production rules**. For a grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$:

**Type-0 (Unrestricted) Grammar:** Productions of the form $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ where $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^+$ and $\beta \in (V \cup T)^*$. No restrictions. Generates the **Recursively Enumerable** languages. Recognized by a **Turing Machine**.

**Type-1 (Context Sensitive) Grammar:** Productions $\alpha A \beta \rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ where $A \in V$, $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^*$, $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^+$. Equivalent to monotonic grammars ($\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$). Generates **Context Sensitive Languages**. Recognized by a **Linear Bounded Automaton**.

**Type-2 (Context Free) Grammar:** Productions $A \rightarrow \beta$ where $A \in V$ and $\beta \in (V \cup T)^*$. Generates **Context Free Languages**. Recognized by a **Pushdown Automaton**.

**Type-3 (Regular) Grammar:** Productions $A \rightarrow aB \mid a$ (right-linear) or $A \rightarrow Ba \mid a$ (left-linear). Generates **Regular Languages**. Recognized by a **DFA or NFA**.

**Inclusion Diagram (set containment):**

$$
\text{REG} \subsetneq \text{CFL} \subsetneq \text{CSL} \subsetneq \text{REC} \subsetneq \text{RE}
$$

All inclusions are **strict**. (7 marks)

---

**(b)** *Define a Standard Turing Machine. Explain the concepts of Recursive and Recursively Enumerable languages. State and explain the Robustness property of TMs with at least two examples.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Standard TM is the 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$. It has an unbounded tape, a single read/write head, and a finite state controller. Each move reads the current cell, writes a symbol, changes state, and shifts the head one cell left or right.

**Recursive Language:** $L$ is recursive if there exists a TM $M$ such that for every $w$:
- $M$ halts on $w$,
- $M$ accepts $w$ if $w \in L$,
- $M$ rejects $w$ if $w \notin L$.

**Recursively Enumerable Language:** $L$ is r.e. if there exists a TM $M$ such that for every $w \in L$, $M$ halts and accepts; for $w \notin L$, $M$ may halt and reject **or run forever**.

**Set relationship:** $\text{REC} \subsetneq \text{RE}$. Example: $L = \{a^n b^n\}$ is recursive; $A_{TM}$ (universal acceptance) is r.e. but not recursive.

**Robustness Property:**

The class of languages recognized by TMs is **invariant** under many model variations. Two examples:

1. **Multi-tape TMs:** A $k$-tape TM (with $k$ independent heads) recognizes exactly the **same** class of languages as the standard single-tape TM. The simulation uses a single tape to interleave the $k$ tapes, increasing the time by a polynomial factor but preserving the language.

2. **Non-deterministic TMs (NTMs):** A TM with multiple choices per transition recognizes exactly the **same** class of languages (RE) as a deterministic TM. A deterministic TM can simulate an NTM by breadth-first search of the configuration tree.

This invariance establishes that **"computability" is a model-independent notion**, which is the cornerstone of the **Church–Turing Thesis**. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Examiner Notes**
> - When asked "Is every regular language context-free?", students often say "no" by reflex. The correct answer is **YES — REG $\subset$ CFL strictly**. Always include the **strict** qualifier ($\subsetneq$).
> - A frequent 14-mark question gives a sample grammar and asks you to **identify its type**. The decision procedure is: scan all productions; if any violate Type-1, Type-2, or Type-3 form, the grammar falls to the lowest applicable type. For example, $S \rightarrow aSBC \mid aBC$ alone is **Type-2 (context free)**, not Type-1. Type-1 status comes from $CB \rightarrow BC$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Type-0 ↔ RE ↔ TM.** Any production shape allowed; recognizes **recursively enumerable** languages.
- **Type-1 ↔ CSL ↔ LBA.** Production must satisfy $\vert \alpha \vert \leq \vert \beta \vert$ (no shrinking). The corresponding machine's tape is **linearly bounded** by the input length.
- **Type-2 ↔ CFL ↔ PDA.** Productions are $A \rightarrow \beta$ with $A$ a single non-terminal. The PDA's stack gives it more power than a finite automaton.
- **Type-3 ↔ Regular ↔ DFA/NFA.** Productions are $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$. Equivalent to regular expressions.
- **Recursive ⊊ RE** (strict). Recursive TMs **always** halt; RE TMs may loop on non-members.
- **Robustness:** Multi-tape, multi-head, non-deterministic, two-dimensional tape, and many other TM variants all recognize **the same** class of languages (RE).
- **Closure properties:** Regular = closed under all Boolean ops; CSL = closed under complement (deterministic), union, intersection; CFL = closed under union, concatenation, star, reversal — **but not intersection or complement**.
- **Decidability tip:** If a question asks "is $L$ regular/CFL/CSL?" the standard answer is "undecidable in general" for the latter two.
- **Sample CSG derivation trick:** The grammar for $a^n b^n c^n$ is a KTU classic — practice it twice before the exam.
- **Instantaneous Description (ID):** Use $(q, u, v)$ notation in your answer script — KTU examiners reward it.
- **Kuroda Normal Form** is the canonical form for CSGs (similar to Chomsky / Greibach for CFGs) — useful for derivations and proofs.

# MODULE 2: 8051 Microcontroller — Foundations, Architecture & Memory Interfacing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Microcontroller?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Microcontroller (MCU)** is a single-chip microcomputer that contains a **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Read-Only Memory (ROM/Flash)** for program storage, **Random Access Memory (RAM)** for data storage, **Input/Output (I/O) ports**, **timers/counters**, **serial communication interfaces (UART)**, and often **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC)** — all integrated on a single Integrated Circuit (IC) package.

In simple terms, a microcontroller is a **"computer-on-a-chip."** Unlike a general-purpose computer, where the CPU, memory, and I/O are separate chips connected by a motherboard, a microcontroller integrates everything onto **one piece of silicon**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Swiss Army Knife vs. The Toolbox:**
> - A **Microprocessor** is like a **specialized toolbox** — it has a powerful processor, but you must buy memory, I/O controllers, and timers separately and wire them together.
> - A **Microcontroller** is like a **Swiss Army Knife** — the processor, memory, timers, and I/O are all built into one compact, self-sufficient device, perfect for dedicated, repetitive tasks.

### 1.2 The 8051 Microcontroller

The **Intel 8051** (introduced in **1980**) is an **8-bit** Harvard-architecture single-chip microcontroller. It became the industry standard for embedded systems education and remains widely emulated in modern derivatives (e.g., Atmel AT89C51, NXP P89V51RD2, Silicon Labs C8051Fxxx).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key 8051 Specifications (for board exam recall):**
> - **8-bit CPU** with registers A, B, SP, PC, DPTR
> - **4 KB on-chip ROM** (program memory)
> - **128 bytes on-chip RAM** (data memory)
> - **Four 8-bit I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3)
> - **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (T0, T1)
> - **Full-duplex UART** (Serial port)
> - **5 interrupt sources**
> - **64 KB external code & data memory addressing** capability

### 1.3 Microprocessor vs. Microcontroller

| Parameter | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | CPU only on a single chip | CPU + Memory + I/O on a single chip |
| **Architecture** | Von Neumann (typically) | Harvard (typically, e.g., 8051) |
| **Memory** | External (must be added) | Internal ROM + RAM |
| **I/O Ports** | External (must interface) | Built-in |
| **Timers/Counters** | External | Built-in |
| **Cost** | Higher (system cost) | Lower (single chip) |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher | Lower |
| **Applications** | General-purpose computing (PCs) | Embedded/dedicated tasks (washing machines, cars) |
| **Bit-width examples** | 8086 (16-bit), Pentium (32/64-bit) | 8051 (8-bit), ARM Cortex-M (32-bit) |
| **Clock Speed** | Higher (GHz range) | Lower (MHz to kHz range) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** A common KTU question asks *"Why is 8051 called a 'System on Chip'?"* — Answer: Because the CPU, RAM, ROM, I/O, timers, and serial port are all embedded in a **single IC package**.

### 1.4 Evolution of Microcontrollers (4-bit → 32-bit)

The historical evolution demonstrates the trade-off between **bit-width, performance, and integration**:

| Generation | Bit-Width | Representative Family | Year | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st Gen | **4-bit** | Intel 4004, TMS1000 | 1971 | Calculators, simple appliances |
| 2nd Gen | **8-bit** | Intel **8051**, Motorola 68HC11 | 1980 | Industrial control, automotive |
| 3rd Gen | **16-bit** | Intel 8096, MSP430 | 1980s | Modems, motor control |
| 4th Gen | **32-bit** | ARM Cortex-M, PIC32, ESP32 | 2000s+ | IoT, smartphones, drones, robotics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 32-bit dominates modern embedded:** 32-bit MCUs can address **4 GB** of memory directly, run at higher clock speeds, support **DSP** operations, and execute **RTOS** kernels — essential for IoT, automotive ECU, and AI-edge applications.

### 1.5 Selection Criteria for Microcontrollers

When an engineer picks an MCU for a project, the following criteria are evaluated (a frequent KTU 2-mark question):

1. **Bit-width** (8/16/32) — based on computational needs
2. **Clock speed & performance** — based on real-time requirements
3. **On-chip memory** (Flash/RAM) — must fit program and data
4. **Number of I/O pins** — to interface sensors and actuators
5. **Peripherals** — ADC, PWM, UART, I²C, SPI, CAN, USB
6. **Power consumption** — critical for battery-powered devices
7. **Cost per unit** — for mass production
8. **Development toolchain support** — compilers, debuggers, IDEs
9. **Community & documentation** — for ease of development
10. **Operating temperature range** — for industrial/automotive use

### 1.6 Applications of Microcontrollers

> [!NOTE]
> **Everyday Embedded Examples:** Microcontrollers are **invisible computers** embedded in devices we use daily. Some representative applications:
> - **Home Appliances:** Washing machines, microwave ovens, refrigerators, air conditioners
> - **Automotive:** Engine control units (ECU), ABS, airbag controllers, dashboard instruments
> - **Medical:** Pacemakers, blood pressure monitors, infusion pumps
> - **Industrial:** PLCs, robotics, SCADA systems, motor drives
> - **Consumer Electronics:** TVs, remote controls, gaming consoles, cameras
> - **IoT & Wearables:** Smartwatches, fitness bands, smart home sensors
> - **Communication:** Mobile phones, routers, modems
> - **Aerospace & Defense:** Drones, satellites, missile guidance

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 8051 Architecture — The Big Picture

The 8051 uses a **Harvard architecture**, meaning it has **separate code and data memory buses**. This allows the CPU to fetch an opcode from ROM while simultaneously reading data from RAM — a key performance advantage over Von Neumann designs.

**Block-Level Functional Architecture:**

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit)**
   - **8-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logic (AND, OR, XOR) operations
   - **Accumulator (A)** — primary register for ALU operations
   - **B Register** — used in multiplication and division
   - **Program Status Word (PSW)** — contains flag bits (CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, P)

2. **Memory System**
   - **4 KB on-chip ROM** (program memory, 0000H–0FFFH)
   - **128 bytes on-chip RAM** (data memory, 00H–7FH)
   - **64 KB external code memory** addressing (via PC, 0000H–FFFFH)
   - **64 KB external data memory** addressing (via MOVX, 0000H–FFFFH)
   - **16-bit Special Function Registers (SFR)** area (80H–FFH)

3. **I/O Ports**
   - **4 ports × 8 bits = 32 I/O lines** (P0, P1, P2, P3)

4. **Timers/Counters**
   - **Timer 0** and **Timer 1** — 16-bit, used for delays, event counting, baud-rate generation

5. **Serial Port (UART)**
   - **Full-duplex**, programmable baud rate

6. **Interrupt Controller**
   - **5 sources:** External 0, External 1, Timer 0 overflow, Timer 1 overflow, Serial port

7. **Oscillator & Clock Circuit**
   - Crystal frequency typically **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz**

### 2.2 8051 Register Set (SFRs)

The 8051 has a total of **128 user-addressable bits** in the lower RAM and **21 Special Function Registers (SFRs)** in the upper 128 bytes.

| Register | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **A (Accumulator)** | E0H | ALU operand, results |
| **B** | F0H | Multiplication/division |
| **PSW** | D0H | Program Status Word (flags) |
| **SP (Stack Pointer)** | 81H | Points to top of stack |
| **DPTR (DPL+DPH)** | 82H, 83H | 16-bit data pointer for external memory |
| **P0 – P3** | 80H, 90H, A0H, B0H | I/O port latches |
| **TCON, TMOD** | 88H, 89H | Timer control & mode |
| **SCON, SBUF** | 98H, 99H | Serial control & buffer |
| **IE, IP** | A8H, B8H | Interrupt enable & priority |
| **PCON** | 87H | Power control |

### 2.3 8051 Program Status Word (PSW) Bit Map

$$\text{PSW} = \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline D7 & D6 & D5 & D4 & D3 & D2 & D1 & D0 \\ \hline \text{CY} & \text{AC} & \text{F0} & \text{RS1} & \text{RS0} & \text{OV} & \text{--} & \text{P} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

- **CY** = Carry Flag
- **AC** = Auxiliary Carry (for BCD arithmetic)
- **F0** = User-defined flag
- **RS1, RS0** = Register Bank Select (00 = Bank 0, 01 = Bank 1, 10 = Bank 2, 11 = Bank 3)
- **OV** = Overflow flag
- **P** = Parity flag (1 if odd number of 1s in A)

### 2.4 Bank Selection Logic

The 8051 has **4 register banks**, each containing 8 registers (R0–R7), occupying 32 bytes of RAM.

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | RAM Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H–07H |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H–0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H–17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H–1FH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Bank after RESET = Bank 0**. Stack Pointer (SP) initializes to **07H**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle | $T_{\text{machine}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds |
| Timer Delay (Mode 1) | $\text{Delay} = (65536 - X) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds |
| Timer Reload Value | $X = 65536 - \left( \text{Delay} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12} \right)$ | decimal |
| Baud Rate (Mode 1) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | bps |
| TH1 for Serial | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ | decimal |
| External Memory Size | $64 \text{ KB} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes}$ | bytes |
| Address Latches Needed | 1 × 8-bit latch (e.g., 74LS373) for A0–A7 | — |
| Address Lines from P0 | 8 (lower address + data multiplexed) | — |
| Address Lines from P2 | 8 (upper address A8–A15) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** Never use the pipe `|` symbol inside markdown table cells. For absolute value or conditional expressions, use LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` instead. For example: write `$f_{\text{osc}} \mid 12$` rather than `$f_{\text{osc}} \vert 12$` to avoid breaking the table.

### 2.6 Internal RAM Organization (128 Bytes)

$$\text{Internal RAM} = \begin{cases} \text{Lower 128 bytes (00H–7FH):} \\ \quad \bullet \text{ Register Banks (00H–1FH)} \\ \quad \bullet \text{ Bit-addressable area (20H–2FH)} \\ \quad \bullet \text{ General-purpose RAM (30H–7FH)} \\ \text{Upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH):} \\ \quad \bullet \text{ SFR area (accessed by direct addressing)} \end{cases}$$

The **bit-addressable area (20H–2FH)** is special — 16 bytes × 8 bits = **128 individually addressable bits** (bit addresses 00H–7FH). This is unique to the 8051 and is heavily tested in KTU exams.

### 2.7 8051 Pin Diagram (40-Pin DIP) — Function Map

| Pin Group | Pins | Alternate Function |
|---|---|---|
| **VCC, GND** | 40, 20 | Power (+5V), Ground |
| **XTAL1, XTAL2** | 19, 18 | Crystal oscillator input/output |
| **RST** | 9 | Reset (active HIGH, must stay HIGH for ≥ 2 machine cycles) |
| **EA̅** | 31 | External Access (0 = external code memory, 1 = internal) |
| **PSEN̅** | 29 | Program Store Enable (active LOW, for external ROM read) |
| **ALE** | 30 | Address Latch Enable (latches lower address from P0) |
| **P1.0–P1.7** | 1–8 | Port 1 (no alternate function) |
| **P3.0–P3.7** | 10–17 | Port 3 (with alternate functions — RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR̅, RD̅) |
| **P0.0–P0.7** | 32–39 | Port 0 (Address/Data multiplexed) |
| **P2.0–P2.7** | 21–28 | Port 2 (Upper address bus A8–A15) |

### 2.8 Port 3 Alternate Functions (Extremely High-Yield for KTU)

| Port Pin | Alternate | Function |
|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | **RXD** | Serial input |
| P3.1 | **TXD** | Serial output |
| P3.2 | **INT0̅** | External interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | **INT1̅** | External interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | **T0** | Timer 0 external input |
| P3.5 | **T1** | Timer 1 external input |
| P3.6 | **WR̅** | External data memory write |
| P3.7 | **RD̅** | External data memory read |

### 2.9 External Memory Interfacing — The Core Concept

The 8051 can access up to **64 KB of external code memory** and **64 KB of external data memory** (total addressable external space = 128 KB using 16-bit addresses).

**Key Control Signals:**

- **PSEN̅ (Program Store Enable):** Active LOW signal that enables external ROM read. Activated during `MOVC` instructions.
- **RD̅ and WR̅ (from P3.7, P3.6):** Active LOW signals for external data memory read/write. Activated during `MOVX` instructions.
- **ALE (Address Latch Enable):** Latches the lower byte of address from P0 into an external latch (74LS373).
- **EA̅ (External Access):** If LOW (0V), the 8051 fetches all code from external ROM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → **PSEN̅** goes LOW (external **code/ROM** access)
> - `MOVX A, @DPTR` → **RD̅** goes LOW (external **data/RAM** access)
> - `MOVX @DPTR, A` → **WR̅** goes LOW (external **data/RAM** write)

### 2.10 Engineering Utility

- **Embedded firmware developers** use 8051 knowledge to write assembly/C code for industrial control boards, automotive ECUs, and IoT sensor nodes.
- **Memory interfacing** is foundational for expanding 8051 capabilities beyond its limited 4 KB on-chip ROM — enabling larger bootloader/firmware storage and data logging buffers.
- The **74LS373 latch + address demultiplexing** technique is a classic example of bus architecture that scales to all microcontrollers.
