## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Timer Delay Calculation (Mode 1)

**Given:** Crystal frequency $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, generate a delay of **50 ms** using Timer 0 in Mode 1.

**Step 1 — Compute Machine Cycle Frequency**

$$f_{\text{machine}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = 921.6 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Machine Cycle Period**

$$T_{\text{machine}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{machine}}} = \frac{1}{921.6 \times 10^3} \approx 1.085 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3 — Total Count Required for 50 ms**

$$N = \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{\text{machine}}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46080 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 4 — Timer Reload Value (X) for Mode 1 (16-bit timer)**

In Mode 1, the timer counts up from the loaded value X to **65536** (rollover). So the number of counts is:

$$N = 65536 - X$$

**Step 5 — Solve for X**

$$X = 65536 - N = 65536 - 46080 = 19456$$

**Step 6 — Convert X to Hexadecimal and Split into TH0/TL0**

$$19456_{10} = 4C00_{16}$$

So:
- **TH0 = 4CH** (upper byte)
- **TL0 = 00H** (lower byte)

**Step 7 — Verification (with overflow from 46080 to 65536)**

$$\text{Delay} = (65536 - 19456) \times \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 46080 \times 1.085 \times 10^{-6} = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Serial Port Baud Rate Calculation

**Given:** $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, target **Baud Rate = 9600 bps**, SMOD = 0, using Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload).

**Step 1 — Baud Rate Formula for Mode 1 Serial**

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Solve for TH1**

$$(256 - \text{TH1}) = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times \text{Baud}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values (SMOD = 0, so $2^{\text{SMOD}} = 1$)**

$$(256 - \text{TH1}) = \frac{1 \times 11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3.6864 \times 10^6} = 3$$

**Step 4 — Solve for TH1**

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}$$

**Step 5 — Verification**

$$\text{Baud} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{36} = 9600 \text{ bps} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz is preferred in serial communication:** This specific crystal frequency divides evenly by standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 19200) with **zero error** — a critical property for UART communication.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: External Memory Interfacing Components

| Component | Role in Interfacing | Active Level |
|---|---|---|
| **74LS373 Octal Latch** | Latches lower address A0–A7 from multiplexed P0 | Latch enabled by **ALE** |
| **74LS138 Decoder** | Generates chip select for up to 8 memory devices | Outputs active **LOW** |
| **External ROM (e.g., 2764)** | Stores program code beyond 4 KB | Selected by **PSEN̅** |
| **External RAM (e.g., 6264)** | Stores runtime data beyond 128 bytes | Selected by **RD̅** / **WR̅** |
| **Port 0** | Carries A0–A7 (low address) + D0–D7 (data) | Multiplexed |
| **Port 2** | Carries A8–A15 (high address) | Dedicated address |
| **EA̅ pin** | Selects internal (1) or external (0) code memory | Active **LOW** for external |

### 3.4 Address Decoding Example (Analytical Derivation)

**Scenario:** Interface a **2764 (8 KB ROM)** and a **6264 (8 KB RAM)** to 8051, mapped at:
- ROM: **0000H – 1FFFH**
- RAM: **2000H – 3FFFH**

**Step 1 — 2764 has 8 KB = $2^{13}$ bytes, so it needs 13 address lines (A0–A12).**
The upper 3 address lines (A13, A14, A15) are decoded to generate chip select.

**Step 2 — ROM address range (0000H–1FFFH) in binary (A15–A0):**
$$0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 \text{ to } 0001\,1111\,1111\,1111_2$$

So A15 = 0, A14 = 0, A13 = 0 for ROM.

**Step 3 — RAM address range (2000H–3FFFH) in binary:**
$$0010\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 \text{ to } 0011\,1111\,1111\,1111_2$$

So A15 = 0, A14 = 0, A13 = 1 for RAM.

**Step 4 — Use 74LS138 (3-to-8 decoder) inputs A, B, C connected to P2.6, P2.5, P2.4 (= A14, A13, A12... well, here we'd map appropriately).**

For simplicity, with 2764 mapped at 0000H–1FFFH, A15 = 0, so we can use it as a qualifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam-Style Note:** In actual KTU problems, you may be given memory map ranges and asked to draw the interface diagram with latches and decoder outputs labeled. The above derivation is the standard KTU expected methodology.

### 3.5 Reset Circuit Analysis

A **power-on reset** requires RST pin (pin 9) to be HIGH for at least **2 machine cycles**.

**Typical Reset Circuit Components:**
- $V_{\text{CC}} = 5\text{V}$, capacitor $C = 10 \text{ μF}$, resistor $R = 8.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, resistor $= 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ pull-down.

**Time constant of RC circuit (V reaches 63.2% in one τ):**
$$\tau = R \times C$$

For voltage to reach logic HIGH (≈ 3.5V out of 5V):
$$t_{\text{rise}} \approx R \times C \times \ln\left(\frac{V_{\text{CC}}}{V_{\text{CC}} - V_{\text{IH}}}\right) = R \times C \times \ln(5) \approx 1.609 \times R \times C$$

For $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 10 \text{ μF}$:
$$t_{\text{rise}} \approx 1.609 \times 10^4 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.161 \text{ s} = 161 \text{ ms}$$

This is much greater than 2 machine cycles (≈ 2 μs), so the reset is reliable on power-up.

```mermaid
    T1[Timer 1 TH1 TL1]:::reg
    TF0[TF0 Flag TCON 5]:::sig
    TF1[TF1 Flag TCON 7]:::sig
    INT[Interrupt to CPU]:::sig

    OSC --> SEL
    SEL --> T0
    SEL --> T1
    C_T --> SEL
    T0 --> TF0
    T1 --> TF1
    TF0 --> INT
    TF1 --> INT

    classDef clk fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef sel fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.6 Selection of Microcontroller — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Project Requirement]:::start
    Q1{Bit width needed?}:::q
    Q2{Clock speed MHz?}:::q
    Q3{On chip memory size?}:::q
    Q4{Peripherals ADC PWM UART?}:::q
    Q5{Power constraint battery?}:::q
    Q6{Unit cost budget?}:::q
    A1[8 bit 8051 PIC16]:::ans
    A2[16 bit MSP430]:::ans
    A3[32 bit ARM Cortex M STM32]:::ans
    A4[AVR ATmega ESP32]:::ans

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- 8 bit --> Q2
    Q1 -- 16 bit --> A2
    Q1 -- 32 bit --> A3
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6
    Q6 -- Low --> A1
    Q6 -- Medium --> A4
    Q6 -- High --> A3

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ans fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. State any three advantages of microcontrollers over microprocessors.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Microprocessor | Microcontroller |
|---|---|
| CPU only on a single chip | CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O on a single chip |
| Memory and I/O must be added externally | Memory and I/O are built-in |
| Higher system cost, larger board | Lower system cost, compact design |
| Higher power consumption | Lower power consumption |
| Used in general-purpose computers | Used in embedded systems |

**Three advantages of microcontrollers:**
1. **Lower cost** because all components are on one chip (no external memory/I/O ICs needed)
2. **Compact size** — ideal for space-constrained embedded applications
3. **Lower power consumption** — suitable for battery-powered devices like IoT sensors
4. **Higher reliability** — fewer external interconnections mean fewer failure points

*(Marking: 1 mark for tabular difference, 1 mark each for any 2 advantages = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List the Special Function Registers (SFRs) used for (i) serial communication, (ii) interrupt control, and (iii) port I/O in 8051. State their addresses.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Function | SFR Name | SFR Address |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Serial Communication | **SCON** (Serial Control) | **98H** |
| | **SBUF** (Serial Buffer) | **99H** |
| (ii) Interrupt Control | **IE** (Interrupt Enable) | **A8H** |
| | **IP** (Interrupt Priority) | **B8H** |
| (iii) Port I/O | **P0** (Port 0 Latch) | **80H** |
| | **P1** (Port 1 Latch) | **90H** |
| | **P2** (Port 2 Latch) | **A0H** |
| | **P3** (Port 3 Latch) | **B0H** |

*(Marking: 1 mark per correct functional group with addresses = 3 marks)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Draw the 8051 pin diagram and explain the function of the following signals: (i) **PSEN̅**, (ii) **ALE**, (iii) **EA̅**, (iv) **RST**. Also list the alternate functions of **Port 3** pins.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **internal RAM organization** of 8051. Differentiate between the **bit-addressable area** and the **register bank area** with addresses.

---

**Model Answer — Question A:**

**(a) 8051 Pin Diagram and Key Signals [7 marks]**

The 8051 is a **40-pin DIP** IC with the following important control signals:

1. **PSEN̅ (Program Store Enable) — Pin 29 [1 mark]**
   - Active LOW output signal
   - Activated during `MOVC` instruction to read from **external code memory (ROM)**
   - Connects to the **OE̅** (output enable) pin of external ROM

2. **ALE (Address Latch Enable) — Pin 30 [1 mark]**
   - Active HIGH output, used to demultiplex the **Port 0** bus
   - Latches the lower 8 bits of address (A0–A7) into an external **74LS373 latch**
   - Frequency of ALE = $f_{\text{osc}} \div 6$ (one pulse per machine cycle)

3. **EA̅ (External Access) — Pin 31 [1 mark]**
   - When HIGH (connected to VCC): 8051 fetches code from **internal ROM** (0000H–0FFFH)
   - When LOW (connected to GND): 8051 fetches **all** code from **external ROM**

4. **RST (Reset) — Pin 9 [1 mark]**
   - Active HIGH input; must remain HIGH for at least **2 machine cycles** to reset
   - Resets PC = 0000H, SP = 07H, clears all SFRs, sets ports to FFH

5. **Port 3 Alternate Functions [3 marks]**
   - P3.0 = **RXD** (Serial input)
   - P3.1 = **TXD** (Serial output)
   - P3.2 = **INT0̅** (External interrupt 0)
   - P3.3 = **INT1̅** (External interrupt 1)
   - P3.4 = **T0** (Timer 0 external input)
   - P3.5 = **T1** (Timer 1 external input)
   - P3.6 = **WR̅** (External data memory write strobe)
   - P3.7 = **RD̅** (External data memory read strobe)

---

**(b) Internal RAM Organization [7 marks]**

The 8051 has **128 bytes** of on-chip RAM, organized as follows:

**Memory Map:**

$$\text{Internal RAM} = \begin{cases} 00\text{H} - 1\text{FH}: & \text{Register Banks (4 banks × 8 registers)} \\ 20\text{H} - 2\text{FH}: & \text{Bit-addressable area (16 bytes, 128 bits)} \\ 30\text{H} - 7\text{FH}: & \text{General-purpose RAM (80 bytes)} \end{cases}$$

**Register Bank Area (00H–1FH) [2 marks]:**

| Bank | RS1 | RS0 | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bank 0 | 0 | 0 | 00H – 07H (R0–R7) |
| Bank 1 | 0 | 1 | 08H – 0FH (R0–R7) |
| Bank 2 | 1 | 0 | 10H – 17H (R0–R7) |
| Bank 3 | 1 | 1 | 18H – 1FH (R0–R7) |

- Default bank after **RESET = Bank 0**
- Switching banks allows fast context save in interrupt service routines

**Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH) [3 marks]:**

- 16 bytes × 8 bits = **128 individually addressable bits** (bit addresses 00H–7FH)
- Allows SETB/CLR bit operations on individual flag-type data
- Example: `SETB 20H.0` sets bit 0 of byte 20H

**General-Purpose Area (30H–7FH) [1 mark]:**
- 80 bytes for user variables and stack
- Stack Pointer (SP) initializes to **07H** and grows upward

**Differences (Summary) [1 mark]:**

| Feature | Register Bank Area | Bit-Addressable Area |
|---|---|---|
| Address Range | 00H–1FH | 20H–2FH |
| Size | 32 bytes | 16 bytes |
| Function | Holds R0–R7 of active bank | Individual bit manipulation |
| Selectable | Via PSW (RS1, RS0) | Always accessible |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare **Harvard** and **Von Neumann** architectures. Explain how the 8051 uses Harvard architecture for accessing **code and data memory separately**.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain **external ROM and RAM interfacing** to 8051. State the role of **PSEN̅, RD̅, WR̅, ALE, and 74LS373 latch** in the interface.

---

**Model Answer — Question B:**

**(a) Harvard vs. Von Neumann Architecture [7 marks]**

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Bus structure | Single shared bus for code + data | Separate buses for code and data |
| Memory | Single unified memory | Separate program and data memory |
| Speed | Slower (bus contention) | Faster (parallel access) |
| Power consumption | Higher (simultaneous access) | Lower (specialized access) |
| Used in | 8086, 8085, ARM7 (with cache) | 8051, PIC, AVR, ARM Cortex-M |
| Instruction fetch + data read | Sequential (one at a time) | Simultaneous (parallel) |

**8051's Harvard Architecture Implementation [3 marks]:**

The 8051 has **two physically separate memory spaces**:
- **Code memory (ROM):** Up to 64 KB, accessed via PC and PSEN̅ signal
- **Data memory (RAM):** Internal 128 bytes + external 64 KB, accessed via DPTR, SP, and RD̅/WR̅

**Key Benefit:** While the CPU is reading an opcode from ROM, it can simultaneously read a data byte from internal RAM — doubling throughput compared to Von Neumann design.

**Example of separate access:**
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — reads from **code** memory (PSEN̅ activated)
- `MOVX A, @DPTR` — reads from **external data** memory (RD̅ activated)

---

**(b) External ROM and RAM Interfacing [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.3 above) [3 marks]**

**Connections and Signal Roles [4 marks]:**

| Signal | Connected To | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **P0 (AD0–AD7)** | 74LS373 latch input | Multiplexed lower address + data |
| **P2 (A8–A15)** | Memory high address pins | Carries upper address byte |
| **74LS373 Latch** | Between P0 and ROM/RAM A0–A7 | Latches lower address when ALE goes HIGH; later releases the bus for data transfer |
| **ALE → G (enable) of 74LS373** | Latch control | When ALE = 1, latch is transparent; when ALE = 0, latched address is held |
| **PSEN̅ → OE̅ of external ROM** | ROM output enable | Activated during external code fetch (`MOVC`) |
| **RD̅ (P3.7) → OE̅ of external RAM** | RAM read strobe | Activated during `MOVX A, @DPTR` |
| **WR̅ (P3.6) → WE̅ of external RAM** | RAM write strobe | Activated during `MOVX @DPTR, A` |

**Read Sequence for External ROM:**
1. CPU places address on P0 and P2
2. ALE = HIGH → 74LS373 latches low address
3. PSEN̅ = LOW → external ROM drives data onto P0
4. CPU reads data from P0

**Write Sequence to External RAM:**
1. CPU places address on P0 and P2, data on P0
2. ALE = HIGH → 74LS373 latches low address
3. WR̅ = LOW → external RAM stores data
4. CPU releases data bus

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PSEN̅ and RD̅:** PSEN̅ is for **ROM/Code** (MOVC), RD̅ is for **RAM/Data** (MOVX). Mixing these is a guaranteed **−2 mark penalty**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 74LS373 latch:** Many students show P0 going directly to memory without demultiplexing. This loses **2 marks** for incomplete interface design.
> 3. **Not stating EA̅:** When using external ROM, EA̅ must be tied to GND. Omitting this loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong 8051 port bit count:** Writing "8051 has 2 ports" loses marks — it has **4 ports** (P0–P3) = 32 I/O lines.
> 5. **Mixing up crystal frequency and machine cycle:** $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ does NOT mean machine cycle = 11.0592 MHz. The machine cycle is $f_{\text{osc}}/12$ = 921.6 kHz. This is a very common conceptual error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [x] **8051 is 8-bit, 40-pin, +5V, Harvard-architecture, single-chip microcontroller** introduced by Intel in 1980.
- [x] **CPU has 8-bit ALU** with registers **A, B, PSW, SP (8-bit), DPTR (16-bit), PC (16-bit)**.
- [x] **Internal memory: 4 KB ROM + 128 B RAM.** External expandable to 64 KB code + 64 KB data.
- [x] **Four 8-bit ports (P0–P3).** P0 is multiplexed address/data; P1 is general I/O; P2 is upper address; P3 has alternate functions.
- [x] **Port 3 alternate functions:** RXD, TXD, INT0̅, INT1̅, T0, T1, WR̅, RD̅.
- [x] **Two 16-bit timers (T0, T1)**, one **full-duplex UART**, and **5 interrupt sources**.
- [x] **Machine cycle = 12 clock periods.** $T_{\text{machine}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$.
- [x] **Bit-addressable area: 20H–2FH (128 bits)**, with bit addresses 00H–7FH.
- [x] **Four register banks: Bank 0 (00H–07H) to Bank 3 (18H–1FH)**, selected by PSW bits RS1, RS0.
- [x] **PSEN̅** = Program Store Enable → for external **ROM** read.
- [x] **RD̅/WR̅** (P3.7/P3.6) → for external **RAM** read/write.
- [x] **ALE** → latches lower address from P0 into **74LS373**.
- [x] **EA̅** → 1 = internal ROM, 0 = external ROM (or both).
- [x] **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred for accurate serial baud rates.
- [x] **Microprocessor** = CPU only; **Microcontroller** = CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals on one chip.
- [x] **Evolution:** 4-bit (4004) → 8-bit (8051) → 16-bit (MSP430) → 32-bit (ARM Cortex-M).
- [x] **Selection criteria** include: bit-width, clock, memory, I/O count, peripherals, power, cost, tools.
- [x] **Applications** span home appliances, automotive, medical, industrial, IoT, and aerospace.
- [x] **RST pin must be HIGH for ≥ 2 machine cycles** to reset the 8051.
- [x] **SP initializes to 07H** after reset; the stack grows **upward** in internal RAM.
- [x] **EA̅ = 0 + external ROM**: required for systems needing more than 4 KB program memory.
- [x] **Remember the formula:** $T_{\text{machine}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$ and Timer reload $X = 65536 - (\text{Delay} \times f_{\text{osc}} / 12)$.
- [x] **Harvard = separate code/data buses (8051). Von Neumann = shared bus (8085/8086).**
- [x] **74LS373 is an octal transparent latch** used for address demultiplexing in 8051 systems.
- [x] **SCON, SBUF, IE, IP** are critical SFRs — know their addresses (98H, 99H, A8H, B8H).

topics.title: Addressing modes and accessing memory using various addressing modes, Instruction set: Arithmetic, Logical, Simple bit, jump, loop and call instructions, Stack, Stack and Subroutine instructions, and Assembly language program examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCERL407 - COMPUTER NETWORKING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Addressing modes and accessing memory using various addressing modes, Instruction set: Arithmetic, Logical, Simple bit, jump, loop and call instructions, Stack, Stack and Subroutine instructions, and Assembly language program examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Addressing Modes (KTU Standard Definition):**
> An **addressing mode** is the method by which the 8051 microprocessor specifies the **operands** (data on which the instruction operates) for an instruction. It defines how the CPU calculates the effective physical memory address from which data is fetched or to which data is written during the execution of an instruction.

In the **MCS-51 (8051) family** of microcontrollers, the Central Processing Unit can reference memory in only a limited number of ways. KTU 2024 Scheme strictly classifies these into **five canonical addressing modes**:

1. **Immediate Addressing Mode** — operand is a constant embedded in the instruction.
2. **Register Addressing Mode** — operand resides in one of the eight internal CPU registers (R0–R7).
3. **Direct Addressing Mode** — the 8-bit address of the operand is given explicitly in the instruction (used for SFRs and lower 128 bytes of internal RAM).
4. **Register Indirect Addressing Mode** — a register (R0, R1, or DPTR) holds the *address* of the operand, not the operand itself.
5. **Indexed Addressing Mode** — used exclusively for program memory look-up tables; combines a base register (DPTR or PC) with an offset (A).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Addressing Modes Matter (KTU Board Perspective):**
> The number of addressing modes supported by a processor directly determines its **instruction flexibility**, **code density**, and **execution speed**. The 8051's compact 5-mode scheme is one reason it remains a pedagogical favourite in KTU's microcontrollers syllabus.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Address System"

Imagine you want a friend to deliver a parcel:

| Real-World Analogy | 8051 Equivalent | Addressing Mode |
|---|---|---|
| "Take **this exact** ₹500 note" | `MOV A, #50H` (data is part of instruction) | Immediate |
| "Use the **money in your left pocket**" | `MOV A, R4` (data in register) | Register |
| "Go to **House No. 45, 2nd Street**" | `MOV A, 45H` (explicit 8-bit address) | Direct |
| "The address is **written on this slip** — go there" | `MOV A, @R0` (R0 holds the address) | Register Indirect |
| "**Starting from** the post office, move **5 steps forward**" | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (base + offset) | Indexed |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick:** "**I-R-D-R-I**" → **I**mmediate, **R**egister, **D**irect, **R**egister-**I**ndirect, **I**ndexed. Memorize this sequence for the viva.

## 1.3 Instruction Set — KTU 2024 Categorization

The 8051 instruction set (111 base instructions, 255 opcodes) is grouped into the following functional families as mandated by the KTU 2024 lab manual for PCERL407:

- **Arithmetic Instructions** — `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `DA A`.
- **Logical Instructions** — `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`, `CLR`, `RL`, `RLC`, `RR`, `RRC`, `SWAP`.
- **Simple Bit Instructions** — `SETB bit`, `CLR bit`, `CPL bit`, `ANL C,bit`, `ORL C,bit`, `MOV C,bit`.
- **Jump Instructions** — `SJMP`, `LJMP`, `AJMP`, `JMP @A+DPTR`.
- **Loop Instructions** — Conditional jumps: `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`.
- **Call Instructions** — `LCALL`, `ACALL`, `RET`, `RETI`.
- **Stack & Subroutine Instructions** — `PUSH`, `POP`, `CALL`, `RET`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Metric:** The 8051 has a **12 MHz** maximum crystal frequency yielding a **1 µs** machine cycle (12 oscillator periods). All instruction timing questions in KTU exams are based on this canonical frequency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Addressing Modes — Operational Deep-Dive

### 2.1.1 Immediate Addressing Mode
- **How it works:** The operand (an 8-bit or 16-bit constant) is embedded directly after the opcode byte in program memory.
- **Opcode format:** `[Opcode] [Immediate Data]`
- **Effective address calculation:** $EA = PC_{current}$ (data follows the opcode).
- **Use case:** Initializing registers, loading constants, setting up timer values.
- **KTU Trap Question:** Immediate data **must be preceded by `#`**. Writing `MOV A, 30H` means "load A with the *contents* of memory location 30H", **not** the value 30H itself.

### 2.1.2 Register Addressing Mode
- **How it works:** Operand is in one of the four selected working registers in the currently active **Register Bank** (selected by RS0, RS1 bits of PSW).
- **Opcode format:** `[Opcode with 3-bit register code]`
- **Why it is fastest:** No memory fetch is required — the data is already inside the ALU.
- **KTU Trap Question:** The selected register bank is determined by the **PSW register bits RS1 and RS0**, not by any explicit instruction. Switching banks requires modifying PSW.

### 2.1.3 Direct Addressing Mode
- **How it works:** The instruction contains the **8-bit absolute address** of the operand in internal RAM (00H–7FH) or SFR area (80H–FFH).
- **Opcode format:** `[Opcode] [Direct Address]`
- **Why it is the only way to access SFRs:** All 8051 special function registers (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, etc.) **must** be accessed using direct addressing.
- **Internal RAM split:** Lower 128 bytes (00H–7FH) are general-purpose; upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH) overlap with SFRs.

### 2.1.4 Register Indirect Addressing Mode
- **How it works:** The chosen register does **not contain data**; it contains the **memory address** of the data. The `@" symbol denotes indirection.
- **Permitted pointer registers:** `R0`, `R1` (for 8-bit internal RAM access), `DPTR` (for 16-bit external data memory access via `MOVX`).
- **Example:** `MOV A, @R0` — *The CPU reads the address stored in R0, then goes to that address to fetch the data into A.*
- **KTU High-Yield Insight:** This mode is the **only way** to access a *variable* data address — essential for array traversal and pointer-based loops.

### 2.1.5 Indexed Addressing Mode
- **How it works:** Used **exclusively with `MOVC` (Move Code) instructions** to read from program memory (ROM). The effective address is computed as the sum of a 16-bit base register and the accumulator.
- **Formula:** $EA = \vert Base\_Register \vert + \vert A \vert$
- **Two variants:**
  - `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — DPTR is base; useful for 64 KB look-up tables.
  - `MOVC A, @A+PC` — PC is base; useful for small localized tables (requires offset compensation).
- **KTU Trap Question:** `MOVC` (with C) accesses **code memory**; `MOVX` (with X) accesses **external data memory**. Confusing them is a guaranteed mark-loss.

## 2.2 Instruction Set Categories — KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **canonical KTU reference** for all 8051 addressing-mode and instruction-classification questions.

| Instruction Family | Sample Mnemonics | Addressing Modes Used | Size (Bytes) | Cycles @ 12 MHz | Flags Affected |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data Transfer (Immediate) | `MOV A, #data` | Immediate | 2 | 1 | — |
| Data Transfer (Register) | `MOV A, Rn` | Register | 1 | 1 | — |
| Data Transfer (Direct) | `MOV A, direct` | Direct | 2 | 1 | — |
| Data Transfer (Indirect) | `MOV A, @Ri` | Register Indirect | 1 | 1 | — |
| Arithmetic — Addition | `ADD A, src` | All 5 | 1–2 | 1 | C, AC, OV |
| Arithmetic — Subtraction | `SUBB A, src` | All 5 | 1–2 | 1 | C, AC, OV |
| Arithmetic — Increment | `INC A` / `INC direct` | Register / Direct | 1 / 2 | 1 | — |
| Logical — AND | `ANL A, src` | All 5 | 1–2 | 1 | — |
| Logical — OR | `ORL A, src` | All 5 | 1–2 | 1 | — |
| Logical — XOR | `XRL A, src` | All 5 | 1–2 | 1 | — |
| Bit Manipulation | `SETB bit` | Bit Address | 2 | 1 | — |
| Unconditional Jump | `LJMP addr16` | — | 3 | 2 | — |
| Conditional Jump | `JZ rel` / `JNZ rel` | — | 2 | 2 | — |
| Compare & Jump | `CJNE A, #data, rel` | — | 3 | 2 | C |
| Decrement & Jump | `DJNZ Rn, rel` | — | 2 | 2 | — |
| Subroutine Call | `LCALL addr16` | — | 3 | 2 | — |
| Return | `RET` | — | 1 | 2 | — |
| Stack Push | `PUSH direct` | Direct | 2 | 2 | — |
| Stack Pop | `POP direct` | Direct | 2 | 2 | — |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Automotive ECUs:** Direct addressing of SFRs enables deterministic port-toggling for sensor sampling.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Register indirect addressing (via @R0/@R1) is used to implement circular buffers in UART communication.
- **IoT Sensor Nodes:** Indexed addressing (`MOVC A,@A+DPTR`) is the cornerstone of **look-up-table** based sensor linearization, replacing costly floating-point math.
- **Medical Devices:** Subroutine call-and-return architecture enables real-time interrupt-driven ECG processing on tiny 8051 cores.

## 2.4 Stack and Subroutine — Architectural Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Topic:** The 8051 stack is a **software-controlled LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** data structure that grows **upward** in internal RAM. It is managed by the 8-bit **Stack Pointer (SP)** SFR (address 81H), which is **reset to 07H** on power-on.

- **PUSH operation:** `PUSH direct` → `SP = SP + 1`, then `RAM[SP] = source byte`.
- **POP operation:** `POP direct` → `dest = RAM[SP]`, then `SP = SP - 1`.
- **Subroutine call:** `LCALL addr16` pushes the 16-bit PC (return address) onto the stack in two bytes (high byte first), then jumps to the target.
- **Subroutine return:** `RET` pops two bytes off the stack into PC, resuming execution at the instruction after the CALL.
- **Stack overflow risk:** The default SP=07H means stack will collide with **Register Bank 0** (R0–R7 at 00H–07H). KTU standard practice: reinitialize SP to **60H** or higher at program start.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Effective Address (EA) Calculation — Worked Examples

### 3.1.1 Example A: `MOV A, #25H` (Immediate)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction bytes in ROM} &= [\text{Opcode: 74H}] \ [\text{Data: 25H}] \\
\text{Opcode 74H} &\rightarrow \text{MOV A, immediate} \\
\text{PC after fetch} &= (PC_{initial}) + 2 \\
\text{Effective Address} &= PC_{initial} + 1 \quad \text{(address of the data byte)} \\
\text{Result} &: A \leftarrow \vert 25H \vert = \vert 0010\;0101_2 \vert = \vert 37_{10} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The CPU first fetches opcode 74H, recognizes "immediate to A", fetches the next byte (25H) and places it directly into accumulator A. No memory addressing is performed on data memory.

### 3.1.2 Example B: `MOV A, 30H` (Direct)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction bytes} &= [\text{Opcode: E5H}] \ [\text{Address: 30H}] \\
\text{Opcode E5H} &\rightarrow \text{MOV A, direct 8-bit} \\
\text{Effective Address} &= 30H \\
\text{Action} &: A \leftarrow RAM[30H] \\
\text{If } RAM[30H] &= 0x55, \text{ then } A = 0x55
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The byte following the opcode **is the address itself** (not the data). The CPU performs one extra memory read to fetch the contents of internal RAM location 30H.

### 3.1.3 Example C: `MOV A, @R0` (Register Indirect)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction bytes} &= [\text{Opcode: E2H}] \quad \text{(single byte)} \\
\text{CPU operation sequence:} & \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad &\text{Read R0 (assume } R0 = 40H) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad &\text{Read } RAM[40H] \text{ (assume } RAM[40H] = 0xAA) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad &A \leftarrow 0xAA \\
\text{Effective Address} &= (R0) = 40H \\
\text{Result} &: A = 0xAA
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A single-byte opcode encodes both the operation *and* the pointer register (R0). The "address of the address" is stored in R0, requiring an indirection step. This is the only way to access a *runtime-computed* address.

### 3.1.4 Example D: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (Indexed)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction bytes} &= [\text{Opcode: 93H}] \quad \text{(single byte)} \\
\text{Pre-conditions:} \quad & A = 0x05, \quad DPTR = 0x2000H \\
\text{Effective Address calculation:} & \\
EA &= (DPTR) + (A) \\
EA &= 0x2000H + 0x05H \\
EA &= 0x2005H \\
\text{Action} &: A \leftarrow CODE[0x2005H]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Indexed mode computes a 16-bit sum; the upper 8 bits come from DPTR, lower 8 bits from A. Wraparound is **modulo 65536** (16-bit). For PC-indexed mode, an **offset compensation** of +2 must be added to A (because PC points to the next instruction after MOVC).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mode Identification at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** In a mixed-instruction question, students are asked to identify the addressing mode of each instruction. Use the matrix below to decode any 8051 mnemonic instantly.

| Mnemonic Pattern | Look For | Address Mode | Example | Memory Accesses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, #X` | `#` symbol present | Immediate | `MOV A, #0FH` | 0 data-memory reads |
| `MOV A, Rn` | Rn (R0–R7) operand | Register | `MOV A, R3` | 0 data-memory reads |
| `MOV A, X` | Bare number, no `#` | Direct | `MOV A, 50H` | 1 internal RAM read |
| `MOV A, @Ri` | `@` symbol with R0/R1 | Register Indirect | `MOV A, @R0` | 1 internal RAM read |
| `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | `MOVC` + `+` | Indexed | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | 1 program ROM read |
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | `MOVX` (capital X) | External Indirect | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | 1 external RAM read |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Assembly Program — Sum of N Numbers (KTU 2024 Model)

**Problem Statement (Direct from KTU Module Experiment 2):**
*Write an 8051 ALP to add N 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM starting at location 30H. Store the 16-bit result in RAM locations 40H (low byte) and 41H (high byte). N is stored at RAM location 20H.*

```asm
;------------------------------------------------------------
; PROGRAM 1: Sum of N 8-bit numbers (KTU 2024 Model)
;------------------------------------------------------------
        ORG 0000H           ; Reset vector
        LJMP MAIN           ; Jump to main program

        ORG 0040H           ; Code starts after interrupt vectors
MAIN:
        MOV SP, #60H        ; Step 1: Initialize Stack Pointer to safe location

        MOV R0, #30H        ; Step 2: R0 points to first data byte
        MOV R1, 20H         ; Step 3: Load N (counter) from RAM 20H
        MOV A, #00H         ; Step 4: Clear accumulator (sum = 0)
        MOV 40H, #00H       ; Step 5: Clear result low byte
        MOV 41H, #00H       ; Step 6: Clear result high byte

LOOP:
        ADD A, @R0          ; Step 7: A = A + RAM[R0]
        JNC SKIP            ; Step 8: If no carry, skip propagation
        INC 41H             ; Step 9: Propagate carry to high byte
SKIP:
        INC R0              ; Step 10: Move to next data byte
        DJNZ R1, LOOP       ; Step 11: Decrement N; if not zero, loop

        MOV 40H, A          ; Step 12: Store low byte of sum
HERE:   SJMP HERE           ; Step 13: Infinite loop (program halt)
        END
```

**Step-by-step trace (assume N=3, data at 30H,31H,32H = A5H, 30H, F0H):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1:} \quad & A = 0x00 + 0xA5 = 0xA5, \; C=0 \\
& R0 \rightarrow 31H, \; R1 \rightarrow 2 \\
\text{Iteration 2:} \quad & A = 0xA5 + 0x30 = 0xD5, \; C=0 \\
& R0 \rightarrow 32H, \; R1 \rightarrow 1 \\
\text{Iteration 3:} \quad & A = 0xD5 + 0xF0 = 0x1C5, \; C=1 \\
& \text{High byte incremented: } RAM[41H] = 0x01 \\
& A = 0xC5 \text{ (low 8 bits)} \\
\text{Final:} \quad & RAM[40H] = 0xC5, \; RAM[41H] = 0x01 \\
\text{Decimal check:} \quad & 165 + 48 + 240 = 453 = 0x01C5 \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Subroutine Example — Multi-byte Delay with PUSH/POP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board questions frequently test **register bank preservation** using PUSH/POP inside subroutines.

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN

        ORG 0040H
MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H
        MOV R0, #05H       ; Call delay 5 times
LOOP:   ACALL DELAY
        DJNZ R0, LOOP
        SJMP $

;------------ DELAY SUBROUTINE (preserves R0-R2) ------------
DELAY:  PUSH 00H            ; Save R0
        PUSH 01H            ; Save R1
        PUSH 02H            ; Save R2
        MOV R0, #0FFH
OUTER:  MOV R1, #0FFH
INNER:  MOV R2, #02H
NESTED: DJNZ R2, NESTED
        DJNZ R1, INNER
        DJNZ R0, OUTER
        POP 02H             ; Restore R2
        POP 01H             ; Restore R1
        POP 00H             ; Restore R0
        RET                 ; Return to caller
        END
```

**Stack trace (KTU marks favour this):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{On ACALL DELAY:} \quad & SP \rightarrow 60H, \text{ then } (PC_{low}) \rightarrow 61H, \; SP=61H \\
& (PC_{high}) \rightarrow 62H, \; SP=62H \\
\text{After PUSH 00H:} \quad & (R0) \rightarrow 63H, \; SP=63H \\
\text{After PUSH 01H:} \quad & (R1) \rightarrow 64H, \; SP=64H \\
\text{After PUSH 02H:} \quad & (R2) \rightarrow 65H, \; SP=65H \\
\text{... delay executes ...} \\
\text{After POP 02H:} \quad & R2 \leftarrow RAM[65H], \; SP=64H \\
\text{After POP 01H:} \quad & R1 \leftarrow RAM[64H], \; SP=63H \\
\text{After POP 00H:} \quad & R0 \leftarrow RAM[63H], \; SP=62H \\
\text{After RET:} \quad & PC \leftarrow RAM[62H] \cdot 256 + RAM[61H], \; SP=60H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key KTU point:** The order of POPs is the **reverse** of PUSHes. Reversing this order corrupts register contents.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — 8051 Addressing Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[8051 Mnemonic Encountered] --> q1{Operand starts with #?}
    q1 -- Yes --> imm[Immediate Mode]
    imm --> immE[Example: MOV A, #25H]

    q1 -- No --> q2{Operand is Rn R0-R7?}
    q2 -- Yes --> reg[Register Mode]
    reg --> regE[Example: MOV A, R3]

    q2 -- No --> q3{Operand is @Ri?}
    q3 -- Yes --> ri[Register Indirect Mode]
    ri --> riE[Example: MOV A, @R0]

    q3 -- No --> q4{Instruction is MOVC with plus?}
    q4 -- Yes --> idx[Indexed Mode]
    idx --> idxE[Example: MOVC A, @A+DPTR]

    q4 -- No --> q5{Operand is bare 8-bit address?}
    q5 -- Yes --> dir[Direct Mode]
    dir --> dirE[Example: MOV A, 30H]

    q5 -- No --> unk[Unknown / Invalid Mnemonic]

    style reg fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

```

```mermaid
    C --> D[Instruction: PUSH 01H]

    E --> F[Instruction: PUSH 02H]
    F --> G[SP 63H, RAM63H 0x3F]
    G --> H[Subroutine body executes]
    H --> I[Instruction: POP 02H]
    I --> J[SP 62H, R2 0x3F restored]

    L --> M[Instruction: POP 00H]
    M --> N[SP 60H, R0 0x05 restored]
    N --> O[Instruction: RET]
    O --> P[PC restored, SP stays 60H]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — LCALL/RET Stack Frame Mechanism

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Caller as Caller Program
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant Stack as Internal RAM Stack
    participant Sub as Subroutine

    Caller->>CPU: Execute LCALL SUB_ADDR
    CPU->>Stack: PUSH PC_low byte (SP++)
    CPU->>Stack: PUSH PC_high byte (SP++)
    CPU->>Sub: Jump to SUB_ADDR
    Sub->>CPU: Execute subroutine body
    Sub->>CPU: Execute RET
    CPU->>Stack: POP PC_high byte (SP--)
    CPU->>Stack: POP PC_low byte (SP--)
    CPU->>Caller: Resume at instruction after LCALL
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Immediate addressing mode and Direct addressing mode in 8051 with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Immediate Addressing | Direct Addressing |
|---|---|---|
| Symbol used | `#` prefix on operand | No prefix; bare address |
| Operand nature | The data itself | The address of the data |
| Memory access | 0 internal RAM reads | 1 internal RAM read |
| Example | `MOV A, #25H` | `MOV A, 25H` |
| Result (if RAM[25H]=AAH) | A = 25H | A = AAH |
| Instruction size | 2 bytes | 2 bytes |
| Use case | Loading constants | Accessing SFRs and RAM variables |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the role of the Stack Pointer (SP) in 8051. What is its reset value and why is it recommended to reinitialize SP at the start of every program?**

**Model Answer:**
- The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is an 8-bit SFR at internal RAM address **81H** that holds the address of the top of the stack.
- On **power-on reset**, SP is set to **07H**, which means the stack will use the same internal RAM area as **Register Bank 0** (locations 00H–07H). This causes **data corruption** whenever PUSH instructions overwrite R0–R7 or when subroutine calls destroy register contents.
- The KTU-recommended best practice is to **reinitialize SP** to a safe upper location such as **60H** or **70H** at the very beginning of `MAIN`:
  ```asm
  MAIN: MOV SP, #60H   ; Reinitialize stack pointer
  ```
- This ensures the stack grows into unused internal RAM (typically 60H–7FH), preventing collisions with active register banks and bit-addressable memory.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ▶ Question A (14 Marks) — Total: Sum + Largest + Sorting (Combined)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** Write an 8051 assembly language program to find the **sum of N 8-bit numbers** stored in internal RAM starting at location 30H. The value of N is stored at RAM location 20H. Store the 16-bit sum in locations 50H (LSB) and 51H (MSB). Explain the role of the carry flag in your program. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to find the **largest of N 8-bit numbers** stored in internal RAM starting at location 30H. N is stored at location 20H. Store the largest value at location 40H. Use `CJNE` and a suitable loop construct. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0040H
MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H
        MOV R0, #30H        ; Pointer to first number
        MOV R1, 20H         ; N = counter
        CLR A               ; A = 0 (sum accumulator)
        MOV 50H, #00H       ; Clear result LSB
        MOV 51H, #00H       ; Clear result MSB

LOOP:   ADD A, @R0          ; A = A + RAM[R0]
        JNC NEXT            ; If no carry, skip high-byte update
        INC 51H             ; Carry → increment MSB
NEXT:   INC R0              ; Advance pointer
        DJNZ R1, LOOP       ; Decrement N, loop if not zero
        MOV 50H, A          ; Store LSB
HERE:   SJMP HERE
        END
```

**Role of Carry Flag (KTU Board Key):**
- The 8051's accumulator is **8 bits wide**, so it can hold values only up to **0xFF (255)**.
- When two 8-bit numbers sum to a value $\geq 256$, the result overflows the accumulator and the **Carry Flag (CY) in PSW is set to 1**.
- The instruction `JNC NEXT` checks this flag; if CY=0, no propagation is needed. If CY=1, the line `INC 51H` increments the high byte to form a true 16-bit sum.
- Without carry-flag handling, sums exceeding 255 would silently wrap and yield wrong results.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0040H
MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H
        MOV R0, #30H
        MOV R1, 20H         ; N
        MOV B, @R0          ; B = first number (assumed largest)
        DEC R1              ; Remaining count = N-1
        INC R0

LOOP:   MOV A, @R0          ; Fetch next number
        CJNE A, B, CHECK     ; Compare A with current max in B
CHECK:  JC NEXT             ; If A < B (carry set on A-B borrow), skip
        MOV B, A            ; Else A >= B, update max
NEXT:   INC R0
        DJNZ R1, LOOP
        MOV 40H, B          ; Store largest
HERE:   SJMP HERE
        END
```

**Logic Explanation (KTU Board):**
- The `CJNE A, B, rel` instruction performs `A - B`, sets carry if `A < B`, and jumps if not equal.
- We exploit the carry flag: `JC NEXT` means "if A < B, do not update". Otherwise, `B ← A` updates the running maximum.
- `DJNZ R1, LOOP` ensures the loop iterates exactly (N-1) times, covering all remaining elements.

---

### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Subroutines + Bit Manipulation

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2]**

**(a)** Explain **any five addressing modes** of the 8051 with one example instruction each. Show, with the help of a memory diagram, how `MOV A, @R0` differs from `MOV A, R0`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to toggle the **MSB of port P1** continuously using bit-manipulation instructions. Use a delay subroutine (with PUSH/POP) to provide a visible blink. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Five addressing modes (KTU standard):**

1. **Immediate:** `MOV A, #0FH` — loads A with the constant 0FH.
2. **Register:** `MOV A, R4` — copies contents of register R4 into A.
3. **Direct:** `MOV A, 30H` — copies contents of internal RAM at 30H into A.
4. **Register Indirect:** `MOV A, @R0` — copies contents of RAM at the *address held in R0* into A.
5. **Indexed:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — reads from program memory at address (DPTR + A).

**Memory Diagram — `MOV A, R0` vs `MOV A, @R0`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Case 1: } & \textbf{MOV A, R0} \\
& R0_{register} = 0x55 \\
& \text{After: } A = 0x55 \quad \text{(direct copy from register)} \\
& \\
\text{Case 2: } & \textbf{MOV A, @R0} \\
& R0_{register} = 0x40 \quad \text{(R0 holds the address 40H)} \\
& RAM[40H] = 0xAA \\
& \text{After: } A = 0xAA \quad \text{(data fetched from address pointed by R0)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The **@ symbol** is the only visual difference, but its semantic effect is profound: the first instruction never touches data memory; the second performs an **indirection** through R0 to reach internal RAM.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0040H
MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H
AGAIN:  CPL P1.7           ; Toggle MSB (bit 7) of Port 1
        ACALL DELAY
        SJMP AGAIN

;--------------------- DELAY SUBROUTINE ---------------------
DELAY:  PUSH 00H
        PUSH 01H
        PUSH 02H
        MOV R0, #0FFH
D1:     MOV R1, #0FFH
D2:     MOV R2, #0FFH
D3:     DJNZ R2, D3
        DJNZ R1, D2
        DJNZ R0, D1
        POP 02H
        POP 01H
        POP 00H
        RET
        END
```

**Explanation (KTU Board):**
- `P1.7` denotes **bit 7 of Port 1** (MSB). The `CPL` (complement) instruction flips this bit every iteration, creating a square wave of ~50% duty cycle.
- The `DELAY` subroutine uses **three nested DJNZ loops** to generate approximately:
$$
T_{delay} \approx 3 \times 255 \times 255 \times 2 \; \mu s \approx 0.39 \; s
$$
- `PUSH/POP` preserves R0, R1, R2 so that any caller is unaffected — a critical discipline emphasized in KTU viva.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Question Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Missing `#` in immediate data:** Writing `MOV A, 30H` instead of `MOV A, #30H` flips the meaning entirely. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this in any part-(a) addressing-mode question.
> 2. **Confusing MOVC and MOVX:** `MOVC` reads code memory (ROM, for tables); `MOVX` reads external data memory (off-chip RAM). Mixing them up in an indexed-mode question costs full sub-part marks.
> 3. **Stack imbalance:** Forgetting to POP after PUSH (or popping in the wrong order) inside a subroutine is a **2-mark deduction** per mismatch. Always match the count.
> 4. **SP not re-initialized:** If your program uses the stack but does not contain `MOV SP, #60H` early in `MAIN`, you lose **1 mark** in viva — the default 07H will collide with Register Bank 0.
> 5. **Carry-flag logic error:** In multi-byte addition, students often check `JC` *after* `ADD` using the wrong condition. Remember: `JC` triggers when carry **is set**, i.e., the sum overflowed.
> 6. **CSEG/ORG mistakes:** `ORG 0000H` is the **reset vector**; placing main code there without an `LJMP MAIN` causes the 8051 to misinterpret interrupt vector bytes as opcodes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Bullet Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

- **Five addressing modes of 8051** (memorize in order): **Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed**.
- **The `#` symbol** distinguishes immediate data from a direct address.
- **The `@` symbol** denotes indirection — register contains an *address*, not data.
- **MOVC** = code memory (program ROM); **MOVX** = external data memory.
- **Direct addressing is mandatory** for all SFRs (P0–P3, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, etc.).
- **Only R0 and R1** can be used as pointer registers in register-indirect mode for internal RAM; **DPTR** is used for 16-bit external access.
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** resets to **07H**; always reinitialize to **60H** for safety.
- **PUSH increments SP, POP decrements SP**; PUSH/POP must be in **LIFO order**.
- **LCALL pushes 16-bit PC** (high byte first, then low byte); **RET pops in reverse**.
- **Carry flag (CY)** is set whenever an arithmetic result exceeds 8-bit range (0–255) or when borrowing occurs in subtraction.
- **CJNE sets CY=1** if the first operand is **less than** the second — a common trick to find max/min.
- **DJNZ** is a single-instruction loop that combines decrement and conditional jump — saves bytes versus separate `DEC` + `JZ`.
- **SJMP** uses an 8-bit signed relative offset (range: -128 to +127 bytes from next instruction).
- **AJMP** uses an 11-bit absolute address (range: 2 KB page); **LJMP** uses a full 16-bit address (range: 64 KB).
- **12 MHz crystal → 1 µs machine cycle** — base for all KTU delay-calculation questions.
- **END directive** must be the last line; **ORG** sets the location counter; both are **assembler directives**, not 8051 instructions.
- **ACALL** can only call subroutines within a 2 KB page; **LCALL** can call anywhere in 64 KB.

# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: 8051 MICROCONTROLLER – PERIPHERAL INTERFACING & COMMUNICATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Timers and Counters

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
The 8051 microcontroller contains **two 16-bit programmable timers/counters**, namely **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**, which can be used to generate precise time delays or count external events. They are accessed via the Special Function Registers (SFRs) **TMOD** (Timer Mode) and **TCON** (Timer Control), with the actual count stored in high-byte (**TH0/TH1**) and low-byte (**TL0/TL1**) register pairs.

> [!NOTE]
> **CORE EXAMINATION FACT:** The 8051 has **ONLY 2 timers** (Timer 0 and Timer 1). Timer 2 is available only in the 8052/8032 derivatives. KTU exams frequently test this distinction.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of a timer as an **egg-timer in your kitchen**. You set a duration (say 3 minutes) by twisting the dial. The timer then counts down and rings a bell. The 8051 timer works similarly — you load a value, it counts machine cycles, and "rings a bell" (sets an overflow flag) when the count reaches zero. A **counter** is the same hardware, but instead of counting internal clock pulses, it counts **external events** (like people entering a room through a door sensor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Magic Number:** For a **12 MHz crystal oscillator**, one machine cycle = **1 µs** (microsecond). This is the foundation of ALL timer delay calculations in 8051 programming.

---

### 1.2 Interrupts

**Formal Definition:**
An **interrupt** is an external or internal event that temporarily halts the main program execution so that the microcontroller can attend to a more urgent task. Upon completion of the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, the processor returns to the main program using the **RETI** instruction. The 8051 provides **5 interrupt sources**: Reset, Two Hardware Interrupts (`INT0`, `INT1`), Two Timer Interrupts (TF0, TF1), and one Serial Port Interrupt (TI/RI).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine you are studying (main program) and your doorbell rings (interrupt). You pause your study, open the door (ISR executes), then return to your book exactly where you left it (RETI returns). The 8051 has **5 such doorbells**, each with a different priority level.

> [!NOTE]
> **PRIORITY ORDER (Highest → Lowest):** Reset → `INT0` → TF0 → `INT1` → TF1 → Serial Port (TI/RI). This is encoded in the **IP (Interrupt Priority)** and **IE (Interrupt Enable)** SFRs.

---

### 1.3 Seven Segment Display (SSD) Interfacing

**Formal Definition:**
A **Seven Segment Display** is an electronic display device consisting of **seven LEDs** (labeled **a, b, c, d, e, f, g**) arranged in the shape of the digit **8**, plus an optional **dp (decimal point)**. It exists in two configurations: **Common Anode (CA)** and **Common Cathode (CC)**. Only one segment type is active at a time, requiring **current limiting resistors** (~220 Ω to 330 Ω).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
The seven-segment display is like a **digital clock display**. Each digit (0–9) is formed by turning on/off a specific combination of the 7 segments. For example, the digit "0" lights up segments a, b, c, d, e, f (but NOT g).

---

### 1.4 LCD Interfacing (16x2 Alphanumeric LCD)

**Formal Definition:**
The **HD44780-based 16x2 LCD** is a flat-panel display module capable of displaying **16 characters per line across 2 lines**, totaling **32 characters**. It uses an 8-bit (or 4-bit) parallel data interface and is controlled via **3 control lines** (RS, RW, EN) and **8 data lines** (D0–D7).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of the LCD as a **small billboard controlled by the microcontroller**. You send it a "command" (like "clear screen" or "move cursor to position 5") or "data" (the actual character to display). The **Enable (EN) pin** acts like a "latch" — only when EN is pulsed from high-to-low, the LCD accepts whatever was on the data lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LCD COMMAND CODES TO MEMORIZE:**
> - `0x01` → Clear Display
> - `0x0E` → Display ON, Cursor ON
> - `0x0C` → Display ON, Cursor OFF
> - `0x38` → 8-bit, 2-line, 5x7 font
> - `0x80` → Force cursor to beginning of Line 1
> - `0xC0` → Force cursor to beginning of Line 2

---

### 1.5 Stepper Motor Control

**Formal Definition:**
A **stepper motor** is a brushless DC electric motor that divides a full rotation into a number of equal **steps**. The motor's position can be controlled precisely without any feedback mechanism (open-loop control). Common types are **unipolar** (5-wire or 6-wire) and **bipolar** (4-wire). The 8051 controls it through a sequence of energizing its 4 (or more) coils, typically using a **ULN2003 driver IC** for current amplification.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A stepper motor is like a **clock's second hand** — it moves in precise, discrete steps (e.g., 1.8° per step = 200 steps per full revolution). You tell it to take 100 steps, and it rotates exactly 180°. No feedback needed!

---

### 1.6 DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)

**Formal Definition:**
A **DAC** converts discrete digital binary numbers into a continuous analog voltage (or current) proportional to the input. The **DAC0808** is an 8-bit DAC commonly interfaced with 8051. The output voltage is given by:

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \frac{D}{256}$$

where **D** is the digital input (0 to 255) and **V_ref** is a reference voltage (typically **+5V** or **+10V**).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A DAC is like a **light dimmer controlled digitally**. Send binary `00000000` → light is OFF. Send `11111111` → light is at full brightness. Any value in between gives a proportional brightness.

---

### 1.7 8051 Serial Communication – Basics

**Formal Definition:**
**Serial communication** is the process of sending data **one bit at a time** over a single communication line, as opposed to parallel communication which sends multiple bits simultaneously. The 8051's built-in **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** supports full-duplex communication and is configured via the **SCON** (Serial Control) SFR and **PCON** (Power Control) registers, with baud rate generated by **Timer 1**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a **narrow tunnel** through which cars (bits) must pass one at a time (serial). A **wide highway** with 8 lanes would allow 8 cars side-by-side (parallel). Serial is slower but uses far fewer wires — perfect for long-distance communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY TERMS TO MEMORIZE:**
> - **Baud Rate:** Number of signal changes per second (bits per second for binary). Common values: 1200, 2400, 9600, 19200, 115200.
> - **Asynchronous:** No shared clock; both ends agree on baud rate beforehand.
> - **Full-Duplex:** Can send AND receive simultaneously.
> - **Frame Format (11 bits):** Start bit (0) + 8 data bits + Stop bit (1) = **10 bits** in Mode 1 (or 11 with TB8 in Mode 2/3).

---

### 1.8 RS-232 Standard

**Formal Definition:**
**RS-232 (Recommended Standard 232)** is a legacy standard introduced in **1960 by the EIA (Electronic Industries Alliance)** for serial binary single-ended data and control signals between a **DTE (Data Terminal Equipment)** and a **DCE (Data Communication Equipment)**. It defines voltage levels, signal names, pin assignments, and connector types (typically **DB-9 or DB-25**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VOLTAGE LEVELS:** RS-232 uses **±3V to ±25V** logic (typically **±12V**). A voltage between **+3V to +25V = Logic 0 (Space)** and between **-3V to -25V = Logic 1 (Mark)**. This is the **INVERTED** logic of TTL (0V to 5V). Therefore, a **MAX232 level converter IC** is required between the 8051 (TTL 0–5V) and the PC (RS-232 ±12V).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
RS-232 is like a **language translator** between two people speaking different "voltage dialects". The 8051 speaks TTL (0–5V), the PC speaks RS-232 (±12V), and the **MAX232 IC** translates between them.

---

### 1.9 9-Pin RS-232 (DB-9) Signals

**Formal Definition:**
The **DB-9 connector** is a 9-pin D-subminiature connector used for RS-232 serial communication. Each pin has a specific function defined by the standard. The most common signals are **TD (Transmit Data)**, **RD (Receive Data)**, and **GND (Signal Ground)**.

> [!NOTE]
> For full pin details, see Section 4 (Diagrams).

---

### 1.10 Simple Serial Port Programming

**Formal Definition:**
Programming the 8051's serial port involves: (1) Setting the **SMOD bit** in PCON for baud rate doubling, (2) Configuring **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** as the baud rate generator, (3) Loading **TH1** with the appropriate reload value, (4) Setting the **SCON register** to Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud rate), and (5) Using **SBUF** to transmit/receive data byte-by-byte. The **TI (Transmit Interrupt)** and **RI (Receive Interrupt)** flags must be cleared in software.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Timer/Counter Operational Modes

The 8051 timers operate in **4 modes** (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3) controlled by the **M1** and **M0** bits in the **TMOD** register.

| Mode | M1 | M0 | Description | Range | KTU Use Case |
|:----:|:--:|:--:|:------------|:-----:|:------------:|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 13-bit Timer (8048 legacy) | 0 to 8191 | Rarely used |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | **16-bit Timer** | 0 to 65535 | **Most common** (long delays) |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | **8-bit Auto-Reload** | 0 to 255 | **Baud rate generation** |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | Split Timer (Timer 0 only) | 8-bit × 2 | Specialized |

> [!NOTE]
> **TMOD Register:** Timer 1 occupies the **upper nibble (bits 7–4)**, Timer 0 occupies the **lower nibble (bits 3–0)**. Bits: **GATE, C/T̄, M1, M0** for each timer.

### Why Mode 1 for Delays?
Mode 1 gives a **full 16-bit range (0 to 65535)**, allowing maximum delay flexibility. When the count **overflows from 65535 to 65536**, the **TF (Timer Flag)** is set, triggering an interrupt (if enabled).

---

## 2.2 Timer Delay Calculation Formula (HIGH-YIELD!)

**For a 12 MHz crystal oscillator:**

$$\text{Machine Cycle Frequency} = \frac{\text{Oscillator Frequency}}{12} = \frac{12 \text{ MHz}}{12} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

$$\text{Machine Cycle Period} = \frac{1}{1 \text{ MHz}} = 1 \text{ μs}$$

$$\text{Delay} = (65536 - \text{Timer Value}) \times 1 \text{ μs}$$

**General formula (any crystal frequency f_osc):**

$$\text{Delay} = (65536 - \text{Timer Value}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

### Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem):
> *"Generate a 50 ms delay using Timer 0 in Mode 1 with a 12 MHz crystal."*

**Solution Steps:**
1. Convert delay to µs: 50 ms = 50,000 µs
2. Timer value to load = 65536 − 50,000 = **15,536** = **0x3CB0**
3. Therefore: **TH0 = 0x3C**, **TL0 = 0xB0**

---

## 2.3 Interrupt Vector Addresses (MEMORIZE!)

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Priority (Default) |
|:---------:|:----:|:--------------:|:------------------:|
| **Reset** | — | **0x0000** | Highest (0) |
| **External Int 0** | IE0 | **0x0003** | 1 |
| **Timer 0 Overflow** | TF0 | **0x000B** | 2 |
| **External Int 1** | IE1 | **0x0013** | 3 |
| **Timer 1 Overflow** | TF1 | **0x001B** | 4 |
| **Serial Port** | TI/RI | **0x0023** | Lowest (5) |

---

## 2.4 Seven Segment Display Encoding Table

| Digit | g | f | e | d | c | b | a | Hex (CC) | Hex (CA) |
|:-----:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:--------:|:--------:|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **0x3F** | **0xC0** |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **0x06** | **0xF9** |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **0x5B** | **0xA4** |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **0x4F** | **0xB0** |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **0x66** | **0x99** |
| 5 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **0x6D** | **0x92** |
| 6 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **0x7D** | **0x82** |
| 7 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **0x07** | **0xF8** |
| 8 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **0x7F** | **0x80** |
| 9 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **0x6F** | **0x90** |

> [!NOTE]
> **CC = Common Cathode** (active HIGH, 0 lights ON) → **CA = Common Anode** (active LOW, 1 lights ON). For CC, simply take CA value and **invert all bits** (XOR with 0xFF).

---

## 2.5 Stepper Motor Step Sequence Tables

**Full-Step Sequence (4 coils, unipolar, 5.625°/step = 64 steps/rev):**

| Step | P1.3 (D) | P1.2 (C) | P1.1 (B) | P1.0 (A) | Hex |
|:----:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---:|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x01 |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x02 |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x04 |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x08 |

**Full-Step Sequence (Wave Drive, one coil at a time):** Same as above.

**Full-Step Sequence (Full Torque / Two-Phase On):**

| Step | P1.3 (D) | P1.2 (C) | P1.1 (B) | P1.0 (A) | Hex |
|:----:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|:--------:|:---:|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x03 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0C |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x09 |

**Half-Step Sequence (8 steps, finer resolution):** Alternates between wave and full-torque patterns. 5.625° × 2 = **2.8125° per half-step** → 128 half-steps/revolution.

---

## 2.6 DAC Output Voltage Formula

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \frac{D}{2^n}}$$

where:
- $V_{out}$ = Analog output voltage
- $V_{ref}$ = Reference voltage (typically **+5V** for 8051 systems)
- $D$ = Digital input (decimal equivalent of binary input)
- $n$ = Number of bits (n = **8** for DAC0808)
- $2^n$ = **256** for 8-bit DAC

### DAC0808 Resolution:

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{255}$$

For V_ref = 5V: Resolution = 5/255 ≈ **19.61 mV per LSB**.

---

## 2.7 UART Frame Format (Mode 1)

```
|<- Start ->|<---- 8 Data Bits (LSB first) ---->|  <-  ->|
|     0     | D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7            |   1    |
```

- **Total frame width:** 10 bits (1 start + 8 data + 1 stop)
- **SBUF:** Two physically separate registers (one for transmit, one for receive) sharing the same address (**0x99**)
- **Baud Rate Source:** Timer 1 overflow (when SM0=0, SM1=1 in SCON)

---

## 2.8 SCON Register Bit Map (CRITICAL FOR EXAMS)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Name | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** |

> [!NOTE]
> **SM0=0, SM1=1** → **Mode 1** (8-bit UART, variable baud rate) — most common.
> **REN=1** → Enable Reception. **TI=1** → Transmission complete. **RI=1** → Reception complete.

---

## 2.9 TMOD Register Bit Map

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Timer | \multicolumn{4}{c}{Timer 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{Timer 0} | |
| Name | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** |

- **GATE = 1:** Hardware control (INTx pin must be HIGH for timer to run)
- **C/T̄ = 0:** Timer mode (internal clock) | **C/T̄ = 1:** Counter mode (external pin T0/T1)
- **M1, M0:** Mode selection bits

---

## 2.10 Baud Rate Calculation (HIGH-YIELD FORMULA!)

For **Mode 1** with **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)**:

$$\boxed{\text{TH1} = 256 - \left\lfloor \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}} \right\rfloor}$$

### Special Case: SMOD = 1 (PCON.7 = 1)

$$\boxed{\text{TH1} = 256 - \left\lfloor \frac{f_{osc}}{192 \times \text{Baud Rate}} \right\rfloor}$$

### Common Values for 11.0592 MHz Crystal:

| Baud Rate | TH1 Value (SMOD=0) | TH1 Value (SMOD=1) | % Error |
|:---------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|:-------:|
| 1200 | 0xE8 (232) | 0xF4 (244) | 0% |
| 2400 | 0xF4 (244) | 0xFA (250) | 0% |
| 9600 | 0xFA (250) | 0xFD (253) | 0% |
| 19200 | 0xFD (253) | 0xFE (254) | 0% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WHY 11.0592 MHz?** This crystal frequency is used because it is a multiple of **standard baud rates × 12 × integer**, resulting in **exactly 0% error** in baud rate generation. The 12 MHz crystal gives small errors (e.g., ~8.5% for 9600 baud).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Timer Delay Calculation for 50 ms Delay

**Problem Statement:** Generate a 50 ms delay using Timer 0, Mode 1, with a 12 MHz crystal.

### Step 1: Calculate Machine Cycle Period
The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to produce the machine cycle frequency.

$$f_{MC} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{12 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}{12} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Machine Cycle Time (Period)
This is the time for one count increment in the timer.

$$T_{MC} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ μs}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Number of Timer Counts Required
Total delay required = 50 ms = 50,000 µs.

$$N = \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ μs}}{1 \text{ μs}} = 50{,}000 \text{ counts}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Timer Initial Value
The 8051 timer counts UP from the loaded value to 65536 (overflow in Mode 1).

$$\text{Timer Value} = 65536 - N = 65536 - 50000 = 15536$$

### Step 5: Convert to Hexadecimal

$$15536 \div 16 = 971 \text{ remainder } 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{LSB} = 0$$

$$971 \div 16 = 60 \text{ remainder } 11 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Next} = \text{B}$$

$$60 \div 16 = 3 \text{ remainder } 12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Next} = \text{C}$$

$$3 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{MSB} = 3$$

$$\therefore 15536_{10} = 3CB0_{16}$$

### Step 6: Split into TH0 and TL0

$$\text{TH0} = 0x3C = 60 \quad \text{(High Byte)}$$

$$\text{TL0} = 0xB0 = 176 \quad \text{(Low Byte)}$$

### Step 7: Verification

$$(65536 - 15536) \times 1 \text{ μs} = 50000 \times 1 \text{ μs} = 50000 \text{ μs} = 50 \text{ ms} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Generating a Square Wave of 1 kHz Using Timer

**Problem:** Generate a 1 kHz square wave on P1.0 using Timer 0 (12 MHz crystal).

### Step 1: Determine Half-Period
For a 1 kHz square wave:

$$f = 1 \text{ kHz} = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

Half-period (HIGH or LOW) = T/2 = **500 µs**

### Step 2: Calculate Timer Counts

$$N = \frac{500 \text{ μs}}{1 \text{ μs}} = 500 \text{ counts}$$

### Step 3: Timer Value

$$\text{Timer Value} = 65536 - 500 = 65036$$

### Step 4: Convert to Hex

$$65036 \div 16 = 4064 \text{ remainder } 12 = \text{C}$$

$$4064 \div 16 = 254 \text{ remainder } 0 = 0$$

$$254 \div 16 = 15 \text{ remainder } 14 = \text{E}$$

$$15 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 15 = \text{F}$$

$$\therefore 65036_{10} = \text{FE0C}_{16}$$

### Step 5: Register Loads

$$\text{TH0} = 0xFE, \quad \text{TL0} = 0x0C$$

### Step 6: Algorithm
1. Set TMOD = 0x01 (Timer 0, Mode 1, software control)
2. Load TH0, TL0
3. Set TR0 = 1 (start timer)
4. Wait in loop: `while (TF0 == 0);`
5. Clear TF0
6. Toggle P1.0
7. Repeat steps 2–6

---

## 3.3 Derivation: DAC Ramp Waveform Generation

**Problem:** Generate a sawtooth (ramp) waveform using DAC0808 connected to 8051 Port 1, with V_ref = 5V.

### Step 1: Output Range
The DAC output spans from:

$$V_{out(min)} = 0 \text{ V} \quad (\text{when D} = 0)$$

$$V_{out(max)} = 5 \text{ V} \times \frac{255}{256} \approx 4.98 \text{ V} \quad (\text{when D} = 255)$$

### Step 2: Step Size (Resolution)

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{256} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{256} \approx 19.53 \text{ mV per step}$$

### Step 3: Ramp Frequency Calculation
If we use a delay `d` microseconds between each step (total 256 steps):

$$T_{ramp} = 256 \times d \quad \text{μs}$$

$$f_{ramp} = \frac{1}{T_{ramp}} = \frac{1}{256 \times d \times 10^{-6}} \text{ Hz}$$

For `d = 10 µs`:
$$T_{ramp} = 2560 \text{ μs} = 2.56 \text{ ms}$$
$$f_{ramp} \approx 390.6 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 4: Complete Algorithm Logic
1. Set Port 1 as output (P1 = 0x00)
2. For value = 0 to 255:
   - Output value to P1 (drives DAC)
   - Call delay subroutine (~10 µs)
3. Repeat (creates continuous ramp)

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Stepper Motor Angle-to-Step Conversion

**Problem:** Rotate a stepper motor (5.625°/step) by 90° clockwise.

### Step 1: Determine Number of Steps
Steps required = Angle / Step angle = 90° / 5.625° = **16 steps**

### Step 2: Determine Step Rate
For smooth rotation, a delay of ~50 ms between steps is typical.

### Step 3: Build Lookup Table
Store the full-step sequence (clockwise):
```
Step 1: 0x09  (1001 - coils A and D)
Step 2: 0x01  (0001 - coil A only - half step approach)
... OR ...
Step 1: 0x0C
Step 2: 0x06
Step 3: 0x03
Step 4: 0x09
```

### Step 4: Total Time for Rotation
Total time = 16 steps × 50 ms = **800 ms = 0.8 seconds**

---

## 3.5 Derivation: UART Transmission of a Character "A"

**Problem:** Transmit ASCII 'A' (0x41 = 0100 0001) using 8051 UART, Mode 1, 9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz crystal.

### Step 1: Configure Timer 1 for 9600 Baud
From the formula (SMOD = 0):

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \left\lfloor \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600} \right\rfloor = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD$$

### Step 2: Configure SCON for Mode 1
$$\text{SCON} = 0x50 \quad (\text{SM0=0, SM1=1, REN=1})$$

### Step 3: Start Timer 1
$$\text{TCON} = 0x40 \quad (\text{TR1=1})$$

### Step 4: Frame Construction (Mode 1, 10 bits total)
The character 0x41 is transmitted **LSB first** within a frame:

| Frame Position | Bit | Logic Level | Duration @ 9600 baud |
|:--------------:|:---:|:-----------:|:--------------------:|
| Start | 0 | LOW | 1/9600 s ≈ 104.17 µs |
| D0 | 1 | HIGH | 104.17 µs |
| D1 | 0 | LOW | 104.17 µs |
| D2 | 0 | LOW | 104.17 µs |
| D3 | 0 | LOW | 104.17 µs |
| D4 | 0 | LOW | 104.17 µs |
| D5 | 0 | LOW | 104.17 µs |
| D6 | 1 | HIGH | 104.17 µs |
| D7 | 0 | LOW | 104.17 µs |
| Stop | 1 | HIGH | 104.17 µs |

### Step 5: Total Frame Time
$$T_{frame} = 10 \times \frac{1}{9600} = 1.0417 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 6: The Three Lines of Code
```
MOV SBUF, #'A'    ; Load character into transmit buffer
JNB TI, $          ; Wait until TI flag is set (transmission complete)
CLR TI              ; Clear TI flag for next transmission
```

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Interfacing Modules

| Parameter | 7-Segment | LCD (16x2) | Stepper Motor | DAC0808 | UART |
|:---------:|:---------:|:----------:|:-------------:|:-------:|:----:|
| **Pins Used** | 7 (or 8 with dp) | 11 (8 data + 3 control) | 4 (via driver) | 8 (Port) | 2 (TX, RX) |
| **Type** | Output (LED) | Output (display) | Output (motor) | Output (analog) | I/O (serial) |
| **Refresh Needed?** | Yes (multiplex) | No | Yes (sequence) | No | No |
| **Response Time** | Immediate | ~1.5 ms per cmd | Mechanical delay | ~1 µs settling | 1.04 ms / char |
| **Driver Required** | Resistors | None (or potentiometer for contrast) | ULN2003 | Op-amp (I→V) | MAX232 |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | High | Very High | Medium | Very High |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Internal Architecture – Timer/Serial/Interrupt Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    osc[Crystal Oscillator<br/>11.0592 MHz / 12 MHz] --> mc[Machine Cycle Generator<br/>Divided by 12]
    mc --> t0[Timer 0<br/>TH0 + TL0]
    mc --> t1[Timer 1<br/>TH1 + TL1]
    t0 --> tmod[TMOD Register<br/>Mode Configuration]
    t1 --> tmod
    tmod --> tcon[TCON Register<br/>TR0, TR1, TF0, TF1]
    t1 --> baud[Baud Rate Generator<br/>for UART]
    baud --> scon[SCON Register<br/>SM0, SM1, REN, TI, RI]
    scon --> sbuf[SBUF Register<br/>Shared TX/RX buffer]
    sbuf --> txpin[TXD Pin - P3.1]
    sbuf --> rxpin[RXD Pin - P3.0]
    int0[INT0 Pin P3.2] --> ie[IE Register<br/>Interrupt Enable]
    int1[INT1 Pin P3.3] --> ie
    tcon --> ip[IP Register<br/>Interrupt Priority]
    ie --> ip
    ip --> cpu[CPU Execution Unit<br/>Program Counter jumps to vector]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Crystal oscillator feeds the machine cycle generator, which clocks both timers. Timer 1's overflow feeds the baud rate generator, which drives the UART subsystem (SCON + SBUF + TXD/RXD). External interrupt pins (INT0, INT1) join with timer overflow flags and serial port interrupts at the IE (Interrupt Enable) register, which is prioritized by the IP register before reaching the CPU.

---

## 4.2 Seven-Segment Display Segment Identification

```mermaid
graph TD
    segA["Segment a - TOP"]:::top
    segB["Segment b - TOP RIGHT"]:::tr
    segC["Segment c - BOTTOM RIGHT"]:::br
    segD["Segment d - BOTTOM"]:::bot
    segE["Segment e - BOTTOM LEFT"]:::bl
    segF["Segment f - TOP LEFT"]:::tl
    segG["Segment g - MIDDLE"]:::mid
    segDP["Segment dp - DECIMAL POINT"]:::dp
    
    segA --- segB
    segB --- segC
    segC --- segD
    segD --- segE
    segE --- segF
    segF --- segA
    segA --- segG
    segB --- segG
    segC --- segG
    segD --- segG
    segE --- segG
    segF --- segG
    segG --- segDP
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each segment is an individual LED. The "8" shape is formed when ALL segments (a through g) are lit. To display "0", light a, b, c, d, e, f (all except g). Common Cathode: 0x3F. Common Anode: 0xC0.

---

## 4.3 8051 to LCD (HD44780) Interfacing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mcu["8051 Microcontroller"] -->|"P0.0-P0.7<br/>8-bit Data Bus"| lcddata["LCD Data Lines<br/>D0 - D7"]
    mcu -->|"P2.0 = RS"| rs["RS Pin<br/>0=Command, 1=Data"]
    mcu -->|"P2.1 = RW"| rw["RW Pin<br/>0=Write, 1=Read"]
    mcu -->|"P2.2 = EN"| en["EN Pin<br/>HIGH-to-LOW pulse latches data"]
    rs --> hd44780["HD44780 Controller<br/>Character ROM + DDRAM"]
    rw --> hd44780
    en --> hd44780
    lcddata --> hd44780
    hd44780 -->|"Controls"| lcdmatrix["16x2 Dot Matrix Display"]
    vcc["+5V"] --> contrast["10K Potentiometer<br/>Contrast Adjust"] --> vee["VEE Pin<br/>Pin 3"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 sends either a **command** (RS=0) to configure the LCD, or **data** (RS=1) to display a character. The EN pin must be **toggled high-then-low** to latch the data into the LCD's internal controller.

---

## 4.4 Stepper Motor Interfacing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mcu8051["8051 Port 1<br/>P1.0 - P1.3"] --> uln["ULN2003<br/>Darlington Driver Array<br/>Current Amplifier"]
    uln -->|"High Current<br/>~500mA per coil"| coilA["Coil A"]
    uln --> coilB["Coil B"]
    uln --> coilC["Coil C"]
    uln --> coilD["Coil D"]
    coilA --> motor["Unipolar Stepper Motor<br/>5.625 degree per step<br/>64 steps per revolution"]
    coilB --> motor
    coilC --> motor
    coilD --> motor
    motor --> shaft["Output Shaft<br/>Rotates in discrete steps"]
    psu["+5V / +12V DC"] --> motor
```

> [!NOTE]
> The ULN2003 acts as a **current amplifier**. The 8051's Port 1 pins can only source ~20 mA, but stepper motor coils need hundreds of mA. ULN2003 boosts this safely.

---

## 4.5 DAC0808 Interfacing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    port1["8051 Port 1<br/>P1.0 - P1.7<br/>8-bit Digital Input"] --> dac0808["DAC0808<br/>8-bit DAC<br/>R-2R Ladder Network"]
    vref["Vref +5V or +10V"] --> dac0808
    dac0808 -->|"Iout (Current)"| opamp["Op-Amp LM741<br/>Current to Voltage Converter"]
    opamp --> vout["Vout<br/>Analog Voltage 0 to Vref"]
    gnd["Ground"] --> dac0808
    gnd --> opamp
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** DAC0808 produces a **current output (Iout)**, not a voltage. An op-amp configured as a current-to-voltage converter is required to produce a usable voltage output. The formula $V_{out} = V_{ref} \times (D / 256)$ applies after the op-amp stage.

---

## 4.6 8051 UART – RS-232 Communication Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC as Personal Computer<br/>DB-9 Serial Port
    participant MAX as MAX232<br/>Level Converter IC
    participant MCU as 8051 Microcontroller
    participant App as User Application
    
    PC->>MAX: RS-232 voltage (+/-12V)<br/>on TX/RX lines
    MAX->>MCU: TTL voltage (0-5V)<br/>translated logic levels
    MCU->>MCU: Receive character into SBUF<br/>Set RI flag
    MCU->>App: Trigger interrupt (if enabled)<br/>or poll RI bit
    App->>MCU: Read SBUF to get received byte
    App->>App: Process character<br/>(display, store, etc.)
    App->>MCU: Write byte to SBUF<br/>for transmission
    MCU->>MAX: TTL signal on TXD pin
    MAX->>PC: Convert to RS-232 levels<br/>(+/-12V)
    PC->>PC: PC application receives<br/>character via COM port
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The MAX232 IC is ESSENTIAL.** Without it, the ±12V RS-232 signals from the PC will **damage** the 8051's 5V-tolerant input pins. The MAX232 contains internal charge pumps to generate the required voltages from a single +5V supply.

---

## 4.7 DB-9 RS-232 Connector Pin Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DB9["DB-9 Male Connector (Front View)"]
        pin1["Pin 1: DCD<br/>Data Carrier Detect"]:::pin
        pin2["Pin 2: RXD<br/>Received Data"]:::pinRX
        pin3["Pin 3: TXD<br/>Transmitted Data"]:::pinTX
        pin4["Pin 4: DTR<br/>Data Terminal Ready"]:::pin
        pin5["Pin 5: GND<br/>Signal Ground"]:::pinGND
        pin6["Pin 6: DSR<br/>Data Set Ready"]:::pin
        pin7["Pin 7: RTS<br/>Request To Send"]:::pin
        pin8["Pin 8: CTS<br/>Clear To Send"]:::pin
        pin9["Pin 9: RI<br/>Ring Indicator"]:::pin
    end
    
    pin1 --- pin2
    pin2 --- pin3
    pin3 --- pin4
    pin4 --- pin5
    pin5 --- pin6
    pin6 --- pin7
    pin7 --- pin8
    pin8 --- pin9
    
    classDef pin fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    classDef pinRX fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    classDef pinTX fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    classDef pinGND fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Connection for 8051-PC Communication:** Only **3 wires** are needed: **Pin 2 (RXD)**, **Pin 3 (TXD)**, and **Pin 5 (GND)**. Note that on a **straight-through cable**, the TXD of one device connects to the RXD of the other.

---

## 4.8 Interrupt Processing Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Main Program Running]) --> checkInt{Interrupt<br/>Occurred?}
    checkInt -->|No| continue[Continue Main Program]
    continue --> checkInt
    checkInt -->|Yes| saveState[Push Current PC onto Stack<br/>Save PSW if needed]
    saveState --> lookup[CPU Looks Up Vector Address<br/>from Interrupt Source]
    lookup --> jump[Jump to ISR<br/>Interrupt Service Routine]
    jump --> execute[Execute ISR Code]
    execute --> reti[Execute RETI Instruction<br/>Pop PC from Stack]
    reti --> back[Resume Main Program<br/>from where it was paused]
    back --> checkInt
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **RETI** instruction is special. Unlike a normal **RET**, it also signals the interrupt controller that the ISR has completed, allowing nested or subsequent interrupts of the same priority level to be processed.

---

## 4.9 Complete 8051 Peripheral Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    central["8051 Microcontroller<br/>CPU + RAM + ROM + SFRs"]:::mcu
    
    central -->|"P0, P1, P2, P3"| peripherals["Peripheral Subsystems"]
    
    peripherals --> timerBlock["Timers/Counters<br/>Timer 0 and Timer 1<br/>TH0/TL0, TH1/TL1"]
    peripherals --> intBlock["Interrupt Controller<br/>5 Sources<br/>IE and IP Registers"]
    peripherals --> ssdBlock["7-Segment Display<br/>via P1 with Resistors"]
    peripherals --> lcdBlock["LCD 16x2<br/>via P0 + P2 Control"]
    peripherals --> stepperBlock["Stepper Motor<br/>via ULN2003 Driver"]
    peripherals --> dacBlock["DAC0808<br/>via P1 + Op-Amp"]
    peripherals --> uartBlock["UART<br/>via TXD/RXD + MAX232"]
    
    uartBlock --> pc["PC COM Port<br/>or External Device"]
    
    classDef mcu fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:3px
```
