# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: 7-Segment Display Encoding** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question:** *Design a lookup table to display digits 0 to 9 on a common cathode seven-segment display. Show the encoding for digit 5 and explain the polarity of segment activation.*

**Model Answer:**

A common cathode seven-segment display has all cathodes tied to ground. To light a segment, we apply a **HIGH (1)** signal. The bit assignment is: bit 0 = segment a, bit 1 = segment b, bit 2 = segment c, bit 3 = segment d, bit 4 = segment e, bit 5 = segment f, bit 6 = segment g.

For **digit 5**, the segments a, c, d, f, g must glow; segments b and e remain OFF.

| Bit Position | 6 (g) | 5 (f) | 4 (e) | 3 (d) | 2 (c) | 1 (b) | 0 (a) |
|:------------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| Segment State | ON (1) | ON (1) | OFF (0) | ON (1) | ON (1) | OFF (0) | ON (1) |

Binary: `1101101` → Hex: **0x6D** (or decimal 109).

> **Polarity:** Common Cathode is **active HIGH** (1 lights the segment). Common Anode would be **active LOW** (0 lights the segment), giving the inverted pattern: `0x92` for digit 5.

---

### **Question 2: RS-232 Voltage Levels** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Question:** *Explain why a MAX232 IC is required when interfacing an 8051 microcontroller with a PC via the serial port. State the voltage levels used in TTL and RS-232.*

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 operates on **TTL voltage levels**:
- Logic 0: **0V to 0.8V**
- Logic 1: **2.0V to 5.0V**

The PC's serial port follows the **RS-232 standard**:
- Logic 0 (Space): **+3V to +25V** (typically +12V)
- Logic 1 (Mark): **-3V to -25V** (typically -12V)

> **The Problem:** RS-232 uses **inverted polarity** and **much higher voltages** (±12V) than TTL (0–5V). Directly connecting the two will result in:
> 1. **Logic inversion** — the receiver will see the opposite of what was sent.
> 2. **Hardware damage** — applying +12V to a 5V TTL input will destroy the microcontroller.

> **The Solution:** The **MAX232 IC** is a **dual-driver/receiver** that contains internal **charge pump capacitors** to generate ±10V from a single +5V supply. It converts TTL signals to RS-232 levels and vice versa, ensuring safe and correct communication.

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Serial Communication Programming** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the SCON register of the 8051 microcontroller with its bit definitions. Configure the 8051 for serial communication in Mode 1 with a 9600 baud rate using an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Show the initialization sequence.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Write an 8051 assembly language program to transmit the string "KTU" continuously to a PC via the serial port.*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**SCON Register (Serial Control, Address 0x98, Bit-Addressable):**

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|:---:|:------:|:---------|
| 7 | **SM0** | Serial Mode bit 0 |
| 6 | **SM1** | Serial Mode bit 1 |
| 5 | **SM2** | Multiprocessor communication enable (0 for Mode 1) |
| 4 | **REN** | Reception Enable (1 = enable serial reception) |
| 3 | **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit (used in Modes 2 & 3) |
| 2 | **RB8** | 9th received bit (used in Modes 2 & 3) |
| 1 | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag (set when transmission complete) |
| 0 | **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag (set when reception complete) |

**Mode 1 selection:** SM0 = 0, SM1 = 1 → 8-bit UART with variable baud rate (Timer 1 overflow).

**SCON value for Mode 1 with reception enabled:** `0b01010000` = **0x50**

**Baud Rate Calculation:**
For 9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz crystal, SMOD = 0:

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \left\lfloor \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600} \right\rfloor = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD$$

**TMOD value:** Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), software control, timer mode → `0b00100000` = **0x20**

**Initialization Sequence:**
```
        MOV SCON, #50H    ; Mode 1, REN=1 (enable reception)
        MOV TMOD, #20H    ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
        MOV TH1, #0FDH    ; 9600 baud rate
        MOV PCON, #00H    ; SMOD=0 (no baud rate doubling)
        SETB TR1          ; Start Timer 1
```

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Complete Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV SCON, #50H      ; Initialize serial port (Mode 1, REN=1)
        MOV TMOD, #20H      ; Timer 1, Mode 2
        MOV TH1, #0FDH      ; 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
        SETB TR1            ; Start Timer 1
AGAIN:  MOV DPTR, #MYDATA   ; Point DPTR to string in code memory
NEXT:   CLR A
        MOVC A, @A+DPTR     ; Read character from code ROM
        JZ FINISH           ; If null terminator, stop
        ACALL TRANS         ; Otherwise, transmit it
        INC DPTR            ; Move to next character
        SJMP NEXT           ; Repeat
FINISH: SJMP AGAIN          ; Loop forever (continuous transmission)

; ----- Transmit Subroutine -----
TRANS:  MOV SBUF, A         ; Load character into serial buffer
WAIT:   JNB TI, WAIT        ; Wait until TI flag set (transmission done)
        CLR TI              ; Clear TI for next transmission
        RET

; ----- String Data (stored in code memory) -----
MYDATA: DB 'K', 'T', 'U', 00H   ; Null-terminated string
        END
```

**Execution Explanation:**
1. The main program initializes the UART and then loops continuously.
2. Each character is fetched from code memory using the `MOVC` instruction.
3. The `TRANS` subroutine waits for the previous transmission to complete (polling TI flag), then loads the next character into SBUF.
4. The `00H` null terminator signals the end of the string.

---

### **Question B: Timer and Interrupt Programming** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the TMOD and TCON registers of the 8051. Calculate the value to be loaded into TH0 and TL0 to generate a delay of 25 ms using Timer 0 in Mode 1 with a 12 MHz crystal.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Write an 8051 assembly program to toggle all bits of Port 1 every 25 ms using Timer 0 overflow interrupt. List the interrupt vector address.*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**TMOD Register (Timer Mode, Address 0x89, NOT bit-addressable):**

For Timer 0 (lower nibble of TMOD):
- **Bit 3 (GATE):** 0 = software control via TR0
- **Bit 2 (C/T̄):** 0 = timer mode (internal clock)
- **Bits 1, 0 (M1, M0):** 01 = Mode 1 (16-bit timer)

For Timer 0, Mode 1, software control, timer mode: `0b00000001` → **TMOD = 0x01**

**TCON Register (Timer Control, Address 0x88, Bit-addressable):**

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|:---:|:------:|:---------|
| 7 | **TF1** | Timer 1 overflow flag (set when Timer 1 overflows) |
| 6 | **TR1** | Timer 1 run control bit (1 = start Timer 1) |
| 5 | **TF0** | Timer 0 overflow flag (set when Timer 0 overflows) |
| 4 | **TR0** | Timer 0 run control bit (1 = start Timer 0) |
| 3 | **IE1** | External Interrupt 1 edge flag |
| 2 | **IT1** | External Interrupt 1 type (0 = level, 1 = edge) |
| 1 | **IE0** | External Interrupt 0 edge flag |
| 0 | **IT0** | External Interrupt 0 type (0 = level, 1 = edge) |

**Delay Calculation (25 ms, Mode 1, 12 MHz):**

$$N = \frac{25{,}000 \text{ μs}}{1 \text{ μs}} = 25{,}000 \text{ counts}$$

$$\text{Timer Value} = 65536 - 25000 = 40536$$

Converting 40536 to hex:
$$40536 \div 16 = 2533 \text{ r } 8$$
$$2533 \div 16 = 158 \text{ r } 5$$
$$158 \div 16 = 9 \text{ r } 14 = \text{E}$$
$$9 \div 16 = 0 \text{ r } 9$$

$$40536_{10} = 9E58_{16}$$

Therefore: **TH0 = 0x9E**, **TL0 = 0x58**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Interrupt Vector Address:** Timer 0 interrupt vector is at **0x000B** in code memory.

**Complete Assembly Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 000BH              ; Timer 0 interrupt vector
        LJMP ISR_T0            ; Jump to ISR

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H        ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
        MOV TH0, #9EH         ; Load high byte
        MOV TL0, #58H         ; Load low byte
        MOV IE, #82H          ; EA=1, ET0=1 (enable Timer 0 interrupt)
        SETB TR0              ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   SJMP HERE             ; Main program idle loop (waiting)

; ----- Timer 0 ISR -----
ISR_T0: MOV TH0, #9EH        ; Reload timer for next 25 ms
        MOV TL0, #58H
        CPL P1                ; Complement (toggle) all bits of P1
        RETI                  ; Return from interrupt
        END
```

**Program Explanation:**
1. **Main Program:** Configures Timer 0 in Mode 1, loads the calculated values, enables the Timer 0 interrupt, and then enters an infinite idle loop.
2. **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine):** Automatically executes every 25 ms. It reloads the timer (necessary in Mode 1, unlike Mode 2 which auto-reloads) and toggles Port 1.
3. **The result:** A perfect 50% duty cycle square wave appears on every pin of Port 1, with each pin toggling every 25 ms (giving a 50 ms full period, or 20 Hz).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Timer ISR Programming:**
> 1. **Forgetting to reload TH0/TL0 in Mode 1 ISR** — This is the #1 mistake. In Mode 1, the timer is 16-bit and does NOT auto-reload. If you forget to reload, the delay will be **65,536 counts** (~65.5 ms) instead of 25 ms.
> 2. **Not clearing the TF0 flag in software when polling** — In polling mode, you must manually clear TF0. In interrupt mode, the hardware clears it automatically, but you must still reload the timer.
> 3. **Wrong priority in IE register** — IE = 0x82 enables **EA (bit 7) and ET0 (bit 1)**. Using IE = 0x80 forgets to enable Timer 0 interrupt; using IE = 0x02 forgets to set the global enable.
> 4. **Confusing TMOD and TCON** — TMOD sets the **mode**; TCON **starts/stops** the timer (TR0) and indicates **overflow** (TF0). Both are required.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST – KTU Module Experiment 2**

### Timers/Counters
- [ ] **8051 has 2 timers** (T0, T1); each has **2 SFRs** (THx high byte, TLx low byte)
- [ ] **TMOD = 0x01** for Timer 0, Mode 1, software control, timer mode
- [ ] **TCON's TR0** starts Timer 0; **TF0** indicates overflow
- [ ] **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator cycles** (e.g., 12 MHz crystal → 1 µs machine cycle)
- [ ] Formula: **Timer Value = 65536 − (Delay / Machine Cycle Period)**
- [ ] **Mode 2 is for baud rate generation** (auto-reload 8-bit)
- [ ] **Mode 1 is for long delays** (16-bit, manual reload)

### Interrupts
- [ ] **5 interrupt sources**: Reset, INT0, TF0, INT1, TF1, Serial Port
- [ ] **Vector addresses**: 0000, 0003, 000B, 0013, 001B, 0023
- [ ] **EA** (IE.7) is the global enable; each interrupt has its own enable bit in IE
- [ ] **RETI** (not RET) must end every ISR; it pops PC AND signals interrupt completion
- [ ] **Priority is fixed by default**; can be changed via the IP register (only 2 levels: high/low)

### 7-Segment Display
- [ ] **Common Cathode (CC)** = active HIGH (0x3F for digit 0)
- [ ] **Common Anode (CA)** = active LOW (0xC0 for digit 0)
- [ ] **7 data bits** per digit (a–g); add 8th bit for decimal point
- [ ] Use **220–330 Ω current limiting resistors**
- [ ] For **multiplexed** displays, switch digits rapidly using BJT/MOSFET drivers

### LCD (HD44780)
- [ ] **3 control pins**: RS, R/W, EN
- [ ] **RS = 0** for command; **RS = 1** for data
- [ ] **EN high-to-low pulse** latches data
- [ ] Must wait **~1.5 ms after power-on** before sending commands
- [ ] **0x38** initializes 8-bit, 2-line, 5x7 font mode
- [ ] **0x80** sets cursor to line 1 start; **0xC0** sets cursor to line 2 start

### Stepper Motor
- [ ] **4 coils** in unipolar; energized **one at a time** (wave) or **two at a time** (full-torque)
- [ ] **ULN2003** driver IC required for current amplification
- [ ] Step angle × number of steps = total rotation angle
- [ ] For **clockwise** rotation, follow sequence forward; for **anticlockwise**, reverse it

### DAC
- [ ] **DAC0808** is 8-bit; output is **current** → needs **op-amp** for voltage
- [ ] **Resolution = Vref / 256** (e.g., 5V/256 ≈ 19.53 mV)
- [ ] Output ranges from **0 to Vref** (theoretical max is Vref × 255/256)

### Serial Communication
- [ ] **SCON = 0x50** for Mode 1, reception enabled
- [ ] **TMOD = 0x20** for Timer 1 in Mode 2 (baud rate generator)
- [ ] **TH1 = 0xFD** for 9600 baud with 11.0592 MHz crystal
- [ ] **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred (0% error on standard baud rates)
- [ ] **SBUF** is one logical address (0x99) but two physical registers (TX and RX)
- [ ] **TI flag** must be cleared in software; same for **RI**
- [ ] **MAX232 IC** is MANDATORY between 8051 and PC serial port

### RS-232
- [ ] **DB-9 has 9 pins**; only 3 are minimum: RXD (Pin 2), TXD (Pin 3), GND (Pin 5)
- [ ] **Voltage: ±3V to ±25V** (inverted logic vs TTL)
- [ ] **Logic 1 (Mark) = negative voltage; Logic 0 (Space) = positive voltage**
- [ ] **Asynchronous** — no shared clock; both sides must agree on baud rate
- [ ] **10-bit frame in Mode 1**: 1 start + 8 data (LSB first) + 1 stop

### KTU Practical Exam Tips
- [ ] **Draw a neat circuit diagram** before writing code (mandatory in lab exam)
- [ ] **Mention crystal frequency** explicitly in all calculations
- [ ] **Use comments** in assembly code (KTU awards marks for clarity)
- [ ] **Calculate hex values step-by-step** — don't skip the decimal-to-hex conversion
- [ ] **State vector addresses** for any interrupt used
- [ ] **Memorize the SCON, TMOD, IE, IP, TCON bit maps** — they are asked verbatim
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the ARM Architecture?

**ARM** stands for **Advanced RISC Machine** (originally **Acorn RISC Machine**). It is a family of **32-bit / 64-bit Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** processor architectures developed by **Arm Holdings** and licensed to hundreds of semiconductor vendors. The architecture is the de-facto standard for mobile, embedded, and IoT computing, and is the silicon brain behind billions of devices including smartphones, microcontrollers, and single-board computers such as the **Raspberry Pi**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *ARM architecture is a 32-bit RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) processor architecture known for its low power consumption, high performance, and simple instruction set. It uses a load-store architecture where data must be loaded into registers before any arithmetic or logical operation can be performed on it.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Brain of a Coffee Vending Machine

Imagine an automatic coffee vending machine:
- The **processor core (CPU)** is the "decision-maker" who decides what drink to dispense.
- The **registers** are the tiny, ultra-fast sticky notes on the operator's desk — the operator can only add milk, sugar, or coffee by reading the values written on these notes. There are very few of them (typically 13–37 in ARM), but they are the fastest storage in the entire machine.
- The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is the *single status monitor* above the desk showing whether the previous cup succeeded, whether the machine is in service mode, and whether a customer pressed the "stop" button (interrupt disable).
- The **pipeline** is the *assembly line* — while the operator is dispensing coffee, another worker is fetching the next cup, and yet another is preparing the next order.
- **Exceptions and Interrupts** are the *emergency buttons* — when a customer presses "Cancel" or the milk runs out, the operator pauses the current task and handles the emergency.
- The **vector table** is the *emergency response chart* pinned to the wall — each button has a specific procedure to follow, listed in a fixed order.
- The **Raspberry Pi 4** is the entire vending kiosk, complete with the touch-screen interface, coin slot, and the ARM brain inside.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Features of ARM

| Feature | Description | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| **Load/Store Architecture** | Only load/store instructions access memory; ALU operates only on registers | Simpler hardware, faster execution |
| **Fixed 32-bit Instructions** | Every standard ARM instruction is exactly 32 bits | Easy single-cycle decode, predictable pipeline |
| **37 Physical Registers** | 31 general-purpose + 6 status registers (in ARM7) | Reduces register pressure |
| **Banked Registers** | Different physical registers for different processor modes | Fast context switch on exception |
| **Conditional Execution** | Almost every instruction can be conditionally executed | Eliminates many branches, improves density |
| **Low Power Design** | Simpler decode logic, smaller die area | Ideal for battery-operated embedded systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ARM dominates embedded systems:**
> 1. **Power efficiency** — RISC design means fewer transistors, less heat, less battery drain.
> 2. **Licensable IP** — Companies like Qualcomm, Samsung, and Broadcom buy ARM designs and customize them, so innovation happens at both the IP and silicon levels.
> 3. **Scalability** — From tiny Cortex-M0 microcontrollers (used in washing machines) to powerful Cortex-A72 application processors (used in the Raspberry Pi 4), the same architectural philosophy scales across product lines.

## 1.4 The ARM Family Tree (Architectural Generations)

| Architecture | Year | Key Milestone |
|---|---|---|
| **ARMv1** | 1985 | First ARM1 processor, 26-bit address space |
| **ARMv2** | 1987 | Added MUL (multiply), co-processors |
| **ARMv3** | 1992 | 32-bit address space, CPSR |
| **ARMv4** | 1994 | Halfword/sign-extended loads |
| **ARMv4T** | 1995 | **Introduced Thumb (16-bit) instruction set** — used in ARM7TDMI |
| **ARMv5TE** | 1999 | Enhanced DSP, BLX, CLZ |
| **ARMv6** | 2001 | SIMD media instructions, improved Thumb-2 |
| **ARMv7** | 2004 | Cortex-A/R/M profiles, NEON SIMD, fully Thumb-2 |
| **ARMv8-A** | 2011 | **Introduced 64-bit AArch64** — used in Raspberry Pi 3/4 |
| **ARMv9** | 2021 | SVE2, enhanced security (CCA, Realm) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **ARM7TDMI** (a member of the **ARMv4T** family) is the canonical teaching example in KTU laboratories. It is a 32-bit RISC processor with a 3-stage pipeline and Thumb support. The Raspberry Pi 4, in contrast, uses a much newer **Cortex-A72 (ARMv8-A, 64-bit)** core — but the fundamental register model and exception philosophy remain the same.

## 1.5 Core Extensions (Architecture Variants)

These single-letter suffixes indicate optional hardware features added to an ARM core:

| Suffix | Meaning | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **T** | **Thumb** | 16-bit compressed instructions for code density |
| **D** | **Debug** | Embedded ICE (In-Circuit Emulator) logic for JTAG debugging |
| **M** | **Multiplier** | Enhanced hardware multiply (32×32 → 64-bit result) |
| **I** | **ICEbreaker** | Embedded ICE macrocell for breakpoints/watchpoints |
| **E** | **Enhanced DSP** | Saturating arithmetic, MAC instructions |
| **J** | **Jazelle** | Hardware Java bytecode execution |
| **F** | **Floating point** | VFP unit for floating-point math |
| **-S** | **Synthesizable** | Deliverable as soft IP (Verilog/VHDL) |
| **-SM** | **Smart Card** | Tamper-resistant variant |

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Decoding "ARM7TDMI-S":**
> - **ARM7** — generation 7, 3-stage pipeline, Von Neumann bus
> - **T** — supports Thumb 16-bit instruction set
> - **D** — has a JTAG debug interface
> - **M** — has the enhanced hardware multiplier
> - **I** — includes the ICEbreaker embedded trace macrocell
> - **-S** — synthesizable version for ASIC/FPGA implementation

## 1.6 Raspberry Pi 4 — Brief Description

The **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B** is a low-cost, credit-card-sized single-board computer designed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation. It is widely used in education, prototyping, IoT gateways, and lightweight servers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raspberry Pi 4 — Key Hardware Specifications:**
> - **SoC:** Broadcom **BCM2711**
> - **CPU:** Quad-core **ARM Cortex-A72 (ARMv8-A)** @ **1.8 GHz**, **64-bit**
> - **GPU:** VideoCore VI (OpenGL ES 3.0, 4K video decode)
> - **RAM:** 1 GB, 2 GB, 4 GB, or **8 GB LPDDR4**
> - **Networking:** Gigabit Ethernet, dual-band **802.11ac Wi-Fi**, Bluetooth 5.0
> - **USB:** 2 × USB 3.0, 2 × USB 2.0
> - **Display:** 2 × micro-HDMI (up to 4K@60 Hz dual display)
> - **Storage:** microSD card slot
> - **GPIO:** 40-pin header (preserved from earlier Pi models)

The Cortex-A72 implements the **AArch64** execution state for 64-bit operation but is fully backward-compatible with **AArch32** (32-bit ARM) code, which is why the classic ARM register model and vector table concepts continue to apply.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ARM Register Architecture (ARM7TDMI)

The ARM7 has **37 physical 32-bit registers**, of which **31 are general-purpose** and **6 are status registers**. They are arranged in a clever *banked* fashion so that each processor mode sees its own subset.

### 2.1.1 Register Classification

| Register Group | Count | Width | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **General-purpose (unbanked: R0–R7)** | 8 | 32-bit | Shared across all modes; used for data and addresses |
| **General-purpose (banked: R8–R12)** | 5 × 2 = 10 | 32-bit | Each has a User-mode copy + a Fast-Interrupt (FIQ) copy |
| **General-purpose (banked: R13–R14)** | 2 × 6 = 12 | 32-bit | Stack pointer (SP) and link register (LR) per mode |
| **Program Counter (R15 / PC)** | 1 | 32-bit | Holds address of next instruction (+8 offset in ARM state) |
| **CPSR** | 1 | 32-bit | Current Program Status Register — single, not banked |
| **SPSR (Saved PSR)** | 5 | 32-bit | One per privileged mode (saves CPSR on exception) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 31 general-purpose and not 16 or 32?**
> Banking R8–R14 in privileged modes means a context switch (e.g., entering an IRQ handler) does **not** need to save R8–R14 to the stack. The FIQ mode has its own private R8–R14, so the FIQ handler can begin executing immediately without stack-push overhead. The 5 SPSRs save the *previous* mode's CPSR for restoration on return.

### 2.1.2 Banked Register Mapping

| Mode | R13 (SP) | R14 (LR) | SPSR | R8–R12 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User** | R13_usr | R14_usr | — | R8–R12 (shared with System) |
| **System** | R13_sys | R14_sys | — | R8–R12 (shared with User) |
| **Supervisor (SVC)** | R13_svc | R14_svc | SPSR_svc | R8–R12 |
| **Abort (ABT)** | R13_abt | R14_abt | SPSR_abt | R8–R12 |
| **Undefined (UND)** | R13_und | R14_und | SPSR_und | R8–R12 |
| **Interrupt (IRQ)** | R13_irq | R14_irq | SPSR_irq | R8–R12 |
| **Fast Interrupt (FIQ)** | R13_fiq | R14_fiq | SPSR_fiq | **R8_fiq–R12_fiq (separate)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In the KTU lab examination, you will often be asked *"Why does FIQ have more banked registers than other modes?"* The answer is **performance**: FIQ is designed for *latency-critical* high-speed peripherals. By having R8–R12_fiq banked separately, the FIQ handler can use these registers immediately without saving the User-mode values to memory, eliminating stack-push latency.

## 2.2 The Current Program Status Register (CPSR)

The **CPSR** is a single 32-bit register that holds:
1. **Condition flags** (result of the last ALU operation)
2. **Interrupt-disable bits**
3. **Thumb state bit**
4. **Processor mode bits**

### 2.2.1 CPSR Bit Layout

| Bit(s) | Field | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **31** | **N** | **Negative** — set if ALU result is negative (bit 31 = 1) |
| **30** | **Z** | **Zero** — set if ALU result is zero |
| **29** | **C** | **Carry** — set if unsigned overflow / borrow occurs |
| **28** | **V** | **oVerflow** — set if signed overflow occurs |
| **27–25** | — | Reserved (should be 1, 0, 0 on ARMv5+) |
| **24** | **J** | **Jazelle** — 1 = processor in Jazelle state |
| **23–20** | — | Reserved |
| **19** | **I** | **IRQ disable** — 1 = normal IRQs masked |
| **18** | **F** | **FIQ disable** — 1 = FIQs masked |
| **17** | **T** | **Thumb** — 1 = processor in Thumb state |
| **16–9** | — | Reserved / GE bits (on some variants) |
| **8–5** | — | Reserved |
| **4–0** | **M[4:0]** | **Mode** field — selects current processor mode |

### 2.2.2 Mode Encoding (M[4:0])

| M[4:0] | Mode | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **10000 (0x10)** | User | Normal user application code |
| **10001 (0x11)** | FIQ | Fast interrupt handling |
| **10010 (0x12)** | IRQ | Normal interrupt handling |
| **10011 (0x13)** | Supervisor | OS kernel, software interrupts (SWI) |
| **10111 (0x17)** | Abort | Memory access failures |
| **11011 (0x1B)** | Undefined | Undefined instructions |
| **11111 (0x1F)** | System | Privileged mode using User registers |
| **10110 (0x16)** *(ARMv6+)* | Monitor | Secure-world / TrustZone switching |

### 2.2.3 Condition Flag Interpretation

| Flag | Symbol | Set When | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N** | `MI` (Minus) | Result[31] = 1 | Signed result is negative |
| **Z** | `EQ` (Equal) | Result = 0 | Equality / loop terminators |
| **C** | `CS` / `CC` | Carry-out from unsigned | Unsigned overflow, no-borrow |
| **V** | `VS` / `VC` | Signed overflow | Signed arithmetic overflow detection |

> [!NOTE]
> The four flags **N, Z, C, V** are commonly memorized as the **"NZCV"** group and constitute the *Condition Code* flags that drive ARM's signature *conditional execution* feature. The mnemonic in exam answers is often: **"N is Negative, Z is Zero, C is Carry, V is oVerflow"**.

## 2.3 The ARM7 Three-Stage Pipeline

The ARM7 uses a classic **3-stage pipeline**:

| Stage | Function | Key Action |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Fetch (F)** | Load instruction from memory | PC → memory, instruction → IR |
| **2. Decode (D)** | Decode operands, read register file | Register read, operand forwarding prepared |
| **3. Execute (E)** | ALU operation, memory access, write-back | ALU result → register, or memory ← / → register |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — The "+8" PC offset:**
> In ARM state, the **PC (R15) always reads as the current instruction address + 8**. Why? Because the pipeline has already fetched two instructions ahead. When you are executing instruction at `0x4000`, the CPU has already fetched `0x4004` and `0x4008`. The architectural PC is therefore `0x4008`. In **Thumb state**, the offset is **+4** (because Thumb instructions are 16-bit, so the pipeline depth in terms of bytes is halved).

### 2.3.1 Pipeline Throughput Illustration

| Cycle → | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Instr 1 (0x4000)** | F | D | E | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Instr 2 (0x4004)** | — | F | D | E | — | — | — | — |
| **Instr 3 (0x4008)** | — | — | F | D | E | — | — | — |
| **Instr 4 (0x400C)** | — | — | — | F | D | E | — | — |

After the **pipeline fill latency** (2 cycles), the processor completes **one instruction per cycle** — the steady-state throughput goal of any RISC design.

### 2.3.2 Evolution of ARM Pipeline Depth

| Core | Pipeline Depth | Year | Example Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ARM7** | 3-stage | 1994 | Embedded microcontrollers, Lego Mindstorms |
| **ARM9** | 5-stage | 1998 | Early smartphones, Nokia N-Gage |
| **ARM11** | 8-stage | 2002 | iPhone 1st generation |
| **Cortex-A7** | 8-stage partial | 2011 | Raspberry Pi 2 (big.LITTLE partner) |
| **Cortex-A53** | 8-stage in-order | 2012 | Raspberry Pi 3 |
| **Cortex-A72** | 15-stage out-of-order | 2015 | **Raspberry Pi 4** |
| **Cortex-X1 / X4** | Up to 20-stage OoO | 2020+ | High-end smartphones |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Deeper pipelines increase maximum clock frequency (each stage does less work) but increase the *cost of a branch misprediction*. This is why the Raspberry Pi 4's Cortex-A72 uses **out-of-order execution** and **speculative branching** — to hide the deep-pipeline penalty.

## 2.4 Exceptions and Interrupts

### 2.4.1 The Seven Exception Sources (ARMv4/v5)

| Exception | Mode Entered | Vector Address | Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reset** | Supervisor (SVC) | **0x00000000** | Power-on / watchdog reset |
| **Undefined Instruction** | Undefined (UND) | **0x00000004** | Encountered an unknown opcode |
| **Software Interrupt (SWI)** | Supervisor (SVC) | **0x00000008** | Explicit `SVC` call from user code |
| **Prefetch Abort** | Abort (ABT) | **0x0000000C** | Instruction fetch from invalid memory |
| **Data Abort** | Abort (ABT) | **0x00000010** | Load/store from invalid memory |
| **IRQ** | IRQ | **0x00000018** | Normal interrupt request from peripheral |
| **FIQ** | FIQ | **0x0000001C** | Fast interrupt request from peripheral |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice that the vector table has 8 slots, but only 7 are used** — the slot at **0x00000014** is reserved on classic ARM. On Cortex-A (ARMv7-A / v8), the vector table may be relocated to **0xFFFF0000** (high-vec) or to a configurable `VBAR` register address.

### 2.4.2 Exception Handling Sequence

When an exception is taken, the ARM core automatically performs **5 hardware steps**:

1. **Save** the next instruction's address `(PC + 4 or + 8)` into **R14 (LR)** of the new mode.
2. **Copy** the current **CPSR** into the **SPSR** of the new mode.
3. **Force** the CPSR mode bits to the corresponding exception mode.
4. **Force** the PC to fetch from the **vector table entry** for that exception.
5. **Disable** further interrupts as appropriate (IRQs disabled on entry, FIQs disabled on Reset only).

To return from the handler, software must perform the reverse:
- `MOVS PC, LR`  *(atomically restores CPSR from SPSR and resumes at LR)*

## 2.5 The Vector Table

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition — Vector Table:**
> *The vector table is a table of 32-bit words stored at a known memory address (usually 0x00000000), where each entry is the address of an exception handler routine. The CPU loads the PC directly from the appropriate vector when an exception occurs.*

The vector table is **not a list of handler functions** — it is a list of **branch instructions** (typically `B <handler>` or `LDR PC, [PC, #offset]`) that jump to the actual handler. Because each vector slot is only 4 bytes, a direct branch (range ±32 MB) is usually sufficient.

### 2.5.1 Sample Vector Table (ARM Assembly)

```asm
        B   Reset_Handler       ; 0x00 — Reset
        B   Undef_Handler       ; 0x04 — Undefined instruction
        B   SVC_Handler         ; 0x08 — Software interrupt
        B   PAbt_Handler        ; 0x0C — Prefetch abort
        B   DAbt_Handler        ; 0x10 — Data abort
        NOP                     ; 0x14 — Reserved (on classic ARM)
        B   IRQ_Handler         ; 0x18 — IRQ
        B   FIQ_Handler         ; 0x1C — FIQ
```

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **mathematical relationships and architectural rules** that are commonly tested in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize these — they appear in nearly every ARM-processor question paper.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PC offset in ARM state** | $PC_{read} = \text{current address} + 8$ | Bytes; due to 2-stage fetch-ahead |
| 2 | **PC offset in Thumb state** | $PC_{read} = \text{current address} + 4$ | Bytes; pipeline offset halved |
| 3 | **Pipeline throughput** | $\text{IPC}_{steady} = 1$ | Instructions per cycle, classic RISC goal |
| 4 | **Pipeline fill latency** | $L_{fill} = N_{stages} - 1$ | For 3-stage pipeline = **2 cycles** |
| 5 | **Branch penalty (not taken)** | $P = 0$ cycles | Always fetched correctly |
| 6 | **Branch penalty (taken, no prediction)** | $P = N_{stages} - 1$ | For ARM7: 2-cycle penalty |
| 7 | **Exception latency (best case)** | $T_{exc} = T_{vector fetch}$ | Usually 2–3 cycles |
| 8 | **Carry flag after unsigned add** | $C = 1 \iff A + B \geq 2^{32}$ | Unsigned overflow detection |
| 9 | **Overflow flag after signed add** | $V = (\overline{A_{31}} \cdot \overline{B_{31}} \cdot R_{31}) + (A_{31} \cdot B_{31} \cdot \overline{R_{31}})$ | Signed overflow logic |
| 10 | **Zero flag** | $Z = 1 \iff R = 0$ | True zero across all 32 bits |
| 11 | **Negative flag** | $N = R_{31}$ | MSB of result |
| 12 | **Thumb code size savings** | $\text{Saving} \approx 30\%$ | Vs equivalent ARM code |
| 13 | **Cortex-A72 clock** (Pi 4) | $f_{clk} = 1.8$ GHz | Default |
| 14 | **Cortex-A72 pipeline depth** | $N_{stages} = 15$ | Out-of-order superscalar |
| 15 | **Raspberry Pi 4 RAM** | $R_{RAM} \in \{1, 2, 4, 8\}$ GB | LPDDR4 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table syntax:** The vertical bars inside expressions like $\vert A_{31} \vert$ are written using `\vert` (or `\mid`) in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. This is a strict KTU-Premier-Engine formatting rule.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile SoCs (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series):** All use ARM cores — understanding the register model is essential for kernel/driver developers.
- **Automotive ECUs:** ARM Cortex-R cores run in safety-critical systems; understanding the exception model and CPSR is essential for ISO 26262 compliance.
- **IoT edge devices:** ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers (STM32, NXP Kinetis) use a *subset* of the same architecture, so ARM7TDMI fundamentals transfer directly.
- **Raspberry Pi programming:** Linux kernel modules, bare-metal OS development, and real-time interrupt handling all depend on a deep understanding of the vector table and CPSR.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why does the ARM PC read as `current_address + 8`?

This is one of the most frequently asked questions in KTU exams. Let us derive it rigorously.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider the ARM7 with a **3-stage pipeline**: **F → D → E**.
Let us trace the execution of an instruction located at memory address `0x4000`.

### 3.1.2 Pipeline Trace

| Cycle | Action | Address Being Fetched | Address Being Executed | Architectural PC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Fetch `INSTR@0x4000` | `0x4000` | — | `0x4008` (read = `+8`) |
| **2** | Decode `INSTR@0x4000` ; Fetch `INSTR@0x4004` | `0x4004` | `0x4000` | `0x400C` |
| **3** | Execute `INSTR@0x4000` ; Decode `INSTR@0x4004` ; Fetch `INSTR@0x4008` | `0x4008` | `0x4000` | `0x4010` |

### 3.1.3 Mathematical Relationship

Define:
- $A_{exec}$ = address of the instruction currently in the **Execute** stage
- $A_{fetch}$ = address of the instruction currently in the **Fetch** stage
- $N_{pipe} = 3$ (number of pipeline stages)

The architectural PC is the address being fetched **2 stages** ahead:

$$PC_{arch} = A_{exec} + 2 \times 4 = A_{exec} + 8$$

because each instruction occupies **4 bytes** in ARM state.

### 3.1.4 General Formula

For a processor with $N_{pipe}$ pipeline stages executing instructions of $W$ bytes each:

$$PC_{offset} = (N_{pipe} - 1) \times W$$

**Verification for ARM7:** $PC_{offset} = (3 - 1) \times 4 = 8$ ✓
**Verification for Thumb (16-bit, $W=2$):** $PC_{offset} = (3 - 1) \times 2 = 4$ ✓
**Verification for Cortex-A72 (15-stage, $W=4$):** $PC_{offset} = (15 - 1) \times 4 = 56$ bytes (architecturally the PC is implementation-defined but points ~2 instructions ahead).

## 3.2 Derivation: Condition Flags from an Arithmetic Operation

### 3.2.1 Setup

Suppose we perform **signed** addition: `R = A + B`, where all values are 32-bit two's complement.

### 3.2.2 Zero Flag (Z)

By definition, the Zero flag is set when the result equals zero. Therefore:

$$Z = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } R = 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

In boolean form, $Z$ is the **NOR of all 32 result bits**:

$$Z = \overline{(R_{31} \vee R_{30} \vee R_{29} \vee \ldots \vee R_{0})}$$

### 3.2.3 Negative Flag (N)

The Negative flag is simply the most-significant bit of the result:

$$N = R_{31}$$

This is because two's-complement negatives have `1` in the MSB.

### 3.2.4 Carry Flag (C) — Unsigned

For **unsigned** addition, the carry flag is the **carry-out** of bit 31:

$$C_{out} = \text{majority function of } (A_{31}, B_{31}, K_{31})$$

where $K_{31}$ is the carry **into** bit 31:

$$C_{out} = (A_{31} \cdot B_{31}) + (A_{31} \cdot K_{31}) + (B_{31} \cdot K_{31})$$

The condition is:

$$C = 1 \iff (A + B) \geq 2^{32} \quad (\text{unsigned overflow})$$

### 3.2.5 Overflow Flag (V) — Signed

Signed overflow occurs when two **same-signed** operands produce a result of the **opposite sign**. Boolean expression:

$$V = (\overline{A_{31}} \cdot \overline{B_{31}} \cdot R_{31}) + (A_{31} \cdot B_{31} \cdot \overline{R_{31}})$$

**Reading the equation:**
- The first product term fires when both operands are **positive** (`A₃₁=0, B₃₁=0`) but the result is **negative** (`R₃₁=1`) — impossible, hence overflow.
- The second product term fires when both operands are **negative** (`A₃₁=1, B₃₁=1`) but the result is **positive** (`R₃₁=0`) — also overflow.

### 3.2.6 Worked Numerical Example

Let $A = 0x7FFFFFFF$ (the largest positive signed int) and $B = 0x00000001$.

**Step 1 — Binary addition:**

$$A = 0111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111$$
$$B = 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0001$$
$$R = 1000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000 = 0x80000000$$

**Step 2 — Interpret result:**

$$\begin{aligned} N &= R_{31} = 1 \\
Z &= (R \neq 0) = 0 \\
C &= \text{carry-out of bit 31} = 0 \quad (\text{no unsigned overflow: } 2^{31}-1 + 1 = 2^{31} < 2^{32}) \\
V &= (A_{31}=0) \cdot (B_{31}=0) \cdot (R_{31}=1) = 1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
- Mathematically, $2{,}147{,}483{,}647 + 1 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648$, which is **outside** the signed 32-bit range $[-2^{31}, 2^{31}-1]$.
- Therefore **V = 1** (signed overflow), and **N = 1** (the result "appears" negative).
- The ARM CPU will set the CPSR flags to **N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=1**, exactly matching our derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common KTU question asks: *"After adding 0x7FFFFFFF and 1 on ARM, what are the N, Z, C, V flags?"* The expected answer is **N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=1**. Be sure to show the work, *not* just the final flags.

## 3.3 Derivation: Exception Vector Table Address Calculation

### 3.3.1 Setup

Suppose the vector table base is at `0x00000000`. Each vector is a 4-byte slot.

### 3.3.2 Address Computation

The address of the $n$-th vector (zero-indexed) is:

$$V_{addr}(n) = V_{base} + n \cdot 4$$

where $V_{base} = 0x00000000$ in default configuration, and $n \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 7\}$.

### 3.3.3 Worked Example: Vector Table Entry Mapping

| Exception | $n$ | $V_{addr}(n)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Reset | 0 | `0x00000000` |
| Undefined Instruction | 1 | `0x00000004` |
| SWI (SVC) | 2 | `0x00000008` |
| Prefetch Abort | 3 | `0x0000000C` |
| Data Abort | 4 | `0x00000010` |
| (Reserved) | 5 | `0x00000014` |
| IRQ | 6 | `0x00000018` |
| FIQ | 7 | `0x0000001C` |

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Vector Option:** On ARM7TDMI, the CPU can be configured at reset (via the `VINITHI` input pin) to fetch vectors from `0xFFFF0000` (high-vec) instead. This is useful in systems where `0x00000000` is RAM (e.g., bootloader scenarios). On Cortex-A, the vector base is given by the **`VBAR`** (Vector Base Address Register, `S3_0_C12_C0_0`).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ARM7 vs. ARM Cortex-A72 (Pi 4)

This matrix is a high-yield comparison table for KTU questions that ask *"Compare ARM7 and the processor used in the Raspberry Pi 4."*

| Parameter | **ARM7TDMI** | **ARM Cortex-A72 (in Pi 4)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | ARMv4T | ARMv8-A |
| **ISA width** | 32-bit (ARM) + 16-bit (Thumb) | 64-bit (AArch64) + 32-bit (AArch32) + Thumb/Thumb-2 |
| **Pipeline stages** | 3 (in-order) | 15 (out-of-order, superscalar, 3-wide decode) |
| **Execution order** | Strictly in-order | Out-of-order with speculation |
| **Issue width** | 1 instruction/cycle | Up to 3 instructions/cycle |
| **Caches** | None on chip (unified memory bus) | 32 KB L1 I-cache + 48 KB L1 D-cache per core, 1 MB L2 shared |
| **Branch prediction** | None | Dynamic (BTB, Gshare, return-stack) |
| **MMU** | None (uses MPU) | Full MMU with virtual memory (required for Linux) |
| **Typical clock** | 50–100 MHz | 1.8 GHz |
| **Power** | < 100 mW per core | ~1 W per core at 1.8 GHz |
| **Vector table location** | `0x00000000` (or `0xFFFF0000`) | Configurable via `VBAR_EL1` / `VBAR_EL2` |
| **Use case** | Embedded microcontrollers, Lego Mindstorms NXT | Smartphones, Raspberry Pi 4, laptops, edge servers |
| **Register count (visible to software)** | 16 (in User mode) + banked | 31 general-purpose X0–X30 (in AArch64) |
| **Endianness** | Bi-endian (configurable) | Bi-endian (default: little-endian) |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: A Software Interrupt (SWI) Call

The `SVC` (formerly `SWI`) instruction is how user code requests OS services. Let us trace it.

### 3.5.1 Steps Performed by the Hardware

**Step 1 — User code executes `SVC #0x42`:**
The opcode contains a 24-bit comment field (often used to encode the syscall number).

**Step 2 — The CPU detects the SVC opcode in the Decode stage and treats it as an exception.**

**Step 3 — Automatic state save:**
- `R14_svc ← PC + 4` (return address to user code)
- `SPSR_svc ← CPSR`
- `CPSR[4:0] ← 0b10011` (enter Supervisor mode)
- `CPSR[7] ← 1` (disable IRQs in Supervisor mode)
- `CPSR[8] ← 1` (disable FIQs — only on Reset; for SVC, FIQ status is preserved)
- `PC ← 0x00000008` (vector table entry for SVC)

**Step 4 — Supervisor handler begins execution.**
The handler can extract the SVC number by reading the comment field from the SVC instruction:
- It knows `LR_svc - 4` points to the SVC instruction.
- It can load that word, mask off the top 8 bits (`BIC r0, r0, #0xFF000000`), leaving the 24-bit comment as the syscall number.

**Step 5 — Return to user code:**
- `MOVS PC, LR`  — atomically:
  - `CPSR ← SPSR_svc` (restore previous flags and mode)
  - `PC ← LR_svc` (resume user code at the instruction after SVC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This 5-step hardware save is the entire reason ARM can deliver fast, deterministic interrupt latency. Compare to a software-driven context switch (e.g., x86 `INT n` historically required a full register push) — the banked registers and dedicated SPSR mean **R0–R12, R13, R14, and CPSR are all preserved in a single cycle**.

## 3.6 Derivation: Code-Size Benefit of Thumb Mode

Suppose an algorithm requires $I_{ARM}$ ARM instructions.

- ARM instruction size: $S_{ARM} = 4$ bytes
- Thumb instruction size: $S_{Thumb} = 2$ bytes

In Thumb mode, similar functionality requires approximately $I_{Thumb} \approx 1.4 \cdot I_{ARM}$ instructions (because Thumb's restricted encoding requires more instructions to do the same work — a 16-bit opcode has fewer operand bits).

**Code-size comparison:**

$$\text{Size}_{ARM} = I_{ARM} \times 4$$

$$\text{Size}_{Thumb} = (1.4 \cdot I_{ARM}) \times 2 = 2.8 \cdot I_{ARM}$$

**Savings:**

$$\Delta = \frac{4 - 2.8}{4} = 0.30 = 30\%$$

Hence, switching to Thumb typically saves ~30% code size — at a small cost (~15%) in execution speed due to more instructions. This is why most modern ARM systems (including the Raspberry Pi 4) use Thumb-2 (a mix of 16- and 32-bit encodings) to balance size and speed.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the CPSR and list its key fields.  *(3 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is a 32-bit register in the ARM processor that holds the current processor state. Its key fields are:

1. **Condition Flags (N, Z, C, V):** Bits 31–28, reflect the result of the last ALU operation.
2. **Interrupt Disable Bits (I, F):** Bits 19 and 18; mask IRQ and FIQ respectively.
3. **Thumb State Bit (T):** Bit 17; 1 = processor executing Thumb (16-bit) instructions.
4. **Mode Bits (M[4:0]):** Bits 4–0; encode the current processor mode (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Breakdown (as per KTU valuation key):** Definition: 1 mark, Fields enumeration: 2 marks.

---

### Question 2: What is a vector table? List the seven ARM exception types with their vector addresses.  *(3 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

The **vector table** is a table of 32-bit words stored at a fixed memory address (typically `0x00000000`) that the ARM processor consults whenever an exception occurs. Each entry contains a branch instruction that jumps to the corresponding exception handler routine.

| Exception | Vector Address |
|---|---|
| Reset | `0x00000000` |
| Undefined Instruction | `0x00000004` |
| Software Interrupt (SVC) | `0x00000008` |
| Prefetch Abort | `0x0000000C` |
| Data Abort | `0x00000010` |
| IRQ | `0x00000018` |
| FIQ | `0x0000001C` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Breakdown:** Definition: 1 mark, Table (7 entries × ~0.25 mark): 2 marks.

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Part B questions offer internal choice between two alternatives. The student attempts **one** of the two. Each question has two sub-parts, typically 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks): ARM Register Model and CPSR  *(Internal Choice 1)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, Model Paper Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the ARM7 register architecture in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the banked register concept and explain why the FIQ mode has more banked registers than other modes.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The ARM7TDMI processor has **37 physical 32-bit registers**, of which 31 are general-purpose and 6 are status registers. These registers are organized into a *banked* structure so that different processor modes have private copies of certain registers, enabling fast context switches.

**Register Classification (3 marks):**
1. **Unbanked registers (R0–R7):** A single physical register for each, shared across all modes.
2. **Banked registers R8–R12:** Two physical copies — one for User/System, one for FIQ.
3. **Banked registers R13 (SP) and R14 (LR):** Separate physical copies for **six** privileged modes (FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System).
4. **R15 (PC):** A single physical register, accessible from all modes.
5. **CPSR:** A single physical register, holding current processor state.
6. **SPSR:** Five physical copies (one per privileged mode except System), used to save CPSR on exception.

**Banked Register Concept (2 marks):**
Banking means that **R8–R14 in a privileged mode are physically different registers** from those in User mode. When the CPU enters an exception, the banked registers of the new mode are automatically selected — there is no need for software to save the previous mode's R8–R14 to the stack. On return (`MOVS PC, LR`), the original User-mode registers become visible again.

**Why FIQ has more banked registers (1 mark):**
The FIQ is designed for **latency-critical, high-speed peripherals** (e.g., DMA completion, high-speed data streams). By giving FIQ its own private **R8_fiq–R12_fiq** (in addition to the standard R13_fiq, R14_fiq, and SPSR_fiq), the FIQ handler can begin data processing **immediately** without any stack-push of R8–R12. This reduces the worst-case interrupt latency. No other exception mode has this optimization because none of them require such low latency.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the Current Program Status Register (CPSR) with a neat bit-layout diagram. Explain the role of N, Z, C, V flags with an example.**

**Model Solution:**

**CPSR Layout (3 marks):** *(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.3.)*
The CPSR is a 32-bit register divided into:
- **Bits 31–28:** Condition flags N, Z, C, V
- **Bit 24:** J (Jazelle state)
- **Bits 19, 18:** I, F (IRQ and FIQ disable)
- **Bit 17:** T (Thumb state)
- **Bits 4–0:** M[4:0] (Processor Mode)

**Role of N, Z, C, V (3 marks):**
- **N (Negative):** Set when the ALU result's MSB is 1, i.e., the result is negative in two's-complement signed interpretation.
- **Z (Zero):** Set when the ALU result is exactly 0.
- **C (Carry):** Set when an unsigned operation overflows past bit 31 (or when a borrow does **not** occur on subtraction).
- **V (oVerflow):** Set when a signed operation produces a result that cannot be represented in 32 bits (i.e., sign changes incorrectly).

**Example (1 mark):**
Computing `0x7FFFFFFF + 0x00000001`:
- Result = `0x80000000` (which is -2,147,483,648 in signed)
- `N = 1` (result MSB is 1)
- `Z = 0` (result is not zero)
- `C = 0` (no unsigned carry-out, since 2,147,483,647 + 1 = 2,147,483,648 < 4,294,967,296)
- `V = 1` (signed overflow: positive + positive = negative)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> A common mistake students make is setting **C = 1** for the above example, confusing *signed* overflow with *unsigned* carry. **C = 0** because there is no carry out of bit 31. The signed overflow is captured by **V**, not C. Marks will be deducted if you mix up the two.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks): Pipelining, Exceptions, and Raspberry Pi 4  *(Internal Choice 2)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, Model Paper Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the 3-stage pipeline of ARM7 with a timing diagram. Show why the PC reads as `current_address + 8` in ARM state.**

**Model Solution:**

**Pipeline Stages (2 marks):**
The ARM7 uses a 3-stage pipeline:
1. **Fetch (F):** Reads the instruction from memory at the address in the PC.
2. **Decode (D):** Decodes the instruction, identifies operands, and reads source registers from the register file.
3. **Execute (E):** Performs the ALU operation, handles memory access (load/store), and writes back the result to the destination register.

**Timing Diagram (3 marks):**

| Cycle | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I1 (0x4000)** | F | D | E | — | — | — | — |
| **I2 (0x4004)** | — | F | D | E | — | — | — |
| **I3 (0x4008)** | — | — | F | D | E | — | — |
| **I4 (0x400C)** | — | — | — | F | D | E | — |

**Why PC reads `+8` (2 marks):**
At any given cycle, the processor is in stage **E** for the instruction at address `A`, while simultaneously fetching the instruction at `A + 8` (two instructions ahead). When the architectural definition of the PC is consulted (e.g., by `MOV PC, LR`), it returns the address of the instruction currently being **fetched**, which is 8 bytes ahead of the currently executing instruction (each ARM instruction is 4 bytes, and the pipeline is 2 stages ahead). Therefore:

$$PC_{read} = A_{execute} + (2 \times 4) = A_{execute} + 8$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the Raspberry Pi 4 board with a neat block diagram. Compare its processor (Cortex-A72) with the ARM7 in terms of pipeline depth, register model, and architecture version.**

**Model Solution:**

**Raspberry Pi 4 Description (3 marks):**
The Raspberry Pi 4 Model B is a single-board computer featuring:
- **SoC:** Broadcom BCM2711
- **CPU:** Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 @ 1.8 GHz, **64-bit (AArch64)**
- **RAM:** 1/2/4/8 GB LPDDR4
- **GPU:** VideoCore VI with 4K video decode
- **Connectivity:** Gigabit Ethernet, 802.11ac Wi-Fi, Bluetooth 5.0
- **I/O:** 2× USB 3.0, 2× USB 2.0, 2× micro-HDMI, 40-pin GPIO header, microSD slot, MIPI CSI/DSI

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | ARM7TDMI | Cortex-A72 (Pi 4) |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | ARMv4T | ARMv8-A |
| ISA | 32-bit ARM + 16-bit Thumb | 64-bit AArch64 + 32-bit AArch32 + Thumb-2 |
| Pipeline | 3-stage in-order | 15-stage out-of-order, 3-wide |
| Registers | 16 visible (User), banked to 37 | 31 × 64-bit (X0–X30), with banked EL1/EL2/EL3 |
| Use case | Embedded MCUs | Smartphones, Pi 4, laptops |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
While ARM7 and Cortex-A72 differ vastly in pipeline depth and execution sophistication, both share the foundational ARM philosophy: **load-store architecture, fixed-length instruction encoding, conditional execution, and a banked register model** — making the transition from textbook ARM7 knowledge to Pi 4 programming a natural progression.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> Students often **state that Cortex-A72 is "32-bit"** or claim it uses the **"ARM7 architecture"**. The Raspberry Pi 4 uses **Cortex-A72 (ARMv8-A)**, which is a **64-bit** core. Mixing up the architecture version will cost you a full mark on the comparison.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### Core ARM Fundamentals
- ✅ **ARM = Advanced RISC Machine**, originally Acorn RISC Machine, a 32-bit (now 64-bit) RISC ISA.
- ✅ **Load-store architecture**: ALU works only on registers; only load/store instructions touch memory.
- ✅ **Fixed 32-bit instructions** in ARM state; **16-bit** in Thumb state.
- ✅ **R15 = PC**, **R13 = SP**, **R14 = LR**.

### Register Architecture
- ✅ **37 physical registers** in ARM7: 31 general-purpose + 6 status.
- ✅ **User mode sees 16 registers** (R0–R15).
- ✅ **Banked registers** R8_fiq–R14_fiq are private to FIQ; R13/R14 are banked for **6** privileged modes.
- ✅ **R8–R12** are banked **only in FIQ** — this is what gives FIQ its low-latency property.
- ✅ **CPSR** is the single status register; **SPSR** is per privileged mode (5 copies, none in User/System).

### CPSR
- ✅ Bit positions to remember: **N=31, Z=30, C=29, V=28, J=24, I=19, F=18, T=17, M=4:0**.
- ✅ **Mode encodings:** User=10000, FIQ=10001, IRQ=10010, SVC=10011, ABT=10111, UND=11011, System=11111.
- ✅ **I = 1 disables IRQ**; **F = 1 disables FIQ**; **T = 1 → Thumb state**.

### Pipeline
- ✅ **ARM7 = 3-stage pipeline** (Fetch, Decode, Execute).
- ✅ **PC reads as `+8` in ARM state**, `+4` in Thumb state.
- ✅ **Cortex-A72 in Pi 4 = 15-stage, out-of-order, superscalar**.
- ✅ **Pipeline fill latency = N_stages − 1**; for ARM7 = 2 cycles.

### Exceptions & Vector Table
- ✅ **Seven exception types**: Reset, Undefined, SVC, Prefetch Abort, Data Abort, IRQ, FIQ.
- ✅ Vector table at **`0x00000000`**, each entry **4 bytes** wide, **reserved slot at 0x14**.
- ✅ On exception, hardware saves **PC+4** (or +8) to **LR_new**, copies **CPSR to SPSR_new**, sets mode bits, jumps to vector.
- ✅ Return with **`MOVS PC, LR`** — atomically restores CPSR from SPSR.

### Extensions
- ✅ **TDMI-S** decoding: T=Thumb, D=Debug, M=Multiplier, I=ICE, S=Synthesizable.

### Architecture Revisions
- ✅ **ARMv4T** introduced Thumb.
- ✅ **ARMv7** introduced Cortex-A/R/M profiles.
- ✅ **ARMv8-A** introduced 64-bit AArch64.
- ✅ **ARMv9** added SVE2 and confidential compute.

### Raspberry Pi 4
- ✅ **SoC**: Broadcom **BCM2711**.
- ✅ **CPU**: Quad-core **Cortex-A72**, **64-bit**, **1.8 GHz**.
- ✅ **RAM**: up to **8 GB LPDDR4**.
- ✅ **GPU**: VideoCore VI.
- ✅ **Connectivity**: Gigabit Ethernet, Wi-Fi 802.11ac, Bluetooth 5.0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In the KTU ESE, expect at least **one Part A (3 marks)** question directly from this module (almost always on CPSR, vector table, or register banking), and **one Part B (14 marks)** question comparing ARM7 with a modern core (e.g., Cortex-A72 in Pi 4). Memorize the comparison table in Section 3.4 — it is the single highest-yield knowledge chunk.
